definition: To lower or depress; to throw or cast down.
To destroy the self-possession of; to confuse or confound, as by exciting suddenly a consciousness of guilt, mistake, or inferiority.
to become less intense or widespread; to reduce in amount or degree
characteristic: Think of a storm calming down or a fever dropping. It describes something powerful gradually fading away.

definition: Father; religious superior; -- in the Syriac, Coptic, and Ethiopic churches, a title given to the bishops, and by the bishops to the patriarch.
A female superior or governess of a nunnery, or convent of nuns, having the same authority over the nuns which the abbots have over the monks.
A monastery or society of persons of either sex, secluded from the world and devoted to religion and celibacy.
The superior or head of an abbey. 2. One of a class of bishops whose sees were formerly abbeys.
To surrender or relinquish, as sovereign power; to withdraw definitely from filling or exercising, as a high office, station, dignity.
The belly, or that part of the body between the thorax and the pelvis.
To take away surreptitiously by force; to carry away (a human being) wrongfully and usually by violence.
The act of abducing or abducting; a drawing apart; a carrying away. Roget.
In bed, or on the bed. Not to be abed after midnight. Shak. 2. To childbed (in the phrase "brought abed," that is, delivered of a child).
a departure from what is normal, usual, or expected, typically unwelcome
characteristic: It is the odd one out, a strange glitch that does not fit the pattern of everything around it.

definition: To instigate or encourage by aid or countenance; -- used in a bad sense of persons and acts.
Expectancy; condition of being undetermined. Note: When there is no person in existence in whom an inheritance (or a dignity) can vest, it is said to be in abeyance, that is, in expectation.
To shrink back with shuddering from; to regard with horror or detestation.
Extreme hatred or detestation; the feeling of utter dislike.
Abhorring; detesting; having or showing abhorrence; loathing; hence, strongly opposed to.
The state of abiding; abode; continuance; compliance (with). The Christians had no longer abidance in the holy hill of Palestine.
To wait; to pause; to delay. Chaucer. 2. To stay; to continue in a place.
Continuing; lasting.
A lady's waiting-maid. Pepys. Her abigail reported that Mrs. Gutheridge had a set of night curls for sleeping in.
Cast down; low-lying. From the safe shore their floating carcasses And broken chariot wheels.
To renounce upon oath; to forswear; to disavow.
On fire; in a blaze, gleaming. Milman. All ablaze with crimson and gold.
The act of washing or cleansing; specifically, the washing of the body, or some part of it, as a religious rite.
To deny and reject; to abjure. Sir E. Sandys. Farrar.
a denial; a renunciation. With abnegation of God, of his honor, and of religion, they may retain the friendship of the court.
Not conformed to rule or system; deviating from the type; anomalous; irregular.
To turn from as ill-omened; to hate in the highest degree, as if with religious dread.
The feeling of extreme disgust and hatred; abhorrence; detestation; loathing.
First; original; indigenous; primitive; native.
The earliest known inhabitants of a country; native races. 2. The original fauna and flora of a geographical area.
Brought forth prematurely. 2. (Biol.) Rendered abortive or sterile; undeveloped.
The act of giving premature birth; particularly, the expulsion of the human fetus prematurely, or before it is capable of sustaining life.
To be in great plenty; to be very prevalent; to be plentiful. The wild boar which abounds in some parts of the continent of Europe.
Above the board or table. Hence: in open sight; without trick, concealment, or deception.
A mystical word or collocation of letters written as in the figure. Worn on an amulet it was supposed to ward off fever.
The act of abrading, wearing, or rubbing off; the wearing away by friction.
Producing abrasion. Ure.
Side by side, with breasts in a line.
To make shorter; to shorten in duration; to lessen; to diminish; to curtail.
At large; widely; broadly; over a wide space.
Abrogated; abolished. Latimer. 1. To annul by an authoritative act; to abolish by the authority of the maker or his successor.
In an abrupt manner; without giving notice, or without the usual forms.
A collection of pus or purulent matter in any tissue or organ of the body, the result of a morbid process.
The act or process of cutting off. "Not to be cured without the abscission of a member." Jer.
to leave hurriedly and secretly, typically to avoid detection or arrest
characteristic: Picture a thief sneaking away in the night with the stolen money before anyone notices.

definition: A state of being absent or withdrawn from a place or from companionship.
The plant absinthium or common wormwood. 2. A strong spirituous liqueur made from wormwood and brandy or alcohol.
To swallow up; to engulf; to overwhelm; to cause to disappear as if by swallowing up.
Swallowing, engrossing.
The act or process of absorbing or sucking in anything, or of being absorbed and made to disappear.
To hold one's self aloof; to forbear or refrain voluntarily, and especially from an indulgence of the passions or appetites.
Abstaining from wine. Under his special eye Abstemious I grew up and thrived amain.
The act or practice of abstaining; voluntary forbearance of any action, especially the refraining from an indulgence of appetite, or from customary gratifications of animal or sensual propensities.
Withdraw; separate. The more abstract . . . we are from the body. Norris.
Concealed or hidden out of the way. The eternal eye whose sight discerns Abstrusest thoughts.
An overflowing fullness; ample sufficiency; great plenty; profusion; copious supply.
Fully sufficient; plentiful; in copious supply; -- followed by in, rarely by with.
To project; to terminate or border; to be contiguous; to meet; -- with on, upon, or against.
Pertaining to, or resembling, an abyss; bottomless; unending; profound.
A bottomless or unfathomed depth, gulf, or chasm; hence, any deep, immeasurable, and, specifically, hell, or the bottomless pit.
One holding the philosophy of Socrates and Plato; a Platonist. Hume. 2.
A garden or grove near Athens (so named from the hero Academus), where Plato and his followers held their philosophical conferences.
To approach; to come forward; -- opposed to recede. T. Gale. 2. To enter upon an office or dignity.
To cause to move faster; to quicken the motion of; to add to the speed of.
The act of accelerating, or the state of being accelerated; increase of motion or action.
One who, or that which, accelerates. Also as an adj.
To pronounce with an accent or with accents. 2. To bring out distinctly.
Capable, worthy, or sure of being accepted or received with pleasure; pleasing to a receiver.
A coming to, or near approach; admittance; admission; accessibility.
Easy of access or approach; approachable.
A coming to; the act of acceding and becoming joined.
Accompanying as a subordinate; aiding in a secondary way; additional; connected as an incident or subordinate to a principal.
Happening by chance, or unexpectedly; taking place not according to the usual course of things.
In an accidental manner; unexpectedly; by chance; unintentionally; casually.
To applaud. "A glad acclaiming train." Thomson. 2. To declare by acclamations.
To habituate to a climate not native; to acclimatize. J. H. Newman.
A slope or inclination of the earth, as the side of a hill, considered as ascending, in opposition to declivity, or descending.
A ceremony formerly used in conferring knighthood, consisting am embrace, and a slight blow on the shoulders with the flat blade of a sword.
Affording, or disposed to afford, accommodation; obliging; as an accommodating man, spirit, arrangement.
The act of fitting or adapting, or the state of being fitted or adapted.
That which accompanies; something that attends as a circumstance, or which is added to give greater completeness to the principal thing, or by way of ornament, or for the sake of symmetry.
The performer in music who takes the accompanying part. Busby.
Completed; effected; established.
The act of accomplishing; entire performance; completion; fulfillment.
Agreement; harmony; conformity. "In strict accordance with the law." Macaulay.
Agreeably; correspondingly; suitably; in a manner conformable. Behold, and so proceed accordingly.
A small, portable, keyed wind instrument, whose tones are generated by play of the wind upon free metallic reeds.
The state of being accountable; liability to be called on to render an account.
To furnish with dress, or equipments, esp. those for military service.
To furnish with dress, or equipments, esp. those for military service.
To put or bring into credit; to invest with credit or authority; to sanction.
To grow together. 2. To adhere; to grow (to); to be added; -- with to.
The act of increasing by natural growth; esp. the increase of organic bodies by the internal accession of parts.
To heap up in a mass; to pile up; to collect or bring together; to amass.
The state of being accurate; freedom from mistakes, this exemption arising from carefulness.
In an accurate manner; exactly; precisely; without error or defect.
Doomed to destruction or misery; cursed; hence, bad enough to be under the curse.
The act of accusing or charging with a crime or with a lighter offense.
Pertaining to, or containing, an accusation.
Headless. 2. (Zoöl.) Without a distinct head; -- a term applied to bivalve mollusks.
Sour or severe.
Sourness of taste, with bitterness and astringency, like that of unripe fruit.
A salt formed by the union of acetic acid with a base or positive radical.
The act of achieving or performing; an obtaining by exertion; successful performance.
Resembling Achilles, the hero of the Iliad; invincible. ACHILLES' TENDON A*chil"les' ten"don, n.
That aches; continuously painful. See Ache. -- Ach"ing*ly, adv. The aching heart, the aching head.
Free from color; transmitting light without decomposing it into its primary colors.
Sour, sharp, or biting to the taste; tart; having the taste of vinegar:.
To make acid; to convert into an acid.
Slightly sour; sub-acid; sourish.
To of or admit the knowledge of; to recognize as a fact or truth; to declare one's belief in.
The act of acknowledging; admission; avowal; owning; confession. "An acknowledgment of fault." Froude.
The top or highest point; the culmination. The very acme and pitch of life for epic poetry.
A pustular affection of the skin, due to changes in the sebaceous glands.
One who has received the highest of the four minor orders in the Catholic church, being ordained to carry the wine and water and the lights at the Mass.
The fruit of the oak, being an oval nut growing in a woody cup or cupule.
Pertaining to the sense of hearing, the organs of hearing, or the science of sounds.
The science of sounds, teaching their nature, phenomena, and laws. Acoustics, then, or the science of sound, is a very considerable branch of physics.
Personally known; familiar. See To be acquainted with, under Acquaint, v.
To rest satisfied, or apparently satisfied, or to rest without opposition and discontent (usually implying previous opposition or discontent).
A silent or passive assent or submission, or a submission with apparent content.
Resting satisfied or submissive; disposed tacitly to submit; assentive.
To gain, usually by one's own exertions; to get as one's own.
The act or process of acquiring. The acquisition or loss of a province.
Acquired. He died not in his acquisitive, but in his native soil. Wotton.
The act of acquitting; discharge from debt or obligation; acquittance.
Acres collectively.
Sharp and harsh, or bitter and not, to the taste; pungent.
Acrid; corrosive.
A quality of bodies which corrodes or destroys others; also, a harsh or biting sharpness.
One who practices rope dancing, high vaulting, or other daring gymnastic feats.
That may be the subject of an action or suit at law.
Having the power or quality of acting; causing change; communicating action or motion.
In an active manner; nimbly; briskly; energetically; also, by one's own action.
The state or quality of being active; nimbleness; agility; vigorous action or operation.
The state of being actual; reality.
A registar or clerk; -- used originally in courts of civil law jurisdiction, but in Europe used for a clerk or registar generally.
Sharpness or acuteness, as of a needle, wit, etc.
Quickness of perception or discernment; penetration of mind; the faculty of nice discrimination.
Pricking with a needle; a needle prick. Specifically (Med.): The insertion of needles into the living tissues for remedial purposes.
An old saying, which has obtained credit by long use; a proverb. Letting "I dare not" wait upon "I would," Like the poor cat i' the adage.
A stone imagined by some to be of impenetrable hardness; a name given to the diamond and other substance of extreme hardness.
The act or process of adapting, or fitting; or the state of being adapted or fitted.
A thing to be added; an appendix or addition. Addendum circle (Mech.), the circle which may be described around a circular spur wheel or gear wheel, touching the crests or tips of the teeth.
The state of being addicted; devotion; inclination. "His addiction was to courses vain." Shak.
To bring forward or offer, as an argument, passage, or consideration which bears on a statement or case.
One fully skilled or well versed in anything; a proficient.
Equal to some requirement; proportionate, or correspondent; fully sufficient.
To stick fast or cleave, as a glutinous substance does; to become joined or united.
The quality or state of adhering. 2. The state of being fixed in attachment.
Sticking; clinging; adhering. Pope. 2. Attached as an attribute or circumstance.
The action of sticking; the state of being attached; intimate union; as the adhesion of glue, or of parts united by growth, cement, or the like.
Sticky; tenacious, as glutinous substances. 2. Apt or tending to adhere.
Good-by; farewell; an expression of kind wishes at parting. A farewell.
Adieu; farewell; good-by; -- chiefly used among Spanish- speaking people.
Joining to; contiguous; adjacent.
To put off or defer to another day, or indefinitely; to postpone; to close or suspend for the day.
To award judicially in the case of a controverted question.
To adjudge; to try and determine, as a court; to settle by judicial decree.
The act of adjuring; a solemn charging on oath, or under the penalty of a curse.
To charge, bind, or command, solemnly, as if under oath, or under the penalty of a curse.
To make exact; to fit; to make correspondent or conformable; to bring into proper relations.
The act of adjusting, or condition of being adjusted; act of bringing into proper relations.
A helper; an assistant. 2. (Mil.) A regimental staff officer, who assists the colonel, or commanding officer of a garrison or regiment, in the details of regimental and garrison duty.
The act of administering; government of public affairs; the service rendered, or duties assumed, in conducting affairs.
Pertaining to administration; administering; executive.
One who administers affairs; one who directs, manages, executes, or dispenses, whether in civil, judicial, political, or ecclesiastical affairs.
A naval officer of the highest rank; a naval officer of high rank, of which there are different grades.
The office or jurisdiction of an admiral. Prescott. 2. The department or officers having authority over naval affairs generally.
One who admires; one who esteems or loves greatly. Cowper.
Expressing admiration.
Entitled to be admitted, or worthy of being admitted; that may be allowed or conceded.
The act or practice of admitting. 2. Power or permission to enter; admittance.
Received as true or valid; acknowledged. -- Ad*mit"ted*ly adv. Confessedly.
Received as true or valid; acknowledged. -- Ad*mit"ted*ly adv. Confessedly.
To warn or notify of a fault; to reprove gently or kindly, but seriously.
Gentle or friendly reproof; counseling against a fault or error; expression of authoritative advice.
That conveys admonition; warning or reproving.
The state of growing up from childhood to manhood or womanhood; youth, or the period of life between puberty and maturity, generally considered to be, in the male sex, from fourteen to twenty-one.
Growing; advancing from childhood to maturity. Schools, unless discipline were doubly strong, Detain their adolescent charge too long.
A youth beloved by Venus for his beauty. He was killed in the chase by a wild boar.
To take by choice into relationship,.
Taken by adoption; taken up as one's own.
The act of adopting, or state of being adopted; voluntary acceptance of a child of other parents to be the same as one's own child.
Pertaining to adoption; made or acquired by adoption; fitted to adopt.
Deserving to be adored; worthy of divine honors. The adorable Author of Christianity.
The act of playing honor to a divine being; the worship paid to God; the act of addressing as a god.
A crystalline substance, C9H13O3N, obtained from suprarenal extract, of which it is regarded as the active principle.
Pertaining to the Adriatic Sea.
Floating at random; in a drifting condition; at the mercy of wind and waves.
Dexterous in the use of the hands or in the exercise of the mental faculties.
To flatter in a servile way. Byron.
Servile flattery; praise in excess, or beyond what is merited. Think'st thou the fiery fever will go out With titles blown from adulation Shak.
That which is used to adulterate anything. -- a. Adulterating.
To defile by adultery. Milton. 2. To corrupt, debase, or make impure by an admixture of a foreign or a baser substance.
To give a faint shadow or slight representation of; to outline; to shadow forth.
The act of adumbrating, or shadowing forth. 2. A faint sketch; an outline.
In the van or front. 2. In the front or before others, as regards progress or ideas.
The period including the four Sundays before Christmas. Advent Sunday (Eccl.), the first Sunday in the season of Advent, being always the nearest Sunday to the feast of St.
Added extrinsically; not essentially inherent; accidental or causal; additional.
One who adventures.
Inclined to adventure; willing to incur hazard; prone to embark in hazardous enterprise.
Acting against, or in a contrary direction; opposed; contrary; opposite.
Opposition; contrariety. Wyclif. Adversity is not without comforts and hopes.
To turn the mind or attention; to refer; to take heed or notice; -- with to.
One who, or that which, advertises.
One who advises.
Having power to advise; containing advice.
The act of pleading for or supporting; work of advocating; intercession.
One who pleads the cause of another. Specifically: One who pleads the cause of another before a tribunal or judicial court.
A shield or protective armor; -- applied in mythology to the shield of Jupiter which he gave to Minerva.
A period of immeasurable duration; also, an emanation of the Deity. See Eon.
The nest of a bird of prey, as of an eagle or hawk; also a brood of such birds.
An aërial navigator; a balloonist.
A flying machine, or a small plane for experiments on flying, which floats in the air only when propelled through it.
A balloon. 2. A balloonist; an aëronaut.
The science that treats of the equilibrium of elastic fluids, or that of bodies sustained in them.
An aerie. Aërial; ethereal; incorporeal; visionary. M. Arnold.
One who makes much or overmuch of æsthetics.
At, to, or from a great distance; far away; -- often used with from preceding, or off following.
Easy to be spoken to or addressed; receiving others kindly and conversing with them in a free and friendly manner.
An attempt to assume or exhibit what is not natural or real; false display.
Regarded with affection; beloved. His affected Hercules. Chapman. 2. Inclined.
With affection; lovingly; fondly; tenderly; kindly.
Bearing or conducting inwards to a part or organ; -- opposed to efferent.
A sworn statement in writing; a declaration in writing, signed and made upon oath before an authorized magistrate.
To adopt; to receive into a family as a son; hence, to bring or receive into close connection.
Adoption; association or reception as a member in or of the same family or society.
Relationship by marriage (as between a husband and his wife's blood relations, or between a wife and her husband's blood relations).
To make firm; to confirm, or ratify; esp. (Law), to assert or confirm, as a judgment, decree, or order, brought before an appelate court for review.
Confirmation of anything established; ratification.
A breath or blast of wind. 2. A divine impartation of knowledge; supernatural impulse.
The cause of continued pain of body or mind, as sickness, losses, etc.
A flowing to or towards; a concourse; an influx. The affluence of young nobles from hence into Spain.
Flowing to; flowing abundantly. "Affluent blood." Harvey. 2. Abundant.
To give forth; to supply, yield, or produce as the natural result, fruit, or issue.
That may be afforded.
To front; to face in position; to meet or encounter face to face. All the sea-coasts do affront the Levant.
Borne on the water; floating; on board ship. On such a full sea are we now afloat.
On foot. We 'll walk afoot a while. Shak. 2. Fig.: In motion; in action.
Said before, or in a preceding part; already described or identified.
Anew; again; once more; newly. They crucify . . . the Son of God afresh.
A second moving; the grass which grows after the first crop of hay in the same season.
Reflection after an act; later or subsequent thought or expedient.
At a later or succeeding time.
Gaping, as with wonder, expectation, or eager attention. Dazzles the crowd and sets them all agape.
Old; having lived long; having lived almost to or beyond the usual time allotted to that species of being.
To wind or collect into a ball; hence, to gather into a mass or anything like a mass.
The act or process of collecting in a mass; a heaping together. An excessive agglomeration of turrets.
To make great; to enlarge; to increase.
To make heavy or heavier; to add to; to increase. "To aggravate thy store." Shak.
The act of aggravating, or making worse; -- used of evils, natural or moral.
Formed by a collection of particulars into a whole mass or sum; collective.
The first attack, or act of hostility; the first act of injury, or first act leading to a war or a controversy.
Tending or disposed to aggress; characterized by aggression; making assaults.
In Turkey, a commander or chief officer. It is used also as a title of respect.
To affright; to terrify. Chaucer. Spenser. See Agast, v. t. Terrified.
Having the faculty of quick motion in the limbs; apt or ready to move.
To move with a violent, irregular action.
The act of agitating, or the state of being agitated; the state of being moved with violence, or with irregular action.
Professing ignorance; involving no dogmatic; pertaining to or involving agnosticism.
In eager desire; eager; astir. All agog to dash through thick and thin.
To writhe with agony; to suffer violent anguish. To smart and agonize at every pore.
Violent contest or striving. The world is convulsed by the agonies of great nations.
Pleasing, either to the mind or senses; pleasant; grateful.
The art or science of cultivating the ground, including the harvesting of crops, and the rearing and management of live stock.
One versed in agronomy; a student of agronomy.
An exclamation to call one's attention; hem.
A term used in hailing.
Indisposition; morbid affection of the body; -- not applied ordinarily to acute diseases.
Any device, as a balloon, aëroplane, etc., for floating in, or flying through, the air.
A walk or a ride in the open air; a short excursion for health's sake.
Consisting of air.

A lateral division of a building, separated from the middle part, called the nave, by a row of columns or piers, which support the roof or an upper wall containing windows, called the clearstory wall.
A compact variety or sulphate of lime, or gypsum, of fine texture, and usually white and translucent, but sometimes yellow, red, or gray.
brisk and cheerful readiness; eager and prompt willingness
characteristic: It is the bright, springy energy of someone who jumps up to help the very second they are asked.

definition: Aroused to vigilance; excited by fear of approaching danger; agitated.
Exciting, or calculated to excite, alarm; causing apprehension of danger.
An exclamation expressive of sorrow, pity, or apprehension of evil; -- in old writers, sometimes followed by day or white.
A web-footed bird, of the genus Diomedea, of which there are several species.
Even though; although; notwithstanding. Chaucer. Albeit so masked, Madam, I love the truth.
A person, whether negro, Indian, or white, in whom by some defect of organization the substance which gives color to the skin, hair, and eyes is deficient or in a morbid state.
A mineral of the feldspar family, triclinic in crystallization, and in composition a silicate of alumina and soda.
A white tablet on which anything was inscribed, as a list of names, etc.
One who practices alchemy. You are alchemist; make gold. Shak.
An imaginary art which aimed to transmute the baser metals into gold, to find the panacea, or universal remedy for diseases, etc.
A diseased condition of the system, brought about by the continued use of alcoholic liquors.
A recessed portion of a room, or a small room opening into a larger one.
The condition, position, or office of an alderman. Fabyan.
A plant (Macrochloa tenacissima) of North Africa; also, its fiber, used in paper making.
That branch of mathematics which treats of the relations and properties of quantity by means of letters and other symbols.
The art of calculating by nine figures and zero. 2. The art of calculating with any species of notation.
Otherwise; otherwise called; -- a term used in legal proceedings to connect the different names of any one who has gone by two or more, and whose true name is for any cause doubtful.
The plea or mode of defense under which a person on trial for a crime proves or attempts to prove that he was in another place when the alleged act was committed.
Capable of being alienated, sold, or transferred to another.
Estranged; withdrawn in affection; foreign; -- with from. O alienate from God.
The act of alienating, or the state of being alienated. 2. (Law) A transfer of title, or a legal conveyance of property to another.
To adjust or form to a line; to range or form in line; to bring into line.
The act of adjusting to a line; arrangement in a line or lines; the state of being so adjusted.
Having resemblance or similitude; similar; without difference. The darkness and the light are both alike to thee.
Pertaining to aliment or food, or to the function of nutrition; nutritious.
Maintenance; means of living. 2. (Law) An allowance made to a wife out of her husband's estate or income for her support, upon her divorce or legal separation from him, or during a suit for the same.
To range or place in a line; to bring into line; to align. Evelyn.

Soda ash; caustic soda, caustic potash, etc. 2. (Chem.) One of a class of caustic bases, such as soda, potash, ammoma, and lithia, whose distinguishing peculiarities are solubility in alcohol and water, uniting with...
The act of alleging or positively asserting. 2. That which is alleged, asserted, or declared.
To bring forward with positiveness; to declare; to affirm; to assert.
The tie or obligation, implied or expressed, which a subject owes to his sovereign or government.
A figurative sentence or discourse, in which the principal subject is described by another subject resembling it in its properties and circumstances.
To lighten or lessen the force or weight of. Should no others join capable to alleviate the expense.
A narrow passage; especially a walk or passage in a garden or park, bordered by rows of trees or bushes.
An alley. ALL FOOLS' DAY All" Fools' Day`. The first day of April, a day on which sportive impositions are practiced.
United; joined; leagued; akin; related. See Ally.
A large carnivorous reptile of the Crocodile family, peculiar to America.
To employ or place so as to make alliteration. Skeat. To compose alliteratively.
The repetition of the same letter at the beginning of two or more words immediately succeeding each other, or at short intervals.
To distribute by lot. 2. To distribute, or parcel out in parts or portions.
The act of allotting; assignment. 2. That which is allotted; a share, part, or portion granted or distributed.
Any combination or compound of metals fused together; a mixture of metals.
That allures; attracting; charming; tempting. -- Al*lur"ing*ly, adv. -- Al*lur"ing*ness, n.
A figurative or symbolical reference. 2. A reference to something supposed to be known, but not explicitly mentioned.
Figurative; symbolical. 2. Having reference to something not fully expressed.
Pertaining to, contained in, or composed of, alluvium; relating to the deposits made by flowing water.
Wash or flow of water against the shore or bank. 2. An overflowing; an inundation.

A book or table, containing a calendar of days, and months, to which astronomical data and various statistics are often added, such as the times of the rising and setting of the sun and moon, eclipses, hours of full...
Unlimited in might; omnipotent; all-powerful; irresistible. I am the Almighty God.

The fruit of the almond tree. Note: The different kinds, as bitter, sweet, thin-shelled, thick- shelled almonds, and Jordan almonds, are the products of different varieties of the one species, Amygdalus communis, a...
Anything given gratuitously to relieve the poor, as money, food, or clothing.
Along or by the side; side by side with; -- often with of.
Same as Alewife. 1. At or from a distance, but within view, or at a small distance.
With a loud voice, or great noise; loudly; audibly. Cry aloud, spare not, lift up thy voice.
The first letter in the Greek alphabet, answering to A, and hence used to denote the beginning.
A raised structure (as a square or oblong erection of stone or wood) on which sacrifices are offered or incense burned to a deity.
The act of altering or making different. Alteration, though it be from worse to better, hath in it incoveniences.
The contend in words; to dispute with zeal, heat, or anger; to wrangle.
Warm contention in words; dispute carried on with heat or anger; controversy.
Space extended upward; height; the perpendicular elevation of an object above its foundation, above the ground, or above a given level, or of one object above another.
Formerly the part sung by the highest male, or counter-tenor, voices; now the part sung by the lowest female, or contralto, voices, between in tenor and soprano.
Regard for others, both natural and moral; devotion to the interests of others.
One imbued with altruism; -- opposed to egoist.
Regardful of others; beneficent; unselfish; -- opposed to Ant: egoistic or Ant: selfish.
The metallic base of alumina. This metal is white, but with a bluish tinge, and is remarkable for its resistance to oxidation, and for its lightness, pertaining a specific gravity of about 2.6.
An alloy of mercury with another metal or metals.
To compound or mix, as quicksilver, with another metal; to unite, combine, or alloy with mercury.
The act or operation of compounding mercury with another metal; -- applied particularly to the process of separating gold and silver from their ores by mixing them with mercury.
A person attached to a particular pursuit, study, or science as to music or painting.
Pertaining to, producing, or expressing, sexual love.
A minister of the highest rank sent a foreign court to represent there his sovereign or country.
Pertaining the faculty of using both hands with equal ease. Sir T. Browne.
Encompassing on all sides; circumfused; investing. "Ambient air." Milton.
Doubtful or uncertain, particularly in respect to signification; capable of being understood in either of two or more possible senses.
Possessing, or controlled by, ambition; greatly or inordinately desirous of power, honor, office, superiority, or distinction.
To go at the easy gait called an amble; -- applied to the horse or to its rider.
A sweet-scented herb; ambrosia. See Ambrosia, 3. Turner.
The fabled food of the gods (as nectar was their drink), which conferred immortality upon those who partook of it.
Consisting of, or partaking of the nature of, ambrosia; delighting the taste or smell.
A field hospital, so organized as to follow an army in its movements, and intended to succor the wounded as soon as possible.
To make better; to improve; to meliorate. In every human being there is a wish to ameliorate his own condition.
Easy to be led; governable, as a woman by her husband. Jacob. 2. Liable to be brought to account or punishment.
To change or modify in any way for the better.
An alteration or change for the better; correction of a fault or of faults.
Compensation for a loss or injury; recompense; reparation. "An honorable amends." Addison.
The quality of being pleasant or agreeable, whether in respect to situation, climate, manners, or disposition.
Attachment to the United States. 2. A custom peculiar to the United States or to America.
A variety of crystallized quartz, of a purple or bluish violet color, of different shades.
Lovable; lovely; pleasing. So amiable a prospect. Sir T. Herbert. 2. Friendly.
Friendly; proceeding from, or exhibiting, friendliness; after the manner of friends.
In the midst or middle of; surrounded or encompassed by; among. "This fair tree amidst the garden." "Unseen amid the throng." "Amidst thick clouds." Milton.
A friend; -- a Spanish term applied in the Philippine Islands to friendly natives.

The Amish Mennonites. Of, pertaining to, or designating, the followers of Jacob Amman, a strict Mennonite of the 17th century, who even proscribed the use of buttons and shaving as "worldly conformity".
Friendship, in a general sense, between individuals, societies, or nations.
A gaseous compound of hydrogen and nitrogen, NH3, with a pungent smell and taste: -- often called volatile alkali, and spirits of hartshorn.
Forgetfulness; also, a defect of speech, from cerebral disease, in which the patient substitutes wrong words or names in the place of those he wishes to employ.
Forgetfulness; cessation of remembrance of wrong; oblivion. 2. An act of the sovereign power granting oblivion, or a general pardon, for a past offense, as to subjects concerned in an insurrection.
Mixed or mingled; surrounded by. They heard, And from his presence hid themselves among The thickest trees.
Inclined to love; having a propensity to love, or to sexual enjoyment.
Having no determinate form; of irregular; shapeless. Kirwan. 2. Without crystallization in the ultimate texture of a solid substance.
The act or right of alienating lands to a corporation, which was considered formerly as transferring them to dead hands, or in mortmain.
To make as if dead; to destroy. Chaucer. 2. (Law) To alienate in mortmain, that is, to convey to a corporation.
The unit of electric current; -- defined by the International Electrical Congress in 1893 and by U.
A word used to describe the character Halliwell.
Having the ability to live both on land and in water, as frogs, crocodiles, beavers, and some plants.
An oval or circular building with rising tiers of seats about an open space called the arena.
Large; great in size, extent, capacity, or bulk; spacious; roomy; widely extended.
State of being ample; extent of surface or space; largeness of dimensions.
In an ample manner.
To prune or lop off, as branches or tendrils. 2. (Surg.) To cut off (a limb or projecting part (of the body).
The act amputating; esp. the operation of cutting of a limb or projecting part of the body.
In a frenzied and reckless. To run amuck, to rush out in a state of frenzy, as the Malays sometimes do under the influence of "bhang," and attack every one that comes in the way.

An ornament, gem, or scroll, or a package containing a relic, etc., worn as a charm or preservative against evils or mischief, such as diseases and witchcraft, and generally inscribed with mystic forms or characters.
Diverted. 2. Expressing amusement.
Giving amusement; diverting.
A misplacing or error in the order of time; an error in chronology by which events are misplaced in regard to each other, esp.
Erroneous in date; containing an anachronism. T. Warton.
Capable of rendering insensible.
Literally, the letters of a word read backwards, but in its usual wider sense, the change or one word or phrase into another by the transposition of its letters.
Pertaining to, or situated near, the anus.
Absence of sensibility to pain. Quain.
Having analogy; corresponding to something else; bearing some resemblance or proportion.
A resemblance of relations; an agreement or likeness between things in some circumstances or effects, when the things are otherwise entirely different.
A resolution of anything, whether an object of the senses or of the intellect, into its constituent or original elements.
One who analyzes; formerly, one skilled in algebraical geometry; now commonly, one skilled in chemical analysis.
To subject to analysis; to resolve (anything complex) into its elements.
An anarch; one who advocates anarchy of aims at the overthrow of civil government.
Absence of government; the state of society where there is no law or supreme power.
A ban or curse pronounced with religious solemnity by ecclesiastical authority, and accompanied by excommunication.
The art of dissecting, or artificially separating the different parts of any organized body, to discover their situation, structure, and economy.
One from whom a person is descended, whether on the father's or mother's side, at any distance of time.
Of, pertaining to, derived from, or possessed by, an ancestor or ancestors.
Condition as to ancestors; ancestral lineage; hence, birth or honorable descent.
The act of anchoring, or the condition of lying at anchor. 2. A place suitable for anchoring or where ships anchor.
Held by an anchor; at anchor; held safely.
Subservient or subordinate, like a handmaid; auxiliary. The Convocation of York seems to have been always considered as inferior, and even ancillary, to the greater province.
A utensil for supporting wood when burning in a fireplace, one being placed on each side.
Uniting both sexes in one, or having the characteristics of both; being in nature both male and female.
Resembling a man. A machine or automaton in the form of a human being.
A northern constellation, supposed to represent the mythical Andromeda.
An instrument for measuring the force or velocity of the wind; a wind gauge.
Same as Anæsthesia, Anæsthetic.
Over again; another time; in a new form; afresh.
Belonging to, or proceeding from, angels; resembling, characteristic of, or partaking of the nature of, an angel.

An aromatic umbelliferous plant (Archangelica officinalis or Angelica archangelica) the leaf stalks of which are sometimes candied and used in confectionery, and the roots and seeds as an aromatic tonic.
An ancient Low German tribe, that settled in Britain, which came to be called Engla-land (Angleland or England).
Intense dread of, or aversion to, England or the English. -- An"glo*phobe, n.
A fabric made from the wool of the Angora goat.
In an angry manner; under the influence of anger.
Relating to an angle or to angles; having an angle or angles; forming an angle or corner.
Destitute of water.
To give natural life to; to make alive; to quicken.
Endowed with life; full of life or spirit; indicating animation; lively.
The act of animating, or giving life or spirit; the state of being animate or alive.
Mere spiritedness or courage. Skelton. Such as give some proof of animosity, audacity, and execution, those she  loveth.
Animating spirit; intention; temper. nimus furandi Etym:  (Law), intention of stealing.
The joint which connects the foot with the leg; the tarsus. Ankle bone, the bone of the ankle.
An ornament or a fetter for the ankle; an ankle ring.
A writer of annals. The monks . . . were the only annalists in those ages.
A relation of events in chronological order, each event being recorded under the year in which it happened.
To subject to great heat, and then cool slowly, as glass, cast iron, steel, or other metal, for the purpose of rendering it less brittle.
To join or attach; usually to subjoin; to affix; to append; -- followed by to.
The act of reducing to nothing, or nonexistence; or the act of destroying the form or combination of parts under which a thing exists, so that the name can no longer be applied to it.
Returning with the year, at a stated time; annual; yearly.
To explain or criticize by notes.
To give public notice, or first notice of; to make known; to publish; to proclaim.
The act of announcing, or giving notice; that which announces; proclamation.
A feeling of discomfort or vexation caused by what one dislikes; also, whatever causes such a feeling.
That annoys; molesting; vexatious. -- An*noy"ing*ly, adv.
A sum of money, payable yearly, to continue for a given number of years, for life, or forever.
To reduce to nothing; to obliterate. Light, the prime work of God, to me's extinct.
The act of annulling; abolition; invalidation.
The act of announcing; announcement; proclamation.
The positive pole of an electric battery, or more strictly the electrode by which the current enters the electrolyte on its way to the other pole.
Serving to assuage pain; soothing. The anodyne draught of oblivion. Burke.
To smear or rub over with oil or an unctuous substance; also, to spread over, as oil.
The act of anointing, or state of being anointed; also, an ointment. Milton.
Deviating from a general rule, method, or analogy; abnormal; irregular.
something that deviates from what is standard, normal, or expected
characteristic: Scientists use it for a strange reading on an instrument that breaks the usual rule.

definition: Nameless; of unknown name; also, of unknown /or unavowed authorship.
In an anonymous manner; without a name. Swift.
Opposition of action; counteraction or contrariety of things or principles.
To contend with; to oppose actively; to counteract. To act in opposition.
Opposite to the northern or arctic pole; relating to the southern pole or to the region near it, and applied especially to a circle, distant from the pole 23º 28min.
Each player's stake, which is put into the pool before (ante) the game begins.
To go before in time or place; to precede; to surpass. Sir M. Hale.
Going before in time; prior; anterior; preceding.
A chamber or apartment before the chief apartment and leading into it, in which persons wait for audience.
Prior date; a date antecedent to another which is the actual date. 2. Anticipation.
Being before noon; in or pertaining to the forenoon. (Abbrev. a. m.).
Being or occurring before the creation of the world. Young.
Before birth. Shelley.
A movable, articulated organ of sensation, attached to the heads of insects and Crustacea.
Before in time; antecedent. Antigonus, who was anterior to Polybius. Sir G.
A room before, or forming an entrance to, another; a waiting room.
Formerly, a hymn sung in alternate parts, in present usage, a selection from the Psalms, or other parts of the Scriptures or the liturgy, set to sacred music.
A discourses on flowers. 2. A collection of flowers; a garland. 3. A collection of flowers of literature, that is, beautiful passages from authors.
A hard, compact variety of mineral coal, of high luster, differing from bituminous coal in containing little or no bitumen, in consequence of which it burns with a nearly non luminous flame.
A carbuncle. (b) A malignant pustule. 2. (Biol.) A microscopic, bacterial organism (Bacillus anthracis), resembling transparent rods.
Resembling man; -- applied especially to certain apes, as the ourang or gorilla.
One who is versed in anthropology.
The science of the structure and functions of the human body. 2. The science of man.
A prefix meaning against, opposite or opposed to, contrary, or in place of.
Old; antique. (Zoöl.) "Lords of antic fame." Phaer. 2. Odd; fantastic.
A denier or opponent of Christ. Specif.: A great antagonist, person or power, expected to precede Christ's second coming.
To be before in doing; to do or take before another; to preclude or prevent by prior action.
The act of anticipating, taking up, placing, or considering something beforehand, or before the proper time in natural order.
A sentence in which the ideas fall, or become less important and striking, at the close.
A movement of the atmosphere opposite in character, as regards direction of the wind and distribution of barometric pressure, to that of a cyclone.
A remedy to counteract the effects of poison, or of anything noxious taken into the stomach.
A contradiction between any words or passages in an author. Sir W. Hamilton.
a deep-seated feeling of dislike or aversion
characteristic: It is a cold, strong feeling of distaste, the opposite of warmth and affection toward someone.

definition: A musical response; alternate singing or chanting. See Antiphony, and Antiphone.
A musical response; also, antiphonal chanting or signing. 2. An anthem or psalm sung alternately by a choir or congregation divided into two parts.
Those who live on the side of the globe diametrically opposite. 2. The country of those who live on the opposite side of the globe.
To make old, or obsolete; to make antique; to make old in such a degree as to put out of use.
Grown old. Hence: Bygone; obsolete; out of use; old-fashioned.
Old; ancient; of genuine antiquity.
Counteracting or preventing putrefaction, or a putrescent tendency in the system.
Opposed to slavery. -- n. Opposition to slavery.
Tending to interrupt or destroy social intercourse; averse to society, or hostile to its existence.
Good against spasms. -- n. A medicine which prevents or allays spasms or convulsions.
In Greek choruses and dances, the returning of the chorus, exactly answering to a previous strophe or movement from right to left.
An opposition or contrast of words or sentiments occurring in the same sentence.
Pertaining to antithesis, or opposition of words and sentiments; containing, or of the nature of, antithesis.

A substance (sometimes the product of a specific micro-organism and sometimes naturally present in the blood or tissues of an animal), capable of producing immunity from certain diseases, or of counteracting the...
The entire horn, or any branch of the horn, of a cervine animal, as of a stag.
An Egyptian deity, the conductor of departed spirits, represented by a human figure with the head of a dog or fox.
The posterior opening of the alimentary canal, through which the excrements are expelled.
An iron block, usually with a steel face, upon which metals are hammered and shaped.
Concern or solicitude respecting some thing o 2. Eager desire. J. D. Forbes 3.
Full of anxiety or disquietude; greatly concerned or solicitous, esp. respecting future or unknown.
In an anxious manner; with painful uncertainty; solicitously.
In any way or manner whatever; at any rate; in any event. Anyhow, it must be acknowledged to be not a simple selforiginated error.
Anywise; at all. Tennyson. Southey.
The great artery which carries the blood from the heart to all parts of the body except the lungs.
A group of nomadic North American Indians including several tribes native of Arizona, New Mexico, etc.
Void of feeling; not susceptible of deep emotion; passionless; indifferent.
Want of feeling; privation of passion, emotion, or excitement; dispassion.
The tip, top, point, or angular summit of anything.
Loss of the power of speech, or of the appropriate use of words, the vocal organs remaining intact, and the intelligence being preserved.
A comprehensive maxim or principle expressed in a few words; a sharply defined sentence relating to abstract truth rather than to practical matters.
In the form of, or of the nature of, an aphorism; in the form of short, unconnected sentences.
Exciting venereal desire; provocative to venery. That which (as a drug, or some kinds of food) excites to venery.
The Greek goddess of love, corresponding to the Venus of the Romans. 2.
One who keeps an apiary.
A place where bees are kept; a stand or shed for bees; a beehouse.
Rearing of bees for their honey and wax.
Each by itself; by the single one; to each; as the share of each.
Assurance of manner or of action; self-possession.
The revelation delivered to St. John, in the isle of Patmos, near the close of the first century, forming the last book of the New Testament.
Pertaining to the Apocrypha. 2. Not canonical. Hence: Of doubtful authority.
That point in the orbit of the moon which is at the greatest distance from the earth.

A deity among the Greeks and Romans. He was the god of light and day (the "sun god"), of archery, prophecy, medicine, poetry, and music, etc., and was represented as the model of manly grace and beauty.
One who makes an apology; one who speaks or writes in defense of a faith, a cause, or an institution.
A story or relation of fictitious events, intended to convey some moral truth.
A figure by which a speaker formally declines to take notice of a favorable point, but in such a manner as to produce the effect desired.
Relating to apoplexy; affected with, inclined to, or symptomatic of, apoplexy.
Sudden diminution or loss of consciousness, sensation, and voluntary motion, usually caused by pressure on the brain.
An abandonment of what one has voluntarily professed; a total desertion of departure from one's faith, principles, or party.
One who has forsaken the faith, principles, or party, to which he before adhered.
Literally: One sent forth; a messenger. Specifically: One of the twelve disciples of Christ, specially chosen as his companions and witnesses, and sent forth to preach the gospel.
A figure of speech by which the orator or writer suddenly breaks off from the previous method of his discourse, and addresses, in the second person, some person or thing, absent or present.
One who prepares and sells drugs or compounds for medicinal purposes. Note: In England an apothecary is one of a privileged class of practitioners -- a kind of sub-physician.
A short, pithy, and instructive saying; a terse remark, conveying some important truth.
The act of elevating a mortal to the rank of, and placing him among, "the gods;" deification.
Such as to appall.
Things provided as means to some end. 2. Hence: A full collection or set of implements, or utensils, for a given duty, experimental or operative.
The act of becoming visible; appearance; visibility. Milton. The sudden apparition of the Spaniards.
That appeals; imploring. -- Ap*peal"*ing*ly, adv. -- Ap*peal"ing*ness, n.
To make quiet; to calm; to reduce to a state of peace; to still; to pacify.
To hang or attach to, as by a string, so that the thing is suspended.
Inflammation of the vermiform appendix.
Something appended or added; an appendage, adjunct, or concomitant. Normandy became an appendix to England.
To belong or pertain, whether by right, nature, appointment, or custom.
Something which creates or whets an appetite.
To show approval of by clapping the hands, acclamation, or other significant sign.
The act of applauding; approbation and praise publicly expressed by clapping the hands, stamping or tapping with the feet, acclamation, huzzas, or other means.
One who apples for something; one who makes request; a petitioner. The applicant for a cup of water.
The act of applying or laying on, in a literal sense.
Very applicable; well adapted; suitable or fit; relevant; pat; -- followed by to.
The act of adding; application; accretion. It grows . . . by the apposition of new matter.
A valuation by an authorized person; an appraisement.
Capable of being appreciated or estimated; large enough to be estimated.
To set a price or value on; to estimate justly; to value. To appreciate the motives of their enemies.
A just valuation or estimate of merit, worth, weight, etc.; recognition of excellence.
Having or showing a just or ready appreciation or perception.
Capable of being apprehended or conceived. "Apprehensible by faith." Bp.
The act of seizing or taking hold of; seizure.
The service or condition of an apprentice; the state in which a person is gaining instruction in a trade or art, under legal agreement.
To give notice, verbal or written; to inform; -- followed by of.
approval or praise, especially when given officially
characteristic: Think of a nod of formal approval, a stamp of warm official praise from those in charge.

definition: Containing or expressing approbation; commendatory. Sheldon.
Set apart for a particular use or person. Hence: Belonging peculiarly.
In an appropriate or proper manner; fitly; properly.
Approbation; sanction. A censor . . . without whose approval nTemple. Syn.
Approaching; proximate; nearly resembling. 2. Near correctness; nearly exact.
With approximation; so as to approximate; nearly.
That which belongs to something else; an adjunct; an appendage; an accessory.
Annexed or pertaining to some more important thing; accessory; incident.
A fruit allied to the plum, of an orange color, oval shape, and delicious taste.
Opportunely or opportune; seasonably or seasonable. A tale extremely apropos.
A natural or acquired disposition or capacity for a particular purpose, or tendency to a particular action or effect.
Water; -- a word much used in pharmacy and the old chemistry, in various signification, determined by the word or words annexed.
An artificial pond, or a globe or tank (usually with glass sides), in which living specimens of aquatic animals or plants are kept.
The Water-bearer; the eleventh sign in the zodiac, which the sun enters about the 20th of January.
Pertaining to water growing in water; living in, swimming in, or frequenting the margins of waters.
A conductor, conduit, or artificial channel for conveying water, especially one for supplying large cities with water.
Partaking of the nature of water, or abounding with it; watery. The aqueous vapor of the air.
Belonging to or like an eagle. 2. Curving; hooked; prominent, like the beak of an eagle.
Fit for plowing or tillage; -- hence, often applied to land which has been plowed or tilled.
A person appointed, or chosen, by parties to determine a controversy between them.
Depending on will or discretion; not governed by any fixed rules.
To hear and decide, as arbitrators.
A kind of latticework formed of, or covered with, vines, branches of trees, or other plants, for shade.
Resembling a tree; becoming woody in stalk; dendritic; having crystallizations disposed like the branches and twigs of a tree.
A place in which a collection of rare trees and shrubs is cultivated for scientific or educational purposes.
The cultivation of trees and shrubs, chiefly for timber or for ornamental purposes.
A series of arches with the columns or piers which support them, the spandrels above, and other necessary appurtenances.
A mountainous and picturesque district of Greece, in the heart of the Peloponnesus, whose people were distinguished for contentment and rural happiness.
Hidden; secret. "The arcane part of divine wisdom." Berkeley.
Any part of a curved line. 2. (Arch.) (a) Usually a curved member made up of separate wedge-shaped solids, with the joints between them disposed in the direction of the radii of the curve.
Relating to archæology, or antiquities.
One versed in archæology; an antiquary. Wright.

The science or study of antiquities, esp. prehistoric antiquities, such as the remains of buildings or monuments of an early epoch, inscriptions, implements, and other relics, written manuscripts, etc.
An ancient, antiquated, or old-fashioned, word, expression, or idiom; a word or form of speech no longer in common use.
A chief angel; one high in the celestial hierarchy. Milton. 2. (Bot.) A term applied to several different species of plants (Angelica archangelica, Lamium album, etc.).

A chief bishop; a church dignitary of the first class (often called a metropolitan or primate) who superintends the conduct of the suffragan bishops in his province, and also exercises episcopal authority in his own...
In England, an ecclesiastical dignitary, next in rank below a bishop, whom he assists, and by whom he is appointed, though with independent authority.
A bowman, one skilled in the use of the bow and arrow.
The use of the bow and arrows in battle, hunting, etc.; the art, practice, or skill of shooting with a bow and arrows.
The original pattern or model of a work; or the model from which a thing is made or formed.
An extinct genus of Bryzoa characteristic of the subcarboniferous rocks.
The Grecian Archipelago, or Ægean Sea, separating Greece from Asia Minor.
A person skilled in the art of building; one who understands architecture, or makes it his occupation to form plans and designs of buildings, and to superintend the artificers employed.
The art or science of building; especially, the art of building houses, churches, bridges, and other structures, for the purposes of civil life.
Pertaining to, or situated under, the northern constellation called the Bear.
Hot or burning; causing a sensation of burning; fiery.
Heat, in a literal sense.
involving or requiring strenuous and prolonged effort; very difficult and tiring
characteristic: Imagine a long, steep mountain climb that leaves you exhausted and drenched in sweat.

definition: The area in the central part of an amphitheater, in which the gladiators fought and other shows were exhibited.
Pertaining to, or resembling, silver; made of, or sounding like, silver.
A secret language or conventional slang peculiar to thieves, tramps, and vagabonds.
A fabulous being of antiquity, said to have had a hundred eyes, who has placed by Juno to guard Io.

An air or song; a melody; a tune. Note: The Italian term is now mostly used for the more elaborate accompanied melodies sung by a single voice, in operas, oratorios, cantatas, anthems, etc., and not so much for...
Exhausted of moisture; parched with heat; dry; barren. "An arid waste." Thomson.
A variety of the gazelle (Antilope, or Gazella, dorcas), found in Arabia and adjacent countries.
One of the aristocracy or people of rank in a community; one of a ruling class.
The science of numbers; the art of computation by figures. 2. A book containing the principles of this science.
A fleet of armed ships; a squadron. Specifically, the Spanish fleet which was sent to assail England, a.
Any edentate animal if the family Dasypidæ, peculiar to America. The body and head are incased in an armor composed of small bony plates.
A chair with arms to support the elbows or forearms. Tennyson.
As much as the arm can hold.
The act of furnishing with, or taking, arms. The arming was now universal.
A cessation of arms for a short time, by convention; a temporary suspension of hostilities by agreement.
Defensive arms for the body; any clothing or covering worn to protect one's person in battle.
Clad with armor.
A place where arms and instruments of war are deposited for safe keeping.
The hollow beneath the junction of the arm and shoulder; the axilla.
The quality or principle of plants or other substances which constitutes their fragrance.
Pertaining to, or containing, aroma; fragrant; spicy; strong- scented.
The past or preterit tense of Arise.
To excite to action from a state of rest; to stir, or put in motion or exertion.
To call or set as a prisoner at the bar of a court to answer to the matter charged in an indictment or complaint.
The act of arraigning, or the state of being arraigned; the act of calling and setting a prisoner before a court to answer to an indictment or complaint.
To put in proper order; to dispose (persons, or parts) in the manner intended, or best suited for the purpose.
The act of arranging or putting in an orderly condition; the state of being arranged or put in order.
Notoriously or preëminently bad; thorough or downright, in a bad sense.
Order; a regular and imposing arrangement; disposition in regular lines.
To stop; to check or hinder the motion or action of.
Striking; attracting attention; impressive. This most solemn and arresting occurrence.
Being without rhythm or regularity, as the pulse.
The act or habit of arrogating, or making undue claims in an overbearing manner.
Making, or having the disposition to make, exorbitant claims of rank or estimation.
To assume, or claim as one's own, unduly, proudly, or presumptuously; to make undue claims to, from vanity or baseless pretensions to right or merit.
A missile weapon of offense, slender, pointed, and usually feathered and barbed, to be shot from a bow.
A water course; a rivulet. 2. The dry bed of a small stream.
The buttocks, or hind part of an animal; the posteriors; the fundament.
A public establishment for the storage, or for the manufacture and storage, of arms and all military equipments, whether for land or naval service.
One of the elements, a solid substance resembling a metal in its physical properties, but in its chemical relations ranking with the nonmetals.
The malicious burning of a dwelling house or outhouse of another man, which by the common law is felony.
Performed with, or characterized by, art or skill. "Artful strains." "Artful terms." Milton.
Any inflammation of the joints, particularly the gout.
Expressed in articles or in separate items or particulars. Bacon. 2. Jointed.
A product of human workmanship; -- applied esp. to the simpler products of aboriginal art as distinguished from natural objects.
A handicraft; a trade; art of making. 2. Workmanship; a skillfully contrived work.
One peculiarly dexterous and tasteful in almost any employment, as an opera dancer, a hairdresser, a cook.
Works of art collectively. 2. Artistic effect or quality. Southey. 3. Artistic pursuits.
Wanting art, knowledge, or skill; ignorant; unskillful. Artless of stars and of the moving sand.
The quality of being artless, or void of art or guile; simplicity; sincerity.
A variety of amphibole or of pyroxene, occurring in long and delicate fibers, or in fibrous masses or seams, usually of a white, gray, or green-gray color.
To move upward; to mount; to go up; to rise; -- opposed to Ant: descend.
Same as Ascendency.
Rising; moving upward.
The act of ascending; a rising; ascent. 2. Specifically: The visible ascent of our Savior on the fortieth day after his resurrection.
The act of rising; motion upward; rise; a mounting upward.
To render (a person) certain; to cause to feel certain; to make confident.
Extremely rigid in self-denial and devotions; austere; severe. The stern ascetic rigor of the Temple discipline.
The condition, practice, or mode of life, of ascetics.
To attribute, impute, or refer, as to a cause.
Not liable to putrefaction; nonputrescent. -- n. An aseptic substance.
Having no distinct; without sexual action.
The earthy or mineral particles of combustible substances remaining after combustion, as of wood or coal.
On shore or on land; on the land adjacent to water; to the shore; to the land.
Sideways; obliquely; with a side glance; with disdain, envy, or suspicion.
Awry; askance; asquint; oblique or obliquely; -- sometimes indicating scorn, or contempt, or entry.
One of several species of poplar bearing this name, especially the Populus tremula, so called from the trembling of its leaves, which move with the slightest impulse of the air.

Mineral pitch, Jews' pitch, or compact native bitumen. It is brittle, of a black or brown color and high luster on a surface of fracture.
The act of causing asphyxia; a state of asphyxia.

Assailing; attacking. Milton. One who, or that which, assails, attacks, or assaults.

Positive; affirming confidently; affirmative; peremptory. In a confident and assertive form.
To value; to make a valuation or official estimate of for the purpose of taxation.
The act of assessing; the act of determining an amount to be paid.

Any article or separable part of one's assets.
Property of a deceased person, subject by law to the payment of his debts and legacies.

Constant in application or attention; devoted; attentive; unremitting.

To bring to a likeness or to conformity; to cause a resemblance between.

Joined as a companion; brought into association; accompanying; combined.
The act of associating, or state of being associated; union; connection, whether of persons of things.

Resemblance of sound. "The disagreeable assonance of Steevens. 2. (Pros.) A peculiar species of rhyme, in which the last accented vowel and those which follow it in one word correspond in sound with the vowels of...
Having a resemblance of sounds. 2. (Pros.) Pertaining to the peculiar species of rhyme called assonance.
To correspond in sound.
Selected; culled.
To soften, in a figurative sense; to allay, mitigate, ease, or lessen, as heat, pain, or grief.
Supposed. 2. Pretended; hypocritical; make-believe.
Pretentious; taking much upon one's self; presumptuous. Burke.
The act of assuming, or taking to or upon one's self; the act of taking up or adopting.
Made sure; safe; insured; certain; indubitable; not doubting; bold to excess.
A starlike body; esp. one of the numerous small planets whose orbits lie between those of Mars and Jupiter.

A defect of the eye or of a lens, in consequence of which the rays derived from one point are not brought to a single focal point, thus causing imperfect images or indistictness of vision.
Very wonderful; of a nature to excite astonishment.
The condition of one who is stunned. Hence: Numbness; loss of sensation.
Pertaining to, coming from, or resembling, the stars; starry; starlike.
Out of the right, either in a literal or in a figurative sense; wandering.
Drawing together the tissues; binding; contracting; -- opposed to laxative.

Critically discerning; sagacious; shrewd; subtle; crafty. Syn. -- Keen.
Apart; separate from each other; into parts; in two; separately; into or in different pieces or places.

The recurrence, or a tendency to a recurrence, of the original type of a species in the progeny of its varieties.

The disbelief or denial of the existence of a God, or supreme intelligent Being.
Pertaining to, implying, or containing, atheism; -- applied to things.
Wanting drink; thirsty. 2. Having a keen appetite or desire; eager; longing.
One who contended for a prize in the public games of ancient Greece or Rome.
The art of training by athletic exercises; the games and sports of athletes.
Across; from side to side of. Athwart the thicket lone. Tennyson. 2. (Naut.) Across the direction or course of.
One who sustains a great burden. 2. (Anat.) The first vertebra of the neck, articulating immediately with the skull, thus sustaining the globe of the head, whence the name.
The whole mass of aëriform fluid surrounding the earth; -- applied also to the gaseous envelope of any celestial orb, or other body.
One who, or that which, atomizes; esp., an instrument for reducing a liquid to spray for disinfecting, cooling, or perfuming.
On or at the top. Milton.
Enormous wickedness; extreme heinousness or cruelty. 2. An atrocious or extremely cruel deed.
A wasting away from want of nourishment; diminution in bulk or slow emaciation of the body or of any part.
A poisonous, white, crystallizable alkaloid, extracted from the Atropa belladonna, or deadly nightshade, and the Datura Stramonium, or thorn apple.
One attached to another person or thing, as a part of a suite or staff.
The act attaching, or state of being attached; close adherence or affection.
One who attacks.
Being present, or in the train; accompanying; in waiting. From the attendant flotilla rang notes triumph.
Heedful; intent; observant; regarding with care or attention. Note: Attentive is applied to the senses of hearing and seeing,.
To make thin or slender, as by mechanical or chemical action upon inanimate objects, or by the effects of starvation, disease, etc., upon living bodies.
The posture, action, or disposition of a figure or a statue. 2. The posture or position of a person or an animal, or the manner in which the parts of his body are disposed.
That attracts. -- At*tract"ing*ly, adv.

An invisible power in a body by which it draws anything to itself; the power in nature acting mutually between bodies or ultimate particles, tending to draw them together, or to produce their cohesion or combination,...
Having the power or quality of attracting or drawing.
To ascribe; to consider (something) as due or appropriate (to); to refer, as an effect to a cause.
The act of rubbing together; friction; the act of wearing by friction, or by rubbing substances together.
That has no type; devoid of typical character; irregular; unlike the type.
A public sale of property to the highest bidder, esp. by a person licensed and authorized for the purpose.
A person who sells by auction; a person whose business it is to dispose of goods or lands by public sale to the highest or best bidder.
showing a willingness to take surprisingly bold risks; daring
characteristic: It describes a fearless, cheeky boldness, like a daredevil attempting an impossible stunt.

definition: Daring spirit, resolution, or confidence; venturesomeness. The freedom and audacity necessary in the commerce of men.
The act of hearing or listening; hearing. Audition may be active or passive.
The part of a church, theater, or other public building, assigned to the audience.
To enlarge or increase in size, amount, or degree; to swell; to make bigger.

An official diviner who foretold events by the singing, chattering, flight, and feeding of birds, or by signs or omens derived from celestial phenomena, certain appearances of quadrupeds, or unusual occurrences.
The art or practice of foretelling events by observing the actions of birds, etc.
A member of one of the religious orders called after St. Augustine; an Austin friar.
A familiar name for an aunt. In the southern United States a familiar term applied to aged negro women.
A familiar name for an aunt. In the southern United States a familiar term applied to aged negro women.
Any subtile, invisible emanation, effluvium, or exhalation from a substance, as the aroma of flowers, the odor of the blood, a supposed fertilizing emanation from the pollen of flowers, etc.
A celestial crown or accidental glory added to the bliss of heaven, as a reward to those (as virgins, martyrs, preachers, etc.) who have overcome the world, the flesh, and the devil.
The external ear, or that part of the ear which is prominent from the head.
Gold-bearing; containing or producing gold. Whence many a bursting stream auriferous plays.
The rising light of the morning; the dawn of day; the redness of the sky just before the sun rises.
Belonging to, or resembling, the aurora (the dawn or the northern lights).
The act of listening or hearkening to. Hickes. 2. (Med.) An examination by listening either directly with the ear (immediate auscultation) applied to parts of the body, as the abdomen.
A divining or taking of omens by observing birds; an omen as to an undertaking, drawn from birds.
Having omens or tokens of a favorable issue; giving promise of success, prosperity, or happiness.
severe or strict in manner or appearance; having no comforts or luxuries
characteristic: Picture a bare gray room with a single hard chair, plain and stripped of all decoration.

definition: Augustinian.
To render authentic; to give authority to, by the proof, attestation, or formalities required by law, or sufficient to entitle to credit.
The quality of being authentic or of established authority for truth and correctness.
Having, or proceeding from, due authority; entitled to obedience, credit, or acceptance.
Legal or rightful power; a right to command or to act; power exercised buy a person in virtue of his office or trust.
The act of giving authority or legal power; establishment by authority.
To clothe with authority, warrant, or legal power; to give a right to act.
A biography written by the subject of it; memoirs of one's life written by one's self.
Independent or self-derived power; absolute or controlling authority; supremacy.
An absolute sovereign; a monarch who holds and exercises the powers of government by claim of absolute right, not subject to restriction.
That which is written with one's own hand; an original manuscript; a person's own signature or handwriting.
Having an inherent power of action or motion. Nothing can be said to be automatic.
In an automatic manner.
Any thing or being regarded as having the power of spontaneous motion or action.
An automobile vehicle or mechanism; esp., a self-propelled vehicle suitable for use on a street or roadway.
Independent in government; having the right or power of self- government.
The power or right of self-government; self-government, or political independence, of a city or a state.
Personal observation or examination; seeing with one's own eyes; ocular view.

Of, belonging to, or peculiar to, autumn.
Conferring aid or help; helping; aiding; assisting; subsidiary; as auxiliary troops.
A large mass or body of snow and ice sliding swiftly down a mountain side, or falling down a precipice.
An excessive or inordinate desire of gain; greediness after wealth; covetousness.
Actuated by avarice; greedy of gain; immoderately desirous of accumulating property.
The descent of a deity to earth, and his incarnation as a man or an animal.
A way or opening for entrance into a place; a passage by which a place may by reached.
A work horse, or working ox. 1. To assert, or prove, the truth of. 2. (Law) To avouch or verify.
A turning away. Adhesion to vice and aversion from goodness. Bp. Atterbury.
To turn aside, or away.
Turned away, esp. as an expression of feeling; also, offended; unpropitious.
A house, inclosure, large cage, or other place, for keeping birds confined.
The art or science of flying.
An experimenter in aviation. (b) A flying machine.
Longing eagerly for; eager; greedy. "Avid of gold, yet greedier of renown." Southey.
Greediness; strong appetite; eagerness; intenseness of desire.
The pulpy fruit of Persea gratissima, a tree of tropical America. It is about the size and shape of a large pear.
A calling away; a diversion. Impulses to duty, and powerful avocations from sin.
To declare openly, as something believed to be right; to own or acknowledge frankly.
To pluck or pull off. Shenstone.
To rouse from sleep or torpor; to awake; to wake. is dispatched Already to awaken whom thou nam'st.
Rousing from sleep, in a natural or a figurative sense; rousing into activity.
To give by sentence or judicial determination; to assign or apportion, after careful regard to the nature of the case.
Washed by the waves or tide; -- said of a rock or strip of shore, or (Naut.) of an anchor, etc., when flush with the surface of the water, so that the waves break over it.
In an awful manner; in a manner to fill with terror or awe; fearfully.
For a while; for some time; for a short time.
A rooflike cover, usually of canvas, extended over or before any place as a shelter from the sun, rain, or wind.
Turned or twisted toward one side; not in a straight or true direction, or position.
A self-evident and necessary truth, or a proposition whose truth is so evident as first sight that no reasoning or demonstration can make it plainer.
The spotted deer (Cervus axis or Axis maculata) of India, where it is called hog deer and parrah (Moorish name).
The pin or spindle on which a wheel revolves, or which revolves with a wheel.
A kind of plum cake.
To utter words indistinctly or unintelligibly; to utter inarticulate sounds.
One of the Old World Quadrumana, of the genera Cynocephalus and Papio.
A Hindoo gentleman; native clerk who writes English; also, a Hindoo title answering to Mr.
Relating to Bacchus or his festival. 2. Engaged in drunken revels; drunken and riotous or noisy.
A feast or an orgy in honor of Bacchus. 2. Hence: A drunken feast; drunken reveler.
The god of wine, son of Jupiter and Semele.
The column of bones in the back which sustains and gives firmness to the frame.
Acting from behind and in concealment; as backdoor intrigues.
Having a back; fitted with a back.
A game of chance and skill, played by two persons on a "board" marked off into twenty-four spaces called "points".
With the hand turned backward.
The act of moving backward, or of putting or moving anything backward.

With the back in advance or foremost.

A distinctive mark, token, sign, or cognizance, worn on the person.
An itinerant licensed dealer in commodities used for food; a hawker; a huckster.
Playful raillery; banter. "He . . . indulged himself only in an elegant badinage." Warburton.
A game, similar to lawn tennis, played with shuttlecocks. 2. A preparation of claret, spiced and sweetened.
Frustrating; discomfiting; disconcerting.
Cloth or other material for bags. 2. The act of putting anything into, or as into, a bag.
Resembling a bag; loose or puffed out, or pendent, like a bag; flabby.

Any substance, esp. food, used in catching fish, or other animals, by alluring them to a hook, snare, inclosure, or net.

The act or process of cooking in an oven, or of drying and hardening by heat or cold.

Full of deadly or pernicious influence; destructive. "Baleful enemies." Shak.

Apt to balk.
Any heavy substance, as stone, iron, etc., put into the hold to sink a vessel in the water to such a depth as to prevent capsizing.

The science or art of hurling missile weapons by the use of an engine.

Having the qualities of balm; odoriferous; aromatic; assuaging; soothing.

Something commonplace, hackneyed, or trivial; the commonplace, in speech.

That which destroys life, esp. poison of a deadly quality. 2. Destruction.
Having poisonous qualities; deadly; destructive; injurious; noxious; pernicious.
Huge; great in size. Forby.
To condemn to exile, or compel to leave one's country, by authority of the ruling power.

A stringed musical instrument having a head and neck like the guitar, and its body like a tambourine.

A trader who secretes himself, or does certain other acts tending to defraud his creditors.
The state of being actually or legally bankrupt. 2. The act or process of becoming a bankrupt.

Lit., May you live ten thousand years; -- used in salutation of the emperor and as a battle cry.

One who administers baptism; -- specifically applied to John, the forerunner of Christ.
To administer the sacrament of baptism to. 2. To christen ( because a name is given to infants at their baptism).
A West Indian island, giving its name to a disease, to a cherry, etc. Barbados cherry (Bot.), a genus of trees of the West Indies (Malpighia) with an agreeably acid fruit resembling a cherry.
The first word in certain mnemonic lines which represent the various forms of the syllogism.
A foreigner. Therefore if I know not the meaning of the voice, I shall be unto him that speaketh a barbarian, and he that speaketh shall be a barbarian unto me.
Of, or from, barbarian nations; foreign; -- often with reference to barbarous nations of east.
A hog, ox, or other large animal roasted or broiled whole for a feast.
Accoutered with defensive armor; -- said of a horse. See Barded ( which is the proper form.) Sir W.
One whose occupation it is to shave or trim the beard, and to cut and dress the hair of his patrons.
A professional poet and singer, as among the ancient Celts, whose occupation was to compose and sing verses in honor of the heroic achievements of princes and brave men.
With the face uncovered; not masked. "You will play barefaced." Shak. 2.
With the feet bare; without shoes or stockings.
A pleasure boat; a vessel or boat of state, elegantly furnished and decorated.
To strip the bark from; to peel. 2. To abrade or rub off any outer covering from.
