A support having handles, and with or without a wheel, on which heavy or bulky things can be transported by hand.
A barkeeper.
To traffic or trade, by exchanging one commodity for another, in distinction from a sale and purchase, in which money is paid for the commodities transferred.
One who barters.
A Bartlett pear, a favorite kind of pear, which originated in England about 1770, and was called Williams' Bonchrétien.
The demesne lands of a manor; also, the manor itself. Burton. 2. A farmyard.
A board, or other woodwork, carried round the walls of a room and touching the floor, to form a base and protect the plastering.

Relating to a base; performing the office of a base in a salt. (b) Having the base in excess, or the amount of the base atomically greater than that of the acid, or exceeding in proportion that of the related neutral...
The slope or angle to which the cutting edge of a tool, as a plane, is ground.
A hollow vessel or dish, to hold water for washing, and for various other uses.
To lie in warmth; to be exposed to genial heat. Basks in the glare, and stems the tepid wave.
Pertaining to Biscay, its people, or their language. 1. One of a race, of unknown origin, inhabiting a region on the Bay of Biscay in Spain and France.
A wind instrument of the double reed kind, furnished with holes, which are stopped by the fingers, and by keys, as in flutes.
A tower or an elevated work, used for the defense, or in the siege, of a fortified place.

The quantity of bread baked at one time. 2. A quantity of anything produced at one operation.
Act of taking a bath or baths. Bathing machine, a small room on wheels, to be driven into the water, for the convenience of bathers, who undress and dress therein.
A weight used in the East, varying according to the locality; in Turkey, the greater batman is about 157 pounds, the lesser only a fourth of this.
A staff or truncheon, used for various purposes.
To make fat by plenteous feeding; to fatten. "Battening our flocks." Milton.
To beat with successive blows; to beat repeatedly and with violence, so as to bruise, shatter, or demolish.
The act of battering or beating. 2. (Law) The unlawful beating of another.
The act of one who bats; the management of a bat in playing games of ball.
Embattled. Tennyson.
Belonging to, or resembling, a bat. "Batty wings." Shak.
Dirty; foul; -- said of clothes. It  is al bawdy and to-tore also. Chaucer.
To cry out with a loud, full sound; to cry with vehemence, as in calling or exultation.
A baker; originally, a female baker.
A pointed instrument of the dagger kind fitted on the muzzle of a musket or rifle, so as to give the soldier increased means of offense and defense.
An inlet from the Gulf of Mexico, from a lake, or from a large river, sometimes sluggish, sometimes without perceptible movement except from tide and wind.
In the East, an exchange, marketplace, or assemblage of shops where goods are exposed for sale.
A small hound, or hunting dog, twelve to fifteen inches high, used in hunting hares and other small game.
The bill or nib of a bird, consisting of a horny sheath, covering the jaws.
Any large piece of timber or iron long in proportion to its thickness, and prepared for use.
Furnished with beams, as the head of a stag. Tost his beamed frontlet to the sky.
Emitting beams; radiant.
A name given to the seed of certain leguminous herbs, chiefly of the genera Faba, Phaseolus, and Dolichos.
Capable of being borne or endured; tolerable. -- Bear"a*bly, adv.

The manner in which one bears or conducts one's self; mien; behavior; carriage.
Pertaining to, or having the form, nature, or habits of, a beast. Beastly divinities and droves of gods.
Having the power to impart or complete blissful enjoyment; blissful. "The beatific vision." South.
To pronounce or regard as happy, or supremely blessed, or as conferring happiness.
Felicity of the highest kind; consummate bliss. 2. Any one of the nine declarations (called the Beatitudes), made in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt.

An amphibious rodent, of the genus Castor. Note: It has palmated hind feet, and a broad, flat tail.
To render calm or quiet; to calm; to still; to appease. Soft whispering airs .
A European fish (Pagellus centrodontus); the sea bream or braise.
Appropriate or fit; congruous; suitable; graceful; befitting. A low and becoming tone.
To daub over; to besmear or soil with anything thick and dirty. Bedaub foul designs with a fair varnish.
A bed and its furniture; the materials of a bed, whether for man or beast.
To deck, ornament, or adorn; to grace. Bedecked with boughs, flowers, and garlands.
To dress or adorn tawdrily or with false taste. Remnants of tapestried hangings, .
A place appropriated to the confinement and care of the insane; a madhouse.
To draggle; to soil, as garments which, in walking, are suffered to drag in dust, mud, etc.
The side of a bed.
The time to go to bed. Shak.
A tree of the genus Fagus. Note: It grows to a large size, having a smooth bark and thick foliage, and bears an edible triangular nut, of which swine are fond.
An animal of the genus Bos, especially the common species, B. taurus, including the bull, cow, and ox, in their full grown state.

A hive for a swarm of bees. Also used figuratively. Note: A common and typical form of beehive was a domeshaped inverted basket, whence certain ancient Irish and Scotch architectural remains are called beehive houses.
A biennial plant of the genus Beta, which produces an edible root the first year and seed the second year.
A heavy mallet, used to drive wedges, beat pavements, etc. 2. A machine in which fabrics are subjected to a hammering process while passing over rollers, as in cotton mills.
To happen to. I beseech your grace that I may know The worst that may befall me.
To involve in a fog; -- mostly as a participle or part. adj. 2. Hence: To confuse.

Advantage; favor; stead; benefit; interest; profit; support; defense; vindication.
To sever the head from; to take off the head of.
An animal, probably the hippopotamus, described in Job xl. 15- 24.
To have in sight; to see clearly; to look at; to regard with the eyes.

, and derivatives. See Behoove, & c.

Delayed beyond the usual time; too late; overtaken by night; benighted.

That believes; having belief. -- Be*liev"ing*ly, adv.

Waging war; carrying on war. "Belligerent powers." E. Everett. 2. Pertaining, or tending, to war.

That which belongs to one; that which pertains to one; hence, goods or effects.

One who, or that which, bends. 2. An instrument used for bending. 3. A drunken spree.
Lower in place, with something directly over or on; under; underneath.
The act of blessing. 2. A blessing; an expression of blessing, prayer, or kind wishes in favor of any person or thing.

, a. Doing or producing good; performing acts of kindness and charity.

The disposition to do good; good will; charitableness; love of mankind, accompanied with a desire to promote their happiness.
well meaning and kindly; generous in spirit
characteristic: Think of a warm, generous person who gives freely and wishes good things for everyone.

definition: The language spoken in Bengal.
Kind; gracious; favorable. -- Be*nig"nant*ly, adv.
The quality of being benign; goodness; kindness; graciousness. "Benignity of aspect." Sir W.
Blessing; beatitude; benediction. Shak. More precious than the benison of friends.

To make destitute; to deprive; to strip; -- with of before the person or thing taken away.

Any small fleshy fruit, as the strawberry, mulberry, huckleberry, etc.
Convenient sea room. (b) A room in which a number of the officers or ship's company mess and reside.

Pellitory of Spain (Anacyclus pyrethrum).

To beset or surround with armed forces, for the purpose of compelling to surrender.

To call to mind; to recall or bring to recollection, reflection, or consideration.
A hospital for lunatics; -- corrupted into bedlam. 2. (Arch.) In the Ethiopic church, a small building attached to a church edifice, in which the bread for the eucharist is made.
To happen to; to befall; to come to.
In good season or time; before it is late; seasonably; early. To measure life learn thou betimes.

Etym:  A short bar used by thieves to wrench doors open. The powerful betty, or the artful picklock.
Any angle other than a right angle; the angle which one surface makes with another when they are not at right angles.
Liquid for drinking; drink; -- usually applied to drink artificially prepared and of an agreeable flavor.
A company; an assembly or collection of persons, especially of ladies.
To lead into perplexity or confusion, as for want of a plain path; to perplex with mazes.
Greatly perplexed.
To gain an ascendency over by charms or incantations; to affect (esp. to injure) by witchcraft or sorcery.
Pertaining to, or derived from, the Bible.
A history or description of books and manuscripts, with notices of the different editions, the times when they were printed, etc.

Command; order; a proclamation or notifying. "Do thou thy master's bidding." Shak.

A corner, bend, or angle; a hollow.

Having two sides; arranged upon two sides; affecting two sides or two parties.

A tree of several species, constituting the genus Betula.

Anything added to raise the sides of a ship. Bailey.

A spiritual overseer, superintendent, or director. Ye were as sheep going astray.

characteristic: It describes an argument so tightly reasoned and persuasive that it is hard to disagree with.

definition: To engage in continuous thought; to think. He that calleth a thing into his mind, whether by impression or recordation, cogitateth and considereth, and he that employeth the faculty of his fancy also cogitateth.
A kind of French brandy, so called from the town of Cognac.
Allied by blood; kindred by birth; specifically (Law), related on the mother's side.
Knowing, or apprehending by the understanding.

The last of the three names of a person among the ancient Romans, denoting his house or family.

The act or state of sticking together; close union. 2. (Physics) That from of attraction by which the particles of a body are united throughout the mass, whether like or unlike.

A headdress, or manner of dressing the hair. Addison.

Like a colt; wanton; frisky. He was all coltish, full of ragery. Chaucer.
America; the United States; -- a poetical appellation given in honor of Columbus, the discoverer.

Relating to, or resembling, coma; drowsy; lethargic.

Pleasing or agreeable to the sight; well-proportioned; good- looking; handsome.

Mildness and suavity of manners; courtesy between equals; friendly equals.
A character or point  marking the smallest divisions of a sentence, written or printed.

The first existence of anything; act or fact of commencing; rise; origin.

The act of commiting, or the state of being committed; commitment.
Adapted to its use or purpose, or to wants and necessities; serviceable.

The mass of the people, as distinguished from the titled chasses or nobility.

Disturbed or violent motion; agitation. commotion in the winds! Shak. 2.
Pertaining to a commune.
The act or fact of communicating.
One who communicates. Boyle.

Capable of existing in harmony; congruous; suitable; not repugnant; -- usually followed by with.

Calm contentment; satisfaction; gratification. The inward complacence we find in acting reasonably and virtuously.

Pertaining to, or furnishing, a diagnosis; indicating the nature of a disease.
A figure or drawing made to illustrate a statement, or facilitate a demonstration.
An instrument, formerly much used for showing the time of day from the shadow of a style or gnomon on a graduated arc or surface.
Means or mode of expressing thoughts; language; tongue; form of speech.
Pertaining to dialectics; logical; argumental. 2. Pertaining to a dialect or to dialects.
One versed in dialectics; a logician; a reasoner.
The art of constructing dials; the science which treats of measuring time by dials.
Diæresis. See Diæresis, 1. 2. (Rhet.) Same as Asyndeton. 3. (Med.) (a) Debility.
Any right line passing through the center of a figure or body, as a circle, conic section, sphere, cube, etc., and terminated by the opposite boundaries.
The daughter of Jupiter and Latona; a virgin goddess who presided over hunting, chastity, and marriage.
Any textile fabric (esp. linen or cotton toweling) woven in diaper pattern.
Allowing light to pass through, as porcelain; translucent or transparent.
A dividing membrane or thin partition, commonly with an opening through it.
A morbidly frequent and profuse discharge of loose or fluid evacuations from the intestines, without tenesmus.
Containing two atoms. (b) Having two replaceable atoms or radicals.
a forceful and bitter verbal attack against someone or something
characteristic: Picture a long, angry rant, a furious speech pouring out harsh criticism without pause.

definition: A sweet preparation or treacle of grape juice, much used in the East. Johnston.
Small cubes used in gaming or in determining by chance; also, the game played with dice.
A cutting in two; a division. A general breach or dichotomy with their church.
The devil. I can not tell what the dickens his name is. Shak.
A seat behind a carriage, for a servant. 2. A false shirt front or bosom.
To tell or utter so that another may write down; to inspire; to compose.
The act of dictating; the act or practice of prescribing; also that which is dictated.
One who dictates; one who prescribes rules and maxims authoritatively for the direction of others.
Pertaining or suited to a dictator; absolute. Military powers quite dictatorial.
The office, or the term of office, of a dictator; hence, absolute power.
A book containing the words of a language, arranged alphabetically, with explanations of their meanings.
An authoritative statement; a dogmatic saying; an apothegm. A class of critical dicta everywhere current.
Fitted or intended to teach; conveying instruction; preceptive; instructive.
One who diets; one who prescribes, or who partakes of, food, according to hygienic rules.
The formal or distinguishing part of the essence of a species; the characteristic attribute of a species.
Relating to or indicating a difference; creating a difference; discriminating.
To distinguish or mark by a specific difference; to effect a difference in, as regards classification.
In a different manner; variously.
The state of being difficult, or hard to do; hardness; arduousness; -- opposed to easiness or facility.
The state of being diffident; distrust; want of confidence; doubt of the power, ability, or disposition of others.
modest or shy because of a lack of self-confidence
characteristic: Imagine someone hanging back at the edge of a room, hesitant and unsure of themselves.

definition: To pour out and cause to spread, as a fluid; to cause to flow on all sides.
Capable of flowing or spreading in all directions; that may be diffused.
The act of diffusing, or the state of being diffused; a spreading; extension.
The act or process of digesting; reduction to order; classification; thoughtful consideration.
One who, or that which, digs. Digger wasp (Zoöl.), any one of the fossorial Hymenoptera.
The act or the place of excavating. 2. pl. Places where ore is dug; especially, certain localities in California, Australia, and elsewhere, at which gold is obtained.
Marked with dignity; stately.
One who possesses exalted rank or holds a position of dignity or honor.
Two signs or characters combined to express a single articulated sound.
To step or turn aside; to deviate; to swerve; especially, to turn aside from the main subject of attention, or course of argument, in writing or speaking.
The act of digressing or deviating, esp. from the main subject of a discourse.
Departing from the main subject; partaking of the nature of digression.
To bring into a condition of decay or partial ruin, by misuse or through neglect.
Decayed; fallen into partial ruin; injured by bad usage or neglect. A deserted and dilapidated buildings.
The act of dilapidating, or the state of being dilapidated, reduced to decay, partially ruined, or squandered.
To expand; to distend; to enlarge or extend in all directions; to swell.
Expanded; enlarged. Shak. 2. (Bot.) Widening into a lamina or into lateral winglike appendages.
slow to act; intended to cause delay
characteristic: Think of someone dragging their feet, stalling and putting things off again and again.

definition: An argument which presents an antagonist with two or more alternatives, but is equally conclusive against him, whichever alternative he chooses.
An admirer or lover of the fine arts; popularly, an amateur; especially, one who follows an art or a branch of knowledge, desultorily, or for amusement only.
The quality of being diligent; carefulness; careful attention; -- the opposite of negligence.
Prosecuted with careful attention and effort; careful; painstaking; not careless or negligent.
In a diligent manner; not carelessly; not negligently; with industry or assiduity.
To make thinner or more liquid by admixture with something; to thin and dissolve by mixing.
A silver coin of the United States, of the value of ten cents; the tenth of a dollar.
Measure in a single line, as length, breadth, height, thickness, or circumference.
Pertaining to dimension.
To make smaller in any manner; to reduce in bulk or amount; to lessen.
The act of diminishing, or of making or becoming less; state of being diminished.
Below the average size; very small; little. 2. Expressing diminution.
In a dim or obscure manner; not brightly or clearly; with imperfect sight.
A slight natural depression or indentation on the surface of some part of the body, esp.
One who dines.
A kind of boat used in the East Indies. Malcom. 2. A ship's smallest boat.
A wild dog found in Australia, but supposed to have introduced at a very early period.
A kind of boat used in the East Indies. Malcom. 2. A ship's smallest boat.
from Dine, a. Note: Used either adjectively or as the first part of a compound.
One of the Dinosauria.
A mode of scenic representation, invented by Daguerre and Bouton, in which a painting is seen from a distance through a large opening.
A letter or writing, usually under seal, conferring some privilege, honor, or power.
A diplomatist.
Pertaining to diplomacy; relating to the foreign ministers at a court, who are called the diplomatic body.
A person employed in, or skilled in, diplomacy; a diplomat. In ability, Avaux had no superior among the numerous able diplomatics whom his country then possessed.
One who, or that which, dips; especially, a vessel used to dip water or other liquid.
The act or process of immersing. 2. The act of inclining downward. 3. The act of lifting or moving a liquid with a dipper, ladle, or the like.
One who has an irrepressible desire for alcoholic drinks.
Ill-boding; portentous.
A piece of music of a mournful character, to accompany funeral rites; a funeral hymn.
State of being disabled; deprivation or want of ability; absence of competent physical, intellectual, or moral power, means, fitness, and the like.
To set free from mistakes; to undeceive; to disengage from fallacy or deception.
Deprivation of advantage; unfavorable or prejudicial quality, condition, circumstance, or the like.
Alienated in feeling; not wholly loyal. J. H. Newman. -- Dis`af*fect"ed*ly, adv.
To fail to accord; not to agree; to lack harmony; to differ; to be unlike.
Not agreeable, conformable, or congruous; Preach you truly the doctrine which you have received, and each nothing that is disagreeable thereunto.
To refuse to allow; to deny the force or validity of; to disown and reject.
To cease to appear or to be perceived; to pass from view, gradually or suddenly.
The act of disappearing; cessation of appearance; removal from sight; vanishing.
To defeat of expectation or hope; to hinder from the attainment of that which was excepted, hoped, or desired.
The act of disappointing, or the state of being disappointed; defeat or failure of expectation or hope.
The act of disapproving; mental condemnation of what is judged wrong, unsuitable, or inexpedient.
To pass unfavorable judgment upon; to condemn by an act of the judgment.
To deprive of arms; to take away the weapons of; to deprive of the means of attack or defense.
Deprived of arms. 2. (Her.) Deprived of claws, and teeth or beaks. Cussans.
To unsettle or disturb the order or due arrangement of; to throw out of order.
To throw into disorder; to break the array of. Who with fiery steeds Oft disarrayed the foes in battle ranged.
To refuse strongly and solemnly to own or acknowledge; to deny responsibility for, approbation of, an the like.
The act of disavowing, disclaiming, or disowning; rejection and denial.
To expel from the bar, or the legal profession; to deprive (an attorney, barrister, or counselor) of his status and privileges as such.
The act of disbelieving;; a state of the mind in which one is fully persuaded that an opinion, assertion, or doctrine is not true.
One who disbelieves, or refuses belief; an unbeliever. Specifically, one who does not believe the Christian religion.
To rid of a burden; to free from a load borne or from something oppressive.
To pay out; to expend; -- usually from a public fund or treasury. The duty of collecting and disbursing his revenues.
A flat round plate; (Biol.) a circular structure either in plants or animals.
To throw out of one's hand, as superfluous cards; to lay aside (a card or cards).
To see and identify by noting a difference or differences; to note the distinctive character of.
Capable of being discerned by the eye or the understanding.
Acute; shrewd; sagacious; sharp-sighted. Macaulay.
Pertaining to discipline; intended for discipline; corrective; belonging to a course of training.
The treatment suited to a disciple or learner; education; development of the faculties by instruction and exercise.
Represented with wings expanded; -- applied to doves and other birds not of prey.
The act of disclosing, uncovering, or revealing; bringing to light; exposure.
To alter the natural hue or color of; to change to a different color; to stain.
To scatter in fight; to put to rout; to defeat. And his proud foes discomfit in victorious field.
To break up the harmonious progress of; to throw into disorder or confusion.
To dissolve the union or connection of; to disunite; to sever; to separate.
The act of discontinuing, or the state of being discontinued; want of continued connection or continuity.
Want of concord or agreement; absence of unity or harmony in sentiment or action.
Disagreeing; incongruous; being at variance; clashing; opposing; not harmonious.
To deduct from an account, debt, charge, and the like; to make an abatement of.
To ruffle or discompose the countenance of; to put of countenance; to put to shame.
To extinguish the courage of; to dishearten; to depress the spirits of.
The power of the mind to reason or infer by running, as it were, from one fact or reason to another, and deriving a conclusion.
The act of discrediting or disbelieving, or the state of being discredited or disbelieved.
Possessed of discernment, especially in avoiding error or evil, and in the adaptation of means to ends.
The state or quality of being discrepant; disagreement; variance; discordance.
Discordant; at variance; disagreeing; contrary; different. The Egyptians were .
Separate; distinct; disjunct. Sir M. Hale. 2. Disjunctive; containing a disjunctive or discretive clause.
Disjunction; separation. Mede. 2. The quality of being discreet; wise conduct and management.
Left to discretion; unrestrained except by discretion or judgment.
Having the difference marked; distinguished by certain tokens. Bacon. To set apart as being different.
Marking a difference; distinguishing. -- Dis*crim"i*na`ting*ly, adv. And finds with keen discriminating sight, Black's not so black.
The act of discriminating, distinguishing, or noting and marking differences.
Passing from one thing to another; ranging over a wide field; roving; digressive.
The act or process of discussing by breaking up, or dispersing, as a tumor, or the like.
A feeling of contempt and aversion; the regarding anything as unworthy of or beneath one.
Afflicted with disease. It is my own diseased imagination that torments me.
To remove from on board a vessel; to put on shore; to land; to debark.
To disfranchise; to deprive of the rights of a citizen. -- Dis`en*fran"chise*ment, n.
To release from that with which anything is engaged, engrossed, involved, or entangled.
To free from entanglement; to release from a condition of being intricately and confusedly involved or interlaced.
To eject or discharge by the throat and mouth; to vomit; to pour forth or throw out with violence, as if from the mouth.
Bringing disgrace; causing shame; shameful; dishonorable; unbecoming.
To dissatisfy; to disaffect; to anger.
To provoke disgust or strong distaste in; to cause (any one) loathing, as of the stomach.
An undress; a loose, negligent dress; deshabille. They breakfast in dishabille.
To discourage; to deprive of courage and hope; to depress the spirits of.
Having in loose disorder; disarranged.
Lack of honor; disgrace; ignominy; shame; reproach. It was not meet for us to see the king's dishonor.
Wanting in honor; not honorable; bringing or deserving dishonor; staining the character, and lessening the reputation.
One who, or that which, washes dishes. 2. (Zoöl.) A European bird; the wagtail.
The act or process of freeing from an illusion, or the state of being freed therefrom.
The state of being disinclined; want of propensity, desire, or affection.
To free from infectious or contagious matter; to destroy putrefaction.
That which disinfects; an agent for removing the causes of infection, as chlorine.
Not noble; unbecoming true honor or dignity; mean; unworthy.

To cut off from an inheritance or from hereditary succession; to prevent, as an heir, from coming into possession of any property or right, which, by law or custom, would devolve on him in the course of descent.
To separate into integrant parts; to reduce to fragments or to powder.
To take out of the grave or tomb; to unbury; to exhume; to dig up. 2. To bring out, as from a grave or hiding place.
Disinterested. The measures they shall walk by shall be disinterest and even.
Not influenced by regard to personal interest or advantage; free from selfish motive.
Separated at the joints; disconnected; incoherent. -- Dis*joint"ed*ly, adv.
The act of disjoining; disunion; separation; a parting.
Tending to disjoin; separating; disjoining. 2. (Mus.) Pertaining to disjunct tetrachords.
A discus; a quoit. Some whirl the disk, and some the javelin dart. Pope.
To displace; to put out of its proper place. Especially, of a bone: To remove from its normal connections with a neighboring bone.
Not loyal; not true to a sovereign or lawful superior, or to the government under which one lives.
To strip or deprive of dress; to divest. 2. To strip of furniture and equipments, guns, etc.
To send away; to give leave of departure; to cause or permit to go; to put away.
Dismission; discharge. Officeholders were commanded faithfully to enforce it, upon pain of immediate dismissal.
To come down; to descend. But now the bright sun ginneth to dismount. Spenser.
Neglect or refusal to obey; violation of a command or prohibition. He is undutiful to him other actions, and lives in open disobedience.
Neglecting or refusing to obey; omitting to do what is commanded, or doing what is prohibited.
Not to obey; to neglect or refuse to obey (a superior or his commands, the laws, etc.).
Not in order; marked by disorder; disarranged; immethodical.
To refuse to own or acknowledge as belonging to one's self; to disavow or deny, as connected with one's self personally.
to regard or represent as being of little worth; to belittle
characteristic: It is talking someone down, mocking their efforts to make them seem small and unimportant.

definition: Unequal; dissimilar; separate. Connecting disparate thoughts, purely by means of resemblances in the words expressing them.
Inequality; difference in age, rank, condition, or excellence; dissimilitude.
Free from passion; not warped, prejudiced, swerved, or carried away by passion or feeling.
To dispose of speedily, as business; to execute quickly; to make a speedy end of.
One who dispatches.
To drive away by scattering, or so to cause to vanish; to clear away; to banish.
A place where medicines are prepared and dispensed; esp., a place where the poor can obtain medical advice and medicines gratuitously or at a nominal price.
The act of dispensing or dealing out; distribution; often used of the distribution of good and evil by God to man, or more generically, of the acts and modes of his administration.
To deal out in portions; to distribute; to give.
One who, or that which, dispenses; a distributer.
To scatter abroad; to drive to different parts; to distribute; to diffuse.
Scattered. -- Dis*pers"ed*ly, adv. -- Dis*pers"ed*ness, n. Dispersed harmony (Mus.), harmony in which the tones composing the chord are widely separated, as by an octave or more.
The act or process of scattering or dispersing, or the state of being scattered or separated.
Depressed in spirits; disheartened; daunted. -- Dis*pir"it*ed*ly, adv.
The act of displacing, or the state of being displaced; a putting out of place.
Unfolded; expanded; exhibited conspicuously or ostentatiously. 2. (Her.) With wings expanded.
Play; sport; pastime; diversion; playfulness. Milton. To play; to wanton.
Subject to disposal; free to be used or employed as occasion may require.
The act of disposing, or disposing of, anything; arrangement; orderly distribution.
The act of disposing, arranging, ordering, regulating, or transferring.
To put out of possession; to deprive of the actual occupancy of, particularly of land or real estate.
Want of proportion in form or quantity; lack of symmetry.
The act of disputing; a reasoning or argumentation in opposition to something, or on opposite sides.
Inclined to dispute; apt to civil or controvert; characterized by dispute.
To deprive of the qualities or properties necessary for any purpose; to render unfit.
A formal or systematic inquiry into, or discussion of, any subject; a full examination or investigation of a matter, with the arguments and facts bearing upon it.
Not to regard; to pay no heed to; to omit to take notice of; to neglect to observe.
Not reputable; of bad repute; not in esteem; dishonorable; disgracing the reputation.
Want of respect or reverence; disesteem; incivility; discourtesy. Impatience of bearing the least affront or disrespect.
Wanting in respect; manifesting disesteem or lack of respect; uncivil.
To divest of a robe; to undress; figuratively, to strip of covering; to divest of that which clothes or decorates.
Rent off; torn asunder; severed; disrupted. To break asunder; to rend.
The act or rending asunder, or the state of being rent asunder or broken in pieces.
Causing, or tending to cause, disruption; caused by disruption; breaking through.
To render unsatisfied or discontented; to excite uneasiness in by frustrating wishes or expectations.
To divide into separate parts; to cut in pieces; to separate and expose the parts of, as an animal or a plant, for examination and to show their structure and relations.
The act of dissecting an animal or plant.
To hide under a false semblance or seeming; to feign (something) not to be what it really is.
One who dissembles; one who conceals his opinions or dispositions under a false appearance.
To sow broadcast or as seed; to scatter for growth and propagation, like seed.
Disagreement in opinion, usually of a violent character, producing warm debates or angry words.
To differ in opinion; to be of unlike or contrary sentiment; to disagree.
Disagreeing; declaring dissent; dissenting. -- n. One who dissents. Macaulay.
Marked by dissensions; apt to breed discord; quarrelsome; contentious.
A formal or elaborate argumentative discourse, oral or written; a disquisition.
Injury; mischief. We shall rather perform good offices unto truth than any disservice unto their relators.
To part in two; to sever thoroughly; to sunder; to disunite; to separate.
No agreeing; dissenting; discordant; different. Our life and manners be dissident from theirs.
Not similar; unlike; heterogeneous.
To scatter completely; to disperse and cause to disappear; -- used esp.
The act of dissipating or dispersing; a state of dispersion or separation.
The act of dissolving, sundering, or separating into component parts; separation.
To separate into competent parts; to disorganize; to break up; hence, to bring to an end by separating the parts, sundering a relation, etc.
Melting; breaking up; vanishing. -- Dis*solv"ing*ly, adv. Dissolving view, a picture which grows dim and is gradually replaced by another on the same field.
A mingling of discordant sounds; an inharmonious combination of sounds.
Sounding harshly; discordant; unharmonious. With clamor of voices dissonant and loud.
To advise or exhort against; to try to persuade (one from a course). Mr.
The act of dissuading; exhortation against a thing; dehortation. In spite of all the dissuasions of his friends.
The staff for holding a bunch of flax, tow, or wool, from which the thread is drawn in spinning by hand.
To tinge with a different color from the natural or proper one; to stain.
Remote from the point of attachment or origin.
Separated; having an intervening space; at a distance; away. One board had two tenons, equally distant.
Unpleasant or disgusting to the taste; nauseous; loathsome. 2. Offensive.
To extend in some one direction; to lengthen out; to stretch. But say, what mean those colored streaks in heaven Distended as the brow of God appeased Milton.
Capable of being distended or dilated.
The act of distending; the act of stretching in breadth or in all directions.
To drop; to fall in drops; to trickle. Soft showers distilled, and suns grew warm in vain.
One who distills; esp., one who extracts alcoholic liquors by distillation.
Marked; special. The most distinguished politeness. Mad. D' Arblay. 2.
Constituting difference, or distinction from everything else; distinctive.
Distorted; misshapen. Her face was ugly and her mouth distort. Spenser.
The act of distorting, or twisting out of natural or regular shape; a twisting or writhing motion.
Mentally disordered; unsettled; mad. My distracted mind. Pope.
Tending or serving to distract.
One who distrains; the party distraining goods or chattels. Blackstone.
Absent-minded; lost in thought; abstracted.
Torn asunder; separated. "His greedy throat . . . distraught." Spenser.
Extreme pain or suffering; anguish of body or mind.
Causing distress; painful; unpleasant. In a distressing manner.
To divide among several or many; to deal out; to apportion; to allot. She did distribute her goods to all them that were nearest of kindred.
That distributes; dealing out. Distributing past office, an office where the mails for a large district are collected to be assorted according to their destination and forwarded.
The act of distributing or dispensing; the act of dividing or apportioning among several or many.
A machine for distributing type. (b) An appliance, as a roller, in a printing press, for distributing ink.
To feel absence of trust in; not to confide in or rely upon; to deem of questionable sufficiency or reality.
An interruption of a state of peace or quiet; derangement of the regular course of things.
The termination of union; separation; disjunction.
A trench made in the earth by digging, particularly a trench for draining wet land, for guarding or fencing inclosures, or for preventing an approach to a town or fortress.
The aforesaid thing; the same (as before). Often contracted to do., or to two "turned commas" ("), or small marks.
A saying or utterance; especially, one that is short and frequently repeated.
Relating to the daytime; belonging to the period of daylight, distinguished from the night.
A prima donna.
A wandering about or going astray; digression. Let us be set down at Queen's Crawley without further divagation.
One who, or that which, dives. Divers and fishers for pearls. Woodward.
To extend from a common point in different directions; to tend from one point and recede from each other.
Receding farther and farther from each other, as lines radiating from one point.
Distinguished by various forms, or by a variety of aspects or objects.
The act of turning aside from any course, occupation, or object.
A state of difference; dissimilitude; unlikeness. They will prove opposite.
To turn aside; to turn off from any course or intended application; to deflect.
Capable of being diverted.
To unclothe; to strip, as of clothes, arms, or equipage; -- opposed to invest.
The act of stripping, or depriving; the state of being divested; the deprivation, or surrender, of possession of property, rights, etc.
Parted; disunited; distributed. 2. (Bot.) Cut into distinct parts, by incisions which reach the midrib.
That divides; separating; marking divisions; graduating. Dividing engine, a machine for graduating circles (as for astronomical instruments) or bars (as for scales).
The act of divining; a foreseeing or foretelling of future events; the pretended art discovering secret or future by preternatural means.
That dives or is used or diving. Diving beetle (Zoöl.), any beetle of the family Dytiscidæ, which habitually lives under water.
The state of being divine; the nature or essence of God; deity; godhead.
Capable of being divided or separated. Extended substance . . . is divisible into parts.
The act or process of diving anything into parts, or the state of being so divided.
The number by which the dividend is divided. Common divisor. (Math.) See under Common, a.
To make public; to several or communicate to the public; to tell (a secret) so that it may become generally known.
A colloquial name for the Southern portion of the United States, esp. during the Civil War.
Giddiness; a whirling sensation in the head; vertigo.
Having in the head a sensation of whirling, with a tendency to fall; vertiginous.
Capable of being done. Carlyle.
The aquatic larva of a large neuropterous insect (Corydalus cornutus), used as bait in angling.
Teachable; easy to teach; docible. 2. Disposed to be taught; tractable.
A small piece of paper or parchment, containing the heads of a writing.
The degree, title, or rank, of a doctor. To make (one) a doctor. He was bred .
One who would apply to political or other practical concerns the abstract doctrines or the theories of his own philosophical system.
Teaching; instruction. He taught them many things by parables, and said unto them in his doctrine, Hearken.
That which is taught or authoritatively set forth; precept; instruction.
Pertaining to written evidence; contained or certified in writing. "Documentary evidence." Macaulay.
To cut off, as wool from sheep's tails; to lop or clip off. Halliwell.
A plant of the genus Cuscuta. It is a leafless parasitical vine with yellowish threadlike stems.
To start suddenly aside, as to avoid a blow or a missile; to shift place by a sudden start.
One who dodges or evades; one who plays fast and loose, or uses tricky devices.
A large, extinct bird (Didus ineptus), formerly inhabiting the Island of Mauritius.
To put off, as dress; to divest one's self of; hence, figuratively, to put or thrust away.
Sullen; morose. The sulky spite of a temper naturally dogged. Sir W. Scott.
Low in style, and irregular in measure.
That which is held as an opinion; a tenet; a doctrine. The obscure and loose dogmas of early antiquity.
inclined to lay down principles as undeniably true, allowing no debate
characteristic: Picture a rigid person insisting they are right and flatly refusing to consider any other view.

definition: Softly; sweetly; with soft, smooth, and delicate execution.
A part of the ocean near the equator, abounding in calms, squalls, and light, baffling winds, which sometimes prevent all progress for weeks.
Full of dole or grief; expressing or exciting sorrow; sorrowful; sad; dismal.
Doleful; dismal; gloomy; sorrowful. -- Dole"some*ly, adv. -- Dole"some*ness, n.
A contrivance, turning on a vertical axis by a handle or winch, and giving a circular motion to the ore to be washed.
Pain; grief; distress; anguish. Of death and dolor telling sad tidings.
Full of grief; sad; sorrowful; doleful; dismal.
A cetacean of the genus Delphinus and allied genera (esp. D. delphis).
Dominion; empire; authority. 2. The territory over which dominion or authority is exerted.
The state of being domestic; domestic character; household life.
An abode or mansion; a place of permanent residence, either of an individual or a family.
Predominance; ascendency; authority.
Ruling; governing; prevailing; controlling; predominant.
To predominate over; to rule; to govern. "A city dominated by the ax." Dickens.
To rule with insolence or arbitrary sway; to play the master; to be overbearing.
Sovereign or supreme authority; the power of governing and controlling.
A kind of hood worn by the canons of a cathedral church; a sort of amice.
Lady; mistress; madam; -- a title of respect used in Spain, prefixed to the Christian name of a lady.
To give; to bestow; to present.
The act of giving or bestowing; a grant. After donation there an absolute change and alienation of the property of the thing given.
One who makes a gift; a donor; a giver.
The person to whom a gift or donation is made. 2. (Law) Anciently, one to whom lands were given.
An ass; or (less frequently) a mule. 2. A stupid or obstinate fellow; an ass.
One who gives or bestows; one who confers anything gratuitously; a benefactor.
A trifler; a simple fellow.
A day of sentence or condemnation; day of death. "My body's doomsday." Shak.
The stone or plank forming a step before an outer door.
The passage of a door; entrance way into a house or a room.
Any thick liquid or pasty preparation, as of opium for medicinal purposes, of grease for a lubricant, etc.
Affected by "dope"; esp., sluggish or dull as though under the influence of a narcotic.
A spiritual or ghostly double or counterpart; esp., an apparitional double of a living person.
A window pierced in a roof, and so set as to be vertical while the roof slopes away from it.
Pertaining to, or situated near, the back, or dorsum, of an animal or of one of its parts.
A European fish. See Doree, and John Doree. 2. (Zoöl.) The American wall-eyed perch.
The administration of medicine in doses; specif., a scheme or system of grading doses of medicine according to age, etc.
The quantity of medicine given, or prescribed to be taken, at one time.
A bundle containing the papers in reference to some matter.
Feebleness or imbecility of understanding or mind, particularly in old age.
Marked with, or made of, dots or small spots; diversified with small, detached objects.
Composed of, or characterized by, dots. 2. Unsteady in gait; hence, feeble.
Two of the same kind; a pair; a couple. 2. (Print.) A word or words unintentionally doubled or set up a second time.
The act of one that doubles; a making double; reduplication; also, that which is doubled.
In twice the quantity; to twice the degree.
That is uncertain; that distrusts or hesitates; having doubts. -- Doubt"ing*ly, adv.
A jet or current of water or vapor directed upon some part of the body to benefit it medicinally.
Paste of bread; a soft mass of moistened flour or meal, kneaded or unkneaded, but not yet baked.
A small cake (usually sweetened) fried in a kettle of boiling lard.
Able; strong; valiant; redoubtable.
To plunge suddenly into water; to duck; to immerse; to dowse. Bp. Stillingfleet.
A pigeon of the genus Columba and various related genera. The species are numerous.

A flaring tenon, or tongue (shaped like a bird's tail spread), and a mortise, or socket, into which it fits tightly, making an interlocking joint between two pieces which resists pulling a part in all directions...

A widow endowed, or having a jointure; a widow who either enjoys a dower from her deceased husband, or has property of her own brought by her to her husband on marriage, and settled on her after his decease.
Showing a vulgar taste in dress; awkward and slovenly in dress; vulgar-looking.
Cast downward; directed to the ground, from bashfulness, modesty, dejection, or guilt.
A sudden fall; a body of things falling. Those cataracts or downfalls aforesaid.
Towards the bottom of a hill.
A pouring or streaming downwards; esp., a heavy or continuous shower.
Down the stream.
From a higher place to a lower; in a descending course.
From a higher place to a lower; in a descending course.
Lit., a dean; the senior member of a body or group.
A low, sluttish woman. King. 2. A lewd wench; a strumpet. Shak. 3. A wooden box, used in salt works for holding the salt when taken out of the boiling pans.
A silver coin among the ancient Greeks, having a different value in different States and at different periods.
The Dragon, a northern constellation within which is the north pole of the ecliptic.
Pertaining to Draco, a famous lawgiver of Athens, 621 b. c. Draconian code, or Draconian laws, a code of laws made by Draco.
Pertaining to, or used for, drawing or pulling (as vehicles, loads, etc.).
A net to be drawn along the bottom of a body of water, as in fishing.
Formerly, a soldier who was taught and armed to serve either on horseback or on foot.
To draw off by degrees; to cause to flow gradually out or off; hence, to cause the exhaustion of.
A draining; a gradual flowing off of any liquid; also, that which flows out of a drain.
The art of carrying off surplus water, as from land. Draining tile. Same as Draintile.

A composition, in prose or poetry, accommodated to action, and intended to exhibit a picture of human life, or to depict a series of grave or humorous actions of more than ordinary interest, tending toward some...
In a dramatic manner; theatrically; vividly.
The author of a dramatic composition; a writer of plays.
To compose in the form of the drama; to represent in a drama; to adapt to dramatic representation.
One who sells cloths; a dealer in cloths.
Acting rapidly and violently; efficacious; powerful; -- opposed to bland.
One who, or that which, draws; as: (a) One who draws liquor for guests.
The act of pulling, or attracting. 2. The act or the art of representing any object by means of lines and shades.
To utter in a slow, lengthened tone. To speak with slow and lingering utterance, from laziness, lack of spirit, affectation, etc.
In a dreadful manner; terribly. Dryden.
One who dreams. 2. A visionary; one lost in wild imaginations or vain schemes of some anticipated good.
Abounding in dreams or given to dreaming; appropriate to, or like, dreams.
One who dresses; one who put in order or makes ready for use; one who on clothes or ornaments.
Dress; raiment; especially, ornamental habiliment or attire. B. Jonson.
A maker of gowns, or similar garments; a mantuamaker.
The act of piercing with a drill. 2. A training by repeated exercises.
One who drinks.
To fall in drops.
A falling in drops, or the sound so made. 2. That which falls in drops, as fat from meat in roasting.
A passage or way along or through which a carriage may be driven.
To rain slightly in very small drops; to fall, as water from the clouds, slowly and in fine particles.
Queer, and fitted to provoke laughter; ludicrous from oddity; amusing and strange.
To drivel, or drop saliva.
The action of causing to drop or of letting drop; falling. 2. pl. That which falls in drops.
Dryness; want of rain or of water; especially, such dryness of the weather as affects the earth, and prevents the growth of plants.
Inclined to drowse; heavy with sleepiness; lethargic; dozy. "When I am drowsy." Shak.
To perform menial work; to labor in mean or unpleasant offices with toil and fatigue.
The act of drudging; disagreeable and wearisome labor; ignoble or slavish toil.
One whose office is to best the drum, as in military exercises and marching.
The act of beating upon, or as if upon, a drum; also, the noise which the male of the ruffed grouse makes in spring, by beating his wings upon his sides.
One who habitually drinks strong liquors immoderately; one whose habit it is to get drunk.
Overcome by strong drink; intoxicated by, or as by, spirituous liquor.
The state of being drunken with, or as with, alcoholic liquor; intoxication.
Adapted or tending to exhaust moisture.
Expressing, or consisting of, the number two; belonging to two.
Doubtful or not settled in opinion; being in doubt; wavering or fluctuating.
The wife or widow of a duke; also, a lady who has the sovereignty of a duchy in her own right.
, from Duck, v. t. & i. Ducking stool, a stool or chair in which common scolds were formerly tied, and plunged into water, as a punishment.
A young or little duck. Gay. DUCKMEAT; DUCK'S-MEAT Duck"meat`, or Duck's"-meat`, n.
Easily led; tractable; complying; yielding to motives, persuasion, or instruction.
Old or inferior clothes; tattered garments. 2. Effects, in general.

The chief lady in waiting on the queen of Spain. Brande. 2. An elderly lady holding a station between a governess and companion, and appointed to have charge over the younger ladies in a Spanish or a Portuguese family.
A composition for two performers, whether vocal or instrumental.
A kind of coarse woolen cloth, having a thick nap or frieze. Good duffel gray and flannel fine.
A mistress; a sweetheart. I must ever have some Dulcinea in my head. Sterne.
Slow of understanding; wanting readiness of apprehension; stupid; doltish.
A stupid person; a dunce. Shak. -- a. Stupid. Bp. Hall.
In a due, fit, or becoming manner; as it (anything) ought to be; properly.
Silent; mute; noiseless; as a dummy engine. 2. Fictitious or sham; feigned.
A roundish mass of dough boiled in soup, or as a sort of pudding; often, a cover of paste inclosing an apple or other fruit, and boiled or baked.
A low hill of drifting sand usually formed on the coats, but often carried far inland by the prevailing winds.
The excrement of an animal. Bacon. 1. To manure with dung. Dryden. 2. (Calico Print.) To immerse or steep, as calico, in a bath of hot water containing cow dung.
A close, dark prison, commondonjon or keep of a castle, these being used as prisons.
One who has been deceived or who is easily deceived; a gull.
Double; twofold. Duplex escapement, a peculiar kind of watch escapement, in which the scape-wheel has two sets of teeth.
Double; twofold. Duplicate proportion or ratio (Math.), the proportion or ratio of squares.
Doubleness; a twofold state. Do not affect duplicities nor triplicities, nor any certain number of parts in your division of things.
Able to endure or continue in a particular condition; lasting; not perishable or changeable.
The state or quality of lasting; continuance in time; the portion of time during which anything exists.
Hardship; constraint; pressure; imprisonment; restraint of liberty. The agreements .
One or a breed of short-horned cattle, originating in the county of Durham, England.
One who, or that which, dusts; a utensil that frees from dust. Specifically: (a) (Paper Making) A revolving wire-cloth cylinder which removes the dust from rags, etc.
Filled, covered, or sprinkled with dust; clouded with dust.
Pertaining to Holland, or to its inhabitants. Dutch auction. See under Auction.
Fulfilling duty; dutiful; having the sentiments due to a superior, or to one to whom respect or service is owed.
Subject to the payment of a duty; as dutiable goods. All kinds of dutiable merchandise.
Performing, or ready to perform, the duties required by one who has the right to claim submission, obedience, or deference.
An animal or plant which is much below the ordinary size of its species or kind.
Habitation; place or house in which a person lives; abode; domicile. Hazor shall be a dwelling for dragons.
That branch of mechanics which treats of the motion of bodies (kinematics) and the action of forces in producing or changing their motion (kinetics).
An explosive substance consisting of nitroglycerin absorbed by some inert, porous solid, as infusorial earth, sawdust, etc.
A dynamo-electric machine.
Sovereignty; lordship; dominion. Johnson. 2. A race or succession of kings, of the same line or family.
The unit of force, in the C. G. S. (Centimeter Gram Second) system of physical units.
A disease attended with inflammation and ulceration of the colon and rectum, and characterized by griping pains, constant desire to evacuate the bowels, and the discharge of mucus and blood.
Pertaining to dyspepsia; having dyspepsia.
In an eager manner.
Any large, rapacious bird of the Falcon family, esp. of the genera Aquila and Haliæetus.
A young eagle, or a diminutive eagle.
A nobleman of England ranking below a marquis, and above a viscount. The rank of an earl corresponds to that of a count (comte) in France, and graf in Germany.
A mark on the ear of sheep, oxen, dogs, etc., as by cropping or slitting.
Seriousness; reality; fixed determination; eagerness; intentness. Take heed that this jest do not one day turn to earnest.
That which is earned; wages gained by work or services; money earned; -- used commonly in the plural.
An ornament consisting of a ring passed through the lobe of the ear, with or without a pendant.
Reach of the ear; distance at which words may be heard. Dryden.
Vessels and other utensils, ornaments, or the like, made of baked clay.
A shaking, trembling, or concussion of the earth, due to subterranean causes, often accompanied by a rumbling noise.
A frame (commonly) of wood serving to hold a canvas upright, or nearly upright, for the painter's convenience or for exhibition.
An annual church festival commemorating Christ's resurrection, and occurring on Sunday, the second day after Good Friday.
Situated or dwelling in the east; oriental.
Capable of being eaten; fit to be eaten; proper for food; esculent; edible.
One who, or that which, eats.
To stand under the eaves, near a window or at the door, of a house, to listen and learn what is said within doors.
The habit of lurking about dwelling houses, and other places where persons meet fro private intercourse, secretly listening to what is said, and then tattling it abroad.
A hard, heavy, and durable wood, which admits of a fine polish or gloss.
A boiling up or over; effervescence. Cudworth.
cheerful and full of energy; overflowing with enthusiasm
characteristic: Think of bubbles fizzing up out of soda, a person bursting with bright, lively excitement.

definition: Deviating or departing from the center, or from the line of a circle.
The state of being eccentric; deviation from the customary line of conduct.
An arrangement of a body of troops when its divisions are drawn up in parallel lines each to the right or the left of the one in advance of it, like the steps of a ladder in position for climbing.
A sound reflected from an opposing surface and repeated to the ear of a listener.
Brilliancy of success or effort; splendor; brilliant show; striking effect.
deriving ideas or style from a broad and diverse range of sources
characteristic: Imagine a playlist mixing jazz, hip-hop, and classical, a colorful blend drawn from many styles.

definition: Theory or practice of an eclectic.

An interception or obscuration of the light of the sun, moon, or other luminous body, by the intervention of some other body, either between it and the eye, or between the luminous body and that illuminated by it.
A great circle of the celestial sphere, making an angle with the equinoctial of about 23º 28'.
Pertaining to the household; domestic. "In this economical misfortune " Milton.
Pertaining to the household; domestic. "In this economical misfortune " Milton.
With economy; with careful management; with prudence in expenditure.
The science of household affairs, or of domestic management. 2. Political economy.
One who economizes, or manages domestic or other concerns with frugality.
To manage with economy; to use with prudence; to expend with frugality.
The management of domestic affairs; the regulation and government of household matters.
General; universal; in ecclesiastical usage, that which concerns the whole church.
The garden where Adam and Eve first dwelt; hence, a delightful region or residence.
Easily irritated; sharp.
Fit to be eaten as food; eatable; esculent.
A public command or ordinance by the sovereign power; the proclamation of a law made by an absolute authority, as if by the very act of announcement.
A building; a structure; an architectural fabric; -- chiefly applied to elegant houses, and other large buildings.
To build; to construct. There was a holy chapel edified. Spenser. 2. To instruct and improve, especially in moral and religious knowledge.
Instructing; improving.
To superintend the publication of; to revise and prepare for publication.
A literary work edited and published, as by a certain editor or in a certain manner.
One who edits; esp., a person who prepares, superintends, revises, and corrects a book, magazine, or newspaper, etc., for publication.
Formed or developed by education.
To bring or draw out; to cause to appear; to produce against counter agency or influence.
Serving to inspire fear, esp. a dread of seeing ghosts; wild; weird.
To cause to disappear (as anything impresses or inscribed upon a surface) by rubbing out, striking out, etc.
Execution; performance; realization; operation.
Having the power to produce an effect or effects; producing a decided or decisive effect.
With effect; powerfully; completely; thoroughly.
The quality of being effective.
Producing, or having adequate power or force to produce, an intended effect.
To bring to pass; to effect; to achieve; to accomplish; to fulfill. A fit instrument to effectuate his desire.
Characteristic quality of a woman, such as softness, luxuriousness, delicacy, or weakness, which is unbecoming a man.
Having some characteristic of a woman, as delicacy, luxuriousness, etc.
To be in a state of natural ebullition; to bubble and hiss, as fermenting liquors, or any fluid, when some part escapes in a gaseous form.
A kind of natural ebullition; that commotion of a fluid which takes place when some part of the mass flies off in a gaseous form, producing innumerable small bubbles.
Gently boiling or bubbling, by means of the disengagement of gas.
No longer capable of producing young, as an animal, or fruit, as the earth.
Possessing the quality of being effective; productive of, or powerful to produce, the effect intended.
Power to produce effects; operation or energy of an agent or force; production of the effect intended.
The quality of being efficient or producing an effect or effects; efficient power.
Causing effects; producing results; that makes the effect to be what it is.
With effect; effectively.
The image, likeness, or representation of a person, whether a full figure, or a part.
Flowering, or state of flowering; the blooming of flowers; blowth. 2. (Med.) A redness of the skin.
That effloresces, or is liable to effloresce on exposure.
Subtile or invisible emanation; exhalation perceived by the sense of smell.
Impudence or boldness in confronting or in transgressing the bounds of duty or decorum.
The state of being effulgent; extreme brilliancy; a flood of light; great luster or brightness.
Diffusing a flood of light; shining; luminous; beaming; bright; splendid.
The act of pouring out.
Pouring out; pouring forth freely. "Washed with the effusive wave." Pope.
A drink consisting of eggs beaten up with sugar, milk, and (usually) wine or spirits.
A plant (Solanum Melongena), of East Indian origin, allied to the tomato, and bearing a large, smooth, edible fruit, shaped somewhat like an egg.

The doctrine of certain extreme adherents or disciples of Descartes and Johann Gottlieb Fichte, which finds all the elements of knowledge in the ego and the relations which it implies or provides for.
One given overmuch to egoism or thoughts of self. I, dullard egoist, taking no special recognition of such nobleness.
The practice of too frequently using the word I; hence, a speaking or writing overmuch of one's self.
One addicted to egotism; one who speaks much of himself or magnifies his own achievements or affairs.
Addicted to, or manifesting, egotism. Syn. -- Conceited; vain; self-important.
outstandingly bad; shocking
characteristic: It describes a mistake so glaringly awful that everyone notices it immediately.

definition: The act of going out or leaving, or the power to leave; departure. Embarred from all egress and regress.
Eight and ten.
Next in order after the seventh. 2. Consisting of one of eight equal divisions of a thing.
Eight times ten; fourscore. 1. The sum of eight times ten; eighty units or objects.
The act of throwing or darting out with a sudden force and rapid flight.
To expel; to dismiss; to cast forth; to thrust or drive out; to discharge.
Wrought with labor; finished with great care; studied; executed with exactness or painstaking.
The act or process of producing or refining with labor; improvement by successive operations.
To slip or glide away; to pass away silently, as time; -- used chiefly in reference to time.
Springing back; having a power or inherent property of returning to the form from which a substance is bent, drawn, pressed, or twisted.
The quality of being elastic; the inherent property in bodies by which they recover their former figure or dimensions, after the removal of external pressure or altering force.
A lifting up by success; exaltation; inriation with pride of prosperity.
The joint or bend of the arm; the outer curve in the middle of the arm when bent.
Older; more aged, or existing longer. Let the elder men among us emulate their own earlier deeds.
Somewhat old; advanced beyond middle age; bordering on old age.
Oldest; longest in duration. Shak. 2. Born or living first, or before the others, as a son, daughter, brother, etc.
Chosen; taken by preference from among two or more. "Colors quaint elect." Spenser.
Pertaining to electricity; consisting of, containing, derived from, or produced by, electricity.
Pertaining to electricity; consisting of, containing, derived from, or produced by, electricity.
An investigator of electricity; one versed in the science of electricity.

A power in nature, a manifestation of energy, exhibiting itself when in disturbed equilibrium or in activity by a circuit movement, the fact of direction in which involves polarity, or opposition of properties in...
The act or process of chemical decomposition, by the action of electricity.
Amber; also, the alloy of gold and silver, called electrum.
A facsimile plate made by electrotypy for use in printing; also, an impression or print from such plate.
Relating to charity, alms, or almsgiving; intended for the distribution of charity.
The state or quality of being elegant; beauty as resulting from choice qualities and the complete absence of what deforms or impresses unpleasantly.
Very choice, and hence, pleasing to good taste; characterized by grace, propriety, and refinement, and the absence of every thing offensive.
Belonging to elegy, or written in elegiacs; plaintive; expressing sorrow or lamentation.
A mournful or plaintive poem; a funereal song; a poem of lamentation. Shak.
Pertaining to the elements, first principles, and primary ingredients, or to the four supposed elements of the material world.
Having only one principle or constituent part; consisting of a single element.
Pertaining to the elephant, or resembling an elephant (commonly, in size).
Elevated; raised aloft. Milton. 1. To bring from a lower place to a higher.
Uplifted; high; lofty; also, animated; noble.
The act of raising from a lower place, condition, or quality to a higher.
Ten and one added.
Next after the tenth.
Elicited; drawn out; made real; open; evident. "An elicit act of equity." Jer.
That may be selected; proper or qualified to be chosen; legally qualified to be elected and to hold office.
To put out of doors; to expel; to discharge; to release; to set at liberty.
The act of expelling or throwing off; (Physiol.) the act of discharging or excreting waste products or foreign substances through the various emunctories.
A choice or select body; the flower.
A tincture with more than one base; a compound tincture or medicine, composed of various substances, held in solution by alcohol in some form.
Pertaining to Queen Elizabeth or her times, esp. to the architecture or literature of her reign.
To run away, or escape privately, from the place or station to which one is bound by duty.

Fluent, forcible, elegant, and persuasive speech in public; the power of expressing strong emotions in striking and appropriate language either spoken or written, thereby producing conviction or persuasion.
fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing
characteristic: Picture a speaker whose graceful, flowing words move the whole audience to tears or cheers.

definition: In any other place.
To make clear or manifest; to render more intelligible; to illustrate.
To avoid slyly, by artifice, stratagem, or dexterity; to escape from in a covert manner.
Act of eluding; adroit escape, as by artifice; a mockery; a cheat; trickery.
Tending to elude; using arts or deception to escape; adroitly escaping or evading.
Pertaining, or the abode of the blessed after death; hence, yielding the highest pleasures.
A dwelling place assigned to happy souls after death; the seat of future happiness.
To lose flesh gradually and become very lean; to waste away in flesh. "He emaciated and pined away." Sir T.
To set free from the power of another; to liberate; as: (a) To set free, as a minor from a parent.
The act of setting free from the power of another, from slavery, subjection, dependence, or controlling influence.
To deprive of virile or procreative power; to castrate power; to castrate.
An edict or order of the government prohibiting the departure of ships of commerce from some or all of the ports within its dominions.
To cause to go on board a vessel or boat; to put on shipboard. 2. To engage, enlist, or invest (as persons, money, etc.) in any affair.
A state of being embarrassed; perplexity; impediment to freedom of action.
The public function of an ambassador; the charge or business intrusted to an ambassador or to envoys.
To lay as in a bed; to lay in surrounding matter; to bed.
To make beautiful or elegant by ornaments; to decorate; to adorn.
The fraudulent appropriation of property by a person to whom it has been intrusted.
To make bitter or sad. See Imbitter.
To depict or represent; -- said of heraldic bearings. See Blazon. 2. To deck in glaring colors.
The act of embodying; the state of being embodied. 2. That which embodies or is embodied.
To form into a body; to invest with a body; to collect into a body, a united mass, or a whole.
To give boldness or courage to; to encourage. Shak. The self-conceit which emboldened him to undertake this dangerous office.
Intercalation; the insertion of days, months, or years, in an account of time, to produce regularity.
To ornament with needlework.
Needlework used to enrich textile fabrics, leather, etc.; also, the art of embroidering.
To throw into confusion or commotion by contention or discord; to entangle in a broil or quarrel.
The first rudiments of an organism, whether animal or plant; as: (a) The young of an animal in the womb, or more specifically, before its parts are developed and it becomes a fetus (see Fetus).
To purge of faults; to make better; to correct; esp., to make corrections in (a literary work).
The act of altering for the better, or correcting what is erroneous or faulty.
A precious stone of a rich green color, a variety of beryl. See Beryl.
To rise out of a fluid; to come forth from that in which anything has been plunged, enveloped, or concealed.
The act of rising out of a fluid, or coming forth from envelopment or concealment, or of rising into view.
Honorably discharged from the performance of public duty on account of age, infirmity, or long and faithful services.
Corundum in the form of grains or powder, used in the arts for grinding and polishing hard substances.
Inducing to vomit; exciting the stomach to discharge its contents by the mouth.
Removing from one country to another; emigrating.
That which is eminent or lofty; a high ground or place; a height. Without either eminences or cavities.
High; lofty; towering; prominent. "A very eminent promontory." Evelyn 2.
An Arabian military commander, independent chieftain, or ruler of a province.
An agent employed to advance, in a covert manner, the interests of his employers.
To send forth; to throw or give out; to cause to issue; to give vent to.
An ant. Emmet hunter (Zoöl.), the wryneck.
Softening; making supple; acting as an emollient. "Emollient applications." Arbuthnot.
The profit arising from office, employment, or labor; gain; compensation.
A moving of the mind or soul; excitement of the feelings, whether pleasing or painful.
A particular stress of utterance, or force of voice, given in reading and speaking to one or more words whose signification the speaker intends to impress specially upon his audience.
To utter or pronounce with a particular stress of voice; to make emphatic.
Uttered with emphasis; made prominent and impressive by a peculiar stress of voice.
Pertaining to, or founded upon, experiment or experience; depending upon the observation of phenomena.
The method or practice of an empiric; pursuit of knowledge by observation and experiment.
One employed by another.
One who employs another.
The act of employing or using; also, the state of being employed. 2. That which engages or occupies.
To give authority to; to delegate power to; to commission; to authorize (having commonly a legal force).
The consort of an emperor. Shak. 2. A female sovereign. 3. A sovereign mistress.
The state of being empty; absence of contents; void space; vacuum.
The act of making empty. Shak. 2. pl. The lees of beer, cider, etc.; yeast.
Formed of pure fire or light; refined beyond aërial substance; pertaining to the highest and purest region of heaven.
Striving to excel; ambitious; emulous. "A most emulate pride." Shak. To strive to equal or to excel in qualities or actions.
A variety of glass, used in ornament, to cover a surface, as of metal or pottery, and admitting of after decoration in color, or used itself for inlaying or application in varied colors.
To inflame with love; to charm; to captivate; -- with of, or with, before the person or thing.
To form and occupy a camp; to prepare and settle in temporary habitations, as tents or huts.
To charm by sorcery; to act on by enchantment; to get control of by magical words and rites.
Under the power of enchantment; possessed or exercised by enchanters.
Having a power of enchantment; charming; fascinating. -- En*chant"ing*ly, adv.
The act of enchanting; the production of certain wonderful effects by the aid of demons, or the agency of supposed spirits.
To form a circle about; to inclose within a circle or ring; to surround.
A tract of land or a territory inclosed within another territory of which it is independent.
Inclosure. See Inclosure. Note: The words enclose and enclosure are written indiscriminately enclose or inclose and enclosure or inclosure.
Bestowing praise; praising; eulogistic; laudatory.
Warm or high praise; panegyric; strong commendation. His encomiums awakened all my ardor.
To circumscribe or go round so as to surround closely; to encircle; to inclose.
Once more; again; -- used by the auditors and spectators of plays, concerts, and other entertainments, to call for a repetition of a particular part.
To come against face to face; to meet; to confront, either by chance, suddenly, or deliberately.
To give courage to; to inspire with courage, spirit, or hope; to raise, or to increase, the confidence of.
The act of encouraging; incitement to action or to practice.
Furnishing ground to hope; inspiriting; favoring. -- En*cour"a*ging*ly, adv.
To enter by gradual steps or by stealth into the possessions or rights of another.
The act of entering gradually or silently upon the rights or possessions of another.
To impede the motion or action of, as with a burden; to retard with something superfluous.
Sent to many persons or places; intended for many, or for a whole order of men.
The circle of arts and sciences; a comprehensive summary of knowledge, or of a branch of knowledge.
Pertaining to, or of the nature of, an encyclopedia; embracing a wide range of subjects.
Making dear or beloved; causing love. -- En*dear"ing*ly, adv.
The act of endearing or the state of being endeared; also, that which manifests, excites, or increases, affection.
Peculiar to a district or particular locality, or class of persons.
Termination; concluding part; result; conclusion; destruction; death. 2.
A composite herb (Cichorium Endivia). Its finely divided and much curled leaves, when blanched, are used for salad.
In an endless manner.
To furnish with money or its equivalent, as a permanent fund for support.
To invest. Latham. Tarry ye in the city of Jerusalem, until ye be endued with power from on high.
Capable of being endured or borne; sufferable. Macaulay. -- En*dur"a*ble*ness, n.
A state or quality of lasting or duration; lastingness; continuance. Slurring with an evasive answer the question concerning the endurance of his own possession.
Lasting; durable; long-suffering.
An injection, or clyster, thrown into the rectum as a medicine, or to impart nourishment.
Having energy or energies; possessing a capacity for vigorous action or for exerting force.
To use strength in action; to act or operate with force or vigor; to act in producing an effect.
to cause someone to feel drained of energy or vitality; to weaken
characteristic: Think of a long heatwave that leaves you limp, tired, and completely sapped of strength.

definition: To make feeble; to deprive of strength; to reduce the strength or force of.
Compelled; forced; not voluntary. "Enforced wrong." "Enforced smiles." Shak.
The act of enforcing; compulsion. He that contendeth against these enforcements may easily master or resist them.
One who enforces.
To set free; to liberate from slavery, prison, or any binding power. Bacon.
To put under pledge; to pledge; to place under obligations to do or forbear doing something, as by a pledge, oath, or promise.
Occupied; employed; busy. 2. Pledged; promised; especially, having the affections pledged.
The act of engaging, pledging, enlisting, occupying, or entering into contest.
Tending to draw the attention or affections; attractive.
To produce by the union of the sexes; to beget. 2. To cause to exist; to bring forth.
A person skilled in the principles and practice of any branch of engineering.
Originally, the art of managing engines; in its modern and extended sense, the art and science by which the mechanical properties of matter are made useful to man in structures and machines.
Made by engraving or ornamented with engraving. 2. (Zoöl.) Having the surface covered with irregular, impressed lines.
To absorb or swallow up as in a gulf. It quite engulfs all human thought.
To raise or lift up; to exalt. Wyclif. Who, naught aghast, his mighty hand enhanced.
A dark, obscure, or inexplicable saying; a riddle; a statement, the hidden meaning of which is to be discovered or guessed.
Relating to or resembling an enigma; not easily explained or accounted for.
To lay upon, as an order or command; to give an injunction to; to direct with authority.
Capable of being enjoyed or of giving joy; yielding enjoyment. Milton.
The condition of enjoying anything; pleasure or satisfaction, as in the possession or occupancy of anything.
To set on fire; to inflame; to kindle. Shak. 2. To excite; to rouse into action.
Made large or larger; extended; swollen. -- En*lar"ged*ly, adv. -- En*lar"ged*ness, n.
To supply with light; to illuminate.
Act of enlightening, or the state of being enlightened or instructed.
To enter on a list; to enroll; to register. 2. To engage for military or naval service, the name being entered on a list or register.
To give life, action, or motion to; to make vigorous or active; to excite.
The quality of being an enemy; hostile or unfriendly disposition. No ground of enmity between us known.
To make noble; to elevate in degree, qualities, or excellence; to dignify.
A feeling of weariness and disgust; dullness and languor of spirits, arising from satiety or want of interest.
The state or quality of exceeding a measure or rule, or of being immoderate, monstrous, or outrageous.
Exceeding the usual rule, norm, or measure; out of due proportion; inordinate.
In an enormous degree.
To fill with rage; to provoke to frenzy or madness; to make furious. Syn.
To transport with pleasure; to delight beyond measure; to enravish. Shenstone.
To cover or shelter, as with a sconce or fort; to place or hide securely.
The whole; all the parts taken together. All at once; together.
To inclose in a shrine or chest; hence, to preserve or cherish as something sacred.
To reduce to slavery; to make a slave of; to subject to a dominant influence.
To catch in a snare. See Insnare.
To twist or interweave in such a manner as not to be easily separated.
Having a disposition for enterprise; characterized by enterprise; resolute, active or prompt to attempt.
One who entertains.
Affording entertainment; pleasing; amusing; diverting. -- En`ter*tain"ing*ly, adv.
To hold in thrall; to enslave. See Inthrall. The bars survive the captive they enthrall.
To seat on a throne; to exalt to the seat of royalty or of high authority.
To make or become enthusiastic.
Inspiration as if by a divine or superhuman power; ecstasy; hence, a conceit of divine possession and revelation, or of being directly subject to some divine impulse.
One moved or actuated by enthusiasm; as: (a) One who imagines himself divinely inspired, or possessed of some special revelation.
To draw on, by exciting hope or desire; to allure; to attract.
That entices; alluring.
The state of being entire; completeness.
A real being, whether in thought (as an ideal conception) or in fact; being.
That part of zoölogy which treats of insects. 2. A treatise on the science of entomology.
Surroundings; specif., collectively, one's attendants or associates. The entourage and mode of life of the mikados were not such as to make of them able rulers.
The internal parts of animal bodies; the bowels; the guts; viscera; intestines.
The act of entering or going into; ingress.
To catch in a trap; to insnare; hence, to catch, as in a trap, by artifices.
To treat, or conduct toward; to deal with; to use. Fairly let her be entreated.
A coming in, or entrance; hence, freedom of access; permission or right to enter.
One who creates a product on his own account; whoever undertakes on his own account an industrial enterprise in which workmen are employed.
The act of entering or passing into or upon; entrance; ingress; hence, beginnings or first attempts.
To twine, twist, or wreathe together or round. Entwined in duskier wreaths her braided locks.
To count; to tell by numbers; to count over, or tell off one after another.
To make a formal statement of; to announce; to proclaim; to declare, as a truth.
That which envelops, wraps up, encases, or surrounds; a wrapper; an inclosing cover.
Act of environing; state of being environed. 2. That which environs or surrounds.
One dispatched upon an errand or mission; a messenger; esp., a person deputed by a sovereign or a government to negotiate a treaty, or transact other business, with a foreign sovereign or government.
An unorganized or unformed ferment, in distinction from an organized or living ferment.
A shoulder ornament or badge worn by military and naval officers, differences of rank being marked by some peculiar form or device, as a star, eagle, etc.
