A shoulder ornament or badge worn by military and naval officers, differences of rank being marked by some peculiar form or device, as a star, eagle, etc.
lasting for a very short time
characteristic: Like a mayfly that lives a single day, or a snowflake that melts the instant it lands.

definition: A hunter's name for the grizzly bear.

Narrated in a grand style; pertaining to or designating a kind of narrative poem, usually called an heroic poem, in which real or fictitious events, usually the achievements of some hero, are narrated in an elevated...
A follower of Epicurus; an Epicurean. Bacon. 2. One devoted to dainty or luxurious sensual enjoyments, esp.
Pertaining to Epicurus, or following his philosophy. "The sect Epicurean." Milton.
A circle, whose center moves round in the circumference of a greater circle.
A curve traced by a point in the circumference of a circle which rolls on the convex side of a fixed circle.
Common to, or affecting at the same time, a large number in a community.
The outer, nonsensitive layer of the skin; cuticle; scarfskin. See Dermis.
A short poem treating concisely and pointedly of a single thought or event.
The "falling sickness," so called because the patient falls suddenly to the ground.
Pertaining to, affected with, or of the nature of, epilepsy. 1. One affected with epilepsy.
A speech or short poem addressed to the spectators and recited by one of the actors, after the conclusion of the play.
An appearance, or a becoming manifest. Whom but just before they beheld transfigured and in a glorious epiphany upon the mount.
A separate incident, story, or action, introduced for the purpose of giving a greater variety to the events related.
The theory or science of the method or grounds of knowledge.
Pertaining to epistles or letters; suitable to letters and correspondence.
A work in which the contents of a former work are reduced within a smaller space by curtailment and condensation.
A fixed point of time, established in history by the occurrence of some grand or remarkable event.
The after song; the part of a lyric ode which follows the strophe and antistrophe, -- the ancient ode being divided into strophe, antistrophe, and epode.
The hypothetical individual who is assumed as the person from whom any race, city, etc., took its name.
Equal and uniform; continuing the same at different times; -- said of motion, and the like.
The condition or quality of being equal; agreement in quantity or degree as compared.
In an equal manner or degree in equal shares or proportion; with equal and impartial justice.
Evenness of mind; that calm temper or firmness of mind which is not easily elated or depressed.
A making equal; equal division; equality; equilibrium. Again the golden day resumed its right, And ruled in just equation with the night.
The imaginary great circle on the earth's surface, everywhere equally distant from the two poles, and dividing the earth's surface into two hemispheres.
Equality of weight or force; an equipoise or a state of rest produced by the mutual counteraction of two or more forces.
Of, pertaining to, or resembling, a horse. The shoulders, body, things, and mane are equine.

Furniture or outfit, whether useful or ornamental; especially, the furniture and supplies of a vessel, fitting her for a voyage or for warlike purposes, or the furniture and necessaries of an army, a body of troops,...
Equality of weight or force; hence, equilibrium; a state in which the two ends or sides of a thing are balanced, and hence equal.
Possessing or exhibiting equity; according to natural right or natural justice.
Equality of rights; natural justice or right; the giving, or desiring to give, to each man his due, according to reason, and the law of God to man.
(Literally, called equally one thing or the other; hence:) Having two significations equally applicable.
to use ambiguous language so as to conceal the truth or avoid commitment
characteristic: Picture a politician giving slippery, vague answers to dodge a yes-or-no question.

definition: To pluck up by the roots; to root up.
To rub or scrape out, as letters or characters written, engraved, or painted.
Rubbed or scraped out; effaced; obliterated. 2. (Her.) Represented with jagged and uneven edges, as is torn off.
One who, or that which, erases; esp., a sharp instrument or a piece of rubber used to erase writings, drawings, etc.
The act of erecting, or raising upright; the act of constructing, as a building or a wall, or of fitting together the parts of, as a machine.
Therefore; consequently; -- often used in a jocular way. Shak.
An early, and now a poetic, name of Ireland.
To eat into or away; to corrode.
Love; the god of love; -- by earlier writers represented as one of the first and creative gods, by later writers as the son of Aphrodite, equivalent to the Latin god Cupid.
The act or operation of eroding or eating away. 2. The state of being eaten away.
A special business intrusted to a messenger; something to be told or done by one sent somewhere for the purpose.
Wandering; deviating from an appointed course, or from a direct path; roving.
Having no certain course; roving about without a fixed destination; wandering.
Wandering; straying; deviating from the right course; -- hence, irregular.
Till then or now; heretofore; formerly.
having or showing great knowledge gained from study; scholarly
characteristic: Think of a learned professor surrounded by books, deeply read on many subjects.

definition: The act of instructing; the result of thorough instruction; the state of being erudite or learned.
To cause to burst forth; to eject.
The act of breaking out or bursting forth; as: (a) A violent throwing out of flames, lava, etc., as from a volcano of a fissure in the earth's crust.
A stairway or incline arranged like an endless belt so that the steps or treads ascend or descend continuously, and one stepping upon it is carried up or down.
The fling of a horse, or ordinary kicking back of his heels; a gambol.
To shun; to avoid, as something wrong, or from a feeling of distaste; to keep one's self clear of.
A body of armed men to attend a person of distinction for the sake of affording safety when on a journey.
A deed, bond, or other written engagement, delivered to a third person, to be held by him till some act is done or some condition is performed, and then to be by him delivered to the grantee.
The surface, usually a shield, upon which bearings are marshaled and displayed.
Designed for, and understood by, the specially initiated alone; not communicated, or not intelligible, to the general body of followers.
The practice or employment of spies; the practice of watching the words and conduct of others, to make discoveries, as spies or secret emissaries.
To betroth; to promise in marriage; to give as spouse. A virgin espoused to a man whose name was Joseph.
Originally, a shield-bearer or armor-bearer, an attendant on a knight.
The constituent elementary notions which constitute a complex notion, and must be enumerated to define it.
In an essential manner or degree; in an indispensable degree; really.
To make stable or firm; to fix immovably or firmly; to set (a thing) in a place and make it stable there.
The act of establishing; a ratifying or ordaining; settlement; confirmation.
An ethereal salt, or compound ether, consisting of an organic radical united with the residue of any oxygen acid, organic or inorganic.
Capable of being estimated or valued.
To judge and form an opinion of the value of, from imperfect data, -- either the extrinsic (money), or intrinsic (moral), value.
The act of estimating. Shak. 2. An opinion or judgment of the worth, extent, or quantity of anything, formed without using precise data.
To withdraw; to withhold; hence, reflexively, to keep at a distance; to cease to be familiar and friendly with.
Without beginning or end of existence; always existing. The eternal God is thy refuge.
In an eternal manner. That which is morally good or evil at any time or in any case, must be also eternally and unchangeably so.
Infinite duration, without beginning in the past or end in the future.
A medium of great elasticity and extreme tenuity, supposed to pervade all space, the interior of solid bodies not excepted, and to be the medium of transmission of light and heat.
Pertaining to the hypothetical upper, purer air, or to the higher regions beyond the earth or beyond the atmosphere.
Of, or belonging to, morals; treating of the moral feelings or duties.
Of, or belonging to, morals; treating of the moral feelings or duties.
The science of human duty; the body of rules of duty drawn from this science.
The science which treats of the division of mankind into races, their origin, distribution, and relations, and the peculiarities which characterize them.
The character, sentiment, or disposition of a community or people, considered as a natural endowment.
The forms required by good breeding, or prescribed by authority, to be observed in social or official life.
That branch of philological science which treats of the history of words, tracing out their origin, primitive significance, and changes of from and meaning.
Pertaining to, or derived from, cloves.
To speak or write in commendation of (another); to extol in speech or writing.
A speech or writing in commendation of the character or services of a person.
A male of the human species castrated; commonly, one of a class of such persons, in Oriental countries, having charge of the women's apartments.
A figure in which a harts or indelicate word or expression is softened.
Pleasing or sweet in sound; euphonic; smooth-sounding. Hallam. -- Eu*pho"ni*ous*ly, adv.
A pleasing or sweet sound; an easy, smooth enunciation of sounds; a pronunciation of letters and syllables which is pleasing to the ear.

The exclamation attributed to Archimedes, who is said to have cried out "Eureka! eureka!" (I have found it! I have found it!), upon suddenly discovering a method of finding out how much the gold of King Hiero's crown...
An easy death; a mode of dying to be desired. "An euthanasia of all thought." Hazlitt.
To get away from by artifice; to avoid by dexterity, subterfuge, address, or ingenuity.
To fix the value of; to rate; to appraise.
Valuation; appraisement. J. S. Mill.
To vanish away; to because dissipated and disappear, like vapor. I believe him to have evanesced or evaporated.
Liable to vanish or pass away like vapor; vanishing; fleeting.
Contained in, or relating to, the four Gospels.
A bringer of the glad tidings of Church and his doctrines. Specially: (a) A missionary preacher sent forth to prepare the way for a resident pastor.
The act of eluding or avoiding, particularly the pressure of an argument, accusation, charge, or interrogation.
Tending to evade, or marked by evasion; elusive; shuffling; avoiding by artifice.
Fair or impartial; unbiased. "Evenhanded justice." Shak. -- E"ven*hand`ed*ly, adv.
With an even, level, or smooth surface; without roughness, elevations, or depression.
Coming or happening as a consequence or result; consequential. Burke. 2.
Lasting or enduring forever; exsisting or continuing without end; immoral.
Used or fit for every day; common; usual.
Clear to the vision; especially, clear to the understanding, and satisfactory to the judgment.
Relating to, or affording, evidence; indicative; especially, relating to the evidences of Christianity.
In an evident manner; clearly; plainly. Before whose eyes Jesus Christ hath been evidently set forth.
To take out the entrails of; to disembowel; to gut.
Calling forth; serving to evoke; developing. Evocative power over all that is eloquent and expressive in the better soul of man.
To call out; to summon forth. To evoke the queen of the fairies. T. Warton.
The act of unfolding or unrolling; hence, in the process of growth; development.
Relating to evolution.
To unfold or unroll; to open and expand; to disentangle and exhibit clearly and satisfactorily.
A kind of widemouthed pitcher or jug; esp., one used to hold water for the toilet.
to make a problem, bad situation, or negative feeling worse

definition: Oppressive or unreasonably severe in making demands or requiring the exact fulfillment of obligations.
The act of demanding with authority, and compelling to pay or yield; compulsion to give or furnish.
Enlarged beyond bounds or the truth. -- Ex*ag"ger*a`ted*ly, adv.
That exaggerates; enlarging beyond bounds. -- Ex*ag"ger*a`ting*ly, adv.

Raised to lofty height; elevated; extolled; refined; dignified; sublime.

To test by any appropriate method; to inspect carefully with a view to discover the real character or state of.

Having power to examine; appointed to examine.
Exasperated; imbittered. Shak. Like swallows which the exasperate dying year Sets spinning.
To hollow out; to form cavity or hole in; to make hollow by cutting, scooping, or digging.
The act of excavating, or of making hollow, by cutting, scooping, or digging out a part of a solid mass.

To a very great degree; beyond what is usual; surpassingly. It signifies more than very.
To go beyond or surpass in good qualities or laudable deeds; to outdo or outgo, in a good sense.
The quality of being excellent; state of possessing good qualities in an eminent degree.
Excellence; virtue; dignity; worth; superiority. His excellency is over Israel.
The act of excepting or excluding; exclusion; restriction by taking out something which would otherwise be included, as in a class, statement, rule.
Liable to exception or objection; objectionable. -- Ex*cep"tion*a*ble*ness, n.
Forming an exception; not ordinary; uncommon; rare; hence, better than the average.
To select; to extract; to cite; to quote. Out of which we have excerpted the following particulars.
The state of surpassing or going beyond limits; the being of a measure beyond sufficiency, necessity, or duty.
Characterized by, or exhibiting, excess; overmuch. Excessive grief  the enemy to the living.
One of the superior courts of law; -- so called from a checkered cloth, which covers, or formerly covered, the table.
The act of excising or cutting out or off; extirpation; destruction. Such conquerors are the instruments of vengeance on those nations that have .
Capable of being excited, or roused into action; susceptible of excitement.
The act of exciting or putting in motion; the act of rousing up or awakening.
To call to activity in any way; to rouse to feeling; to kindle to passionate emotion.
The act of exciting, or the state of being roused into action, or of having increased action.
A loud calling or crying out; outcry; loud or emphatic utterance; vehement vociferation.
To shut out; to hinder from entrance or admission; to debar from participation or enjoyment.
The act of excluding, or of shutting out, whether by thrusting out or by preventing admission.
Having the power of preventing entrance; debarring from participation or enjoyment.
To strip or wear off the skin of; to abrade; to gall; to break and remove the cuticle of, in any manner, as by rubbing, beating, or by the action of acrid substances.
An excrescent appendage,.
To separate and throw off; to excrete urine. "The mucus thus excreted." Hooper.
The act of excreting. To promote secretion and excretion. Pereira. 2. That which is excreted.
Excruciated; tortured. And here my heart long time excruciate. Chapman.
. Torturing; racking. "Excruciating pain." V. Knox. "Excruciating fears." Bentley -- Ex*cru"ci*a`ting*ly, adv.
To clear from alleged fault or guilt; to prove to be guiltless; to relieve of blame.
A running or going out or forth; an expedition; a sally. Far on excursion toward the gates of hell.
Prone to make excursions; wandering; roving; exploring.
That may be excused, forgiven, justified, or acquitted of blame; pardonable.
Deserving to be execrated; accursed; damnable; detestable; abominable.
To denounce evil against, or to imprecate evil upon; to curse; to protest against as unholy or detestable.
The act of cursing; a curse dictated by violent feelings of hatred; imprecation.
One who executes; an executer. Bacon. 2. One who puts to death in conformity to legal warrant, as a hangman.
Designed or fitted for execution, or carrying into effect.
Exposition; explanation; especially, a critical explanation of a text or portion of Scripture.
A model, original, or pattern, to be copied or imitated; a specimen; sometimes.
To show or illustrate by example. He did but . . . exemplify the principles in which he had been brought up.
The act of exercising; a setting in action or practicing; employment in the proper mode of activity.
The act of exerting, or putting into motion or action; the active exercise of any power or faculty.
To breathe out. Hence: To emit, as vapor; to send out, as an odor; to evaporate.
To draw or let out wholly; to drain off completely.
Capable of being exhausted, drained off, or expended. Johnson.
Producing exhaustion.
The act of draining out or draining off; the act of emptying completely of the contents.
Serving or tending to exhaust; exhibiting all the facts or arguments.
The act of exhibiting for inspection, or of holding forth to view; manifestation.
That exhilarates; cheering; gladdening. -- Ex*hil"a*ra`ting*ly, adv.
To incite by words or advice; to animate or urge by arguments, as to a good deed or laudable conduct.
To dig out of the ground; to take out of a place of burial; to disinter.
The state of being exigent; urgent or exacting want; pressing necessity or distress.
Exacting or requiring immediate aid or action; pressing; critical. "At this exigent moment." Burke.
Scantiness; smallness; thinness. Boyle.
The state of existing or being; actual possession of being; continuance in being.
He (or she ) goes out, or retires from view.
A going out; particularly (the Exodus), the going out or journey of the Israelites from Egypt under the conduct of Moses.
To unload; to disburden; to discharge. All exonerate themselves into one common duct.
A going out of or beyond the usual or due limit; hence, enormity; extravagance.
Departing from an orbit or usual track; hence, deviating from the usual or due course.
To cast out, as a devil, evil spirits, etc., by conjuration or summoning by a holy name, or by certain ceremonies.
Introduced from a foreign country; not native; extraneous; foreign.
To lay open by extending; to open wide; to spread out; to diffuse.
That expands, or may be expanded; extending; spreading; enlarging. Expanding bit, Expanding drill (Mech.), a bit or drill made adjustable for holes of various sizes.
The act of expanding or spreading out; the condition of being expanded.
Having a capacity or tendency to expand or dilate; diffusive; of much expanse.
To range at large, or without restraint. Bids his free soul expatiate in the skies.
To banish; to drive or force (a person) from his own country; to make an exile of.
The act of expecting ; expectation. Milton. 2. That which is expected, or looked or waited for with interest.
The act or state of expecting or looking forward to an event as about to happen.
To eject from the trachea or lungs; to discharge, as phlegm or other matter, by coughing, hawking, and spitting.
Hastening or forward; hence, tending to further or promote a proposed object.
Free of impediment; unimpeded. To make the way plain and expedite. Hooker.
The quality of being expedite; efficient promptness; haste; dispatch; speed.
Possessed of, or characterized by, expedition, or efficiency and rapidity in action.
To drive or force out from that within which anything is contained, inclosed, or situated.
To lay out, apply, or employ in any way; to consume by use; to use up or distribute, either in payment or in donations.
The act of expending; a laying out, as of money; disbursement. our expenditure purchased commerce and conquest.
Taught by practice or by repeated observations; skillful or wise by means of trials, use, or observation.
Pertaining to experiment; founded on, or derived from, experiment or trial.
The act of expiring; as: (a)(Physiol.) The act or process of breathing out, or forcing air from the lungs through the nose or mouth.
Filling up; hence, added merely for the purpose of filling up; superfluous.
Capable of being explicated; that may be explained or accounted for; admitting explanation.
Evolved; unfolded. Jer. Taylor. 1. To unfold; to expand; to lay open. "They explicate the leaves." Blackmore.
One who unfolds or explains; an expounder; an explainer.
A word formerly used (as finis is now) at the conclusion of a book to indicate the end.
In an explicit manner; clearly; plainly; without disguise or reservation of meaning.
The act of exploiting or utilizing. J. D. Whitney.
The act of exploring, penetrating, or ranging over for purposes of discovery, especially of geographical discovery.
One who explores; also, an apparatus with which one explores, as a diving bell.
Employed in, or designed for, exploration. "Exploring parties." Bancroft.
The act of exploding; detonation; a chemical action which causes the sudden formation of a great volume of expanded gas.
Driving or bursting out with violence and noise; causing explosion.
A number, letter, or any quantity written on the right hand of and above another quantity, and denoting how many times the latter is repeated as a factor to produce the power indicated.
To set forth; to set out to public view; to exhibit; to show; to display.
The act of exposing or laying open; a setting out or displaying to public view.
Pertaining to, or containing, exposition; serving to explain; explanatory.
The act of expostulating or reasoning with a person in opposition to some impropriety of conduct.
The act of exposing or laying open, setting forth, laying bare of protection, depriving of care or concealment, or setting out to reprobation or contempt.
To lay open; to expose to view; to examine. He expounded both his pockets.
The act of expressing; the act of forcing out by pressure.
Serving to express, utter, or represent; indicative; communicative; -- followed by of.
In an express manner; in direct terms; with distinct purpose; particularly.
To put out of one's possession; to surrender the ownership of; also, to deprive of possession or proprietary rights.
The act of expelling; a driving or forcing out; summary removal from membership, association, etc.
To blot out, as with pen; to rub out; to efface designedly; to obliterate.
To purify; to clear from anything noxious, offensive, or erroneous; to cleanse.
Carefully selected or sought out; hence, of distinguishing and surpassing quality.
Standing out or above any surface; protruded. That part of the teeth which is extant above the gums.
Composed, performed, or uttered on the spur of the moment, or without previous study.
To speak extempore; especially, to discourse without special preparation.
To stretch out; to prolong in space; to carry forward or continue in length.
Capable of being extended, whether in length or breadth; susceptible of enlargement.
The act of extending or the state of being extended; a stretching out.
To a great extent; widely; largely.
A muscle which serves to extend or straighten any part of the body, as an arm or a finger.
External; outward; pertaining to that which is external; -- opposed to interior.
One who, or that which, exterminates. Buckle.
Outward; exterior; relating to the outside, as of a body; being without.
The act of extinguishing or making extinct; a putting an end to; the act of putting out or destroying light, fire, life, activity, influence, etc.
To quench; to put out, as a light or fire; to stifle; to cause to die out.
One who, or that which, extinguishes; esp., a hollow cone or other device for extinguishing a flame, as of a torch or candle.
To pluck up by the stem or root; to root out; to eradicate, literally or figuratively.
To place on high; to lift up; to elevate. Who extolled you in the half-crown boxes, Where you might sit and muster all the beauties.
The act of extorting; the act or practice of wresting anything from a person by force, by threats, or by any undue exercise of power.
The act of extracting, or drawing out.
To deliver up by one government to another, as a fugitive from justice.
The surrender or delivery of an alleged criminal by one State or sovereignty to another having jurisdiction to try charge.
Out of or beyond the proper authority of a court or judge; beyond jurisdiction.
Not belonging to, or dependent upon, a thing; without or beyond a thing.
Beyond or out of the common order or method; not usual, customary, regular, or ordinary.
A wandering beyond proper limits; an excursion or sally from the usual way, course, or limit.
Wandering beyond one's bounds; roving; hence, foreign. The extravagant and erring spirit hies To his confine.
At the utmost point, edge, or border; outermost; utmost; farthest; most remote.
A supporter of extreme doctrines or practice; one who holds extreme opinions.
The extreme part; the utmost limit; the farthest or remotest point or part.
To free, as from difficulties or perplexities; to disentangle; to disembarrass.
Not contained in or belonging to a body; external; outward; unessential.
To thrust out; to force, press, or push out; to expel; to drive off or away.
The state of being exuberant; an overflowing quantity; a copious or excessive production or supply.
Characterized by abundance or superabundance; plenteous; rich; overflowing.
To discharge through pores or incisions, as moisture or other liquid matter.
To be in high spirits; figuratively, to leap for joy; to rejoice in triumph or exceedingly.
The ball or globe of the eye.
The brow or hairy arch above the eye. Shak.
Heaving (such or so many) eyes; -- used in composition; as sharp-eyed.
Sight of the eye; the sense of seeing; view; observation. Josephus sets this down from his own eyesight.
One who sees a thing done; one who has ocular view anything. We . . . were eyewitnesses of his majesty.
A journey in circuit of certain judges called justices in eyre (or in itinere).
The nest of a bird of prey or other large bird that builds in a lofty place.
The nest of a bird of prey or other large bird that builds in a lofty place.
Of, pertaining to, or in the manner of, the Roman general, Quintus Fabius Maximus Verrucosus.
A Feigned story or tale, intended to instruct or amuse; a fictitious narration intended to enforce some useful truth or precept.
To form into a whole by uniting its parts; to frame; to construct; to build.
The act of fabricating, framing, or constructing; construction; manufacture.
Feigned, as a story or fable; related in fable; devised; invented; not real.
The front of a building; esp., the principal front, having some architectural pretensions.
Having (such) a face, or (so many) faces.
A little face; a small, plane surface.
Given to wit and good humor; merry; sportive; jocular.
Easy to be done or performed: not difficult; performable or attainable with little labor.
To make easy or less difficult; to free from difficulty or impediment.
The quality of being easily performed; freedom from difficulty; ease.
A covering in front, for ornament or other purpose; an exterior covering or sheathing.
A copy of anything made, either so as to be deceptive or so as to give every part and detail of the original.
One of the divisions or parties of charioteers (distinguished by their colors) in the games of the circus.
Given to faction; addicted to form parties and raise dissensions, in opposition to government or the common good.
Made by art, in distinction from what is produced by nature; artificial.
A person employed to do all kinds of work or business. B. Jonson.
That has lost freshness, color, or brightness; grown dim. "His faded cheek." Milton.
Conforming to the scale used by Gabriel Daniel Fahrenheit in the graduation of his thermometer.
A failing short; a becoming deficient; failure; deficiency; imperfection.
Syncope, or loss of consciousness owing to a sudden arrest of the blood supply to the brain, the face becoming pallid, the respiration feeble, and the heat's beat weak.
In a faint, weak, or timidmanner.
The state of being fair, or free form spots or stains, as of the skin.
Full of faith, or having faith; disposed to believe, especially in the declarations and promises of God.
One of the circles or windings of a cable or hawser, as it lies in a coil.
Embodying or pertaining to a fallacy; illogical; fitted to deceive; misleading.
Deceptive or false appearance; deceitfulness; that which misleads the eye or the mind.
Liable to fail, mistake, or err; liable to deceive or to be deceived.
Pale red or pale yellow.
Want of truth or accuracy; an untrue assertion or representation; error.
In a false manner; erroneously; not truly; perfidiously or treacherously.
To make false; to represent falsely. The Irish bards use to forge and falsify everything as they list, to please or displease any man.
To thrash in the chaff; also, to cleanse or sift, as barley. Halliwell.
Public report or rumor. The fame thereof was heard in Pharaoh's house.
The state of being familiar; intimate and frequent converse, or association.
General scarcity of food; dearth; a want of provisions; destitution. "Worn with famine." Milton.
To starve, kill, or destroy with hunger. Shak. 2. To exhaust the strength or endurance of, by hunger.
In a famous manner; in a distinguished degree; greatly; splendidly. Then this land was famously enriched With politic grave counsel.
Pertaining to, or indicating, fanaticism; extravagant in opinions; ultra.
Characteristic of, or relating to, fanaticism; fanatic. - Fa*nat"ic*al*ly, adv.
Excessive enthusiasm, unreasoning zeal, or wild and extravagant notions, on any subject, especially religion.
Formed or conceived by the fancy; unreal.
One who is governed by fancy. "Not reasoners, but fanciers." Macaulay.
Full of fancy; guided by fancy, rather than by reason and experience; whimsical.
The faculty by which the mind forms an image or a representation of anything perceived before.
A flourish of trumpets, as in coming into the lists, etc.; also, a short and lively air performed on hunting horns during the chase.
Existing only in imagination; fanciful; imaginary; not real; chimerical.
Go well; good-by; adieu; -- originally applied to a person departing, but by custom now applied both to those who depart and those who remain.
Brought from far, or from a remote place. Every remedy contained a multitude of farfetched and heterogeneous ingredients.
One who farms; as: (a) One who hires and cultivates a farm; a cultivator of leased ground.
A dwelling house on a farm; a farmer's residence.
Pertaining to agriculture; devoted to, adapted to, or engaged in, farming.
A little of pigs. Shak. To bring forth (young); -- said only of swine.
Most distant or remote.
To influence in an uncontrollable manner; to operate on by some powerful or irresistible charm.
The act of fascinating, bewhiching, or enchanting; enchantment; witchcraft.
Conforming to the fashion or established mode; according with the prevailing form or style.
Having a certain style or fashion; as old-fashioned; new- fashioned.
very attentive to and concerned about accuracy and detail; hard to please
characteristic: Picture someone wiping every speck of dust, fussy and exacting about the smallest detail.

definition: The doctrine that all things are subject to fate, or that they take place by inevitable necessity.
The state of being fatal, or proceeding from destiny; invincible necessity, superior to, and independent of, free and rational control.
Having the power of serving or accomplishing fate. "The fateful steel." J.
One's native land; the native land of one's fathers or ancestors.
Destitute of a living father.
A measure of length, containing six feet; the space to which a man can extend his arms.
Incapable of being fathomed; immeasurable; that can not be sounded. And buckle in a waist most fathomless.
Weariness from bodily labor or mental exertion; lassitude or exhaustion of strength.
Containing fat, or having the qualities of fat; greasy; gross.
Feeble in mind; weak; silly; stupid; foolish; fatuitous. Glanvill. 2. Without reality.
A fixture for drawing a liquid, as water, molasses, oil, etc., from a pipe, cask, or other vessel, in such quantities as may be desired.
Containing faults, blemishes, or defects; imperfect; not fit for the use intended.
A god of fields and shipherds, diddering little from the satyr. The fauns are usually represented as half goat and half man.
The animals of any given area or epoch.
Countenanced; aided; regarded with kidness.
The disposition to favor and promote the interest of one person or family, or of one class of men, to the neglect of others having equal claims.
A young deer; a buck or doe of the first year. See Buck. 2. The young of an animal.
Fidelity to one's lord; the feudal obligation by which the tenant or vassal was bound to be faithful to his lord.
Full of fera, apprehension, or alarm; afraid; frightened. Anxious amidst all their success, and fearful amidat all their power.
Free from fear. Syn. -- Bold; courageous; interpid; valor -- Fear"less*ly, adv.
Frightful; causing fear  "This fearsome wind." Sir W. Scott 2 . Easily frightened.
Capable of being done, executed, or effected; practicable. Always existing before their eyes as a thing feasible in practice.
A festival; a holiday; a solemn, or more commonly, a joyous, anniversary.
Clothed, covered, or fitted with (or as with) feathers or wings.
Shaped; fashioned. How noble, young, how rarely featured! Shak. 2. Having features.
Pertaining to fever; indicating fever, or derived from it.
The second month in the year, said to have been introduced into the Roman calendar by Numa.
dregs; sediment; excrement. See FÆces.
Spiritless; weak; worthless.
Fruitful in children; prolific. Graunt.
The quality or power of producing fruit; fruitfulness; especially (Biol.), the quality in female organisms of reproducing rapidly and in great numbers.
United by compact, as sovereignties, states, or nations; joined in confederacy.
The act of uniting in a league; confederation. 2. A league; a confederacy.
Deficient in physical strenght; weak; infirm; debilitated. Carried all the feeble of them upon asses.
One who, or that which, gives food or supplies nourishment; steward. A couple of friends, his chaplain and feeder.
the act of eating, or of supplying with food; the process of fattening.
To give a mental existence to, as to something not real or actual; to imagine.
Feigned; counterfeit. Dressed up into any feint appearance of it. Locke.
Characterized by felicity; happy; prosperous; delightful; skilful; successful.
The state of being happy; blessedness; blissfulness; enjoyment of good.
Catlike; of or pertaining to the genus Felis, or family Felidæ.
One who, or that which, fells, knocks or cuts down; a machine for felling trees.
The state or relation of being or associate. 2. Companionship of persons on equal and friendly terms.
A person who has committed a felony. 2. A person guilty or capable of heinous crime.
Having the quality of felony; malignant; malicious; villainous; traitorous.
An act on the part of the vassal which cost him his fee by forfeiture.
A woman. See Feme, n. Femme de chambre. Etym:  A lady's maid; a chambermaid.
Pertaining to the femur or thigh.
The thigh bone. (b) The proximal segment of the hind limb containing the thigh bone.
That which fends off attack or danger; a defense; a protection; a cover.
The art or practice of attack and defense with the sword, esp. with the s,allword.
One who or that which defends or protects by warding off harm; as: (a) A screen to prevent coals or sparks of an open fire from escaping to the floor.
A perennial plant of the genus Fæniculum (F.vulgare), having very finely divided leaves.
The process of undergoing an effervescent change, as by the action of yeast.
A place for rearing ferns.
Fierce; savage; wild; indicating cruelty; ravenous; rapacious.
Savage wildness or fierceness; fury; cruelty.
A sword bearing the mark of one of the Ferrara family of Italy. These swords were highly esteemed in England and Scotland in the 16th and 17th centuries.
An animal of the Weasel family (Mustela or Putorius furo), about fourteen inches in length, of a pale yellow or white color, with red eyes.
Pertaining to, or derived from, iron; -- especially used of compounds of iron in which the iron has its lower valence.
To carry or transport over a river, strait, or other narrow water, in a boat.
Producing fruit or vegetation in abundance; fruitful; able to produce abundantly.
The state or quality of being fertile or fruitful; fruitfulness; productiveness.
To make fertile or enrich; to supply with nourishment for plants; to make fruitful or productive.
One who fertilizes; the agent that carries the fertilizing principle, as a moth to an orchid.
having or displaying a passionate intensity
characteristic: Think of a glowing, hot devotion, a believer whose feelings burn with passion.

definition: Very hot; burning; boiling. The mounted sun Shot down direct his fervid rays.
Heat; excessive warmth. The fevor of ensuing day. Waller. 2. Intensity of feeling or expression.
To generate pus; to become imflamed and suppurate.
Pertaining to a fest; festive; festal; appropriate to a festival; joyous.
Pertaining to, or becoming, a feast; festal; joyous; gay; mirthful; sportive.
Pertaining to, or connected with, a fetus.
A feat. Chaucer. Feet. Chaucer. A festival. Fête champêtre ( Etym: , a festival or entertainment in the open air.

A material object supposed among certain African tribes to represent in such a way, or to be so connected with, a supernatural being, that the possession of it gives to the possessor power to control that being.
Having an offensive smell; stinking. Most putrefactions . . . smell either fetid or moldy.

A material object supposed among certain African tribes to represent in such a way, or to be so connected with, a supernatural being, that the possession of it gives to the possessor power to control that being.
A chain or shackle for the feet; a chain by which an animal is confined by the foot, either made fast or disabled from free and rapid motion.
The young or embryo of an animal in the womb, or in the egg; often restricted to the later stages in the development of viviparous and oviparous animals, embryo being applied to the earlier stages.
A combination of kindred to avenge injuries or affronts, done or offered to any of their blood, on the offender and all his race.
The feudal system; a system by which the holding of estates in land is made dependent upon an obligation to render military service to the kind or feudal superior.
Having a fever; suffering from, or affected with, a moderate degree of fever.
A betrothed woman.
A complete or ridiculous failure, esp. of a musical performance, or of any pretentious undertaking.
An authoritative command or order to do something; an effectual decree.
One of the delicate, threadlike portions of which the tissues of plants and animals are in part constituted.
One of the delicate, threadlike portions of which the tissues of plants and animals are in part constituted.
Not fixed or firm; liable to change; unstable; of a changeable mind; not firm in opinion or purpose.
The act of feigning, inventing, or imagining.
Pertaining to, or characterized by, fiction; fictitious; romantic."Fictional rather than historical." Latham.
Feigned; imaginary; not real; fabulous; counterfeit; false; not genuine.
A stringed instrument of music played with a bow; a violin; a kit. 2. (Bot.) A kind of dock (Rumex pulcher) with fiddle-shaped leaves.
One who plays on a fiddle or violin. 2. (Zoöl.) A burrowing crab of the genus Gelasimus, of many species.
Faithfulness; adherence to right; careful and exact observance of duty, or discharge of obligations.
To move uneasily one way and the other; to move irregularly, or by fits and starts.
Having faith or trust; confident; undoubting; firm. "Fiducial reliance on the promises of God." Hammond.
Involving confidence or trust; confident; undoubting; faithful; firm.
An estate held of a superior on condition of military service; a fee; a feud.
The act of playing as a fielder.
An implacable or malicious foe; one who is diabolically wicked or cruel.
Furious; violent; unrestrained; impetuous.
Consisting of, containing, or resembling, fire.
Among Spanish, a religious festival; a saint's day or holiday; also, a holiday or festivity.
A small shrill pipe, resembling the piccolo flute, used chiefly to accompany the drum in military music.
Five and ten; one more than fourteen. 1. The sum of five and ten; fifteen units or objects.
Five times ten.
One who fights; a combatant; a warrior. Shak.
An invention; a fiction; something feigned or imagined. Social figments, feints, and formalism.

Representing by a figure, or by resemblance; typical; representative. This, they will say, was figurative, and served, by God's appointment, but for a time, to shadow out the true glory of a more divine sanctity.
The form of anything; shape; outline; appearance. Flowers have all exquisite figures.
The figure, statue, or bust, on the prow of a ship. 2. A person who allows his name to be used to give standing to enterprises in which he has no responsible interest or duties.
A very small figure, whether human or of an animal; especially, one in terra cotta or the like.
To steal or take privily (commonly, that which is of little value); to pilfer.
A lawless military adventurer, especially one in quest of plunder; a freebooter.

Ornamental work, formerly with grains or breads, but now composed of fine wire and used chiefly in decorating gold and silver to which the wire is soldered, being arranged in designs frequently of a delicate and...
A fragment or particle rubbed off by the act of filing.
A little band, especially one intended to encircle the hair of the head.
That which is used to fill a cavity or any empty space, or to supply a deficiency.
A female foal or colt; a young mare. Cf. Colt, Foal. Neighing in likeness of a filly foal.
Any porous substance, as cloth, paper, sand, or charcoal, through which water or other liquid may passed to cleanse it from the solid or impure matter held in suspension.
Foul matter; anything that soils or defiles; dirt; nastiness. 2. Anything that sullies or defiles the moral character.
Defiled with filth, whether material or moral; nasty; dirty; polluted.
Close; termination; as: (a) (Mus.) The last movement of a symphony, sonata, concerto, or any instrumental composition.
The state of being final, finished, or complete; a final or conclusive arrangement.
At the end or conclusion; ultimately; lastly.
The income of a ruler or of a state; revennue; public money; sometimes, the income of an individual.
Pertaining to finance. "Our financial and commercial system." Macaulay.
In a dfinancial manner. Burke.
One charged with the administration of finance; an officer who administers the public revenue.
One who, or that which, finds; specifically (Astron.), a small telescope of low power and large field of view, attached to a larger telescope, for the purpose of finding an object more readily.
In a fine or finished manner.
One who fines or purifies.
Subtilty of contrivance to gain a point; artifice; stratagem. This is the artificialest piece of finesse to persuade men into slavery.
Having fingers. 2. (Bot.) Having leaflets like fingers; digitate. 3. (Mus.) Marked with figures designating which finger should be used for each note.
Finical; unduly particular.
Polished to the highest degree of excellence; complete; perfect.
The act or process of completing or perfecting; the final work upon or ornamentation of a thing.
Having a limit; limited in quantity, degree, or capacity; bounded; -- opposed to infinite.
A gun, pistol, or any weapon from a shot is discharged by the force of an explosive substance, as gunpowder.
A ball filled with powder or other combustibles, intended to be thrown among enemies, and to injure by explosion.
A piece of burning wood. L'Estrange. 2. One who inflames factions, or causes contention and mischief.
Any luminous winged insect, esp. luminous beetles of the family Lampyridæ.
A man whose business is to extinguish fires in towns; a member of a fire company.
The part a chimney appropriated to the fire; a hearth; -- usually an open recess in a wall, in which a fire may be built.
Wood for fuel.
In a firm manner.
Preceding all others of a series or kind; the ordinal of one; earliest.
First brought forth; first in the order of nativity; eldest; hence, most excellent.
In the first place; before anything else; -- sometimes improperly used for first.
One who fishes. 2. (Zoöl.) A carnivorous animal of the Weasel family (Mustela Canadensis).
One whose occupation is to catch fish. 2. (Naut.) A ship or vessel employed in the business of taking fish, as in the cod fishery.
A dealer in fish.
Consisting of fish; fishlike; having the qualities or taste of fish; abounding in fish.
A narrow opening, made by the parting of any substance; a cleft.
Full of fits; irregularly variable; impulsive and unstable. After life's fitful fever, he sleeps well.
The state or quality of being fit.
Anything used in fitting up; especially (pl.), necessary fixtures or apparatus.
A son; -- used in compound names, to indicate paternity, esp. of the illegitimate sons of kings and princes of the blood.
A kind of play with a ball against a wall, resembling tennis; - - so named because three fives, or fifteen, are counted to the game.
The act of fixing, or the state of being fixed. An unalterable fixation of resolution.
The act or process of making fixed. 2. That which is fixed; a fixture.
That which is fixed or attached to something as a permanent appendage.
To make a hissing sound, as a burning fuse. A hising sound.
Yielding to the touch, and easily moved or shaken; hanging loose by its own weight.
Yielding to pressure for want of firmness and stiffness; soft and weak.
To hang loose without stiffness; to bend down, as flexible bodies; to be loose, yielding, limp.
To whip; to scourge; to flog. 1. Flagelliform. 2. (Zoöl.) Of or pertaining to the Flagellata.
A pavement or sidewalk of flagstones; flagstones, collectively. Growing languid, weak, or spiritless.
Flaming; inflamed; glowing; burning; ardent. The beadle's lash still flagrant on their back.
The vessel which carries the commanding officer of a fleet or squadron and flies his distinctive flag or pennant.

An instrument for threshing or beating grain from the ear by hand, consisting of a wooden staff or handle, at the end of which a stouter and shorter pole or club, called a swipe, is so hung as to swing freely.
A paling; a hurdle. 2. A platform of hurdles, or small sticks made fast or interwoven, supported by stanchions, for drying codfish and other things.
Consisting of flakes or of small, loose masses; lying, or cleaving off, in flakes or layers.
Characterized by waving or flamelike curves, as in the tracery of windows, etc.
A stream of burning vapor or gas, emitting light and heat; darting or streaming fire.
Emitting flames; afire; blazing; consuming; illuminating. 2. Of the color of flame.
Any bird of the genus Phoenicopterus. The flamingoes have webbed feet, very long legs, and a beak bent down as if broken.
The fleshy or muscular part of the side of an animal, between the rids and the hip.
A soft, nappy, woolen cloth, of loose texture. Shak. Adam's flannel. (Bot.) See under Adam.
The creation of an artifical flood by the sudden letting in of a body of water.
Dazzling for a moment; making a momentary show of brilliancy; transitorily bright.
To reduce to an even surface or one approaching evenness; to make flat.
One who, or that which, makes flat or flattens. 2. (Metal Working) (a) A flat-faced fulling hammer.
That flatters (in the various senses of the verb).
The act or practice of flattering; the act of pleasing by artiful commendation or compliments.
The state or quality of being flatulent.
That quality of anything which affects the smell; odor; fragrances.
Free from flaws. Boyle.
To skin; to strip off the skin or surface of.
The act of bending, or state of being bent. 2. The variation of words by declension, comparison, or conjugation.
Feathered; furnished with feathers or wings; able to fly. Hfledge with wings.
A young bird just fledged.
To run away, as from danger or evil; to avoid in an alarmed or cowardly manner.
The entire coat of wood that covers a sheep or other similar animal; also, the quantity shorn from a sheep, or animal, at one time.
Passing swiftly away; not durable; transient; transitory.
Pertaining to Flanders, or the Flemings. -- n. The language or dialect spoken by the Flemings.
To feather, as an arrow. Bp. Warburton. fletched their complaint, by adding: "America loved his brother." Bancroft.
The state or quality of being flexible; flexibleness; pliancy; pliability.
Capable of being flexed or bent; admitting of being turned, bowed, or twisted, without breaking.
To whip lightly or with a quick jerk; to flap.
To flutter; to flap the wings without flying. And flickering on her nest made short essays to sing.
One who flies or flees; a runaway; a fugitive. Shak. 2. (Mach.) A fly.
Fleeting; swift; transient. The flighty purpose never is o'ertook, Unless the deed go with it.
Weak; feeble; limp; slight; vain; without strength or solidity; of loose and unsubstantial structure.
To withdraw from any suffering or undertaking, from pain or danger; to fail in doing or perserving.
A massive, somewhat impure variety of quartz, in color usually of a gray to brown or nearly black, breaking with a conchoidal fracture and sharp edge.
A mixture of beer, spirit, etc., stirred and heated by a hot iron. Flip dog, an iron used, when heated, to warm flip.
The state or quality of being flippant. This flippancy of language. Bp.
A broad flat limb used for swimming, as those of seals, sea turtles, whales, etc.
Buoyed upon or in a fluid; a, the floating timbers of a wreck; floating motes in the air.
A low, flat mass of floating ice. Floe rat (Zoöl.), a seal (Phoca foetida).
To beat or strike with a rod or whip; to whip; to lash; to chastise with repeated blows.
from Flog, v. t. Flogging chisel (Mach.), a large cold chisel, used in chipping castings.
A great flow of water; a body of moving water; the flowing stream, as of a river.
The filling or covering with water or other fluid; overflow; inundation.
To clap or strike, as a bird its wings, a fish its tail, etc.; to flap.
Having a tendency to flop or flap.
The goddess of flowers and spring. 2. (Bot.) The complete system of vegetable species growing without cultivation in a given locality, region, or period.
Pertaining to Flora, or to flowers; made of flowers.
An ancient gold coin of the time of Edward III., of six shillings sterling value.
A bursting into flower; a blossoming. Martyn.
Covered with flowers; abounding in flowers; flowery. Fruit from a pleasant and florid tree.
A cultivator of, or dealer in, flowers. 2. One who writes a flora, or an account of plants.
The slender styles of the pistillate flowers of maize; also called silk.
A little fleet, or a fleet of small vessels.
Goods lost by shipwreck, and floating on the sea; -- in distinction from jetsam or jetson.
A flatfish of the family Pleuronectidæ, of many species. Note: The common English flounder is Pleuronectes flesus.
The finely ground meal of wheat, or of any other grain; especially, the finer part of meal separated by bolting.
To mock or insult; to treat with contempt. Phillida flouts me. Walton.
Having conspicuous flowers; -- used as an epithet with many names of plants.
Full of flowers; abounding with blossoms. 2. Highly embellished with figurative language.
That flows or for flowing (in various sense of the verb); gliding along smoothly.
To move as a wave; to roll hither and thither; to wave; to float backward and forward, as on waves.
A motion like that of waves; a moving in this and that direction.
An inclosed passage way for establishing and directing a current of air, gases, etc.
The quality of being fluent; smoothness; readiness of utterance; volubility.
Nap or down; flue; soft, downy feathers.
Pertaining to, or resembling, fluff or nap; soft and downy. "The carpets were fluffy." Thackeray.
Having particles which easily move and change their relative position without a separation of the mass, and which easily yield to pressure.
The part of an anchor which fastens in the ground; a flook. See Anchor.
To fail, as on a lesson; to back out, as from an undertaking, through fear.
Having the property of fluorescence.
To flow and spread suddenly; to rush.
A heavy, coarse cloth manufactured from shoddy; -- commonly in the  2.
To make hot and rosy, as with drinking; to heat; hence, to throw into agitation and confusion.
A musical wind instrument, consisting of a hollow cylinder or pipe, with holes along its length, stopped by the fingers or by keys which are opened by the fingers.
Thin; fine; clear and mellow; flutelike.
To vibrate or move quickly.
Belonging to rivers; growing or living in streams or ponds.
The act of flowing; a continuous moving on or passing by, as of a flowing stream.
One that uses wings. 2. The fly of a flag: See Fly, n., 6. 3. Anything that is scattered abroad in great numbers as a theatrical programme, an advertising leaf, etc.
The young of any animal of the Horse family (Equidæ); a colt; a filly.
The white substance, consisting of an aggregation of bubbles, which is formed on the surface of liquids,or in the mouth of an animal, by violent agitation or fermentation.
Belonging to,or concerning, a focus.
Filled or abounding with fog, or watery exhalations; misty.
Weak; feeble. Lord Herbert. 1. A moral weakness; a failing; a weak point.
To tread under foot; to trample. King Richard . . . caused the ensigns of Leopold to be pulled down and foiled under foot.
One who, or that which, folds; esp., a flat, knifelike instrument used for folding paper.
The act of making a fold or folds; also, a fold; a doubling; a plication.
A leaf of a book or manuscript. 2. A sheet of paper once folded. 3. A book made of sheets of paper each folded once (four pages to the sheet).
. Tales, legends, or superstitions long current among the people. Trench.
One who follows; a pursuer; an attendant; a disciple; a dependent associate.
The state of being foolish; want of good sense; levity, weakness, or derangement of mind.
To apply a warm lotion to; to bathe with a cloth or sponge wet with warm water or medicated liquid.
The act of fomenting; the application of warm, soft, medicinal substances, as for the purpose of easing pain, by relaxing the skin, or of discussing tumors.
To treat or handle with tenderness or in a loving manner; to caress.
A dish made of cheese, eggs, butter, etc., melted together.

A complete assortment of printing type of one size, including a due proportion of all the letters in the alphabet, large and small, points, accents, and whatever else is necessary for printing with that variety of types.
The practice of folly; the behavior of a fool; absurdity. Folly in fools bears not so strong a note, As foolery in the wise, when wit doth dote.
Daring without judgment; foolishly adventurous and bold. Howell. Syn. -- Rash.
Marked with, or exhibiting, folly; void of understanding; weak in intellect.
In a foolish manner.
The quality of being foolish. 2. A foolish practice; an absurdity. The preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness.
A holding with the feet; firm L'Estrange.
Ground for the foot; place for the foot to rest on; firm foundation to stand on.
The impression of the foot; a trace or footmark.
The behavior, dress, or other indication of a fop; coxcombry; affectation of show.
Foplike; characteristic of a top in dress or manners; making an ostentatious display of gay clothing.
The act of foraging; search for provisions, etc. He  from forage will incline to play.
A sudden or irregular incursion in border warfare; hence, any irregular incursion for war or spoils.
An ancestor; a forefather; -- usually in the plural. "Your forbears of old." Sir W.
The act of forbearing or waiting; the exercise of patience. He soon shall findForbearance no acquittance ere day end.
Prohibited; interdicted. I kniw no spells, use no forbidden arts. Milton.

novice; one in the rudiments of knowledge; especially, a student during his fist year in a college or university.

sparing or economical with regard to money or food; thrifty
