Clear; notorious; open and bold; barefaced.
To furnish (a window, a house, a sash, a ease, etc.) with glass. Two cabinets daintily paved, richly handed, and glazed with crystalline glass.
One whose business is to set glass. Glazier's diamond. See under Diamond.
To disgorge filth, as a hawk. 1. A shoot of light; a small stream of light.
To gather after a reaper; to collect in scattered or fragmentary parcels, as the grain left by a reaper, or grapes left after the gathering.
A secluded and narrow valley; a dale; a depression between hills. And wooes the widow's daughter of the glen.
Smooth; slippery.
The glede or kite. 1. To move gently and smoothly; to pass along without noise, violence, or apparent effort.
One who, or that which, glides.
A sudden flash; transient luster. LIght as the lightning glimpse they ran.
To sparkle or shine; especially, to shine with a mild, subdued, and fitful luster.
To sparkle with light; to shine with a brilliant and broken light or showy luster.
To look steadfastly; to gaz In vengeance gloating on another's pain. Byron.
A round or spherical body, solid or hollow; a body whose surface is in every part equidistant from the center.
Having a rounded form resembling that of a globe; globular, or nearly so.
Globe-shaped; having the form of a ball or sphere; spherical, or nearly so.
Partial or total darkness; thick shade; obscurity.
Imperfectly illuminated; dismal through obscurity or darkness; dusky; dim.
A doxology (beginning Gloria Patri, Glory be to the Father), sung or said at the end of the Psalms in the service of the Roman Catholic and other churches.
A collection of glosses or explanations of words and passages of a work or author.
Smooth and shining; reflecting luster from a smooth surface; highly polished.
A cover for the hand, or for the hand and wrist, with a separate sheath for each finder.
One whose trade it is to make or sell gloves. Glover's suture or stitch, a kind of stitch used in sewing up wounds, in which the thread is drawn alternately through each side from within outward.
To shine with an intense or white heat; to give forth vivid light and heat.
to look intently; to stare angrily or with a scowl. Thackeray.
A variety of sugar occurring in nature very abundantly, as in ripe grapes, and in honey, and produced in great quantities from starch, etc., by the action of heat and acids.
A hard brittle brownish gelatin, obtained by boiling to a jelly the skins, hoofs, etc., of animals.
Sullenness. Skelton. Moody; silent; sullen. I frighten people by my glun face.
To swallow, or to swallow greedlly; to gorge. Though every drop of water swear against it, And gape at widest to glut him.

The viscid, tenacious substance which gives adhesiveness to dough. Note: Gluten is a complex and variable mixture of glutin or gliadin, vegetable fibrin, vegetable casein, oily material, etc., and ia a very...
Given to gluttony; eating to excess; indulging the appetite; voracious.
To growl; to snarl. And wolves are gnarling who shall gnaw thee first.
Knotty; full of knots or gnarls; twisted; crossgrained. The unwedgeable and gnarléd oak.
Full of knots; knotty; twisted; crossgrained.
To strike together, as in anger or pain.
An imaginary being, supposed by the Rosicrucians to inhabit the inner parts of the earth, and to be the guardian of mines, quarries, etc.
A pointed instrument used to urge on a beast; hence, any necessity that urges or stimulates.
A part of a man's beard on the chin or lower lip which is allowed to grow, and trimmed so as to resemble the beard of a goat.
To swallow or eat greedily or hastily; to gulp. Supper gobbled up in haste.
An evil or mischievous spirit; a playful or malicious elf; a frightful phantom.
A female god; a divinity, or deity, of the female sex. When the daughter of Jupiter presented herself among a crowd of goddesses, she was distinguished by her graceful stature and superior beauty.
Having, or acknowledging, no God; without reverence for God; impious; wicked.
A woman who becomes sponsor for a child in baptism. See Godfather.
Something sent by God; an unexpected acquisiton or piece of good fortune.
A male for whom one has stood sponsor in baptism. See Godfather.
Success; prosperous journeying; -- a contraction of the phrase, "God speed you."  Receive him not into house, neither bid him God speed.
To roll the eyes; to stare. And wink and goggle like an owl. Hudibras.
A small domesticated cyprinoid fish (Carassius auratus); -- so named from its color.
The European goldfinch. (b) The yellow-hammer.
A long, narrow boat with a high prow and stern, used in the canals of Venice.
A contagious inflammatory disease of the genitourinary tract, affecting especially the urethra and vagina, and characterized by a mucopurulent discharge, pain in urination, and chordee.
A bonbon, cake, or the like; -- usually in the pl. 2. (Zoöl.) An American fish.
One of several North American burrowing rodents of the genera Geomys and Thomomys, of the family Geomyidæ.
The throat; the gullet; the canal by which food passes to the stomach.
A large, arboreal, anthropoid ape of West Africa. It is larger than a man, and is remarkable for its massive skeleton and powerful muscles, which give it enormous strength.
Covered with gore or clotted blood. Thou canst not say I did it; never shake Thy gory locks at me.
A young or unfledged goose. 2. A catkin on nut trees and pines. Bailey.
A fine, filmy substance, like cobwebs, floating in the air, in calm, clear weather, especially in autumn.
Pertaining to the Goths.
A chisel, with a hollow or semicylindrical blade, for scooping or cutting holes, channels, or grooves, in wood, stone, etc.
A fleshy, three-celled, many-seeded fruit, as the melon, pumpkin, cucumber, etc., of the order Cucurbitaceæ.
A greedy or ravenous eater; a glutton. See Gormand. That great gourmand, fat Apicius B.
A connoisseur in eating and drinking; an epicure.
A drop; a clot or coagulation. On thy blade and dudgeon gouts of blood.
To direct and control, as the actions or conduct of men, either by established laws or by arbitrary will.
A female governor; a woman invested with authority to control and direct.
Holding the superiority; prevalent; controlling.
A loose, flowing upper garment; especially: (a) The ordinary outer dress of a woman.
Displaying grace or beauty in form or action; elegant; easy; agreeable in appearance.
Abounding in grace or mercy; manifesting love,. or bestowing mercy; characterized by grace.
The act of progressing by regular steps or orderly arrangement; the state of being graded or arranged in ranks.
One who grades, or that by means of which grading is done or facilitate.
Moving by steps; walking.
To mark with degrees; to divide into regular steps, grades, or intervals, as the scale of a thermometer, a scheme of punishment or rewards, etc.
Marked with, or divided into, degrees; divided into grades. 2. (Zoöl.) Tapered.
The act of graduating, or the state of being graduated.
A steward; an overseer. is nothing but a servant, overseer, or graff, and not the head, which is a title belonging only to Christ.
Inscriptions, figure drawings, etc., found on the walls of ancient sepulchers or ruins, as in the Catacombs, or at Pompeii.
A small shoot or scion of a tree inserted in another tree, the stock of which is to support and nourish it.
An instrument for recording, preserving, and reproducing sounds, the record being a tracing of a phonautograph etched in some solid material.
A storehouse or repository for grain, esp. after it is thrashed or husked.
A son's or daughter's child; a child in the second degree of descent.
The daughter of one's son or daughter.
The state or quality of being grand; vastness; greatness; splendor; magnificence.
The use of lofty words or phrases; bombast; -- usually in a bad sense.
Speaking in a lofty style; pompous; bombastic.
Impressive or elevating in effect; vimposing; splendid; striking; -- in a good sense.
A son's or daughter's son.
A crystalline, granular rock, consisting of quartz, feldspar, and mica, and usually of a whitish, grayish, or flesh-red color.
A grandmother; a grandam; familiarly, an old woman. Granny's bend, or Granny's knot (Naut.), a kind of insecure knot or hitch.
The person to whom a grant or conveyance is made. His grace will not survive the poor grantee he despises.
The person by whom a grant or conveyance is made.
Consisting of, or resembling, grains.
To form into grains or small masses.
A little grain a small particle; a pellet.
A well-known edible berry growing in pendent clusters or bunches on the grapevine.
A vine or climbing shrub, of the genus Vitis, having small green flowers and lobed leaves, and bearing the fruit called grapes.
The art or the science of drawing; esp. of drawing according to mathematical rules, as in perspective, projection, and the like.
Native carbon in hexagonal crystals, also foliated or granular massive, of black color and metallic luster, and so soft as to leave a trace on paper.
To seize; to lay fast hold of; to attack at close quarters:.
A laying fast ho1d of; also, that by which anything is seized and held, a grapnel.
To seize and hold by clasping or embracing with the fingers or arms; to catch to take possession of.
Seizing; embracing; catching. 2. Avaricious; greedy of gain; covetous.
Any jumping, orthopterous insect, of the families Acrididæ and Locustidæ.
The act of gratifying, or pleasing, either the mind, the taste, or the appetite.
To please; to give pleasure to; to satisfy; to soothe; to indulge.
A partition, covering, or frame of parallel or cross bars; a latticework resembling a window grate.
For nothing; without fee or recompense; freely; gratuitously.
The state of being grateful; warm and friendly feeling toward a benefactor.
Given without an equivalent or recompense; conferred without valuable consideration.
Something given freely or without recompense; a free gift; a present. Swift.
Small stones, or fragments of stone; very small pebbles, often intermixed with particles of sand.
In a grave manner.
The sediment of melted tallow. Same as Greaves. GRAVES' DISEASE Graves"' dis*ease".
A yard or inclosure for the interment of the dead; a cemetery.
The state of having weight; beaviness.
The juice or other liquid matter that drips from flesh in cooking, made into a dressing for the food when served up.
White mixed with black, as the color of pepper and salt, or of ashes, or of hair whitened by age.
To feed or supply (cattle, sheep, etc.) with grass; to furnish pasture for.
The act of one who, or that which, grazes. 2. A pasture; growing grass.
In a great degree; much. I will greatly multiply thy sorrow. Gen. iii.
An eager desire or longing; greediness.
Having a keen appetite for food or drink; ravenous; voracious; very hungry.
A house in which tender plants are cultivated and sheltered from the weather.
Expression of kindness or joy; salutation at meeting; a compliment from one absent.
fond of company; sociable
characteristic: Picture the life of the party, someone who thrives in a crowd and loves being around people.

definition: A hollow ball or shell of iron filled with powder of other explosive, ignited by means of a fuse, and thrown from the hand among enemies.
Originaly, a soldier who carried and threw grenades; afterward, one of a company attached to each regiment or battalion, taking post on the right of the line, and wearing a peculiar uniform.
A slender, graceful breed of dogs, remarkable for keen sight and swiftness.
A grating of thin parallel bars, similar to a gridiron.
A cause of uneasiness and complaint; a wrong done and suffered; that which gives ground for remonstrance or resistance, as arising from injustice, tyranny, etc.
Sad; sorrowful; causing grief. -- n. The act of causing grief; the state of being grieved.
Causing grief or sorrow; painful; afflictive; hard to bear; offensive.
An Anglo-Indian name for a person just arrived from Europe. H. Kingsley.
A gridiron. make grills of  to broil their meat. Cotton. 2. That which is broiled on a gridiron, as meat, fish, etc.
A distortion of the countenance, whether habitual, from affectation, or momentary aad occasional, to express some feeling, as contempt, disapprobation, complacency, etc.
To reduce to powder by friction, as in a mill, or with the teeth; to crush into small fragments.
One who, or that which, grinds. 2. One of the double teeth, used to grind or masticate the food.
from Grind. Grinding frame, an English name for a cotton spinning machine.
A flat, circular stone, revolving on an axle, for grinding or sharpening tools, or shaping or smoothing objects.
Among Spanish Americans, a foreigner, esp. an Englishman or American; -- often used as a term of reproach.
Frightful; horrible; dreadful; harsh.
Sand or gravel; rough, hard particles. 2. The coarse part of meal. 3. pl.
Containing sand or grit; consisting of grit; caused by grit; full of hard particles.
Somewhat gray; grizzled. Old squirrels that turn grizzly. Bacon. Grizzly bear (Zoöl.), a large and ferocious bear (Ursus horribilis) of Western North America and the Rocky Mountains.
To give forth a low, moaning sound in breathing; to utter a groan, as in pain, in sorrow, or in derision.
A trader who deals in tea, sugar, spices, coffee, fruits, and various other commodities.
The commodities sold by grocers, as tea, coffee, spices, etc.; -- in the United States almost always in the plural form, in this sense.
A mixture of spirit and water not sweetened; hence, any intoxicating liquor.
Overcome with grog; tipsy; unsteady on the legs. 2. Weakened in a fight so as to stagger.
A boy or young man; a waiter; a servant; especially, a man or boy who has charge of horses, or the stable.
Great; large; bulky; fat; of huge size; excessively large. "A gross fat man." Shak.
Like the figures found in ancient grottoes; grottolike; wildly or strangely formed.
A natural covered opening in the earth; a cave; also, an artificial recess, cave, or cavernlike apartment.
) Any of the numerous species of gallinaceous birds of the family Tetraonidæ, and subfamily Tetraoninæ, inhabiting Europe, Asia, and North America.
The original sense seems to have been a lane cut through trees. See Grave, v., and cf.
To creep on the earth, or with the face to the ground; to lie prone, or move uneasily with the body prostrate on the earth.
To utter a deep guttural sound, sa an angry dog; to give forth an angry, grumbling sound.
The process of growing; the gradual increase of an animal or a vegetable body.
Dirty; unclean. The grubby game of marbles. Lond. Sat. Rev. Any species of Cottus.
A light, liquid food, made by boiling meal of maize, oatmeal, or fiour in water or milk.
To murmur or mutter with discontent; to make ill-natured complaints in a low voice and a surly manner.
Surly; dissatisfied; grouty. Ferby.
To make a deep, short noise, as a hog; to utter a short groan or a deep guttural sound.
Any one of many species of large gallinaceous birds of Certal and South America, belonging to Penelope, Pipile, Ortalis, and allied genera.

In law and common usage: A promise to answer for the payment of some debt, or the performance of some duty, in case of the failure of another person, who is, in the first instance, liable to such payment or performance.
Cautious; wary; circumspect.
One who guards, preserves, or secures; one to whom any person or thing is committed for protection, security, or preservation from injury.
An irregular mode of carrying on war, by the constant attacks of independent bands, adopted in the north of Spain during the Peninsular war.
A loud burst of laughter, a horse laugh. "A hearty low guffaw." Carlyle.
The act or result of guiding; the superintendence or assistance of a guide.
Craft; deceitful cunning; artifice; duplicity; wile; deceit; treachery.
Free from guile; artless. -- Guile"less*ly, adv. Guile"less*ness, n.
A machine for beheading a person by one stroke of a heavy ax or blade, which slides in vertical guides, is raised by a cord, and let fall upon the neck of the victim.
The criminality and consequent exposure to punishment resulting from willful disobedience of law, or from morally wrong action.
Customary way of speaking or acting; custom; fashion; manner; behavior.
To deceive; to cheat; to mislead; to trick; to defraud. The rulgar, gulled into rebellion, armed.
Easily gulled; that may be duped. -- Gul"li*bii`i*ty, n. Burke.
To swallow eagerly, or in large draughts; to swallow up; to take down at one swallow.
A soup thickened with the mucilaginous pods of the okra; okra soup. 2.
Consisting of gum; viscous; adhesive; producing or containing gum; covered with gum or a substance resembling gum.
Capacity; shrewdness; common sense. One does not have gumption till one has been properly cheated.
One who works a gun, whether on land or sea; a cannoneer. 2. A warrant officer in the navy having charge of the ordnance on a vessel.
That branch of military science which comprehends the theory of projectiles, and the manner of constructing and using ordnance.
The act or practice of hunting or shooting game with a gun. The art of gunning was but little practiced.
A strong, coarse kind of sacking, made from the fibers (called jute) of two plants of the genus Corchorus (C.
A black, granular, explosive substance, consisting of an intimate mechanical mixture of niter, charcoal, and sulphur.
A spiritual teacher, guide, or confessor amoung the Hindoos. Malcom.
To issue with violence and rapidity, as a fluid; to rush forth as a fluid from confinement.
Rushing forth with violence, as a fluid; flowing copiously.
Pertaining to, or subservient to, the sense of taste.
Nice or keen appreciation or enjoyment; relish; taste; fancy. Dryden.
Subject to, or characterized by, gusts or squalls; windy; stormy; tempestuous.
To swallow liquor greedily; to drink much or frequently. Those that came to guzzle in his wine cellar.
A place or building where athletic exercises are performed; a school for gymnastics.
One who teaches or practices gymnastic exercises; the manager of a gymnasium.
Athletic or disciplinary exercises; the art of performing gymnastic exercises.
Government by a woman, female power; gyneocracy. Bailey.
The science which treats of the structure and diseases of women. -- Gyn`e*col"o*gist.
Winding or coiled round; curved into a circle; taking a circular course.
A rotating wheel, mounted in a ring or rings, for illustrating the dynamics of rotating bodies, the composition of rotations, etc.
Capable of being inhabited; that may be inhabited or dwelt in.
The natural abode, locality or region of an animal or plant. 2. Place where anything is commonly found.
Formed or acquired by habit or use. An habitual knowledge of certain rules and maxims.
Habitual attitude; usual or accustomed state with reference to something else.
A large estate where work of any kind is done, as agriculture, manufacturing, mining, or raising of animals.
A frame or grating of various kinds.
One who, or that which, hacks. Specifically: A cutting instrument for making notches.
A horse for riding or driving; a nag; a pony. Chaucer. 2. A horse or pony kept for hire.
lacking significance through having been overused; unoriginal and trite
characteristic: Think of a worn-out cliche you have heard a thousand times, stale and predictable.

definition: The nether world (according to classical mythology, the abode of the shades, ruled over by Hades or Pluto).
A Mohammedan pilgrim to Mecca; -- used among Orientals as a respectful salutation or a title of honor.
To cut roughly or hack; to cut into small pieces; to notch or cut in an unskillful manner.
Small roundish masses of ice precipitated from the clouds, where they are formed by the congelation of vapor.
A brush for cleansing and smoothing the hair.
One who dresses or cuts hair; a barber.
Destitute of hair. Shak.
A pin, usually forked, or of bent wire, for fastening the hair in place, -- used by women.
Bearing or covered with hair; made of or resembling hair; rough with hair.
A wise man; a physician, esp. a Mohammedan. A Mohammedan title for a ruler.
A kingfisher. By modern ornithologists restricted to a genus including a limited number of species having omnivorous habits, as the sacred kingfisher (Halcyon sancta) of Australia.
In the middle; at half the distance; imperfectly; partially.
A large, northern, marine flatfish (Hippoglossus vulgaris), of the family Pleuronectidæ.
Praise ye Jehovah; praise ye the Lord; -- an exclamation used chiefly in songs of praise or thanksgiving to God, and as an expression of gratitude or adoration.
To make holy; to set apart for holy or religious use; to consecrate; to treat or keep as sacred.
The act of hallucinating; a wandering of the mind; error; mistake; a blunder.
A luminous circle, usually prismatically colored, round the sun or moon, and supposed to be caused by the refraction of light through crystals of ice in the atmosphere.
A commercial city of Germany, near the mouth of the Elbe. Black Hamburg grape.
A small village; a little cluster of houses in the country. The country wasted, and the hamlets burned.
An instrument for driving nails, beating metals, and the like, consisting of a head, usually of steel or iron, fixed crosswise to a handle.
A shark of the genus Sphyrna or Zygæna, having the eyes set on projections from the sides of the head, which gives it a hammer shape.
A swinging couch or bed, usually made of netting or canvas about six feet wide, suspended by clews or cords at the ends.
A large basket, usually with a cover, used for the packing and carrying of articles.
A small European rodent (Cricetus frumentarius). It is remarkable for having a pouch on each side of the jaw, under the skin, and for its migrations.
One of the great tendons situated in each side of the ham, or space back of the knee, and connected with the muscles of the back of the thigh.
A book of reference, to be carried in the hand; a manual; a guidebook.
A fastening, consisting of an iron ring around the wrist, usually connected by a chain with one on the other wrist.
With hands joined; hand in hand. Into their inmost bower, Handed they went.
Work done by the hands; hence, any work done personally. The firmament showeth his handiwork.
A piece of cloth, usually square and often fine and elegant, carried for wiping the face or hands.
A touching, controlling, managing, using, etc., with the hand or hands, or as with the hands.
Manufactured by hand.
In a handsome manner. 2. (Naut.) Carefully; in shipshape style.
The cast or form of writing peculiar to each hand or person; chirography.
A base, degraded person; a sneak; a gallows bird. , Low; sneaking; ashamed.
One who hangs, or causes to be hanged; a hangman. 2. That by which a thing is suspended.
One who hangs another; esp., one who makes a business of hanging; a public executioner.
The Jewish Feast of the Dedication, instituted by Judas Maccabæus, his brothers, and the whole congregation of Israel, in 165 b.
Extra hazard; chance; accident; random. We take our principles at haphazard, upon trust.
Without hap or luck; luckless; unfortunate; unlucky; unhappy.
A speech addressed to a large public assembly; a popular oration; a loud address a multitude.
The act of harassing, or state of being harassed; worry; annoyance; anxiety.
One who provides lodgings; especially, the officer of the English royal household who formerly preceded the court when traveling, to provide and prepare lodgings.
To make hard or harder; to make firm or compact; to indurate.
Made hard, or compact; made unfeeling or callous; made obstinate or obdurate.
Boldness, united with firmness and constancy of mind; bravery; intrepidity.
That which is hard to hear, as toil, privation, injury, injustice, etc.
Ware made of metal, as cutlery, kitchen utensils, and the like; ironmongery.
Bold; brave; stout; daring; resolue; intrepid. Hap helpeth hardy man alway.
The apartments or portion of the house allotted to females in Mohammedan families.
To listen; to hearken. Hudibras. Hark away! Hark back! Hark forward! (Sporting), cries used to incite and guide hounds in hunting.
Full of harm; injurious; hurtful; mischievous. " Most harmful hazards." Strype.
Free from harm; unhurt.
A musical instrument, consisting of a series of hemispherical glasses which, by touching the edges with the dampened finger, give forth the tones.
Adapted to each other; having parts proportioned to each other; symmetrical.
The just adaptation of parts to each other, in any system or combination of things, or in things, or things intended to form a connected whole.
Originally, the complete dress, especially in a military sense, of a man or a horse.
A player on the harp; a minstrel. The murmuring pines and the hemlocks .
Pertaining to the harp.
A spear or javelin used to strike and kill large fish, as whales; a harping iron.
An implement of agriculture, usually formed of pieces of timber or metal crossing each other, and set with iron or wooden teeth.
Rough; disagreeable; grating; esp.:(a) To the touch."Harsh sand." Boyle.
In a harsh manner; gratingly; roughly; rudely. 'T will sound harshly in her ears.
A stag; the male of the red deer. See the Note under Buck. Goodliest of all the forest, hart and hind.
The Hartford grape, a variety of grape first raised at Hartford, Connecticut, from the Northern fox grape.
, from Harvest, v. t. Harvesting ant (Zoöl.), any species of ant which gathers and stores up seeds for food.
That which is hashed or chopped up; meat and vegetables, especially such as have been already cooked, chopped into small pieces and mixed.
To press; to drive or urge forward; to push on; to precipitate; to accelerate the movement of.
In haste; with speed or quickness; speedily; nimbly. 2. Without due reflection.
Early fruit or vegetables; especially, early pease. Mortimer.
One who hatches, or that which hatches; a hatching apparatus; an incubator.
A small ax with a short handle, to be used with one hand. 2. Specifically, a tomahawk.
A mode of execution in engraving, drawing, and miniature painting, in which shading is produced by lines crossing each other at angles more or less acute.
Manifesting hate or hatred; malignant; malevolent. And worse than death, to view with hateful eyes His rival's conquest.
One who hates. An enemy to God, and a hater of all good. Sir T. Browne.
Strong aversion; intense dislike; hate; an affection of the mind awakened by something regarded as evil.
The quality of being haughty; disdain; arrogance. Syn. -- Arrogance; disdain.
High; lofty; bold. To measure the most haughty mountain's height. Spenser.
To pull or draw with force; to drag. Some dance, some haul the rope. Denham.
Inhabited by, or subject to the visits of, apparitions; frequented by a ghost.
Haughty manner or spirit; haughtiness; pride; arrogance.
A bay, recess, or inlet of the sea, or the mouth of a river, which affords anchorage and shelter for shipping.
Wide and general destruction; devastation; waste. As for Saul, he made havoc of the church.
Belonging to Hawaii or the Sandwich Islands, or to the people of Hawaii.
One of numerous species and genera of rapacious birds of the family Falconidæ.
A large rope made of three strands each containing many yarns. Note: Three hawsers twisted together make a cable.
A thorny shrub or tree (the Cratægus oxyacantha), having deeply lobed, shining leaves, small, roselike, fragrant flowers, and a fruit called haw.
A stack or conical pile of hay in the open air.
An officer who is appointed to guard hedges, and to keep cattle from breaking or cropping them, and whose further duty it is to impound animals found running at large.
A game of chance played with dice. Chaucer. 2. The uncertain result of throwing a die.
Exposed to hazard; dangerous; risky. To enterprise so hazardous and high! Milton.
Light vapor or smoke in the air which more or less impedes vision, with little or no dampness.
A shrub or small tree of the genus Corylus, as the C. avellana, bearing a nut containing a kernel of a mild, farinaceous taste.
Thick with haze; somewhat obscured with haze; not clear or transparent.
Pain in the head; ceph "Headaches and shivering fits." Macaulay.
A fillet; a band for the head. "The headbands and the tablets." Is. iii.
With the head foremost.
A light, with a powerful reflector, placed at the head of a locomotive, or in front of it, to throw light on the track at night, or in going through a dark tunnel.
The line at the head or top of a page. 2. (Naut.) See Headrope.
With the head foremost.
A head or leading man, especially of a village community.
The quarters or place of residence of any chief officer, as the general in command of an army, or the head of a police force.
The principal stone in a foundation; the chief or corner stone. Ps. cxviii.
Not easily restrained; ungovernable; obstinate; stubborn. Not let headstrong boy my will control.
The progress made by a ship in motion; hence, progress or success of any kind.
Willful; rash; precipitate; hurried on by will or passion; ungovernable.
One who, or that which, heals.
Tending to cure; soothing; mollifying.
Being in a state of health; enjoying health; hale; sound; free from disease.
A crowd; a throng; a multitude or great number of persons. The wisdom of a heap of learned men.
To listen; to lend the ear; to attend to what is uttered; to give heed.

Report; rumor; fame; common talk; something heard from another. Much of the obloquy that has so long rested on the memory of our great national poet originated in frivolous hearsays of his life and conversation.
Sorrow; anguish of mind; mental pang. Shak.
Crushing sorrow or grief; a yielding to such grief. Shak.
Causing overpowering sorrow.
Overcome by crushing sorrow; deeply grieved.
An uneasy, burning sensation in the stomach, often attended with an inclination to vomit.
To encourage; to animate; to incite or stimulate the courage of; to embolden.
Hearty; sincere.
The pavement or floor of brick, stone, or metal in a chimney, on which a fire is made.
From the heart; with all the heart; with sincerity. I heartily forgive them.
Without a heart. You have left me heartess; mine is in your bosom. J. Webster.
Causing intense grief; overpowering with anguish; very distressing.
Pertaining to, or proceeding from, the heart; warm; cordial; bold; zealous.
One who, or that which, heats. 2. Any contrivance or implement, as a furnace, stove, or other heated body or vessel, etc., used to impart heat to something, or to contain something to be heated.
