The condition or quality of being immobile; fixedness in place or state.
To sacrifice; to offer in sacrifice; to kill, as a sacrificial victim.
Not moral; inconsistent with rectitude, purity, or good morals; contrary to conscience or the divine law.
The quality or state of being immortal; exemption from death and annihilation.
Incapable of being moved; firmly fixed; fast; -- used of material things.
Exempt; protected by inoculation. -- Im*mu"nize, v. t.
Freedom or exemption from any charge, duty, obligation, office, tax, imposition, penalty, or service.
To wall around; to surround with walls. Sandys. 2. To inclose whithin walls, or as within walls.
Not mutable; not capable or susceptible of change; unchangeable; unalterable.
To drive close; to press firmly together: to wedge into a place. Woodward.
Driven together or close. Impacted fracture (Surg.), a fracture in which the fragments are driven into each other so as to be immovable.
To make worse; to diminish in quantity, value, excellence, or strength.
To pierce with a pale; to put to death by fixing on a sharp stake. See Empale.
Not palpable; that cannot be felt; extremely fine, so that no grit can be perceived by touch.
Not partial; not favoring one more than another; treating all alike; unprejudiced.
Incapable of being passed; not admitting a passage.
An impassable road or way; a blind alley; cul-de-sac; fig., a position or predicament affording no escape.
Incapable of suffering; inaccessible to harm or pain; not to be touched or moved to passion or sympathy.
Actuated or characterized by passion or zeal; showing warmth of feeling.
Not susceptible of pain or suffering; apathetic; impassible; unmoved. Impassive as the marble in the quarry.
The quality of being impatient; want of endurance of pain, suffering, opposition, or delay.
Not liable to sin; exempt from the possibility of doing wrong. -- n. One who is impeccable.
having little or no money; penniless
characteristic: Think of empty pockets and a thin wallet, someone who is broke and cannot pay the bills.

definition: To hinder; to stop in progress; to obstruct.
That which impedes or hinders progress, motion, activity, or effect. Thus far into the bowels of the land Have we marched on without impediment.
To drive or urge forward or on; to press on; to incite to action or motion in any way.
Hanging over; overhanging; suspended so as to menace; imminet; threatening.
Incapable of being penetrated or pierced; not admitting the passage of other bodies.
Expressive of command; containing positive command; authoritatively or absolutely directive.
Not perceptible; not to be apprehended or cognized by the souses; not discernible by the mind.
The power or character of an emperor; imperial authority; the spirit of empire.
To bring into peril; to endanger.
Commanding; ascendant; imperial; lordly; majestic. "A vast and imperious mind." Tilloison.
Not permeable; not permitting passage, as of a fluid. through its substance.
Not permissible.
Not personal; not representing a person; not having personality. An almighty but impersonal power, called Fate.
To invest with personality; to endow with the form of a living being. 2.
Not to be persuaded; obstinate; unyielding; impersuasible. -- Im`per*suad"a*ble*ness, n.
The condition or quality of being impertnent; absence of pertinence, or of adaptedness.
Not pertinent; not pertaining to the matter in hand; having no bearing on the subject.
Incapable of being disturbed or disconcerted.
Not pervious; not admitting of entrance or passage through.
The condition or quality of being impetuous; fury; violence. 2. Vehemence, or furiousnes of temper.
Rushing with force and violence; moving with impetus; furious; forcible.
A property possessed by a moving body in virtue of its weight and its motion.
The quality of being impious; want of piety; irreverence toward the Supreme Being.
To fall or dash against; to touch upon; to strike; to hit; to ciash with.
Not pious; wanting piety; irreligious; irreverent; ungodly; profane; wanting in reverence for the Supreme Being.
Not placable; not to be appeased; incapable of being pacified; inexorable.
To plant, or infix, for the purpose of growth; to fix deeply; to instill.
Not plausible; not wearing the appearance of truth or credibility, and not likely to be believed.
Not pliable; inflexible; inyielding.
To infold; to fold together; to interweave. The meeting boughs and implicated leaves.
The act of implicating, or the state of being implicated. Three principal causes of firmness are.
Infolded; entangled; complicated; involved. Milton. In his woolly fleece I cling implicit.
Virtually involved or included; involved in substance; inferential; tacitly conceded.
Not polite; not of polished manners; wanting in good manners; discourteous.
Not politic; contrary to, or wanting in, policy; unwise; imprudent; indiscreet.
Not ponderable; without sensible or appreciable weight; incapable of being weighed.
To bring in from abroad; to introduce from without; especially, to bring (wares or merchandise) into a place or country from a foreign country, in the transactions of commerce.
In an important manner.
Laying as a duty; enjoining. 2. Adapted to impress forcibly; impressive.
The act of imposing, laying on, affixing, enjoining, inflicting, obtruding, and the like.
One who imposes upon others; a person who assumes a character or title not his own, for the purpose of deception.
The act or conduct of an impostor; deception practiced under a false or assumed character.
To shut up or place in an inclosure called a pound; hence, to hold in the custody of a court.
To make poor; to reduce to poverty or indigence.
Not practicable; incapable of being performed, or accomplished by the means employed, or at command.
Not practical.
To call down by prayer, as something hurtful or calamitous. Imprecate the vengeance of Heaven on the guilty empire.
The act of imprecating, or unvoking evil upon any one; a player that a curse or calamnity may fall on any one.
Not to be stormed, or taken by assault; incapable of being subdued; able to resist attack.
To make pregnant; to cause to conceive; to render prolific; to get with child or young.
The projector, manager, or conductor, of an opera or concert company.
Liable or subject to impression; capable of being molded; susceptible.
A license to print or publish a book, paper, etc.; also, in countries subjected to the censorship of the press, approval of that which is published.
To impress; to mark by pressure; to indent; to stamp. And sees his num'rous herds imprint her sands.
To put in prison or jail; To arrest and detain in custody; to confine.
The act of imprisoning, or the state of being imprisoned; confinement.
Not probable; unlikely to be true; not to be expected under the circumstances or in the usual course of events.
Offhand; without previous study; extemporaneous; extempore.
The quality of being improper; unfitness or unsuitableness to character, time place, or circumstances.
The act of improving; advancement or growth; promotion in desirable qualities.
Not provident; wanting foresight or forethought; not foreseeing or providing for the future.
Tending to improve, beneficial; growing better. -- Im*prov"ing*ly, adv.
To compose, recite, or sing extemporaneously, especially in verse; to extemporize.
The quality or state of being imprudent; want to caution, circumspection, or a due regard to consequences.
Not prudent; wanting in prudence or discretion; indiscreet; injudicious.
The quality of being impudent; assurance, accompanied with a disregard of the presence or opinions of others.
Bold, with contempt or disregard; unblushingly forward; impertinent; wanting modesty.
To attack by words or arguments; to contradict; to assail; to call in question.
Lack of power; inability. Bacon. Their own impuissance and weakness. Holland.
The act of impelling or driving onward, or the state of being impelled.
Having the power of driving or impelling; giving an impulse; moving; impellent.
Exemption or freedom from punishment, harm, or loss. Heaven, though slow to wrath, Is neimpunity defied.
The act of imputing or charging; attribution; ascription; also, anything imputed or charged.
To charge; to ascribe; to attribute; to set to the account of; to charge to one as the author, responsible originator, or possessor.
The quality or state of being unable; lack of ability; want of sufficient power, strength, resources, or capacity.
Not accessible; not to be reached, obtained, or approached.
Not accurate; not according to truth; inexact; incorrect; erroneous.
Not active; having no power to move; that does not or can not produce results.
Not adequate; unequal to the purpose; insufficient; deficient.
Not admissible; not proper to be admitted, allowed, or received.
The quality of being inadvertent; lack of heedfulness or attentiveness.
Not turning the mind to a matter; heedless; careless; negligent; inattentive.
Not advisable. -- In`ad*vis"a*ble*ness, n.
Incapable of being alienated, surrendered, or transferred to another; not alienable.
A woman in love; a mistress. "The fair inamorata." Sherburne.
Inanition; void space; vacuity; emptiness. 2. Want of seriousness; aimlessness.
Not instrument (to); not appropriate; unbecoming; unsuitable; not specially fitted.
Unapt; not apt; unsuitable; inept. -- In*apt"ly, adv. -- In*apt"ness, n.
Not uttered with articulation or intelligible distinctness, as speech or words.
In like degree; in like manner; seeing that; considering that; since; -- followed by as.
Not audible; incapable of being heard; silent. -- In*au"di*ble*ness, n.
The act of inuagurating, or inducting into office with solemnity; investiture by appropriate ceremonies.
Born in or with; implanted by nature; innate.
Bred within; innate.
An emperor or monarch of Peru before, or at the time of, the Spanish conquest.
A white heat, or the glowing or luminous whiteness of a body caused by intense heat.
White, glowing, or luminous, with intense heat.
The act or process of using formulas sung or spoken, with occult ceremonies, for the purpose of raising spirits, producing enchantment, or affecting other magical results.
Wanting in ability or qualification for the purpose or end in view; not large enough to contain or hold.
To deprive of capacity or natural power; to disable; to render incapable or unfit.
Want of capacity; lack of physical or intellectual power; inability. 2.
To imprison; to confine in a jail or priso 2. To confine; to shut up or inclose.
The act of confining, or the state of being confined; imprisonment. Glanvill.
Flesh-colored; of a carnation or pale red color. Lovelace. To dye red or crimson.
Any person who maliciously sets fire to a building or other valuable or other valuable property.
To set on fire; to inflame; to kindle; to burn. Twelve Trojan princes wait on thee, and labor to incense Thy glorious heap of funeral.
Inciting; encouraging or moving; rousing to action; stimulative. Competency is the most incentive to industry.
Beginning; expressing or indicating beginning.
Continuing or following without interruption; unceasing; unitermitted.
The crime of cohabitation or sexual commerce between persons related within the degrees wherein marriage is prohibited by law.
A piece an inch long. By inchmeal, by small degrees; by inches. Shak. Little by little.
just begun and so not fully formed or developed; rudimentary
characteristic: Like a vague idea still taking shape, a sketch that is barely started and undefined.

definition: Expressing or pertaining to a beginning; inceptive.
Falling or striking upon, as a ray of light upon a reflecting surface.
Happening, as an occasional event, without regularity; coming without design.
Beginning; commencement; incipient state.
Beginning to be, or to show itself; commencing; initial.
Having the quality of incising, cutting, or penetrating, as with a sharp instrument.
One of the teeth in front of the canines in either jaw; an incisive tooth.
To move to action; to stir up; to rouse; to spur or urge on. Anthiochus, when he incited Prusias to join in war, set before him the greatness of the Romans.
The act of inciting. 2. That which incites the mind, or moves to action.
Not clement; destitute of a mild and kind temper; void of tenderness; unmerciful.
The act of inclining, or state of being inclined; a leaning.
To deviate from a line, direction, or course, toward an object; to lean.
Having a leaning or tendency towards, or away from, a thing; disposed or moved by wish, desire, or judgment.
Inclosed; confined. Included stamens (Bot.), such as are shorter than the floral envelopes, or are concealed within them.
Inclosing; encircling; surrounding. The inclusive verge Of golden metal that must round my brow.
Not to be coerced; incapable of being compelled or forced. 2. (Physics) Not capable of being reduced to the form of a liquid by pressure.
Without being known; in disguise; in an assumed character, or under an assumed title.
The quality or state of being incoherent; want of coherence; want of cohesion or adherence.
Not coherent; wanting cohesion; loose; unconnected; physically disconnected.
Not combustible; not capable of being burned, decomposed, or consumed by fire.
Tending to incommode; not commodious; not affording ease or advantage.
Not comparable; admitting of no comparison with others; unapproachably eminent.
Not compatible; so differing as to be incapable of harmonious combination or coexistence.
The quality or state of being incompetent; want of physical, intellectual, or moral ability.
Not competent; wanting in adequate strength, power, capacity, means, qualifications, or the like.
Not complete; not filled up; not finished; not having all its parts, or not having them all adjusted.
Not compressible; incapable of being reduced by force or pressure into a smaller compass or volume.
Not conceivable; incapable of being conceived by the mind; not explicable by the human intellect, or by any known principles or agencies.
Not conclusive; leading to no conclusion; not closing or settling a point in debate, or a doubtful question.
Incongruous. Sir T. Elyot.
The quality or state of being incongruous; want of congruity; unsuitableness.
Not congruous; reciprocally disagreeing; not capable of harmonizing or readily assimilating.
Not considerable; unworthy of consideration or notice; unimportant; small.
The quality or state of being inconsistent; discordance in respect to sentiment or action.
Not consistent; showing inconsistency; irreconcilable; discordant; at variance, esp.
The quality or state of being inconstant; want of constancy; mutability.
Not constant; not stable or uniform; subject to change of character, appearance, opinion, inclination, or purpose, etc.
The quality or condition of being inconvenient; want of convenience; unfitness.
Not becoming or suitable; unfit; inexpedient. 2. Not convenient; giving trouble, uneasiness, or annoyance.
Not consisting of matter; not having a material body; incorporeal; spiritual.
United in one body; formed into a corporation; made a legal entity.
Not corporeal; not having a material body or form; not consisting of matter.
Not correct; not according to a copy or model, or to established rules.
Not corrigible; incapable of being corrected or amended; bad beyond correction.
More and more.
In an incredible manner.
The state or quality of being i Of every species of incredulity, religious unbelief is the most irrational.
Not credulous; indisposed to admit or accept that which is related as true, skeptical.
The act or process of increasing; growth in bulk, guantity, number, value, or amount.
To accuse; to charge with a crime or fault; to criminate.

The act of incrusting, or the state of being incrusted. 2. A crust or hard coating of anything upon or within a body, as a deposit of lime, sediment, etc., from water on the inner surface of a steam boiler.
To sit, as on eggs for hatching; to brood; to brood upon, or keep warm, as eggs, for the purpose of hatching.
A sitting on eggs for the purpose of hatching young; a brooding on, or keeping warm, (eggs) to develop the life within, by any process.
That which incubates, especially, an apparatus by means of which eggs are hatched by artificial heat.
A demon; a fiend; a lascivious spirit, supposed to have sexual intercourse with women by night.
To teach and impress by frequent repetitions or admonitions; to urge on the mind.
To blame; to impute guilt to; to accuse; to involve or implicate in guilt.
Lying; resting; reclining; recumbent; superimposed; superincumbent. Two incumbent figures, gracefully leaning upon it.
Not capable of being cured; beyond the power of skill or medicine to remedy.
Brought into debt; being under obligation; held to payment or requital.
Not decent; unfit to be seen or heard; offensive to modesty and delicacy.
Want of decision; want of settled purpose, or of firmness; indetermination.
Not decisive; not bringing to a final or ultimate issue.
Not decorous; violating good manners; contrary to good breeding or etiquette.
Incapable of being fatigued; not readily exhausted; unremitting in labor or effort.
Not defensible; not capable of being defended, maintained, vindicated, or justified.
In an indefinite manner or degree; without any settled limitation; vaguely.
To save harmless; to secure against loss or damage; to insure. The states must at last engage to the merchants here that they will indemnify them from all that shall fall out.
Security; insurance; exemption from loss or damage, past or to come; immunity from penalty, or the punishment of past offenses.
The act of indenting or state of being indented. 2. A notch or recess, in the margin or border of anything.
The act of indenting, or state of being indented. 2. (Law) A mutual agreement in writing between two or more parties, whereof each party has usually a counterpart or duplicate.
The state or quality of being independent; freedom from dependence; exemption from reliance on, or control by, others.
Not dependent; free; not subject to control by others; not relying on others.
In an independent manner; without control.
Incapable of being described. -- In`de*scrib"a*bly, adv.
Not destructible; incapable of decomposition or of being destroyed. -- In`de*struc"ti*ble*ness, n.
Not determinate; not certain or fixed; indefinite; not precise.
Serving to point out, as a remedy; indicating. That which indicates or points out.
To point out; to discover; to direct to a knowledge of; to show; to make known.
Shown; denoted; registered; measured. Indicated power. See Indicated horse power, under Horse power.
Pointing out; bringing to notice; giving intimation or knowledge of something not visible or obvious.
One who, or that which, shows or points out.
To write; to compose; to dictate; to indite. 2. To appoint publicly or by authority.
The act of indicting, or the state of being indicted. 2. (Law) The formal statement of an offense, as framed by the prosecuting authority of the State, and found by the grand jury.
A name designating the East Indies, also the West Indies. Our king has all the Indies in his arms.
The quality or state of being indifferent, or not making a difference.
Not mal Dangers are to me indifferent. Shak. Everything in the world is indifferent but sin.
The condition of being indigent; want of estate, or means of comfortable subsistence.
Native; produced, growing, or living, naturally in a country or climate.
Wanting; void; free; destitute; -- used with of. Bacon. 2. Destitute of property or means of comfortable subsistence.
Not digestible; not readily soluble in the digestive juices; not easily convertible into products fitted for absorption.
Lack of proper digestive action; a failure of the normal changes which food should undergo in the alimentary canal.
Affected with indignation; wrathful; passionate; irate; feeling wrath, as when a person is exasperated by unworthy or unjust treatment, by a mean action, or by a degrading accusation.
The feeling excited by that which is unworthy, base, or disgraceful; anger mingled with contempt, disgust, or abhorrence.
Any action toward another which manifests contempt for him; an offense against personal dignity.
A kind of deep blue, one of the seven prismatic colors. 2. (Chem.) A blue dyestuff obtained from several plants belonging to very different genera and orders.
In an direct manner; not in a straight line or course; not in express terms.
Not to be discerned; imperceptible; not discoverable or visible. Secret and indiscernible ways.
Not discreet; wanting in discretion. So drunken, and so indiscreet an officer.
The quality or state of being indiscreet; want of discretion; imprudence.
Not discriminate; wanting discrimination; undistinguishing; not making any distinction.
Not dispensable; impossible to be omitted, remitted, or spared; absolutely necessary or requisite.
Not disputable; incontrovertible; too evident to admit of dispute. Syn.
Not dissoluble; not capable of being dissolved, melted, or liquefied; insoluble.
Not distinct or distinguishable; not separate in such a manner as to be perceptible by itself.
In an indistinct manner; not clearly; confusedly; dimly.
Not divided, or not to be divided; existing as one entity, or distinct being or object.
The quality or state of being individual or constituting an individual.
In an individual manner or relation; as individuals; separately; each by itself.
Not divisible; incapable of being divided, separated, or broken; not separable into parts.
To instruct in the rudiments or principles of learning, or of a branch of learning.
Free from toil, pain, or trouble. 2. Indulging in ease; avoiding labor and exertion.
Not to be subdued; untamable; invincible.
Done or being within doors; within a house or institution; domestic.
Within the house; -- usually separated, in doors.
Not dubitable or doubtful; too evident to admit of doubt; unquestionable.
Undoubtedly; unquestionably; in a manner to remove all doubt. Oracles indubitably clear and infallibly certain.
The act of inducing, or the state of being induced. 2. That which induces.
To bring in; to introduce; to usher in. The independent orator inducting himself without further ceremony into the pulpit.
To be complacent toward; to give way to; not to oppose or restrain; (a) when said of a habit, desire, etc.: to give free course to.
The act of indulging or humoring; the quality of being indulgent; forbearance of restrain or control.
Prone to indulge; yielding to the wishes, humor, or appetites of those under one's care.
Hardened; not soft; indurated. Tyndale. 2. Without sensibility; unfeeling.
Consisting in industry; pertaining to industry, or the arts and products of industry.
Given to industry; characterized by diligence; constantly, regularly, or habitually occupied.
Habitual diligence in any employment or pursuit, either bodily or mental.
Drunkenness; inebriation. E. Darwin.
Not edible; not fit for food. -- In*ed`i*bil"i*ty (#), n.
too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words
characteristic: Think of a feeling of awe so overwhelming that no words could ever capture it.

definition: Not effective; ineffectual; futile; inefficient; useless.
Not producing the proper effect; without effect; inefficient; weak; useless.
The quality of being inefficient; want of power or energy sufficient; want of power or energy sufficient for the desired effect.
Not efficient; not producing the effect intended or desired; inefficacious.
Not eligible; not qualified to be chos Burke.
Not to be overcome by struggling; irresistible; inevitable. Bp. Pearson.
Not apt or fit; unfit; unsuitable; improper; unbecoming. The Aristotelian philosophy is inept for new discoveries.
Want of equity; injustice; wrong. "Some form of inequity." H. Spencer.
Exemption from error. The absolute inerrancy odf the Bible. The Century.
Destitute of the power of moving itself, or of active resistance to motion.
That property of matter by which it tends when at rest to remain so, and when in motion to continue in motion, and in the same straight line or direction, unless acted on by some external force.
Incapable of being estimated or computed; especially, too valuable or excellent to be measured or fully appreciated.
Impossibility to be avoided or shunned; inevitableness. Shelford.
Not evitable; incapable of being shunned; unavoidable; certain. "The inevitable hour." Gray.
Without possibility of escape or evasion; unavoidably; certainly. Inevitably thou shalt die.
Not excusable; not admitting excuse or justification.
Incapable of being exhausted, emptied, or used up; unfailing; not to be wasted or spent.
Not to be persuaded or moved by entreaty or prayer; firm; determined; unyielding.
Not expedient; not tending to promote a purpose; not tending to the end desired.
Not expensive; cheap.
Absence or want of experience; lack of personal and experimental knowledge.
Not having experience unskilled. "Inexperienced youth." Cowper.
Not explicable; not explainable; incapable of being explained, interpreted, or accounted for.
Not capable of expression or utterance in language; ineffable; unspeakable.
Not capable of being extended; not elastic.
Not fallible; not capable of erring; entirely exempt from liability to mistake.
Total loss of reputation; public disgrace; dishonor; ignominy; indignity.
Infatuated. Bp. Hall. 1. To make foolish; to affect with folly; to weaken the intellectual powers of, or to deprive of sound judgment.
Overcome by some foolish passion or desire; affected by infatuation.
The act of infatuating; the state of being infatuated; folly; that which infatuates.
The act or process of infecting. There was a strict order against coming to those pits, and that was only to prevent infection.
Having qualities that may infect; communicable or caused by infection.
Not felicitous; unhappy; unfortunate; not fortunate or appropriate in application.
To bring on; to induce; to occasion. Harvey. 2. To offer, as violence.
The act or process of inferring by deduction or induction. Though it may chance to be right in the conclusions, it is yet unjust and mistaken in the method of inference.
Lower in place, rank, excellence, etc.; less important or valuable; subordinate.
The infernal regions; hell. Also used fig. At each sudden explosion in the inferno below they sprang back from the brink .
The act of infesting or state of being infested; molestation; vexation.
Not holding the faith; -- applied esp. to one who does not believe in the inspiration of the Scriptures, and the supernatural origin of Christianity.
Want of faith or belief in some religious system; especially, a want of faith in, or disbelief of, the inspiration of the Scriptures, of the divine origin of Christianity.
To enter by penetrating the pores or interstices of a substance; to filter into or through something.
The act or process of infiltrating, as if water into a porous substance, or of a fluid into the cells of an organ or part of the body.
Unlimited or boundless, in time or space.
Without bounds or limits; beyond or below assignable limits.
Infinitely or indefinitely small; less than any assignable quantity or value.
Unlimited extent of time, space, or quantity; eternity; boundlessness.
A hospital, or place where the infirm or sick are lodged and nursed gratuitously, or where out-patients are treated.
The state of being infirm; feebleness; an imperfection or weakness; esp., an unsound, unhealthy, or debilitated state.
Set on fire; enkindled; heated; congested; provoked; exasperated. 2. (Her.) Represented as burning, or as adorned with tongues of flame.
Capable of being easily set fire; easily enkindled; combustible.
The act of inflaming, kindling, or setting on fire; also, the state of being inflamed.
Tending to inflame, kindle, or irritate. 2. Tending to excite anger, animosity, tumult, or sedition.
That may be inflated.
Blown in; inflated. Chaucer. 1. To swell or distend with air or gas; to dilate.
Filled, as with air or gas; blown up; distended.
The act or process of inflating, or the state of being inflated, as with air or gas.
Not capable of being bent; stiff; rigid; firm; unyielding. 2. Firm in will or purpose.
To give, cause, or produce by striking, or as if by striking; to apply forcibly.
Exerting or possessing influence or power; potent; efficacious; effective.
An epidemic affection characterized by acute nasal catarrh, or by inflammation of the throat or the bronchi, and usually accompanied by fever.
Without regular form; shapeless; ugly; deformed. Cotton. 1. To give form or share to.
Having power to inform, animate, or vivify. Dr. H. More.
The act of infracting or breaking; breach; violation; nonobservance; infringement.
Seldom happening or occurring; rare; uncommon; unusual. The act whereof is at this day infrequent or out of use among all sorts of men.
Enraged; rading; furiously angry; infuriated. Milton. Inflamed beyond the most infuriate wrath.
Possessed of genius, or the faculty of invention; skillful or promp to invent.
An ingenuous or naïve girl or young woman, or an actress representing such a person.
The quality or power of ready invention; quickness or acuteness in forming new combinations.
To take into, or as into, the stomach or alimentary canal. Sir T. Browne.
Not glorious; not bringing honor or glory; not accompanied with fame, honor, or celebrity.
To insert, as a scion of one tree, shrub, or plant in another for propagation.
Ingrateful. Bacon. An ungrateful person. Milton.
To introduce or commend to the favor of another; to bring into favor; to insinuate.
Want of gratitude; insensibility to, forgetfulness of, or ill return for, kindness or favors received.
That which enters into a compound, or is a component part of any combination or mixture.
To live or dwell in; to occupy, as a place of settled residence.
The act of inhaling; also, that which is inhaled.
To breathe or draw into the lungs; to inspire.
One who inhales. 2. An apparatus for inhaling any vapor or volatile substance, as ether or chloroform, for medicinal purposes.
The state of inhering; permanent existence in something; innateness; inseparable and essential connection.
Permanently existing in something; inseparably attached or connected; naturally pertaining to.
By inherence; inseparably. Matter hath inherently and essentially such an internal energy.
The act or state of inheriting.
To check; to hold back; to restrain; to hinder. Their motions also are excited or inhibited .
Not hospitable; not disposed to show hospitality to strangers or guests.
Destitute of the kindness and tenderness that belong to a human being.
To deposit, as a dead body, in the earth; to bury; to inter. Weeping they bear the mangled heaps of slain, Inhume the natives in their native plain.
Having the disposition or temper of an enemy; unfriendly; unfavorable.
Not capable of being imitated, copied, or counterfeited; beyond imitation.
Characterized by iniquity; unjust; wicked.
Absence of, or deviation from, just dealing; want of rectitude or uprightness.
In an initial or incipient manner or degree; at the beginning. Barrow.
The act of initiating, or the process of being initiated or introduced.
Serving to initiate; inceptive; initiatory; introductory; preliminary.
The act of injecting or throwing in; -- applied particularly to the forcible throwing in of a liquid, or aëriform body, by means of a syringe, pump, etc.
The act of enjoining; the act of directing, commanding, or prohibiting.
To do harm to; to impair the excellence and value of; to hurt; to damage.
Any damage or violation of, the person, character, feelings, rights, property, or interests of an individual.
Want of justice and equity; violation of the rights of another or others.
A hint; an intimation. The least inkling or glimpse of this island. Bacon.
Within the land; more or less remote from the ocean or from open water.
A passage by which an inclosed place may be entered; a place of ingress.
Deepest within; farthest from the surface or external part; innermost.
Further in; interior; internal; not outward.
An innholder.
The state or quality of being innocent; freedom from that which is harmful or infurious.
Harmless; producing no ill effect; innocent. A patient, innocuous, innocent man.
The act of innovating; introduction of something new, in customs, rites, etc.
Characterized by, or introducing, innovations. Fitzed. Hall.
An oblique hint; a remote allusion or reference, usually derogatory to a person or thing not named.
Not capable of being counted, enumerated, or numbered, for multitude; countless.
Not opportune; inconvenient; unseasonable.
Not limited to rules prescribed, or to usual bounds; irregular; excessive.
Inquiry; quest; search. Spenser. The laborious and vexatious inquest that the soul must make after science.
Given to inquiry; disposed to investigate causes; curious.
The act of inquiring; a seeking for information by asking questions; interrogation.
Disposed to ask questions, especially in matters which do not concern the inquirer.
Not salubrious or healthful; unwholesome.
Without reason; madly; foolishly.
The state of being insane; unsoundness or derangement of mind; madness.
Not satiable; incapable of being satisfied or appeased; very greedy.
To write or engrave; to mark down as something to be read; to imprint.
The act or process of inscribing. 2. That which is inscribed; something written or engraved.
Unsearchable; incapable of being searched into and understood by inquiry or study.

One of the Insecta; esp., one of the Hexapoda. See Insecta. Note: The hexapod insects pass through three stages during their growth, viz., the larva, pupa, and imago or adult, but in some of the orders the larva...
Not secure; not confident of safety or permanence; distrustful; suspicious.
The condition or quality of being insecure; want of safety; danger; hazard.
Wanting sensibility; destitute of sense; stupid; foolish. The silence and the calm Of mute, insensate things.
Destitute of the power of feeling or perceiving; wanting bodily sensibility.
Not sensitive; wanting sensation, or wanting acute sensibility. Tillotson.
Not sentient; not having perception, or the power of perception. The .
Not separable; incapable of being separated or disjoined. The history of every language is inseparable from that of the people by whom it is spoken.
To set within something; to put or thrust in; to introduce; to cause to enter, or be included, or contained.
Situated upon, attached to, or growing out of, some part; -- said especially of the parts of the flower.
Lying in wait; watching an opportunity to insnare or entrap; deceitful.
A sight or view of the interior of anything; a deep inspection or view.
Distinguishing marks of authority, office, or honor; badges; tokens; decorations.
The condition or quality of being insignificant; want of significance, sense, or meaning.
Not significant; void of signification, sense, or import; meaningless.
To introduce gently or slowly, as by a winding or narrow passage, or a gentle, persistent movement.
Winding, creeping, or flowing in, quietly or stealthily; suggesting; winning favor and confidence insensibly.
lacking flavor, vigor, or interest; dull
characteristic: Picture a bowl of watery, flavorless soup, or a boring movie with no spark at all.

definition: The quality of insisting, or being urgent or pressing; the act of dwelling upon as of special importance.
Standing or resting on something.
The quality of being unusual or novel. Spenser. 2. The quality of being insolent.
Deviating from that which is customary; novel; strange; unusual. If one chance to derive any word from the Latin which is insolent to their ears .
The condition of being insolvent; the state or condition of a person who is insolvent.
Not solvent; not having sufficient estate to pay one's debts; unable to pay one's debts as they fall due, in the ordinary course of trade and business.
Want of sleep; inability to sleep; wakefulness; sleeplessness.
So; to such a degree; in such wise; -- followed by that or.
Carelessness; heedlessness; thoughtlessness; unconcern.
Careless; heedless; indifferent; unconcerned. J. S. Mill.
The act or process of inspecting or looking at carefully; a strict or prying examination.
One who inspects, views, or oversees; one to whom the supervision of any work is committed.
The act of inspiring or breathing in; breath; specif. (Physiol.), the drawing of air into the lungs, accomplished in mammals by elevation of the chest walls and flattening of the diaphragm.
Pertaining to inspiration.
Breathed in; inhaled. 2. Moved or animated by, or as by, a supernatural influence.
Animating; cheering; moving; exhilarating.
The quality or condition of being unstable; want of stability, firmness, or steadiness.
To set in a seat; to give a place to; establish (one) in a place. She installed her guest hospitably by the fireside.
The act of installing or giving possession of an office, rank, or order, with the usual rites or ceremonies.
Pressing; urgent; importunate; earnest. Rejoicing in hope; patient in tribulation.
Done or occurring in an instant, or without any perceptible duration of time.
Without the least delay or interval; at once; immediately. Macaulay. 2.
To set, place, or establish, as in a rank, office, or condition; to install.
To goad or urge forward; to set on; to provoke; to incite; -- used chiefly with reference to evil actions.
One who instigates or incites. Burke.
To drop in; to pour in drop by drop; hence, to impart gradually; to infuse slowly.
In an instinctive manner; by force of instinct; by natural impulse.
The act of instructing, teaching, or furnishing with knowledge; information.
Conveying knowledge; serving to instruct or inform.
One who instructs; one who imparts knowledge to another; a teacher.
That by means of which any work is performed, or result is effected; a tool.
Acting as an instrument; serving as a means; contributing to promote; conductive.
Not submitting to authority; disobedient; rebellious; mutinous.
Incapable of being suffered, borne, or endured; insupportable; unendurable.
The quality or state of being insufficient; want of sufficiency; deficiency.
Not sufficient; not enough; inadequate to any need, use, or purpose.
The state or quality of being an island or consisting of islands; insulation.
The act of insulating, or the state of being insulated; detachment from other objects.
Containing, or characterized by, insult or abuse; tending to insult or affront.
Incapable of being passed over or surmounted; insurmountable.
That can not be suppressed or concealed; irrepressible. Young. -- In`sup*press"i*bly, adv.
To make sure or secure.
A state of insurrection; an uprising; an insurrection. A moral insurgence in the minds of grave men against the Court of Rome.
Rising in opposition to civil or political authority, or against an established government.
Incapable of being passed over, surmounted, or overcome; insuperable.
A rising against civil or political authority, or the established government.
Untouched, especially by anything that harms, defiles, or the like; uninjured.
The place where water or air is taken into a pipe or conduit; -- opposed to outlet.
Not tangible; incapable of being touched; not perceptible to the touch.
Lacking nothing of completeness; complete; perfect; uninjured; whole; entire.
To form into one whole; to make entire; to complete; to renew; to restore.
The act or process of making whole or entire. 2. (Math.) The operation of finding the primitive function which has a given function for its differential coefficient.
The state or quality of being entire or complete; wholeness; entireness.
That which naturally invests or covers another thing, as the testa or the tegmen of a seed.
The part or faculty of the human soul by which it knows, as distinguished from the power to feel and to will.
Belonging to, or performed by, the intellect; mental.
In an intellectual manner.
Capable of being understood or comprehended.
The act of becoming, or state of being, intemperate; excess in any kind of action or indulgence.
Strained; tightly drawn; kept on the stretch; strict; very close or earnest.
To render more intense.
A straining, stretching, or bending; the state of being strained.
The state or quality of being intense; intenseness; extreme degree.
Stretched; admitting of intension, or increase of degree; that can be intensified.
Closely directed; strictly attentive; bent; -- said of the mind, thoughts, etc.
Done by intention or design; intended; designed.
In an intentional manner; with intention; by design; of purpose.
To deposit and cover in the earth; to bury; to inhume.
A short act or piece between others, as in a play; an interlude; hence, intermediate employment or time.
Intermediate action. 2. Mutual or reciprocal action or influence.
To take or seize by the way, or before arrival at the destined place; to cause to stop on the passage.
The act of interceding; mediation; interposition between parties at variance, with a view to reconcilation.
One who goes between, or intercedes; a mediator. (a) One who interposes between parties at variance, with a view to reconcile them.
A This sweet intercourse Of looks and smiles. Milton. Sexual intercourse, sexual or carnal connection.
To forbid; to prohibit or debar.
In an interesting manner.
To come in collision; to be in opposition; to clash.
The act or state of interfering.
The meantime; time intervening; interval between events, etc. All the interim is Like a phantasms, or a hideous dream.
Being within any limits, inclosure, or substance; inside; internal; inner.
The act of interjecting or throwing between; also, that which is interjected.
To unite, embrace, communicate with, or flow into, one another; to be connected in one system.
One who takes part in dialogue or conversation; a talker, interpreter, or questioner.
Consisting of, or having the nature of, dialogue; conversational. Interlocutory discourses in the Holy Scriptures.
One who interlopes; one who interlopes; one who unlawfully intrudes upon a property, a station, or an office.
A short entertainment exhibited on the stage between the acts of a play, or between the play and the afterpiece, to relieve the tedium of waiting.
Lying, coming, or done, between; intermediate.
Lying or being in the middle place or degree, or between two extremes.
The act or ceremony of depositing a dead body in the earth; burial; sepulture.
Without termination; admitting no limit; boundless; endless; wearisomely protracted.
To cause to cease for a time, or at intervals; to interrupt; to suspend.
Coming and going at intervals; alternating; recurrent; periodic.
Inwardly; within the enveloping surface, or the boundary of a thing; within the body.
Involving, or accompanied by, mutual slaughter; mutually destructive. Internecine quarrels, horrible tumults, stain the streets with blood.
Mutual action or influence; interaction.
The act of introducing or inserting anything, especially that which is spurious or foreign.
The act of interposing, or the state of being interposed; a being, placing, or coming between.
To explain or tell the meaning of; to expound; to translate orally into intelligible or familiar language or terms.
One who or that which interprets, explains, or expounds; a translator.
The time during which a throne is vacant between the death or abdication of a sovereign and the accession of his successor.
The act of interrogating or questioning; examination by questions; inquiry.
Denoting a question; expressed in the form of a question.
A formal question or inquiry; esp. (Law), a question asked in writing.
Broken; intermitted; suddenly stopped. 2. (Bot.) Irregular; -- said of any arrangement whose symmetry is destroyed by local causes, as when leaflets are interposed among the leaves in a pinnate leaf.
The act of interrupting, or breaking in upon. 2. The state of being interrupted.
To cut into or between; to cut or cross mutually; to divide into parts.
The act, state, or place of intersecting. 2. (Geom.) The point or line in which one line or surface cuts another.
Pertaining to the mutual relations of States; existing between, or including, different States.
Between or among the stars.
That which intervenes between one thing and another; especially, a space between things closely set, or between the parts which compose a body.
A space between things; a void space intervening between any two objects.
A tract of low ground between hills, or along the banks of a stream, usually alluvial land, enriched by the overflowings of the river, or by fertilizing deposits of earth from the adjacent hills.
The act of intervening; interposition. Sound is shut out by the intervention of that lax membrane.
One who interviews; especially, one who obtains an interview with another for the purpose of eliciting his opinions or obtaining information for publication.
The act or custom of holding an interview or interviews. An article on interviewing in the "Nation" of January 28, 1869, .
The state of being intestate, or of dying without having made a valid will.
Without having made a valid will; without a will.
The state of being intimate; close familiarity or association; nearness in friendship.
Innermost; inward; internal; deep-seated; hearty. "I knew from intimate impulse." Milton.
In an intimate manner.
The act of intimating; also, the thing intimated. 2. Announcement; declaration.
To make timid or fearful; to inspire of affect with fear; to deter, as by threats.
The act of making timid or fearful or of deterring by threats; the state of being intimidated.
Not tolerable; not capable of being borne or endured; not proper or right to be allowed.
Want of capacity to endure.
Not enduring; not able to endure. The powers of human bodies being limited and intolerant of excesses.
That which intoxicates; an intoxicating agent.
Intoxicated. 2. Overexcited, as with joy or grief. Alas, good mother, be not intoxicate for me.
Producing intoxication.
A poisoning, as by a spirituous or a narcotic substance. 2. The state of being intoxicated or drunk.
Within a cell.
Not tractable; not easily governed, managed, or directed; indisposed to be taught, disciplined, or tamed.
Being within the walls, as of a city. 2. (Anat. & Med.) Being within the substance of the walls of an organ.
unwilling or refusing to change one's views or to agree about something
characteristic: Think of a stubborn negotiator planted like a rock who will not budge an inch.

definition: Not trembling or shaking with fear; fearless; bold; brave; undaunted; courageous.
The state or quality of being intricate or entangled; perplexity; involution.
To form a plot or scheme; to contrive to accomplish a purpose by secret artifice.
Inward; internal; hence, true; genuine; real; essential; inherent; not merely apparent or accidental.
The act of introducing, or bringing to notice. 2. The act of formally making persons known to each other.
Serving to introduce something else; leading to the main subject or business.
The act of going in; entrance. Blount.
To send in or put in; to insert or introduce. Greenhill. 2. To allow to pass in.
To look into or within; to view the inside of. Bailey.
A view of the inside or interior; a looking inward; specifically, the act or process of self-examination, or inspection of one's own thoughts and feelings.
Inspecting within; seeing inwardly; capable of, or exercising, inspection.
The act of introverting, or the state of being introverted; the act of turning the mind inward.
To turn or bend inward. "Introverted toes." Cowper. 2. To look within.
One who intrudes; one who thrusts himself in, or enters without right, or without leave or welcome.
The act of intruding, or of forcing in; especially, the forcing (one's self) into a place without right or welcome.
Seeing clearly.
To cover with a flood; to overflow; to deluge; to flood.
The act of inundating, or the state of being inundated; an overflow; a flood.
To apply in use; to train; to discipline; to use or accustom till use gives little or no pain or inconvenience.
One who invades; an assailant; an encroacher; an intruder.
To render invalid; to weaken or lessen the force of; to destroy the authority of.
Valuable beyond estimation; inestimable; priceless; precious.
Not given to variation or change; unalterable; unchangeable; always uniform.
The act of invading; the act of encroaching upon the rights or possessions of another.
Tending to invade; characterized by invasion; aggressive. "Invasive war." Hoole.
Characterized by invection; critical; denunciatory; satirical; abusive.
To declaim or rail (against some person or thing); to utter censorious and bitter language.
To lead astray as if blind; to persuade to something evil by deceptive arts or flattery.
The act of finding out or inventing; contrivance or construction of that which has not before existed.
Able and apt to invent; quick at contrivance; ready at expedients.
One who invents or finds out something new; a contriver; especially, one who invents mechanical devices.
An account, catalogue, or schedule, made by an executor or administrator, of all the goods and chattels, and sometimes of the real estate, of a deceased person.
Opposite in order, relation, or effect; reversed; inverted; reciprocal.
The act of inverting, or turning over or backward, or the state of being inverted.
Changed to a contrary or counterchanged order; reversed; characterized by inversion.
To follow up step by step by patient inquiry or observation; to trace or track mentally.
Given to investigation; inquisitive; curious; searching.
One who searches diligently into a subject.
The act or ceremony of investing, or the of being invested, as with an office.
The act of investing, or the state of being invested. 2. That with which anyone is invested.
One who invests.
Old; long-established. It is an inveterate and received opinion. Bacon.
Envious; malignant. Evelyn. 2. Worthy of envy; desirable; enviable. Such a person appeareth in a far more honorable and invidious state than any prosperous man.
To give vigor to; to strengthen; to animate; to give life and energy to.
Incapable of being conquered, overcome, or subdued; unconquerable; insuperable.
Not violable; not susceptible of hurt, wound, or harm (used with respect to either physical or moral damage).
Not violated; uninjured; unhurt; unbroken. His fortune of arms was still inviolate.
The state or quality of being invisible; also, that which is invisible.
Alluring; tempting.
The act or form of calling for the assistance or presence of some superior being.
A written account of the particulars of merchandise shipped or sent to a purchaser, consignee, factor, etc., with the value or prices and charges annexed.
To call on for aid or protection; to invite earnestly or solemnly; to summon.
Not having will of the power of choice. 2. Not under the influence or control of the will.
The act of involving or infolding. 2. The state of being entangled or involved.
Same as Involute.
The act of involving, or the state of being involved. Lew Wallace.
Incapable of being wounded, or of receiving injury. Neither vainly hope To be invulnerable in those bright arms.
A nonmetallic element, of the halogen group, occurring always in combination, as in the iodides.
The ninth letter of the Greek alphabet (i) corresponding with the English i.
The native name of Persia.
Prone to anger; easily provoked or inflamed to anger; choleric; irritable.
Angry; incensed; enraged. The irate colonel . . . stood speechless. Thackeray.
Exhibition of colors like those of the rainbow; the quality or state of being iridescent.
Having colors like the rainbow; exhibiting a play of changeable colors.
The goddess of the rainbow, and swift-footed messenger of the gods. Shak.
A man born in Ireland or of the Irish race; an Hibernian. Irishman's hurricane (Naut.), a dead calm.
Wearisome; tedious; disagreeable or troublesome by reason of long continuance or repetition.
Clad in iron; protected or covered with iron, as a vessel for naval warfare.
Ironical. Sir T. Herbert.
Pertaining to irony; containing, expressing, or characterized by, irony.
The act or process of smoothing, as clothes, with hot flatirons. 2. The clothes ironed.
Made or consisting of iron; partaking of iron; iron.
The act of irradiating; emission of rays of light. 2. That which irradiates or is irradiated.
To throw rays of light upon; to illuminate; to brighten; to adorn with luster.
Not rational; void of reason or understanding.
Not reconcilable; implacable; incompatible; inconsistent; disagreeing.
Incapable of being reduced, or brought into a different state; incapable of restoration to its proper or normal condition.
Not refragable; not to be gainsaid or denied; not to be refuted or overthrown.
Not refrangible; that can not be refracted in passing from one medium to another.
Incapable of being refuted or disproved; indisputable. -- Ir`re*fut"a*ble*ness, n.
Not regular; not conforming to a law, method, or usage recognized as the general rule.
Destitute of religion; not controlled by religious motives or principles.
Not to be remedied, corrected, or redressed; incurable.
Not reparable; not capable of being repaired, recovered, regained, or remedied.
Not capable of being repressed, restrained, or controlled.
Not reproachable; above reproach; not deserving reproach; blameless. He  erred, -- and who is free from error -- but his intentions were irreproachable.
That can not be successfully resisted or opposed; superior to opposition.
Not resolute; not decided or determined; wavering; given to doubt or irresolution.
Nor responsible; not liable or able to answer fro consequences; innocent.
Not retrievable; irrecoverable; irreparable.
The state or quality of being irreverent; want of proper reverence; disregard of the authority and character of a superior.
Not reverent; showing a want of reverence; expressive of a want of veneration.
Incapable of being reversed or turned about or back; incapable of being made to run backward.
Incapable of being recalled or revoked; unchangeable; irreversible; unalterable.
To water; to wet; to moisten with running or dropping water; to bedew.
The act or process of irrigating, or the state of being irrigated; especially, the operation of causing water to flow over lands, for nourishing plants.
Capable of being irriated. 2. Very susceptible of anger or passion; easily inflamed or exasperated.
The state or quality of being null and void; invalidity; forfeiture. Burrill.
Rendering null and void; conditionally invalidating. The states elected Harry, Duke of Anjou, for their king, with this clause irritant.
The act of irritating, or exciting, or the state of being irritated; excitement.
A bursting in; a sudden, violent rushing into a place.
A brownish yellow color.
The principal goddess worshiped by the Egyptians. She was regarded as the mother of Horus, and the sister and wife of Osiris.
The religion of the Mohammedans; Mohammedanism; Islamism. Their formula of faith is: There is no God but Allah, and Mohammed is his prophet.
A little island.

A line connecting or marking places upon the surface of the earth where height of the barometer reduced to sea level is the same either at a given time, or for a certain period (mean height), as for a year.
To place in a detached situation; to place by itself or alone; to insulate.
Placed or standing alone; detached; separated from others. Isolated point of a curve.
The act of isolating, or the state of being isolated; insulation; separation.
Relating to equality of temperature. (b) (Phys. Geog.) Having reference to the geographical distribution of temperature, as exhibited by means of isotherms.
The act of passing or flowing out; a moving out from any inclosed place.
A neck or narrow slip of land by which two continents are connected, or by which a peninsula is united to the mainland.
To have an uneasy sensation in the skin, which inclines the person to scratch the part affected.
Infected with the itch, or with an itching sensation. Cowper.
Passing or traveling about a country; going or preaching on a circuit.
Itinerant; traveling; passing from place to place; done on a journey. It was rather an itinerary circuit of justice than a progress.
To wander without a settled habitation; to travel from place or on a circuit, particularly for the purpose of preaching, lecturing, etc.
The hard, white, opaque, fine-grained substance constituting the tusks of the elephant.
To talk rapidly, indistinctly, or unintelligibly; to utter gibberish or nonsense.
Any one of several species of carnivorous animals inhabiting Africa and Asia, related to the dog and wolf.
The male ass; a donkey. 2. A conceited dolt; a perverse blockhead. Jackass bark (Naut.), a three-masted vessel, with only the foremast square-rigged.
A landsman's nickname for a seaman, resented by the latter. (b) English gin.
Having jags; having rough, sharp notches, protuberances, or teeth; cleft.
A large and powerful feline animal (Felis onca), ranging from Texas and Mexico to Patagonia.
The keeper of a jail or prison.
One of the West India is islands. Jamaica ginger, a variety of ginger, called also white ginger, prepared in Jamaica from the best roots, which are deprived of their epidermis and dried separately.
To sound harshly or discordantly, as bells out of tune. 2. To talk idly.
Producing discordant sounds. "A jangling noise." Milton. 1. Idle babbling.
A door-keeper; a porter; one who has the care of a public building, or a building occupied for offices, suites of rooms, etc.
The first month of the year, containing thirty-one days. Note: Before the adoption of New Style, the commencement of the year was usually reckoned from March 25.
A Latin deity represented with two faces looking in opposite directions.
Confused, unintelligible language; gibberish; hence, an artificial idiom or dialect.
Shaking; disturbing; discordant. "A jarring sound." Dryden. 1. A shaking.
A shrubby plant of the genus Jasminum, bearing flowers of a peculiarly fragrant odor.
An opaque, impure variety of quartz, of red, yellow, and other dull colors, breaking with a smooth surface.

A morbid condition, characterized by yellowness of the eyes, skin, and urine, whiteness of the fæces, constipation, uneasiness in the region of the stomach, loss of appetite, and general languor and lassitude.
affected with jaundice. Jaundiced eyes seem to see all objects yellow.
Airy; showy; finical; hence, characterized by an affected or fantastical manner.
One of the islands of the Malay Archipelago belonging to the Netherlands.
The quality of being jealous; earnest concern or solicitude; painful apprehension of rivalship in cases nearly affecting one's happiness.
Mocking; scoffing. -- n. A mocking utterance. -- Jeer"ing*ly, adv.
A Scripture name of the Supreme Being, by which he was revealed to the Jews as their covenant God or Sovereign of the theocracy.
Lacking matter; empty; void of substance. 2. Void of interest; barren.
Anything brought to a gelatinous condition; a viscous, translucent substance in a condition between liquid and solid.
Any one of the acalephs, esp. one of the larger species, having a jellylike appearance.
name of contempt for a flatterer of persons high in social or official life.
To expose to loss or injury; to risk; to jeopard. That he should jeopardize his willful head Only for spite at me.
A tale of sorrow, disappointment, or complaint; a doleful story; a dolorous tirade.
The act of pulling, pushing, or throwing, with a jerk. -- Jerk"ing*ly, adv.
Moving by jerks and starts; characterized by abrupt transitions.
The finest of wool separated from the rest; combed wool; also, fine yarn of wool.
The chief city of Palestine, intimately associated with the glory of the Jewish nation, and the life and death of Jesus Christ.
A short strap of leather or silk secured round the leg of a hawk, to which the leash or line, wrapped round the falconer's hand, was attached when used.
A buffoon; a merry-andrew; a court fool. This . . . was Yorick's skull, the king's jester.
Goods which sink when cast into the sea, and remain under water; -- distinguished from flotsam, goods which float, and ligan, goods which are sunk attached to a buoy.
Goods which sink when cast into the sea, and remain under water; -- distinguished from flotsam, goods which float, and ligan, goods which are sunk attached to a buoy.
The throwing overboard of goods from necessity, in order to lighten a vessel in danger of wreck.
An ornament of dress usually made of a precious metal, and having enamel or precious stones as a part of its design.
One who makes, or deals in, jewels, precious stones, and similar ornaments.
The art or trade of a jeweler. Cotgrave. 2. Jewels, collectively.
A bold, vicious woman; a termagant. Spectator.
To shift, as the boom of a fore-and-aft sail, from one side of a vessel to the other when the wind is aft or on the quarter.
A moment; an instant.
To wriggle or frisk about; to move awkwardly; to shake up and down.
A religious war against infidels or Mohammedan heretics; also, any bitter war or crusade for a principle or belief.
A young woman; a sweetheart. See Gill. Beau. & Fl.
To sound with a fine, sharp, rattling, clinking, or tinkling sound.
The act or process of producing a jingle; also, the sound itself; a chink.
A word used as a jocular oath. "By the living jingo." Goldsmith. 2. A statesman who pursues, or who favors, aggressive, domineering policy in foreign affairs.
The policy of the Jingoes, so called. See Jingo, 2.
A person, object, influence, or supernatural being which is supposed to bring bad luck or to cause things to go wrong.
A professional rider of horses in races. Addison. 2. A dealer in horses.
Given to jokes and jesting; containing a joke, or abounding in jokes; merry.
Given to jesting; jocose.
Merry; cheerful; gay; airy; lively; sportive. Night's candles are burnt out, and jocund day Stands tiptoe on the misty mountain tops.
The act of giving a jog or jogs; traveling at a jog.
To shake slightly; to push suddenly but slightly, so as to cause to shake or totter.
A Portuguese gold coin of the value of eight dollars, named from the figure of King John which it bears;- often contracted into joe.
One who makes jokes or jests. 2. (Card Playing) See Rest bower, under 2d Bower.
Noisy mirth; gayety; merriment; festivity; boisterous enjoyment. Chaucer.
Full of life and mirth; jovial; joyous; merry; mirthful. Like a jolly troop of huntsmen.
To shake with short, abrupt risings and fallings, as a carriage moving on rough ground.
The Hebrew prophet, who was cast overboard as one who endangered the ship.
A Chinese household divinity; a Chinese idol. "Critic in jars and josses." Colman (1761).
To run against and shake; to push out of the way; to elbow; to hustle.
To enter or record in a journal or diary. Johnson. to conduct or contribute to a public journal.
To engage in mock combat on horseback, as two knights in the lists; to tilt.
Full of joy; having or causing joy; very glad.
Glad; gay; merry; joyful; also, affording or inspiring joy; with of before the word or words expressing the cause of joy.
Uttering songs of triumph; shouting with joy; triumphant; exulting. "The jubilant age." Coleridge.
A triumphant shouting; rejoicing; exultation. "Jubilations and hallelujahs." South.
The religious doctrines and rites of the Jews as enjoined in the laws of Moses.
The disciple who betrayed Christ. Hence: A treacherous person; one who betrays under the semblance of friendship.
