A spear armed with three or more prongs, for striking fish. One who makes a list or roll.
The act or process of one who lists (in any sense of the verb).
Having no desire or inclination; indifferent; heedless; spiritless. " A listless unconcern." Thomson.
A solemn form of supplication in the public worship of various churches, in which the clergy and congregation join, the former leading and the latter responding in alternate sentences.
A measure of capacity in the metric system, being a cubic decimeter, equal to 61.022 cubic inches, or 2.113 American pints, or 1.76 English pints.
State of being literate.
Learning; acquaintance with letters or books. 2. The collective body of literary productions, embracing the entire results of knowledge and fancy preserved in writing.
Pliant; limber; flexible; supple; nimble; lissom. -- Lithe"some*ness, n.
A metallic element of the alkaline group, occurring in several minerals, as petalite, spodumene, lepidolite, triphylite, etc., and otherwise widely disseminated, though in small quantities.
To trace on stone by the process of lithography so as to transfer the design to paper by printing.
A kind of stereotype plate made by lithotypy; also, that which in printed from it.
Disposed to litigate; contending in law; engaged in a lawsuit.
To make the subject of a lawsuit; to contest in law; to prosecute or defend by pleadings, exhibition of evidence, and judicial debate in a court.
The act or process of litigating; a suit at law; a judicial contest.
Inclined to judicial contest; given to the practice of contending in law.
A diminution or softening of statement for the sake of avoiding censure or increasing the effect by contrast with the moderation shown in the form of expression.
A measure of capacity in the metric system, being a cubic decimeter, equal to 61.022 cubic inches, or 2.113 American pints, or 1.76 English pints.
An established formula for public worship, or the entire ritual for public worship in a church which uses prescribed forms.
One who, or that which, lives. And try if life be worth the liver's care.
Black and blue; grayish blue; of a lead color; discolored, as flesh by contusion.
The American white mullet (Mugil curema).
Any one of the numerous species of reptiles belonging to the order Lacertilia.
A South American ruminant (Auchenia llama), allied to the camels, but much smaller and without a hump.
The act of putting a load on or into. 2. A load; cargo; burden. Shak.
Any thick lump, mass, or cake; especially, a large regularly shaped or molded mass, as of bread, sugar, or cake.
One who loafs; a lazy lounger. Lowell.
A kind of soil; an earthy mixture of clay and sand, with organic matter to which its fertility is chiefly due.
Hateful; odious; disliked. Chaucer. 2. Filled with disgust or aversion.
To feel extreme disgust at, or aversion for. Loathing the honeyed cakes, I Ionged for bread.
Fitted to cause loathing; exciting disgust; disgusting. The most loathsome and deadly forms of infection.
A passage or hall of communication, especially when large enough to serve also as a waiting room.
Any projection or division, especially one of a somewhat rounded form.
Any large macrurous crustacean used as food, esp. those of the genus Homarus.
With respect to place; in place.
To place; to set in a particular spot or position. The captives and emigrants whom he brought with him were located in the trans-Tiberine quarter.
Indicating place, or the place where, or wherein.
One who locates, or is entitled to locate, land or a mining claim.
One who, or that which, locks. 2. A drawer, cupboard, compartment, or chest, esp.
A small lock; a catch or spring to fasten a necklace or other ornament.
An artificer whose occupation is to make or mend locks.
A place where persons under arrest are temporarily locked up; a watchhouse.
A direction in written or printed music to return to the proper pitch after having played an octave higher.
The act of moving from place to place. " Animal locomotion." Milton. 2.
Moving from place to place; changing place, or able to change place.
A water course or way; a reach of water. Down that long, dark lode . .
A shelter in which one may rest; as: (a) A shed; a rude cabin; a hut.
Lying down; -- used of beasts of the chase, as couchant is of beasts of prey.
One who, or that which, lodges; one who occupies a hired room in another's house.
The act of one who, or that which, lodges. 2. A place of rest, or of temporary habitation.
Lifted high up; having great height; towering; high. See lofty Lebanon his head advance.
A rocking or balanced stone. Gwill.
Made slow and heavy in movement; water-logged. Beaconsfield.
The business of felling trees, cutting them into logs, and transporting the logs to sawmills or to market.
The science or art of exact reasoning, or of pure and formal thought, or of the laws according to which the processes of pure thinking should be conducted.
In a logical manner.
A person skilled in logic. Bacon. Each fierce logician still expelling Locke.
That branch of the military art which embraces the details of moving and supplying armies.
That part of a human being or quadruped, which extends on either side of the spinal column between the hip bone and the false ribs.
The evil deity, the author of all calamities and mischief, answering to the African of the Persians.
To act lazily or indolently; to recline; to lean; to throw one's self down.
A kind of sugar confection which dissolves easily in the mouth. Thackeray.
The condition of being lonely; solitude; seclusion. 2. The state of being unfrequented by human beings.
Secluded from society; not frequented by human beings; solitary. Like one that on a lonesome road Doth walk in fear and dread.
Long duration of life; length of life. The instances of longevity are chiefly amongst the abstemious.
An eager desire; a craving; a morbid appetite; an earnest wish; an aspiration.
Length; measure or distance along the longest line; -- distinguished from breadth or thickness.
One who looks. Looker-on, a spectator; one that looks on, but has no agency or part in an affair.
A careful looking or watching for any object or event. 2. The place from which such observation is made.
The indistinct and magnified appearance of objects seen in particular states of the atmosphere.

A sorry fellow; a worthless person; a rogue. Any one of several aquatic, wed-footed, northern birds of the genus Urinator (formerly Colymbus), noted for their expertness in diving and swimming under water.
A mass of iron in a pasty condition gathered into a ball for the tilt hammer or rolls.
A small opening, as in the walls of fortification, or in the bulkhead of a ship, through which small arms or other weapons may be discharged at an enemy.
In a loose manner.
To make loose; to free from tightness, tension, firmness, or fixedness.
The act of plundering. 2. Plunder; booty; especially, the boot taken in a conquered or sacked city.
Leaning to one side because of some defect of structure.
tending to talk a great deal; very talkative
characteristic: Think of someone who never stops chatting, filling every silence with a flood of words.

definition: A little or insignificant lord. Goldsmith.
The state or condition of being a lord; hence (with his or your), a title applied to a lord (except an archbishop or duke, who is called Grace) or a judge (in Great Britain), etc.
A small cart or wagon, as those used on the tramways in mines to carry coal or rubbish.
A washing, especially of the skin for the purpose of rendering it fair.
A game of chance, played with cards, on which are inscribed numbers, and any contrivance (as a wheel containing numbered balls) for determining a set of numbers by chance.
A name of several kinds of water lilies; as Nelumbium speciosum, used in religious ceremonies, anciently in Egypt, and to this day in Asia.
In a loud manner. Denham.
A loch or lake; -- so spelt in Ireland. of Laugh. Chaucer. LOUIS D'OR Lou"is d'or`.
To spend time lazily, whether lolling or idly sauntering; to pass time indolently.
Any one of numerous species of small, wingless, suctorial, parasitic insects belonging to a tribe (Pediculina), now usually regarded as degraded Hemiptera.
Infested with lice. 2. Mean; contemptible.
Clownish; rude; awkward. "Loutish clown." Sir P. Sidney. -- Lout"ish*ly, adv.
A small lantern. See Lantern, 2 (a)  Louver boards or boarding, the sloping boards set to shed rainwater outward in openings which are to be left otherwise unfilled.
Having qualities that excite, or are fitted to excite, love; worthy of love.
With love; affectionately.
Dark and threatening; gloomy; sullen.
Not high; not elevated in place; low. "Lowly lands." Dryden. 2. Low in rank or social importance.
An open box car used on railroads. Compare Lorry.
Faithful to law; upholding the lawful authority; faithful and true to the lawful government.
The state or quality of being loyal; fidelity to a superior, or to duty, love, etc.
Lubricating. That which lubricates; specifically, a substance, as oil, grease, plumbago, etc., used for reducing the friction of the working parts of machinery.
To make smooth or slippery.
Smoothness; freedom from friction; also, property, which diminishes friction.
A pike when full grown. Halliwell.
Shining; bright; resplendent. " The sun's lucent orb." Milton.
Shining; bright; resplendent.
The planet Venus, when appearing as the morning star; -- applied in Isaiah by a metaphor to a king of Babylon.
In a lucky manner; by good fortune; fortunately; -- used in a good sense.
Yielding lucre; gainful; profitable; making increase of money or goods.
Gain in money or goods; profit; riches; -- often in an ill sense. The lust of lucre and the dread of death.
The act of lucubrating, or studying by candlelight; nocturnal study; meditation.
Adapted to excite laughter, without scorn or contempt; sportive. Broome.
That which is lugged; anything cumbrous and heavy to be carried; especially, a traveler's trunks, baggage, etc., or their contents.
Mournful; indicating sorrow, often ridiculously or feignedly; doleful.
Moderately warm; neither cold nor hot; tepid; not ardent; not zealous.
To cause to rest by soothing influences; to compose; to calm; to soothe.
Of, pertaining to, or near, the loins.
A pawnbroker's shop, or room for storing articles put in pawn; hence, a pledge, or pawn.
A unit of illumination, being the amount of illumination of a unit area of spherical surface, due to a light of unit intensity placed at the center of the sphere.
Any body that gives light, especially one of the heavenly bodies. " Radiant luminary." Skelton.

Any emission of light not ascribable directly to incandescence, and therefore occurring at low temperatures, as in phosphorescence and fluorescence or other luminous radiation resulting from vital processes, chemical...
Shining with a light due to any of the various causes which produce luminescence.
The quality or state of being luminous; luminousness.
Shining; emitting or reflecting light; brilliant; bright.
A small mass of matter of irregular shape; an irregular or shapeless mass.
Full of lumps, or small compact masses.
The moon. 2. (Alchemy) Silver. Luna cornea (Old Chem.), horn silver, or fused silver chloride, a tough, brown, translucent mass.
Insanity or madness; properly, the kind of insanity which is broken by intervals of reason, -- formerly supposed to be influenced by the changes of the moon.
Affected by lunacy; insane; mad. Lord, have mercy on my son; for he is lunatic.
An organ for aërial respiration; -- commonly in the plural. My lungs began to crow like chanticleer.
A cutaneous disease occurring under two distinct forms. Note: Lupus erythematosus is characterized by an eruption of red patches, which become incrusted, leaving superficial scars.
A contrivance somewhat resembling a bird, and often baited with raw meat.
Pale yellow; ghastly pale; wan; gloomy; dismal. Fierce o'er their beauty blazed the lurid flame.
To lie hid; to lie in wait. Like wild beasts, lurking in loathsome den.
Full of juice or succulence. Tennyson. How lush and lusty the grass looks! how green! Shak.
One who lusts. A period of five years; a lustrum. Both of us have closed the tenth luster.
Bright; shining; luminous. " Good sparks and lustrous." Shak. -- Lus"trous*ly, adv.
A cement of clay or other tenacious infusible substance for sealing joints in apparatus, or the mouths of vessels or tubes, or for coating the bodies of retorts, etc., when exposed to heat.
The state or quality of being luxuriant; rank, vigorous growth; excessive abundance produced by rank growth.
Exuberant in growth; rank; excessive; very abundant.
To grow exuberantly; to grow to superfluous abundance. " Corn luxuriates in a better mold." Burton.
To inflict punishment upon, especially death, without the forms of law, as when a mob captures and hangs a suspected person.
A stringed instrument of music; a kind of harp much used by the ancients, as an accompaniment to poetry.
To cover, as a road, or street, with small, broken stones, so as to form a smooth, hard, convex surface.
A money of account in China equal to one tenth of a tael; also, a weight of 57.98 grains.
To make lean; to cause to waste away. Harvey. 2. To subdue the appetites of by poor and scanty diet.

A large heavy knife resembling a broadsword, often two or three feet in length, -- used by the inhabitants of Spanish America as a hatchet to cut their way through thickets, and for various other purposes.
To plan; to contrive; esp., to form a scheme with the purpose of doing harm.
The act of machinating. Shak. 2. That which is devised; a device; a hostile or treacherous scheme.
Machines, in general, or collectively. 2. The working parts of a machine, engine, or instrument.
A constrictor of machines and engines; one versed in the principles of machines.
The striped mullet of California (Mugil cephalus, or Mexicanus).
The great world; that part of the universe which is exterior to man; -- contrasted with microcosm, or man.
To spot; to stain; to blur. Maculate the honor of their people. Sir T.
Having spots or blotches; maculate.
To make mad; to drive to madness; to craze; to excite violently with passion.
A plant of the Rubia (R. tinctorum). The root is much used in dyeing red, and formerly was used in medicine.
A French title of courtesy given to a girl or an unmarried lady, equivalent to the English Miss.
The barn owl. (b) The magpie.
A house where insane persons are confined; an insane asylum; a bedlam.
In a mad manner; without reason or understanding; wildly.
A man who is mad; lunatic; a crazy person. When a man mistakes his thoughts for person and things, he is mad.
The condition of being mad; insanity; lunacy. 2. Frenzy; ungovernable rage.
My lady; -- a term of address in Italian formerly used as the equivalent of Madame, but for which Signora is now substituted.
A large silk-and-cotton kerchief, usually of bright colors, such as those often used by negroes for turbans.
A little amorous poem, sometimes called a pastoral poem, containing some tender and delicate, though simple, thought.
A celebrated whirlpool on the coast of Norway. 2. Also Fig.
A master in any art, especially in music; a composer.
A secret society which organized in Sicily as a political organization, but is now widespread among Italians, and is used to further or protect private interests, reputedly by illegal methods.
The footless larva of any fly. See Larval. 2. A whim; an odd fancy. Hudibras.
Pertaining to the hidden wisdom supposed to be possessed by the Magi; relating to the occult powers of nature, and the producing of effects by their agency.
In a magical manner; by magic, or as if by magic.
One skilled in magic; one who practices the black art; an enchanter; a necromancer.
The office or dignity of a magistrate. Blackstone. 2. The collective body of magistrates.
A person clothed with power as a public civil officer; a public civil officer invested with the executive government, or some branch of it.
Any crude mixture of mineral or organic matters in the state of a thin paste.
The quality of being magnanimous; greatness of mind; elevation or dignity of soul.
very generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival or less powerful person
characteristic: Picture a champion who graciously praises the opponent they just defeated.

definition: A person of rank; a noble or grandee; a person of influence or distinction in any sphere.
A light silver-white metallic element, malleable and ductile, quite permanent in dry air but tarnishing in moist air.
The loadstone; a species of iron ore (the ferrosoferric or magnetic ore, Fe3O4) which has the property of attracting iron and some of its ores, and, when freely suspended, of pointing to the poles.
The property, quality, or state, of being magnetic; the manifestation of the force in nature which is seen in a magnet.
To communicate magnetic properties to.
The act of doing what magnificent; the state or quality of being magnificent.
Doing grand things; admirable in action; displaying great power or opulence, especially in building, way of living, and munificence.
Speaking pompously; using swelling discourse; bombastic; tumid in style.
Extent of dimensions; size; -- applied to things that have length, breath, and thickness.
A large wine bottle. They passed the magnum to one another freely. Sir W.
Any one of numerous species of the genus Pica and related genera, allied to the jays, but having a long graduated tail.
A large tree of the genus Swietenia (S. Mahogoni), found in tropical America.
The state of being a maid or a virgin; virginity. Shak. 2. Newness; freshness.
Protected by an external coat, or covering, of scales or plates. Spotted.
To deprive of the use of a limb, so as to render a person on fighting less able either to defend himself or to annoy his adversary.
One of the New England States. Maine law, any law prohibiting the manufacture and sale of intoxicating beverages, esp.
The continent; the principal land; -- opposed to island, or peninsula.
To hold or keep in any particular state or condition; to support; to sustain.
The act of maintaining; sustenance; support; defense; vindication. Whatsoever is granted to the church for God's honor and the maintenance of his service, is granted to God.
A large species of American grass of the genus Zea (Z. Mays), widely cultivated as a forage and food plant.
Possessing or exhibiting majesty; of august dignity, stateliness, or imposing grandeur.
That with which one makes shift; a temporary expedient. James Mill. I am not a model clergyman, only a decent makeshift.
A lemur. See Lemur.
Evils; wrongs; offenses against right and law. Mala in se Etym:  (Law), offenses which are such from their own nature, at common law, irrespective of statute.
Any disease of the human body; a distemper, disorder, or indisposition, proceeding from impaired, defective, or morbid organic functions.
An indefinite feeling of uneasiness, or of being sick or ill at ease.
A grotesque misuse of a word; a word so used.
Unseasonable or unseasonably; unsuitable or unsuitably.
Air infected with some noxious substance capable of engendering disease.
discontented; uneasy; dissatisfied; especially, dissatisfied with the government.
A proclaiming of evil against some one; a cursing; imprecation; a curse or execration.
Doing evil to others; harmful; mischievous.
Doing evil, harm, or mischief.
The quality or state of being malevolent; evil disposition toward another.
Wishing evil; disposed to injure others; rejoicing in another's misfortune.
The doing of an act which a person ought not to do; evil conduct; an illegal deed.
Enmity of heart; malevolence; ill will; a spirit delighting in harm or misfortune to another.
Indulging or exercising malice; harboring ill will or enmity. I grant him bloody, .
Having an evil disposition toward others; harboring violent enmity; malevolent.
Disposed to do harm, inflict suffering, or cause distress; actuated by extreme malevolence or enmity.
To act the part of a malingerer; to feign illness or inability.
In the army, a soldier who feigns himself sick, or who induces or protracts an illness, in order to avoid doing his duty.
A drake; the male of Anas boschas. 2. (Zoöl.) A large wild duck (Anas boschas) inhabiting both America and Europe.
Capable of being extended or shaped by beating with a hammer, or by the pressure of rollers.
A small maul with a short handle, -- used esp. for driving a tool, as a chisel or the like.
An Offensive to the sense of smell; ill-smelling. -- Mal*o"dor*ous*ness.
Offensive to the sense of smell; ill-smelling. -- Mal*o"dor*ous*ness. n.
Evil practice; illegal or immoral conduct; practice contrary to established rules.
Barley or other grain, steeped in water and dried in a kiln, thus forcing germination until the saccharine principle has been evolved.
To treat ill; to abuse; to treat roughly.
Mother; -- word of tenderness and familiarity. Tell tales papa and mamma.
One of the Mammalia. Age of mammals. See under Age, n., 8.
An extinct, hairy, maned elephant (Elephas primigenius), of enormous size, remains of which are found in the northern parts of both continents.
A child's name for mamma, mother.
A handcuff; a shackle for the hand or wrist; -- usually in the plural.
Such as can be managed or used; suffering control; governable; tractable.
The act or art of managing; the manner of treating, directing, carrying on, or using, for a purpose.
A Chinese public officer or nobleman; a civil or military official in China and Annam.
An official or authoritative command; an order or injunction; a commission.
Containing a command; preceptive; directory. Same as Mandatary.
The long and heavy hair growing on the upper side of, or about, the neck of some quadrupedal animals, as the horse, the lion, etc.
Management; dexterous movement; specif., a military or naval evolution, movement, or change of position.
The scab or itch in cattle, dogs, and other beasts. Mange insect (Zoöl.), any one of several species of small parasitic mites, which burrow in the skin of cattle.
A trough or open box in which fodder is placed for horses or cattle to eat.
To cut or bruise with repeated blows or strokes, making a ragged or torn wound, or covering with wounds.
The fruit of the mango tree. It is rather larger than an apple, and of an ovoid shape.
Infected with the mange; scabby.
A hole through which a man may descend or creep into a drain, sewer, steam boiler, parts of machinery, etc., for cleaning or repairing.
The state of being man as a human being, or man as distinguished from a child or a woman.
Violent derangement of mind; madness; insanity. Cf. Delirium. 2. Excessive or unreasonable desire.
Raving with madness; raging with disordered intellect; affected with mania.
Affected with, or characterized by, madness; maniac. -- Ma*ni"a*cal*ly, adv.
A person who makes a business of taking care of people's hands, especially their nails.
Evident to the senses, esp. to the sight; apparent; distinctly perceived.
The act of manifesting or disclosing, or the state of being manifested.

A public declaration, usually of a prince, sovereign, or other person claiming large powers, showing his intentions, or proclaiming his opinions and motives in reference to some act done or contemplated by him.
Various in kind or quality; many in number; numerous; multiplied; complicated.
To treat, work, or operate with the hands, especially when knowledge and dexterity are required.
The act or process of manipulating, or the state of being manipulated.
The human race; man, taken collectively. The proper study of mankind is man.
Like man, or like a man, in form or nature; having the qualities of a man, esp.
The quality or state of being manly.
Having qualities becoming to a man; not childish or womanish; manlike, esp.
Having a certain way, esp a. polite way, of carrying and conducting one's self.
Adherence to a peculiar style or manner; a characteristic mode of action, bearing, or treatment, carried to excess, especially in literature or art.
The muller, or crushing and grinding stone, used in grinding corn on a metate.
Management; dexterous movement; specif., a military or naval evolution, movement, or change of position.
The land belonging to a lord or nobleman, or so much land as a lord or great personage kept in his own hands, for the use and subsistence of his family.
The slaying of a human being; destruction of men. Milton. 2. (Law) The unlawful killing of a man, either in negligenc.
The finish around a fireplace, covering the chimney-breast in front and sometimes on both sides.
Any one of numerous species of voracious orthopterous insects of the genus Mantis, and allied genera.
A loose garment to be worn over other garments; an enveloping robe; a cloak.
A prayer; an invocation; a religious formula; a charm. Note: Among the Hindoos each caste and tribe has a mantra peculiar to itself.
The operation of making wares or any products by hand, by machinery, or by other agency.
One who manufactures.
Employed, or chiefly employed, in manufacture.
The act of manumitting, or of liberating a slave from bondage. "Given to slaves at their manumission." Arbuthnot.
To release from slavery; to liberate from personal bondage or servitude.
Written with or by the hand; not printed.

A tree of the genus Acer, including about fifty species. A. saccharinum is the rock maple, or sugar maple, from the sap of which sugar is made, in the United States, in great quantities, by evaporation.
The principal or ruling evil spirit. E. Arnold. A female demon who torments people in sleep by crouching on their chests or stomachs, or by causing terrifying visions.
To rove in quest of plunder; to make an excursion for booty; to plunder.
A rover in quest of booty or plunder; a plunderer; one who pillages. De Quincey.
A massive, compact limestone; a variety of calcite, capable of being polished and used for architectural and ornamental purposes.
The refuse matter which remains after the pressure of fruit, particularly of grapes.
,fr. March, v. Marching money (Mil.), the additional pay of officer or soldier when his regiment is marching.
The female of the horse and other equine quadrupeds. Sighing, suffocative panting, intercepted utterance, with a sense of pressure across the chest, occurring during sleep.
Border; margin; edge; verge. Tennyson. Along the river's stony marge. Wordsworth.
A border; edge; brink; verge.
The daisy (Bellis perennis). The name is often applied also to the ox-eye daisy and to the China aster.
Pertaining to the Virgin Mary, or sometimes to Mary, Queen of England, daughter of Henry VIII.
A name for several plants with golden yellow blossoms, especially the Calendula officinalis (see Calendula), and the cultivated species of Tagetes.
Bordering on, or situated near, the ocean; connected with the sea by site, interest, or power.
Designated or distinguished by, or as by, a mark; hence; noticeable; conspicuous.
One who or that which marks. Specifically: (a) One who keeps account of a game played, as of billiards.
The act of selling or of purchasing in, or as in, a market. 2. Articles in, or from, a market.
The act of one who, or that which, marks; the mark or marks made; arrangement or disposition of marks or coloring.
One skillful to hit a mark with a missile; one who shoots well. 2. (Law) One who makes his mark, instead of writing his name, in signing documents.
The American great marbled godwit (Limosa fedoa). Applied also to the red-breasted godwit (Limosa hæmatica).
A preserve or confection made of the pulp of fruit, as the quince, pear, apple, orange, etc., boiled with sugar, and brought to a jamlike consistence.
In the West Indies and Guiana, a fugitive slave, or a free negro, living in the mountains.
A nobleman in England, France, and Germany, of a rank next below that of duke.
The wife of a marquis; a marchioness.
A general term for certain kinds of fabrics, which are formed of two series of threads interlacing each other, thus forming double cloth, quilted in the loom.
A tract of soft wet land, commonly covered partially or wholly with water.
Having a pouch for carrying the immature young; of or pertaining to the Marsupialia.
Of, pertaining to, or suited for, war; military.
In military language, a strict disciplinarian; in general, one who lays stress on a rigid adherence to the details of discipline, or to forms and fixed methods.
One who, by his death, bears witness to the truth of the gospel; one who is put to death for his religion.
The condition of a martyr; the death of a martyr; the suffering of death on account of adherence to the Christian faith, or to any cause.
Exciting wonder or surprise; astonishing; wonderful. This is the Lord's doing.
A person who is supposed to bring good luck to the household to which he or she belongs.
The state or quality of being masculine; masculineness.
Wearing a mask or masks; characterized by masks; cincealed; hidden. 2.
One whose occupation is to build with stone or brick; also, one who prepares stone for building purposes.
A rubbing or kneading of the body, especially when performed as a hygienic or remedial measure.
One who performs massage.
One who performs massage.
Forming, or consisting of, a large mass; compacted; weighty; heavy; massy.
In a heavy mass.
The fruit of the oak and beech, or other forest trees; nuts; acorns. Oak mast, and beech, .
Anything done or made with extraordinary skill; a capital performance.
To grind or crush with, or as with, the teeth and prepare for swallowing and digestion.
Onanism; self-pollution.

The killer; the man appointed to kill the bull in bullfights. 2. (Card Playing) In the game of quadrille or omber, the three principal trumps, the ace of spades being the first, the ace of clubs the third, and the...
One who makes matches for burning or kinding. 2. One who tries to bring about marriages.
The doctrine of materialists; materialistic views and tenets. The irregular fears of a future state had been supplanted by the materialism of Epicurus.
To invest wich material characteristics; to make perceptible to the senses.
The state of being a mother; the character or relation of a mother.
One versed in mathematics.

That science, or class of sciences, which treats of the exact relations existing between quantities or magnitudes, and of the methods by which, in accordance with these relations, quantities sought are deducible from...
A reception, or a musical or dramatic entertainment, held in the daytime.
The mother and ruler of a family or of her descendants; a ruler by maternal right.
The murder of a mother by her son or daughter. 2. Etym:  One who murders one's own mother.
To enroll; to enter in a register; specifically, to enter or admit to membership in a body or society, particularly in a college or university, by enrolling the name in a register.
The union of man and woman as husband and wife; the nuptial state; marriage.
The womb. All that openeth the matrix is mine. Ex. xxxiv. 19. 2. Hence, that which gives form or origin to anything.
A wife or a widow, especially, one who has borne children; a woman of staid or motherly manners.
A quilted bed; a bed stuffed with hair, moss, or other suitable material, and quilted or otherwise fastened.
Brought by natural process to completeness of growth and development; fitted by growth and development for any function, action, or state, appropriate to its kind.
The state or quality of being mature; ripeness; full development.
A gray plaid; -- used by shepherds in Scotland.
Tearful; easily moved to tears; exciting to tears; excessively sentimental.
A heavy wooden hammer or beetle. 1. To beat and bruise with a heavy stick or cudgel.
To beg. B. Jonson. Beau. & Fl. 2. To mutter; to mumble; to grumble; to speak indistinctly or disconnectedly.
A magnificent tomb, or stately sepulchral monument.

A color of a delicate purple, violet, or lilac. Mauve aniline (Chem.), a dyestuff produced artificially by the oxidation of commercial aniline, and the first discovered of the so-called coal- tar, or aniline, dyes.
In the southwestern part of the united States, a bullock or heifer that has not been branded, and is unclaimed or wild.
The European throstle or song thrush (Turdus musicus).
Apt to cause satiety or loathing; nauseous; disgusting. So sweetly mawkish', and so smoothly dull.
An established principle or proposition; a condensed proposition of important practical truth.
A gold coin of Bavaria, of the value of about 13s. 6d. sterling, or about three dollars and a quarter.
To increase to the highest degree. Bentham.
The greatest quantity or value attainable in a given case; or, the greatest value attained by a quantity which first increases and then begins to decrease.

Designating, or pertaining to, an American Indian linguistic stock occupying the Mexican States of Veracruz, Chiapas, Tabasco, Campeche, and Yucatan, together with a part of Guatemala and a part of Salvador.
The maiming of a person by depriving him of the use of any of his members which are necessary for defense or protection.
A sauce compounded of raw yolks of eggs beaten up with olive oil to the consistency of a sirup, and seasoned with vinegar, pepper, salt, etc.
A fermented drink made of water and honey with malt, yeast, etc.; metheglin.
A tract of low or level land producing grass which is mown for hay; any field on which grass is grown for hay.
A winding, crooked, or involved course.
The intervening time.
The intervening time.
Infected with measles. 2. (Zoöl.) Containing larval tapeworms; -- said of pork and beef.
A standard of dimension; a fixed unit of quantity or extent; an extent or quantity in the fractions or multiples of which anything is estimated and stated.
Regulated or determined by a standard; hence, equal; uniform; graduated.
The act or result of measuring; mensuration.
Used in, or adapted for, ascertaining measurements, or dividing by measure.
That science, or branch of applied mathematics, which treats of the action of forces on bodies.
The arrangement or relation of the parts of a machine; the parts of a machine, taken collectively.
A piece of metal in the form of a coin, struck with a device, and intended to preserve the remembrance of a notable event or an illustrious person, or to serve as a reward.
A large medal or memorial coin. 2. A circular or oval (or, sometimes, square) tablet bearing a figure or figures represented in relief.
Given to meddling; apt to interpose in the affairs of others; officiously intrusive.
Meddlesome. Macaulay.
Being between the two extremes; middle; interposed; intervening; intermediate.
The act of mediating; action or relation of anything interposed; action as a necessary condition, means, or instrument.
One who mediates; especially, one who interposes between parties at variance for the purpose of reconciling them.
In a medical manner; with reference to healing, or to the principles of the healing art.
The act or process of medicating.
Having curative or palliative properties; used for the cure or alleviation of bodily disorders.
To keep the mind in a state of contemplation; to dwell on anything in thought.
The Gorgon; or one of the Gorgons whose hair was changed into serpents, after which all who looked upon her were turned into stone.
Combining forms signifying: (a) Great, extended, powerful.
A device to magnify sound, or direct it in a given direction in a greater volume, as a very large funnel used as an ear trumpet or as a speaking trumpet.
Given to melancholy; depressed; melancholy; dejected; unhappy. Just as the melancholic eye Sees fleets and armies in the sky.
Depression of spirits; a gloomy state continuing a considerable time; deep dejection.
A mixture; a medley.
A fight in which the combatants are mingled.
To make better; to improve; to ameliorate; to soften; to make more tolerable.
The doctrine that there is a tendency throughout nature toward improvement.
Flowing as with honey; smooth; flowing sweetly or smoothly.
Soft or tender by reason of ripeness; having a tender pulp.
Containing, or producing, melody; musical; agreeable to the ear by a sweet succession of sounds.
Formerly, a kind of drama having a musical accompaniment to intensify the effect of certain scenes.
A sweet or agreeable succession of sounds. Lulled with sound of sweetest melody.
The juicy fruit of certain cucurbitaceous plants, as the muskmelon, watermelon, and citron melon.
Honey of roses.
Liquefaction; the act of causing (something) to melt, or the process of becoming melted.
The state of being a member. 2. The collective body of members, as of a society.
A thin layer or fold of tissue, usually supported by a fibrous network, serving to cover or line some part or organ, and often secreting or absorbing certain fluids.
A hint, suggestion, token, or memorial, to awaken memory; that which reminds or recalls to memory.
A memorial account; a history composed from personal experience and memory.
A memorial account; a history composed from personal experience and memory.
Things remarkable and worthy of remembrance or record; also, the record of them.
Worthy to be remembered; very important or remarkable. -- Mem"o*ra*ble*ness, n.
To address or petition by a memorial; to present a memorial to.
The show of an intention to inflict evil; a threat or threatening; indication of a probable evil or catastrophe to come.
A piace where animals are kept and trained. 2. A collection of wild or exotic animals, kept for exhibition.
To repair, as anything that is torn, broken, defaced, decayed, or the like.
Given to deception or falsehood; lying.
The quality or state of being mendacious; a habit of lying. Macaulay. 2.
Practicing beggary; begging; living on alms.
Belonging to a retinue or train of servants; performing servile office.
Inflammation of the membranes of the brain or spinal cord. Cerebro-spinal meningitis.
The period of natural cessation of menstruation. See Change of life, under Change.
Quality or state of mind. "The same hard mentality." Emerson.
In the mind; in thought or meditation; intellectually; in idea.
A wise and faithful counselor or monitor.
The details of a banquet; a bill of fare.
Acting for reward; serving for pay; paid; hired; hireling; venal.
Originally, a dealer in any kind of goods or wares; now restricted to a dealer in textile fabrics, as silks or woolens.
The objects of commerce; whatever is usually bought or sold in trade, or market, or by merchants.
Full of mercy; having or exercising mercy; disposed to pity and spare offenders.
Destitute of mercy; cruel; unsparing; -- said of animate beings, and also, figuratively, of things.
Having the qualities fabled to belong to the god Mercury; swift; active.
Purely; unmixedly; absolutely. Ulysses was to force forth his access, Though merely naked.
To cause to be swallowed up; to immerse; to sink; to absorb. To merge all natural ...
One who, or that which, merges. 2. (Law) An absorption of one estate, or one contract, in another, or of a minor offense in a greater.
A delicate pastry made of powdered sugar and the whites of eggs whipped up, -- with jam or cream added.
Possessing merit; deserving of reward or honor; worthy of recompense; valuable.
The European blackbird. See Blackbird. Drayton.
A small European falcon (Falco lithofalco, or F. æsalon).
A fabled marine creature, typically represented as having the upper part like that of a woman, and the lower like a fish.
In a merry manner; with mirth; with gayety and laughter; jovially. See Mirth, and Merry.
A high tableland; a plateau on a hill. Bartlett.
The opening or space inclosed by the threads of a net between knot and knot, or the threads inclosing such a space.
The art of inducing an extraordinary or abnormal state of the nervous system, in which the actor claims to control the actions, and communicate directly with the mind, of the recipient.
To bring into a state of mesmeric sleep.
One who bears a message; the bearer of a verbal or written communication, notice, or invitation, from one person to another, or to a public body.
The expected king and deliverer of the Hebrews; the Savior; Christ. And told them the Messiah now was born.
Sirs; gentlemen; -- abbreviated to Messrs., which is used as the plural of Mr.

The act or process, by which living tissues or cells take up and convert into their own proper substance the nutritive material brought to them by the blood, or by which they transform their cell protoplasm into...
The art of working metals, comprehending the whole process of separating them from other matters in the ore, smelting, refining, and parting them.
To change into a different form; to transform; to transmute. And earth was metamorphosed into man.
Change of form, or structure; transformation. 2. (Biol.) A change in the form or function of a living organism, by a natural process of growth or development.
The transference of the relation between one set of objects to another set for the purpose of brief explanation.
One who is versed in metaphysics.

The science of real as distinguished from phenomenal being; ontology; also, the science of being, with reference to its abstract and universal conditions, as distinguished from the science of determined or concrete...
The passage of the soul, as an immortal essence, at the death of the animal body it had inhabited, into another living body, whether of a brute or a human being.
Any phenomenon or appearance in the atmosphere, as clouds, rain, hail, snow, etc.
A mass of stone or iron which has fallen to the earth from space; an aërolite.
The science which treats of the atmosphere and its phenomena, particularly of its variations of heat and moisture, of its winds, storms, etc.
A light, colorless, gaseous, inflammable hydrocarbon, CH4; marsh gas. See Marsh gas, under Gas.
It seems to me; I think. See Me. In all ages poets have been had in special reputation, and, methinks, not without great cause.
An orderly procedure or process; regular manner of doing anything; hence, manner.
Arranged with regard to method; disposed in a suitable manner, or in a manner to illustrate a subject, or to facilitate practical observation.
Timid; fearful. -- Me*tic"u*lous*ly, adv.
A trope in which one word is put for another that suggests it.
Relating to measurement; involving, or proceeding by, measurement. 2. Of or pertaining to the meter as a standard of measurement.
An instrument consisting of a short pendulum with a sliding weight. It is set in motion by clockwork, and serves to measure time in music.
The mother city; the chief city of a kingdom, state, or country. gray metropolis of the North.
Substance or quality of temperament; spirit, esp. as regards honor, courage, fortitude, ardor, etc.
Full of spirit; possessing constitutional ardor; fiery.
An alley where there are stables; a narrow passage; a confined place. Mr.
Infectious particles or germs floating in the air; air made noxious by the presence of such particles or germs.
pl of Mouse.
A little world; a miniature universe. Hence (so called by Paracelsus), a man, as a supposed epitome of the exterior universe or great world.
An instrument, used with a telescope or microscope, for measuring minute distances, or the apparent diameters of objects which subtend minute angles.

An instrument for intensifying and making audible very feeble sounds. It produces its effects by the changes of intensity in an electric current, occasioned by the variations in the contact resistance of conducting...
An optical instrument, consisting of a lens, or combination of lenses, for making an enlarged image of an object which is too minute to be viewed by the naked eye.
The use of the microscope; investigation with the microscope.
The middle part of the day; noon. Of or pertaining to noon; meridional.
An agent between two parties; a broker; a go-between; any dealer between the producer and the consumer.
Any one of many small, delicate, long-legged flies of the Chironomus, and allied genera, which do not bite.
A minute bloodsucking fly. 2. A very diminutive person.
The middle of summer. Shak. Midsummer daisy (Bot.), the oxeye daisy.
The middle of the way or distance; a middle way or course. Shak. Paths indirect, or in the midway faint.
A woman who assists other women in childbirth; a female practitioner of the obstetric art.
Aspect; air; manner; demeanor; carriage; bearing. Vice is a monster of so frightful mien, As, to be hated, needs but to be seen.
A petty falling out; a tiff; a quarrel; offense. Fielding. To offend slightly.
Migratory. Sir T. Browne. -- n. A migratory bird or other animal.
To remove from one country or region to another, with a view to residence.
The act of migrating.
Removing regularly or occasionally from one region or climate to another.
The popular designation of the hereditary sovereign of Japan.
Lit., my lady; hence (as used on the Continent), an English noblewoman or gentlewoman.
Gentle; pleasant; kind; soft; bland; clement; hence, moderate in degree or quality.
In a mild manner.
A certain measure of distance, being equivalent in England and the United States to 320 poles or rods, or 5,280 feet.
An allowance for traveling expenses at a certain rate per mile. 2. Aggregate length or distance in miles.
A stone serving the same purpose as a milepost.
Environment. The intellectual and moral milieu created by multitudes of self- centered, cultivated personalities.
Engaged in warfare; fighting; combating; serving as a soldier. -- Mil"i*tant*ly, adv.
A military state or condition; reliance on military force in administering government.
To make war; to fight; to contend; -- usually followed by against and with.

A white fluid secreted by the mammary glands of female mammals for the nourishment of their young, consisting of minute globules of fat suspended in a solution of casein, albumin, milk sugar, and inorganic salts.
A man who sells milk or delivers is to customers.
Consisting of, or containing, milk. Pails high foaming with a milky flood.
A money of account of the United States, having the value of the tenth of a cent, or the thousandth of a dollar.
A thousand years; especially, thousand years mentioned in the twentieth chapter in the twentieth chapter of Revelation, during which holiness is to be triumphant throughout the world.
A lineal measure in the metric system, containing the thousandth part of a meter.
One whose wealth is counted by millions of francs, dollars, or pounds.
Lit., my lord; hence (as used on the Continent), an English nobleman or gentleman.
The spleen. (a) The spermatic fluid of fishes. (b) The testes, or spermaries, of fishes when filled with spermatozoa.
Imitative; mimetic. Oft, in her absence, mimic fancy wakes To imitate her.
The act or practice of one who mimics; ludicrous imitation for sport or ridicule.
An ancient weight or denomination of money, of varying value. The Attic mina was valued at a hundred drachmas.
A slender, lofty tower attached to a mosque and surrounded by one or more projecting balconies, from which the summon to prayer is cried by the muezzin.
Threatening; menacing. Bacon.
That minces; characterized by primness or affected nicety.
Disposed; inclined; having a mind. Joseph... was minded to put her away privily.
Bearing in mind; regardful; attentive; heedful; observant. What is man, that thou art mindful of him Ps.
Regard; mindfulness.
Not indued with mind or intellectual powers; stupid; unthinking. 2. Unmindful.
One who mines; a digger for metals, etc.; one engaged in the business of getting ore, coal, or precious stones, out of the earth.
The goddess of wisdom, of war, of the arts and sciences, of poetry, and of spinning and weaving.
Of, pertaining to, or having a character of, a minim or minimum; least.
To reduce to the smallest part or proportion possible; to reduce to a minimum.
The least quantity assignable, admissible, or possible, in a given case.
The act or business of making mines or of working them. Of or pertaining to mines.
The act of ministering; service; ministry. "The days of his ministration." Luke i.
The act of ministering; ministration; service. "With tender ministry." Thomson.
A carnivorous mammal of the genus Putorius, allied to the weasel. The European mink is Putorius lutreola.
Inferior in bulk, degree, importance, etc.; less; smaller; of little account.
A fabled monster, half man and half bull, confined in the labyrinth constructed by Dædalus in Crete.
Less; requiring to be subtracted; negative.
Any very small, minute object. 2. A small Roman letter which is neither capital nor uncial.
The sixtieth part of an hour; sixty seconds. (Abbrev. m.
A minute particular; a small or minor detail; -- used chiefly in the plural.
A remarkable variable star in the constellation Cetus (o Ceti).
An optical effect, sometimes seen on the ocean, but more frequently in deserts, due to total reflection of light at the surface common to two strata of air differently heated.
Merriment; gayety accompanied with laughter; jollity. Then will I cause to cease ...
The common title of honor in Persia, prefixed to the surname of an individual.
Mischance; misfortune; ill lick; unlucky accident; ill adventure. Chaucer.
A hater of mankind; a misanthropist.
Hating or disliking mankind.
Hatred of, or dislike to, mankind; -- opposed to philanthropy. Orrery.
To take in a wrong sense; to misunderstand. Locke.
To appropriate wrongly; to use for a wrong purpose.
To behave ill; to conduct one's self improperly; -- often used with a reciprocal pronoun.
Improper, rude, or uncivil behavior; ill conduct. Addison.
Unfortunate event or issue of an undertaking; failure to attain a desired result or reach a destination.
A mixing of races; amalgamation, as by intermarriage of black and white.
Mixed; mingled; consisting of several things; of diverse sorts; promiscuous.
Ill luck; ill fortune; mishap. Chaucer. Never come mischance between us twain.
Causing mischief; harmful; hurtful; -- now often applied where the evil is done carelessly or in sport.
Erroneous conception; false opinion; wrong understanding. Harvey.
Wrong conduct; bad behavior; mismanagement. Addison. Syn. -- Misbehavior.
To construe wrongly; to interpret erroneously. Do not, great sir, misconstrue his intent.
To count erroneously. An erroneous counting.
One who holds a false religious faith; a misbeliever. Spenser. De Quincey.
An evil deed; a wicked action. Evils which our own misdeeds have wrought.
Ill behavior; evil conduct; fault. Shak. 2. (Law) A crime less than a felony.
A wretched person; a person afflicted by any great misfortune. Spenser.
In a miserable; unhappily; calamitously; wretchedly; meanly. They were miserably entertained.
Like a miser; very covetous; sordid; niggardly. Syn. -- Avaricious; niggardly.
Evil premonition; doubt; distrust. "Suspicious and misgivings." South.
To interpret erroneously; to understand or to explain in a wrong sense.
To lay in a wrong place; to ascribe to a wrong source. The fault is generally mislaid upon nature.
To lead into a wrong way or path; to lead astray; to guide into error.
Leading astray; delusive.
To manage ill or improperly.
The misnaming of a person in a legal instrument, as in a complaint or indictment.
A woman hater. Fuller.
Hatred of women. Johnson.
To put in a wrong place; to set or place on an improper or unworthy object.
To read amiss; to misunderstand in reading.
To represent incorrectly (almost always, unfacorably); to give a false erroneous representation of, either maliciously, ignirantly, or carelessly.
To rule badly; to misgovern. 1. The act, or the result, of misruling. 2.
The book containing the service of the Mass for the entire year; a Mass book.
Capable of being thrown; adapted for hurling or to be projected from the hand, or from any instrument or rngine, so as to strike an object at a distance.
One who is sent on a mission; especially, one sent to propagate religion.
Specially sent; intended or prepared to be sent.
Visible watery vapor suspended in the atmosphere, at or near the surface of the earth.
Being in error; judging wrongly; having a wrong opinion or a misconception.
By mistake. Goldsmith.
An error; a mistake. Shak.
A parasitic evergreen plant of Europe (Viscum album), bearing a glutinous fruit.
A false or erroneous trial; a trial which has no result.
Want of confidence or trust; suspicion; distrust. Milton. 1. To regard with jealousy or suspicion.
Accompained with mist; characterized by the presence of mist; obscured by, or overspread with, mist.
To misconceive; to mistake; to miscomprehend; to take in a wrong sense.
A minute arachnid, of the order Acarina, of which there are many species.
A covering for the head, worn on solemn occasions by church dignitaries.
to make less severe, serious, or painful
characteristic: Like a cushion softening a fall, it eases and lessens something harmful.

definition: A mitten; also, a covering for the wrist and hand and not for the fingers.
One who, or that which, mixes.
The act of mixing, or the state of being mixed.
Assisting in memory.
The art of memory; a system of precepts and rules intended to assist the memory.
A deep trench around the rampart of a castle or other fortified place, sometimes filled with water.
The quality or state of being mobile.
To put in a state of readiness for active service in war, as an army corps.
A condition in which the lower classes of a nation control public affairs without respect to law, precedents, or vested rights.
A shoe made of deerskin, or other soft leather, the sole and upper part being one piece.
A seaport town of Arabia, on the Red Sea. 2. A variety of coffee brought from Mocha.
The act of mocking, deriding, and exposing to contempt, by mimicry, by insincere imitation, or by a false show of earnestness.
Imitating, esp. in derision, or so as to cause derision; mimicking; derisive.
By way of derision; in a contemptuous or mocking manner.
Manner of doing or being; method; form; fashion; custom; way; style.
The act or art of making a model from which a work of art is to be executed.
Kept within due bounds; observing reasonable limits; not excessive, extreme, violent, or rigorous.
The act of moderating, or of imposing due restraint. 2. The state or quality of being mmoderate.
One who, or that which, moderates, restrains, or pacifies. Sir W. Raleigh.
Modernness; something modern. Walpole.
To render modern; to adapt to modern person or things; to cause to conform to recent or present usage or taste.
Restraining within due limits of propriety; not forward, bold, boastful, or presumptious.
A little; a small quantity; a measured simply. "Modicums of wit." Shak.
The act of modifying, or the state of being modified; a modified form or condition.
To change somewhat the form or qualities of; to alter somewhat.
According to the mode, or customary manner; conformed to the fashion; fashionable.
To form, as sound, to a certain key, or to a certain portion. 2. To vary or inflect in a natural, customary, or musical manner.
A person of the Mongolian race. 2. (Railroad) A heavy locomotive for freight traffic, having three pairs of connected driving wheels and a two-wheeled truck.
One of two equal parts; a half.
The deity who assigns to every man his lot.
A moderate degree of wetness. Bacon. 2. That which moistens or makes damp or wet.
The thick, brown or dark colored, viscid, uncrystallizable sirup which drains from sugar, in the process of manufacture.
The act or process of shaping in or on a mold, or of making molds; the art or occupation of a molder.
Overgrown with, or containing, mold.
Pertaining to, connected with, produced by, or consisting of, molecules.
One of the very small invisible particles of which all matter is supposed to consist.
One who molests.
Minor; in the minor mode.
To soften; to make tender; to reduce the hardness, harshness, or asperity of.
The fire god of the Ammonites in Canaan, to whom human sacrifices were offered.
Melted; being in a state of fusion, esp. when the liquid state is produced by a high degree of heat.
A fabulous herb of occult power, having a black root and white blossoms, said by Homer to have been given by Hermes to Ulysses to counteract the spells of Circe.
A minute portion of time; a point of time; an instant.
Every moment; from moment to moment. Shenstone.
Done in a moment; continuing only a moment; lasting a very short time.
The quantity of motion in a moving body, being always proportioned to the quantity of matter multiplied into the velocity.
A small, handsome, long-tailed West American monkey (Cercopithecus mona).
A sole or supreme ruler; a sovereign; the highest ruler; an emperor, king, queen, prince, or chief.
A state or government in which the supreme power is lodged in the hands of a monarch.
A house of religious retirement, or of secusion from ordinary temporal concerns, especially for monks.
A monk. 1. Of or pertaining to monasteries, or to their occupants, rules, etc.,.
One of the Mongols. -- a. Of or pertaining to Mongolia or the Mongols.
One of the great races of man, including the greater part of the inhabitants of China, Japan, and the interior of Asia, with branches in Northern Europe and other parts of the world.
A species of ichneumon (Herpestes griseus), native of India. Applied also to other allied species, as the African banded mongoose (Crossarchus fasciatus).
The progeny resulting from a cross between two breeds, as of domestic animals.
Instruction or advice given by way of caution; an admonition; a warning.
One who admonishes; one who warns of faults, informs of duty, or gives advice and instruction by way of reproof or caution.
Giving admonition; instructing by way of caution; warning. Losses, miscarriages, and disappointments, are monitory and instructive.
A man who retires from the ordinary temporal concerns of the world, and devotes himself to religion.
The black howler of Central America (Mycetes villosus).
Consisting of one color, or presenting rays of light of one color only.
A painting or drawing in a single color; a picture made with a single color.
An eyeglass for one eye. Simmonds.
Government by a single person; undivided rule. Sydney Smith.
Upholding, or practicing, monogamy. 2. (Bot.) Same as Monogamian. 3. (Zoöl.) Mating with but one of the opposite sex.
Single marriage; marriage with but one person, husband or wife, at the same time.
A written account or description of a single thing, or class of things.
A single stone, especially one of large size, shaped into a pillar, statue, or monument.
A speech uttered by a person alone; soliloquy; also, talk or discourse in company, in the strain of a soliloquy.
Derangement of the mind in regard of a single subject only; also, such a concentration of interest upon one particular subject or train of ideas to show mental derangement.
The exclusive power, or privilege of selling a commodity; the exclusive power, right, or privilege of dealing in some article, or of trading in some market.
A word of one syllable.
The doctrine or belief that there is but one God.
A single unvaried tone or sound. 2. (Rhet.) The utterance of successive syllables, words, or sentences, on one unvaried key or line of pitch.
Uttered in one unvarying tone; continued with dull uniformity; characterized by monotony.
A frequent recurrence of the same tone or sound, producing a dull uniformity.
An oxide containing one atom of oxygen in each molecule.
A wind blowing part of the year from one direction, alternating with a wind from the opposite direction.
The state of being monstrous, or out of the common order of nature; that which is monstrous.
A favorite gambling game among Spaniards, played with dice or cards.
Something which stands, or remains, to keep in remembrance what is past.
Of, pertaining to, or suitable for, a monument.
The quality or state of being moody; specifically, liability to strange or violent moods.
Subject to varying moods, especially to states of mind which are unamiable or depressed.
A ray of light from the moon.
The light of the moon. -- a. Occurring during or by moonlight; characterized by moonlight.
Illumined by the moon. "The moonlit sea." Moore. "Moonlit dells." Lowell.
A large cervine mammal (Alces machlis, or A. Americanus), native of the Northern United States and Canada.
The moral condition, or the condition in other respects, so far as it is affected by, or dependent upon, moral considerations, such as zeal, spirit, hope, and confidence.
One who moralizes; one who teaches or animadverts upon the duties of life.
To apply to a moral purpose; to explain in a moral sense; to draw a moral from.
In a moral or ethical sense; according to the rules of morality. By good, good morally so called, "bonum honestum" ought chiefly to be understood.
A tract of soft, wet ground; a marsh; a fen. Morass ore. (Min.) See Bog ore, under Bog.
A period during which an obligor has a legal right to delay meeting an obligation, esp.
Not sound and healthful; induced by a diseased or abnormal condition; diseased.
Biting; given to biting; hence, figuratively, sarcastic; severe; scathing.
Biting; caustic; sarcastic; keen; severe. 2. (Dyeing & Calico Printing) Serving to fix colors.
Beyond what has been said; further; besides; in addition; furthermore.
Customs; habits; esp., customs conformity to which is more or less obligatory.

Pertaining to, in the manner of, or designating, a kind of marriage, called also left-handed marriage, between a man of superior rank and a woman of inferior, in which it is stipulated that neither the latter nor her...
A place where the bodies of persons found dead are exposed, that they may be identified, or claimed by their friends.
In a dying state; dying; at the point of death. The patient was comatose and moribund.
The first part of the day; the morning; -- used chiefly in poetry. From morn To noun he fell, from noon to dewy eve.
A small abscess or tumor having a resemblance to a mulberry. Dunglison.
A fine kind of leather, prepared commonly from goatskin (though an inferior kind is made of sheepskin), and tanned with sumac and dyed of various colors.
A person whose intellectual development proceeds normally up to about the eighth year of age and is then arrested so that there is little or no further development.
A bitter white crystalline alkaloid found in opium, possessing strong narcotic properties, and much used as an anodyne.
That branch of biology which deals with the structure of animals and plants, treating of the forms of organs and describing their varieties, homologies, and metamorphoses.
A Moorish dance, usually performed by a single dancer, who accompanies the dance with castanets.
The walrus. See Walrus. A clasp for fastening garments in front. Fairholt.
A little bite or bit of food. Chaucer. Every morsel to a satisfied hunger is only a new labor to a tired digestion.
Subject to death; destined to die.
The condition or quality of being mortal; subjection to death or to the necessity of dying.
A conveyance of property, upon condition, as security for the payment of a debt or the preformance of a duty, and to become void upon payment or performance according to the stipulated terms.
A sort of ecclesiastical heriot, a customary gift claimed by, and due to, the minister of a parish on the death of a parishioner.
A surface decoration made by inlaying in patterns small pieces of variously colored glass, stone, or other material.
A large flatboat, used in the West Indies for taking freight from shore to ship.
A Mohammedan church or place of religious worship.
Any one of various species of gnats of the genus Culex and allied genera.
A cryptogamous plant of a cellular structure, with distinct stem and simple leaves.
The state of being a mother; the character or office of a mother.
The country of one's ancestors; -- same as fatherland.
Having powers of self-motion, though unconscious.
Capability of motion; contractility.
Without motion; being at rest.
To provide with a motive; to move; impel; induce; incite. -- Mo`ti*va"tion (#), n.
Movement; manner of movement; particularly, movement with increased rapidity.
One who, or that which, imparts motion; a source of mechanical power. 2.
A bicycle having a motor attached so as to be self-propelled. In Great Britain the term motor cycle is treated by statute (3 Ed VII.
To mark with spots of different color, or shades of color, as if stained.
Marked with spots of different colors; variegated; spotted.
A sentence, phrase, or word, forming part of an heraldic achievment. 2.
A ball or globe forming part of the regalia of an emperor or other sovereign.
Seated or serving on horseback or similarly.
The act of one that mounts. 2. That by which anything is prepared for use, or set off to advantage.
To express or to feel grief or sorrow; to grieve; to be sorrowful; to lament.
Full of sorrow; expressing, or intended to express, sorrow; mourning; grieving.
The act of sorrowing or expressing grief; lamentation; sorrow. 2. Garb, drapery, or emblems indicative of grief, esp.
A frozen dessert of a frothy texture, made of sweetened and flavored whipped cream, sometimes with the addition of egg yolks and gelatin.
As much as is usually put into the mouth at one time. 2. Hence, a small quantity.
The part of a musical or other instrument to which the mouth is applied in using it.
The act of moving; change of place or posture; transference, by any means, from one situation to another.
A person or thing that moves, stirs, or changes place. 2. A person or thing that imparts motion, or causes change of place.
One who, or that which, mows; a mowing machine.
The act of one who, or the operation of that which, mows. 2. Land from which grass is cut.
Mud poured out from volcanoes during eruptions; -- so called in South America.
, abbreviation of Amuck. To run a muck. See Amuck. 1. Dung in a moist state.
A viscid fluid secreted by mucous membranes, which it serves to moisten and protect.
Abounding in mud; besmeared or dashed with mud.
A light, spongy, cylindrical cake, used for breakfast and tea.
The bare end of the nose between the nostrils; -- used esp. of ruminants.
Anything used in muffling; esp., a scarf for protecting the head and neck in cold weather.
The common crocodile (Crocodilus palustris) of India, the East Indies, etc.
Moist; damp; moldy.
A bolter from the Republican party in the national election of 1884; an Independent.
The offspring of a negress by a white man, or of a white woman by a negro, -- usually of a brownish yellow complexion.
The berry or fruit of any tree of the genus Morus; also, the tree itself.
A fine or penalty, esp. a pecuniary punishment or penalty. 2. A blemish or defect.
A hybrid animal; specifically, one generated between an ass and a mare, sometimes a horse and a she-ass.
One who drives mules.
Like a mule; sullen; stubborn. -- Mul"ish*ly, adv. -- Mul"ish*ness, n.
One who, or that which, mulls. 2. A vessel in which wine, etc., is mulled over a fire.
Having multiplicity; having great diversity or variety; of various kinds.
Having many forms, shapes, or appearances. A plastic and multiform unit.
Containing more than once, or more than one; consisting of more than one.
The quality of being multiple, manifold, or various; a state of being many.
A great number of persons collected together; a numerous collection of persons.
To speak with the lips partly closed, so as to render the sounds inarticulate and imperfect.
Low; indistinct; inarticulate. -- Mum"bling*ly, adv.
To chew with a grinding, crunching sound, as a beast chews provender; to chew deliberately or in large mouthfuls.
lacking interest or excitement; dull and ordinary

definition: A municipal district; a borough, city, or incorporated town or village.
Very liberal in giving or bestowing; lavish.
The state of being murky.
Dark; obscure; gloomy. "The murkiest den." Shak. A murky deep lowering o'er our heads.
A low, confused, and indistinct sound, like that of running water. 2. A complaint half suppressed, or uttered in a low, muttering voice.
Uttering murmurs; making low sounds; complaining. -- Mur"mur*ing*ly, adv.
A potato. Thackeray.
An infectious and fatal disease among cattle. Bacon. A murrain on you, may you be afflicted with a pestilent disease.
An organ which, by its contraction, produces motion. See Illust. of Muscles of the Human Body, in Appendix.
Meal (esp. Indian meal) boiled in water; hasty pudding; supawn. To notch, cut, or indent, as cloth, with a stamp.
An edible fungus (Agaricus campestris), having a white stalk which bears a convex or oven flattish expanded portion called the pileus.
Soft like mush; figuratively, good-naturedly weak and effusive; weakly sentimental.
In a musical manner.
One skilled in the art or science of music; esp., a skilled singer, or performer on a musical instrument.
A substance of a reddish brown color, and when fresh of the consistence of honey, obtained from a bag being behind the navel of the male musk deer.
The male of the sparrow hawk. 2. A species of firearm formerly carried by the infantry of an army.
Having an odor of musk, or somewhat the like. Milton.
That part of the beard which grows on the upper lip; hair left growing above the mouth.
The half-wild horse of the plains in Mexico, California, etc. It is small, hardy, and easily sustained.
Having the rank, pungent, offencive odor and taste which substances of organic origin acquire during warm, moist weather.
The quality of being mutable, or subject to change or alteration, either in form, state, or essential character.
Capable of alteration; subject to change; changeable in form, qualities, or nature.
Change; alteration, either in form or qualities. The vicissitude or mutations in the superior globe are no fit matter for this present argument.
To cast off; to molt. Have I muted all my feathers Beau. & Fl. To eject the contents of the bowels.
Deprived of, or having lost, an important part; mutilated. Sir T. Browne.
The act of mutilating, or the state of being mutilated; deprivation of a limb or of an essential part.
Disposed to mutiny; in a state of mutiny; characterized by mutiny; seditious.
Insurrection against constituted authority, particularly military or naval authority.
To utter words indistinctly or with a low voice and lips partly closed.
Reciprocally acting or related; reciprocally receiving and giving; reciprocally given and received.
In a mutual manner.
Pertaining to, or affected with, or characterized by, myopia; nearsighted.
The number of ten thousand; ten thousand persons or things. 2. An immense number.
A species of the genus Myrtus, especially Myrtus communis. The common myrtle has a shrubby, upright stem, eight or ten feet high.
In a mysterious manner.
Remote from or beyond human comprehension; baffling human understanding.
Remote from or beyond human comprehension; baffling human understanding.
The act of mystifying, or the state of being mystied; also, something designed to, or that does, mystify.
A story of great but unknown age which originally embodied a belief regarding some fact or phenomenon of experience, and in which often the forces of nature and of the soul are personified.
The science which treats of myths; a treatise on myths. 2. A body of myths.
That point of the heavens, or lower hemisphere, directly opposite the zenith.
Fault-finding; teasing; persistently annoying.
Having native or unaffected simplicity; ingenuous; artless; frank.
Native simplicity; unaffected plainness or ingenuousness; artlessness.
, n. Naïveté. Carlyle.
Without a name; not having been given a name.
Any one of three species of South American ostriches of the genera Rhea and Pterocnemia.
A diminutive of Ann or Anne, the proper name. Nanny goat, a female goat.
The complex mixture of volatile, liquid, inflammable hydrocarbons, occurring naturally, and usually called crude petroleum, mineral oil, or rock oil.
A French gold coin of twenty francs, or about $3.86.
The act or process of raising a nap, as on cloth. 2. (Hat Making) A sheet of partially felted fur before it is united to the hat body.
Having the properties of a narcotic; operating as a narcotic. -- Nar*cot"ic*ness, n.
To tell, rehearse, or recite, as a story; to relate the particulars of.
The act of telling or relating the particulars of an event; rehearsal.
One who narrates; one who relates a series of events or transactions.
Commencing, or in process of development; beginning to exist or to grow.
The act of floating on the water; swimming. Sir T. Browne.
One who advocates national unity and independence; one of a party favoring Irish independence.
The quality of being national, or strongly attached to one's own nation.
The coming into life or into the world; birth; also, the circumstances attending birth, as time, place, manner, etc.
Neat; tidy; spruce. -- Nat"ti*ly, adv. -- Nat"ti*ness, n.
In a natural manner or way; according to the usual course of things; spontaneously.
Seasickness; hence, any similar sickness of the stomach accompanied with a propensity to vomit.
To become squeamish; to feel nausea; to turn away with disgust. 1. To affect with nausea.
Causing, or fitted to cause, nausea; sickening; loathsome; disgusting.
The only existing genus of tetrabranchiate cephalopods. About four species are found living in the tropical Pacific, but many other species are found fossil.
Having to do with shipping; of or pertaining to ships or a navy; consisting of ships.
The block in the center of a wheel, from which the spokes radiate, and through which the axle passes.
A mark or depression in the middle of the abdomen; the umbilicus. See Umbilicus.belly button in humans 2.
Capable of being navigated; deep enough and wide enough to afford passage to vessels.
To joirney by water; to go in a vessel or ship; to perform the duties of a navigator.
The act of navigating; the act of passing on water in ships or other vessels.
One who navigates or sails; esp., one who direct the course of a ship, or one who is skillful in the art of navigation.
A deputy ruler or viceroy in India; also, a title given by courtesy to other persons of high rank in the East.
Of, pertaining to, or named from, the Neanderthal, a valley in the Rhine Province, in which were found parts of a skeleton of an early type of man.
The tongue or pole of a cart or other vehicle drawn by two animals. Low.
Cattle of the genus Bos, as distinguished from horses, sheep, and goats.
In a neat manner; tidily; tastefully.
Cloudy; hazy; misty. 2. (Astron.) Of, pertaining to, or having the appearance of, a nebula.
In a necessary manner; by necessity; unavoidably; indispensably.
Such as must be; impossible to be otherwise; not to be avoided; inevitable.

The quality or state of being necessary, unavoidable, or absolutely requisite.
A string of beads, etc., or any continuous band or chain, worn around the neck as an ornament.
A scarf, band, or kerchief of silk, etc., passing around the neck or collar and tied in front.
An account of deaths, or of the dead; a register of deaths; a collection of obituary notices.
One who practices necromancy; a sorcerer; a wizard.
The art of revealing future events by means of a pretended communication with the dead.
Mortification or gangrene of bone, or the death of a bone or portion of a bone in mass, as opposed to its death by molecular disintegration.

Work executed with a needle; sewed work; sewing; embroidery; also, the business of a seamstress.

definition: The act of denying; assertion of the nonreality or untruthfulness of anything.

Not to attend to with due care or attention; to forbear one's duty in regard to.

That may neglicted, disregarded, or left out of consideration. Within very negligible limits of error.
Capable of being negotiated; transferable by assigment or indorsement to another person.
One who negotiates; a person who treats with others, either as principal or agent, in respect to purchase and sale, or public compacts.

A black man; especially, one of a race of black or very dark persons who inhabit the greater part of tropical Africa, and are distinguished by crisped or curly hair, flat noses, and thick protruding lips.
Living or being near; adjacent.
The goddess of retribution or vengeance; hence, retributive justice personified.

A new convert or proselyte; -- a name given by the early Christians, and still given by the Roman Catholics, to such as have recently embraced the Christian faith, and been admitted to baptism, esp.

One of the whitish and elastic bundles of fibers, with the accompanying tissues, which transmit nervous impulses between nerve centers and various parts of the animal body.

Situated down or below; lying beneath, or in the lower part; having a lower position.

A fabric of threads, cords, or wires crossing each other at certain intervals, and knotted or secured at the crossings, thus leaving spaces or meshes between them.

Not engaged on either side; not taking part with or assisting either of two or more contending parties.

Not the less; notwithstanding; in spite of that; yet. No chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous.
Recently born. Shak.
One who has lately come.

A room where news is collected and disseminated, or periodicals sold; a reading room supplied with newspapers, magazines, etc.
Any one of several species of small aquatic salamanders. The common British species are the crested newt (Triton cristatus) and the smooth newt (Lophinus punctatus).
Connection; tie. Man is doubtless one by some subtile nexus ... extending from the new-born infant to the superannuated dotard.
To bite by little at a time; to seize gently with the mouth; to eat slowly or in small bits.
In a nice manner.
The quality or state of being nice (in any of the senses of that word.).

Meanly covetous or avarcious in dealing with others; stingy; niggard. Where the owner of the house will be bountiful, it is not for the steward to be niggardly.
A negro; -- in vulgar derision or depreciation.
A cap worn in bed to protect the head, or in undress. 2. A potion of spirit drank at bedtime.

A loose gown used for undress; also, a gown used for a sleeping garnment.

Nothingness; nihility. 2. The doctrine that nothing can be known; scepticism as to all knowledge and all reality.

Light and quick in motion; moving with ease and celerity; lively; swift.
Nine and ten; eighteen and one more; one less than twenty.
Nine times ten; eighty-nine and one more.
A fool; a simpleton. Shak.
Following the eight and preceding the tenth; coming after eight others.

In the Buddhist system of religion, the final emancipation of the soul from transmigration, and consequently a beatific enfrachisement from the evils of wordly existence, as by annihilation or absorption into the divine.
A salt of nitric acid. Nitrate of silver, a white crystalline salt (AgNO3), used in photography and as a cauterizing agent.
A colorless nonmetallic element, tasteless and odorless, comprising four fifths of the atmosphere by volume.
Of, pertaining to, or containing, niter; of the quality of niter, or resembling it.
A patriarch of Biblical history, in the time of the Deluge. Noah's ark.
The quality or state of being noble; superiority of mind or of character.
Possessing eminence, elevation, dignity, etc.; above whatever is low, mean, degrading, or dishonorable.
One of the nobility; a noble; a peer; one who enjoys rank above a commoner, either by virtue of birth, by office, or by patent.
Of, pertaining to, done or occuring in, the night.
Curved so that the apex hangs down; having the top bent downward.

A knot, a knob; a protuberance; a swelling. 2. Specifically: (a) (Astron.) One of the two points where the orbit of a planet, or comet, intersects the ecliptic, or the orbit of a satellite intersects the plane of the...
Making, or causing, no noise or bustle; without noise; silent.
Noxious to health; hurtful; mischievous; unwholesome; insalubrious; destructive.

Customary; ordinary; -- applied to the usual English spelling, in distinction from strictly phonetic methods.
