Indifference; carelessness; coolness.
Indifferent; careless; cool.
A state of not being committed or pledged; forbearance or refusal to commit one's self.
Not hitherto described; novel; hence, odd; abnormal; unclassifiable. A thing not yet described.

Not having existence.

That which is not sense, or has no sense; words, or language, which have no meaning, or which convey no intelligible ideas.
A simpleton; a blockhead; a stupid person; a ninny. The chuckling grin of noodles.
A narrow place formed by an angle in bodies or between bodies; a corner.
A running knot, or loop, which binds the closer the more it is drawn. To tie in a noose.
Short for Norfolk Jacket.
A rule or authoritative standard; a model; a type. 2. (Biol.) A typical, structural unit.
A norm; a principle or rule; a model; a standard. J. S. Mill. 2. A mason's or a carpenter's square or rule.
A wooden bar, or iron pin. W. C. Russell. Of or pertaining to Normandy or to the Normans.
The point between the north and east, at an equal distance from each; the northeast part or region.
The point in the horizon between the north and west, and equally distant from each.
Of, pertaining to, or being in, the northwest; in a direction toward the northwest.
A bleeding at the nose. 2. (Bot.) The yarrow. See Yarrow.
A bunch of odorous and showy flowers; a bouquet; a posy. Pope.
That part of the treadboard of a stair which projects over the riser; hence, any like projection, as the projecting edge of a molding.
Homesickness; esp., a severe and sometimes fatal form of melancholia, due to homesickness.
A medicine, the ingredients of which are kept secret for the purpose of restricting the profits of sale to the inventor or proprietor.
One who records in shorthand what is said or done.
A hollow cut in anything; a nick; an indentation. And on the stick ten equal notches makes.
A book in which notes or memorandums are written. 2. A book in which notes of hand are registered.
Well known by reputation or report; eminent; celebrated.
Nihility; nonexistence. 2. The state of being of no value; a thing of no value.
Capable of being observed; worthy of notice; likely to attract observation.

To make known; to declare; to publish.
The quality or condition of being notorious; the state of being generally or publicly known.
Generally known and talked of by the public; universally believed to be true.

Intellect; understanding; talent; -- used humorously.
A new star, usually appearing suddenly, shining for a brief period, and then sinking into obscurity.
The quality or state of being novel; newness; freshness; recentness of origin or introduction.

Not anywhere; not in any place or state.
Neat cattle.
Hurtful; harmful; baneful; pernicious; injurious; destructive; unwholesome.
The nose; the snout; hence, the projecting vent of anything.
A shade of difference; a delicate gradation.
Having a nucleus; nucleated. To gather, as about a nucleus or center.
A kernel; hence, a central mass or point about which matter is gathered, or to which accretion is made.
Bare; naked; unclothed; undraped.
To touch gently, as with the elbow, in order to call attention or convey intimation.
The quality or state of being nude; nakedness. 2. That which is nude or naked.
Trifling; vain; futile; insignificant. 2. Of no force; inoperative; ineffectual.
A lump; a mass, esp. a native lump of a precious metal.
That which annoys or gives trouble and vexation; that which is offensive or noxious.
To make void; to render invalid; to deprive of legal force or efficacy.
The act or art of numbering. Numeration is but still the adding of one unit more, and giving to the whole a new name or sign.
One skilled in numismatics; a numismatologist.
A house in which nuns reside; a cloister or convent in which women reside for life, under religious vows.
One who nourishes; a person who supplies food, tends, or brings up; as: (a) A woman who has the care of young children.
Supplying or taking nourishment from, or as from, the breast.
The act of nourishing or nursing; thender care; education; training. A man neither by nature nor by nurture wise.
An instrument for cracking nuts. 2. (Zoöl.) (a) A European bird (Nucifraga caryocatactes), allied to the magpie and crow.
The kernel of the fruit of the nutmeg tree (Myristica fragrans), a native of the Molucca Islands, but cultivated elsewhere in the tropics.
Nutritious; nourishing; promoting growth. -- n. Any substance which has nutritious qualities, i.
That which nourishes; anything which promotes growth and repairs the natural waste of animal or vegetable life.
In the broadest sense, a process or series of processes by which the living organism as a whole (or its component parts or organs) is maintained in its normal condition of life and growth.
Nourishing; promoting growth, or preventing decay; alimental. -- Nu*tri"tious*ly, adv.
The shell or hard external covering in which the kernel of a nut is inclosed.
A gatherer of nuts.
Abounding in nuts. 2. Having a flavor like that of nuts.
A goddess of the mountains, forests, meadows, or waters. Where were ye, nymphs, when the remorseless deep Closed o'er the head of your loved Lycidas Milton.
Made or consisting of oaks or of the wood of oaks. "In oaken bower." Milton.
The material obtained by untwisting and picking into loose fiber old hemp ropes.
A fertile or green spot in a waste or desert, esp. in a sandy desert. My one oasis in the dust and drouth Of city life.
A solemn affirmation or declaration, made with a reverent appeal to God for the truth of what is affirmed.
Meal made of oats. Gay. 2. (Bot.) A plant of the genus Panicum; panic grass.
Hardened in feelings, esp. against moral or mollifying influences; unyielding.
The act of obeying, or the state of being obedient; compliance with that which is required by authority.
Subject in will or act to authority; willing to obey; submissive to restraint, control, or command.
Obedience. Chaucer. 2. A manifestation of obedience; an expression of difference or respect.
An upright, four-sided pillar, gradually tapering as it rises, and terminating in a pyramid called pyramidion.
Excessively corpulent; fat; fleshy.
The state or quality of being obese; incumbrance of flesh.
To give ear to; to execute the commands of; to yield submission to; to comply with the orders of.
to render obscure, unclear, or unintelligible; to confuse deliberately
characteristic: Like throwing a fog over the facts, it muddies the water so the truth is hard to see.

definition: The act of objecting.
In the manner or state of an object.
One who objects; one who offers objections to a proposition or measure.
To chide; to reprove.
The act of objurgating; reproof. While the good lady was bestowing this objurgation on Mr.Ben Allen.
The act of offering, or of making an offering. Locke. 2. Anything offered or presented in worship or sacred service.
To bring or place under obligation, moral or legal; to hold by a constraining motive.
The act of obligating. 2. That which obligates or constrains; the binding power of a promise, contract, oath, or vow, or of law.
Binding in law or conscience; imposing duty or obligation; requiring performance or forbearance of some act.
Putting under obligation; disposed to oblige or do favors; hence, helpful.
Not erect or perpendicular; neither parallel to, nor at right angles from, the base.

To erase or blot out; to efface; to render undecipherable, as a writing.
The act of forgetting, or the state of being forgotten; cessation of remembrance.
Promoting oblivion; causing forgetfulness. "The oblivious pool." Milton.
Subject; liable; exposed; answerable; amenable; -- with to. The writings of lawyers, which are tied obnoxious to their particular laws.
Covered over, shaded, or darkened; destitute of light; imperfectly illuminated.
The quality or state of being obscure; darkness; privacy; inconspicuousness.
obedient or attentive to an excessive or servile degree

definition: A place or building for making observations on the heavenly bodies. The new observatory in Greenwich Park.
To take notice of by appropriate conduct; to conform one's action or practice to.
Giving particular attention; habitually attentive to what passes.
To besiege; to beset. Sir T. Elyot.
The act of besieging. Johnson. 2. The state of being besieged; -- used specifically of a person beset by a spirit from without.
A kind of glass produced by volcanoes. It is usually of a black color, and opaque, except in thin splinters.
The state of becoming obsolete.
Going out of use; becoming obsolete; passing into desuetude.
No longer in use; gone into disuse; disused; neglected.
That which stands in the way, or opposes; anything that hinders progress.
One skilled in obstetrics; an accoucheur.
The science of midwifery; the art of assisting women in parturition, or in the trouble incident to childbirth.
A fixedness in will, opinion, or resolution that can not be shaken at all, or only with great difficulty.
Pertinaciously adhering to an opinion, purpose, or course; persistent.
Attended by, or making, a loud and tumultuous noise; clamorous; noisy.
To block up; to stop up or close, as a way or passage; to place an obstacle in, or fill with obstacles or impediments that prevent or hinder passing.
The act of obstructing, or state of being obstructed. 2. That which obstructs or impedes.
One who hinders progress; one who obstructs business, as in a legislative body.
To hold; to keep; to possess. His mother, then, is mortal, but his Sire He who obtains the monarchy of heaven.
To thrust impertinently; to present without warrant or solicitation.
Disposed to obtrude; inclined to intrude or thrust one's self or one's opinions upon others, or to enter uninvited.
Not pointed or acute; blunt; -- applied esp. to angles greater than a right angle, or containing more than ninety degrees.
To turn toward. If its base be obverted towards us. I. Watts.
To meet in the way. Not to stir a step to obviate any of a different religion.
A falling out, happening, or coming to pass; hence, that which falls out or happens.
In an occasional manner; on occasion; at times, as convenience requires or opportunity offers.
The part of the horizon where the sun last appears in the evening; that part of the earth towards the sunset.
To shut up; to close. Sir T. Browne. 2. (Chem.) To take in and retain.
Hidden from the eye or the understanding; inviable; secret; concealed.
The act or process of occupying or taking possession; actual possession and control.
A coming or happening.
A plane figure of eight sides and eight angles. 2. Any structure (as a fortification) or place with eight sides or angles.
The eighth day after a church festival, the festival day being included.
A book composed of sheets each of which is folded into eight leaves; hence, indicating more or less definitely a size of book so made.
The tenth month of the year, containing thirty-one days. 2. Ale or cider made in that month.
A person eighty years, or more, of age.
Depending on, or perceived by, the eye; received by actual sight; personally seeing or having seen.
One skilled in treating diseases of the eye.
The quality or state of being odd; singularity; queerness; peculiarity.
The supreme deity of the Scandinavians; -- the same as Woden, of the German tribes.
Hateful; deserving or receiving hatred.
Hatred; dislike.
Any smell, whether fragrant or offensive; scent; perfume. Meseemed I smelt a garden of sweet flowers, That dainty odors from them threw around.
Bearing or yielding an odor; perfumed; usually, sweet of scent; fragrant.
Having or emitting an odor or scent, esp. a sweet odor; fragrant; sweet-smelling.
An epic poem attributed to Homer, which describes the return of Ulysses to Ithaca after the siege of Troy.
The rejected or waste parts of a butchered animal. 2. A dead body; carrion.
One who offends; one who violates any law, divine or human; a wrongdoer.
Giving offense; causing displeasure or resentment; displeasing; annoying.
The act of offering, or the thing offered. Bacon. Bp. Fell. 2. (R.C.Ch.) (a) An anthem chanted, or a voluntary played on the organ, during the offering and first part of the Mass.
Instant; ready; extemporaneous.
By the proper officer; by virtue of the proper authority; in pursuance of the special powers vested in an officer or office.
Pertaining to, or being in accordance with, duty. If there were any lie in the case, it could be no more than as officious and venial one.
Shy or distant in manner.
In general, that which is set off, from, before, or against, something.
That which shoots off or separates from a main stem, channel, family, race, etc.
From the shore.
To view or look at with side glances, as in fondness, or with a design to attract notice.
An imaginary monster, or hideous giant of fairy tales, who lived on human beings.
Consisting of oil; containing oil; having the nature or qualities of oil.
That which serves to anoint; any soft unctuous substance used for smearing or anointing.
The sense by which the impressions made on the olfactory organs by the odorous particles in the atmosphere are perceived.
Of, pertaining to, or connected with, the sense of smell.
A tree (Olea Europæa) with small oblong or elliptical leaves, axillary clusters of flowers, and oval, one-seeded drupes.
The last letter of the Greek alphabet. See Alpha. 2. The last; the end.
Eggs beaten up with a little flour, etc., and cooked in a frying pan.
An occurrence supposed to portend, or show the character of, some future event.
A Hebrew measure, the tenth of an ephah. See Ephah. Ex. xvi. 36.
The act of omitting; neglect or failure to do something required by propriety or duty.
The state of being omnipotent; almighty power; hence, one who is omnipotent.
Able in every respect and for every work; unlimited in ability; all-powerful.
Present in all places at the same time; ubiquitous.
The quality or state of being omniscient; -- an attribute peculiar to God.
Having universal knowledge; knowing all things; infinitely knowing or wise.
All-devouring; eating everything indiscriminately.
Burdensome; oppressive. "Too onerous a solicitude." I. Taylor. Onerous cause (Scots Law), a good and legal consideration.
A reflexive form of the indefinite pronoun one. Commonly writen as two words, one's self.
The act of going forward; progress; (pl.) affairs; business; current events.
A liliaceous plant of the genus Allium (A.cepa), having a strong-flavored bulb and long hollow leaves.
The formation of words in imitation of sounds; a figure of speech in which the sound of a word is imitative of the sound of the thing which the word represents.
A rushing onward.
A burden; an obligation. Onus probandi ( Etym: , obligation to furnish evidence to prove a thing.
Moving in a forward direction; tending toward a contemplated or desirable end.
Soft mud or slime; earth so wet as to flow gently, or easily yield to pressure.
A mineral consisting, like quartz, of silica, but inferior to quartz in hardness and specific gravity.
A reflection of a milky or pearly light from the interior of a mineral, as in the moonstone.
Reflecting a milky or pearly light from the interior; having an opaline play of colors.
Impervious to the rays of light; not transparent.
One who, or that which, opens. "True opener of my eyes." Milton.
In an open manner; publicly; not in private; without secrecy. How grossly and openly do many of us contradict the precepts of the gospel by our ungodliness! Tillotson.
A drama, either tragic or comic, of which music forms an essential part.
To perform a work or labor; to exert power or strengh, physical or mechanical.
Having the power of acting; hence, exerting force, physical or moral; active in the production of effects.
One who, or that which, operates or produces an effect. 2. (Surg.) One who performs some act upon the human body by means of the hand, or with instruments.
A short, light, musical drama.
To have an opinion; to judge; to think; to suppose. South.
The inspissated juice of the Papaver somniferum, or white poppy. Note: Opium is obtained from incisions made in the capsules of the plant, and the best flows from the first incision.
Situated in front; opposite; hence, opposing; adverse; antagonistic. Pope.
The art or practice of taking advantage of opportunities or circumstances, or of seeking immediate advantage with little regard for ultimate consequences.
One who advocates or practices opportunism.
The act of opposing; an attempt to check, restrain, or defeat; resistance.
Unreasonably burdensome; unjustly severe, rigorous, or harsh.
Expressive of opprobrium; attaching disgrace; reproachful; scurrilous.
Disgrace; infamy; reproach mingled with contempt; abusive language. Being both dramatic author and dramatic performer, he found himself heir to a twofold opprobrium.
That branch of physical science which treats of the nature and properties of light, the laws of its modification by opaque and transparent bodies, and the phenomena of vision.
The opinion or doctrine that everything in nature, being the work of God, is ordered for the best, or that the ordering of things in the universe is such as to produce the highest good.
One who holds the opinion that all events are ordered for the best. 2.
Involving an option; depending on the exercise of an option; left to one's discretion or choice.
One who is skilled in or practices optometry.
Measurement of the range of vision, esp. by means of the optometer. 2.
Wealth; riches; affluence. Swift.
Having a large estate or property; wealthy; rich; affluent.
A work; specif. (Mus.), a musical composition. Note: Each composition, or set of pieces, as the composer may choose, is called an opus, and they are numbered in the order of their issue.
Uttered by the mouth, or in words; spoken, not written; verbal.
An elaborate discourse, delivered in public, treating an important subject in a formal and dignified manner.
A public speaker; one who delivers an oration; especially, one distinguished for his skill and power as a public speaker.

A more or less dramatic text or poem, founded on some Scripture nerrative, or great divine event, elaborately set to music, in recitative, arias, grand choruses, etc., to be sung with an orchestral accompaniment, but...
A place of orisons, or prayer; especially, a chapel or small room set apart for private devotions.
The path described by a heavenly body in its periodical revolution around another body.

The space in a theater between the stage and the audience; -- originally appropriated by the Greeks to the chorus and its evolutions, afterward by the Romans to persons of distinction, and by the moderns to a band of...
Any plant of the order Orchidaceæ. See Orchidaceous.
To set in order; to arrange according to rule; to regulate; to set; to establish.
An ancient form of test to determine guilt or innocence, by appealing to a supernatural decision, -- once common in Europe, and still practiced in the East and by savage tribes.
Disposition; distribution; management. South.
Conformed to order; in order; regular.
Indicating order or succession.
According to established rules or settled method; as a rule; commonly.
Heavy weapons of warfare; cannon, or great guns, mortars, and howitzers.
Organic structure; organization. "The advantageous organism of the eye." Grew.
The act of organizing; the act of arranging in a systematic way for use or action.
One who organizes.
Eager or immoderate excitement or action; the state of turgescence of any organ.
A sacrifice accompanied by certain ceremonies in honor of some pagan deity.
A frantic revel; drunken revelry. See Orgies.
The act or process of orientating; determination of the points of the compass, or the east point, in taking bearings.
A mouth or aperture, as of a tube, pipe, etc.; an opening.
The first existence or beginning of anything; the birth. This mixed system of opinion and sentiment had its origin in the ancient chivalry.
The quality or state of being original. Macaulay.
In the original time, or in an original manner; primarily; from the beginning or origin.
To give an origin or beginning to; to cause to be; to bring into existence.
A large and bright constellation on the equator, between the stars Aldebaran and Sirius.
A prayer; a supplication. Chaucer. Shak. Lowly they bowed, adoring, and began Their orisons, each morning duly paid.
A cloth made of worsted and cotton, -- used for wearing apparel. 2. A variety of the plum.
That which embellishes or adorns; that which adds grace or beauty; embellishment.
Adorned; decorated; beautiful. "So bedecked, ornate, and gay." Milton.
One skilled in ornithology; a student of ornithology; one who describes birds.
That branch of zoölogy which treats of the natural history of birds and their classification.
Characterized by fullness, clearness, strength, and smoothness; ringing and musical.
A child bereaved of both father and mother; sometimes, also, a child who has but one parent living.
The state of being an orphan; orphanhood; orphans, collectively. 2. An institution or asylum for the care of orphans.
The famous mythic Thracian poet, son of the Muse Calliope, and husband of Eurydice.
Sound in opinion or doctrine, especially in religious doctrine; hence, holding the Christian faith.
Soundness of faith; a belief in the doctrines taught in the Scriptures, or in some established standard of faith.
Right-angled; rectangular.
The art or practice of writing words with the proper letters, according to standard usage.
Pertaining to, or employed in, orthopedy; relating to the prevention or cure of deformities of children, or, in general, of the human body at any age.
To move backward and forward; to vibrate like a pendulum; to swing; to sway.
The act of oscillating; a swinging or moving backward and forward, like a pendulum.
To kiss. 2. (Geom.) To touch closely, so as to have a common curvature at the point of contact.
One of the principal divinities of Egypt, the brother and husband of Isis.
Composed of bone; resembling bone; capable of forming bone; bony; ossific.
To form into bone; to change from a soft animal substance into bone, as by the deposition of lime salts.
Capable of being shown; proper or intended to be shown. Walpole. 2. Shown.
Showing; exhibiting. Ostensive demonstration (Math.), a direct or positive demonstration, as opposed to the apagogical or indirect method.
characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress
characteristic: Picture a flashy gold car and dripping jewelry, showing off wealth to impress everyone.

definition: Banishment by popular vote, -- a means adopted at Athens to rid the city of a person whose talent and influence gave umbrage.
To exile by ostracism; to banish by a popular vote, as at Athens. Grote.
A large bird of the genus Struthio, of which Struthio camelus of Africa is the best known species.
Any carnivorous animal of the genus Lutra, and related genera. Several species are described.
A socket or bezel holding a precious stone; hence, a jewel or ornament worn on the person.
A bursting forth; eruption; insurrection. "Mobs and outbreaks." J. H. Newman.
A bursting forth.
That which comes out of, or follows from, something else; issue; result.
A vehement or loud cry; a cry of distress, alarm, opposition, or detestation.
To go beyond in performance; to excel; to surpass. An imposture outdoes the original.
Being, or done, in the open air; being or done outside of certain buildings, as poorhouses, hospitals, etc.
Abread; out of the house; out of doors.
A fitting out, or equipment, as of a ship for a voyage, or of a person for an expedition in an unoccupied region or residence in a foreign land.
A small house or building at a little distance from the main house; an outbuilding.
Foreign; not native. Him did outlandish women cause to sin. Neh. xiii.
To exceed in duration; to survive; to endure longer than. Milton.
A person excluded from the benefit of the law, or deprived of its protection.
The line which marks the outer limits of an object or figure; the exterior line or edge.
To live beyond, or longer than; to survive. They live too long who happiness outlive.
To surpass, or get an advantage of, in maneuvering; to outgeneral.
To exceed in number.
A post or station without the limits of a camp, or at a distance from the main body of an army, for observation of the enemy.
The amount of coal or ore put out from one or more mines, or the quantity of material produced by, or turned out from, one or more furnaces or mills, in a given time.
To reach beyond.
Any spar or projecting timber run out for temporary use, as from a ship's mast, to hold a rope or a sail extended, or from a building, to support hoisting teckle.
Immediately; without delay; at once.
To exceed, or leave behind, in running; to run faster than; to outstrip.
A setting out, starting, or beginning. "The outset of a political journey." Burke.
One not belonging to the concern, institution, party, etc., spoken of.
A part remote from the center; outer edge; border; -- usually in the plural.
Speaking, or spoken, freely, openly, or boldly.
That stands out; undischarged; uncollected; not paid.
To stretch out. Milton.
To go faster than; to outrun; to advance beyond; to leave behing. Appetites which .
To exceed in weight or value.
A lesser kind of triumph allowed to a commander for an easy, bloodless victory, or a victory over slaves.
A place arched over with brick or stonework, and used for baking, heating, or drying.
A kind of loose trousers worn over others to protect them from soiling.
Overpowering; subduing; repressing. I. Watts. 2. Aggressively haughty.
Over the side of a ship; hence, from on board of a ship, into the water.
A coat worn over the other clothing; a greatcoat; a topcoat.
Conquering; subduing. -- O`ver*com"ing*ly, adv.
To dose to excess; to give an overdose, or too many doses, to. Too great a dose.
To drive too hard, or far, or beyond strength.
Due and more than due; delayed beyond the proper time of arrival or payment, etc.
To flow over; to cover woth, or as with, water or other fluid; to spread over.
An overflow; that which overflows; exuberance; copiousness. He was ready to bestow the overflowings of his full mind on anybody who would start a subject.
To haul or drag over; hence, to turn over for examination; to inspect.
Aloft; above; in or attached to the ceiling or roof; in the story or upon the floor above.
To hear more of (anything) than was intended to be heard; to hear by accident or artifice.
To lap over; to lap. 1. The lapping of one thing over another.
To leap over or across; hence, to omit; to ignore. "Let me o'erleap that custom." Shak.
To load or fill to excess; to load too heavily. An excessive load; the excess beyond a proper load.
To look down upon from a place that is over or above; to look over or view from a higher position.
One who is lord over another or others; a superior lord; a master. Freeman.
To pay too much to; to reward too highly.
To excel or exceed in power; to cause to yield; to vanquish; to subdue.
Excessive production; supply beyond the demand. J. S. Mill.
To reach above or beyond in any direction. 2. To deceive, or get the better of, by artifice or cunning.
To run over; to grow or spread over in excess; to invade and occupy; to take possession of.
Over the sea; abroad. Milton. Tennyson.
One who oversees; a superintendent; a supervisor.
To throw a shadow, or shade, over; to darken; to obscure. There was a cloud that overshadowed them.
To stride over or beyond.
Open to view; public; apparent; manifest. Overt and apparent virtues bring forth praise.
To throw over; to overturn; to upset; to turn upside down. His wife overthrew the table.
Time beyond, or in excess of, a limit; esp., extra working time.
One of the harmonics faintly heard with and above a tone as it dies away, produced by some aliquot portion of the vibrating sting or column of air which yields the fundamental tone.
To turn or throw from a basis, foundation, or position; to overset.
Unduly confident; arrogant; presumptuous; conceited. -- O`ver*ween"ingly, adv.
Overpowering; irresistible. -- O`ver*whelm"ing*ly, adv.
Wrought upon excessively; overworked; overexcited.
Resembling an egg in shape; egg-shaped; ovate.
Had or held under obligation of paying; due. There is more owing her than is paid.
The state of being an owner; the right to own; exclusive right of possession.
A binary compound of oxygen with an atom or radical, or a compound which is regarded as binary.

A colorless, tasteless, odorless, gaseous element occurring in the free state in the atmosphere, of which it forms about 23 per cent by weight and about 21 per cent by volume, being slightly heavier than nitrogen.
A figure in which an epithet of a contrary signification is added to a word.
Any marine bivalve mollusk of the genus Ostrea. They are usually found adhering to rocks or other fixed objects in shallow water along the seacoasts, or in brackish water in the mouth of rivers.
A colorless gaseous substance (O.
One of the Pachydermata.
To make to be at peace; to appease; to calm; to still; to quiet; to allay the agitation, excitement, or resentment of.
A person whose business is to pack things; especially, one who packs food for preservation.
A small pack or package; a little bundle or parcel.
An agreement; a league; a compact; a covenant. Bacon. The engagement and pact of society whish goes by the name of the constitution.
The act or process of making a pad or of inserting stuffing. 2. The material with which anything is padded.
Low; mean; boorish; vagabond. "Such pady persons." Digges (1585). "The paddy persons." Motley.
An ancient Greek hymn in honor of Apollo as a healing deity, and, later, a song addressed to other deities.
One who worships false goods; an idolater; a heathen; one who is neither a Christian, a Mohammedan, nor a Jew.
The marking or numbering of the pages of a book.
A vessel of wood or tin, etc., usually cylindrical and having a bail, -- used esp.
Free from pain; without pain. -- Pain"less*ly, adv. -- Pain"less*ness, n.
Labor; toilsome effort; care or trouble taken; -- plural in form, but used with a singular or plural verb, commonly the former.
Careful in doing; diligent; faithful; attentive. "Painstaking men." Harris.
Covered or adorned with paint; portrayed in colors. As idle as a painted ship Upon a painted ocean.
A rope at the bow of a boat, used to fasten it to anything. Totten. The panther, or puma.
The act or process of uniting or arranging in pairs or couples. 2. See To pair off, under Pair, v.
Originally, in India, loose drawers or trousers, such as those worn, tied about the waist, by Mohammedan men and women.
A knight-errant; a distinguished champion.

An inclosed carriage or litter, commonly about eight feet long, four feet wide, and four feet high, borne on the shoulders of men by means of two projecting poles, -- used in India, China, etc., for the conveyance of...
Agreeable to the palate or taste; savory; hence, acceptable; pleasing.
Talk; conversation; esp., idle or beguiling talk; talk intended to deceive.
The science which treats of the ancient life of the earth, or of fossils which are the remains of such life.
A thin, oval or square board, or tablet, with a thumb hole at one end for holding it, on which a painter lays and mixes his pigments.
A parchment which has been written upon twice, the first writing having been erased to make place for the second.
An ode recanting, or retracting, a former one; also, a repetition of an ode.
A small and mean bed; a bed of straw. Milton. 1. (Paint.) Same as Palette.
Covered with a mant  Bp. Hall. 2. Eased; mitigated; alleviated. Bp. Fell.
Deficient in color; pale; wan.
One who palms or cheats, as at cards or dice. A wandering religious votary.
Bearing palms; abounding in palms; derived from palms.
Capable of being touched and felt; perceptible by the touch.
To beat rapidly and more strongly than usual; to throb; to bound with emotion or exertion.
Paralysis, complete or partial. See Paralysis. "One sick of the palsy." Mark ii.
Mean; vile; worthless; despicable; contemptible; pitiful; trifling.
Pale; wanting color; dim. Shak. Whittier. Divided into four or more equal parts by perpendicular lines, and of two different tinctures disposed alternately.
To feed to the full; to feed luxuriously; to glut.
Fed luxuriously; indulged to the full; hence, luxuriant. "Pampered boughs." Milton.
A writer of pamphlets; a scribbler. Dryden. Macaulay. To write or publish pamphlets.
A remedy for all diseases; a universal medicine; a cure-all; catholicon.
A plume or bunch of feathers, esp. such a bunch worn on the helmet; any military plume, or ornamental group of feathers.
A thin cake of batter fried in a pan or on a griddle; a griddlecake; a flapjack.
The sweetbread, a gland connected with the intestine of nearly all vertebrates.
A small Asiatic mammal (Ailurus fulgens) having fine soft fur. It is related to the bears, and inhabits the mountains of Northern India.
Affecting a whole people or a number of countries; everywhere epidemic.
The great hall or council chamber of demons or evil spirits. Milton. 2.
A male bawd; a pimp; a procurer. Thou art the pander to her dishonor. Shak.
A beautiful woman (all-gifted), whom Jupiter caused Vulcan to make out of clay in order to punish the human race, because Prometheus had stolen the fire from heaven.
The narrow edge of a hammer head. See Peen. 1. A division; a distinct piece, limited part, or compartment of any surface.
An oration or eulogy in praise of some person or achievement; a formal or elaborate encomium.
A sunken compartment with raised margins, molded or otherwise, as in ceilings, wainscotings, etc.
A plant of the genus Panicum; panic grass; also, the edible grain of some species of panic grass.
Defensive armor in general; a full suit of defensive armor. Milton. We had need to take the Christian panoply, to put on the whole armor of God.
A complete view in every direction. 2. A picture presenting a view of objects in every direction, as from a central point.
Of, pertaining to, or like, a panorama. Panoramic camera. See under Camera.
A plant of the genus Viola (V. tricolor) and its blossom, originally purple and yellow.
The doctrine that the universe, taken or conceived of as a whole, is God.
A temple dedicated to all the gods; especially, the building so called at Rome.
A large dark-colored variety of the leopard, by some zoölogists considered a distinct species.
An apartment or closet in which bread and other provisions are kept.
An old Italian silver coin, worth about ten cents.
The office and dignity of the pope, or pontiff, of Rome; papal jurisdiction.
Like pap; soft; succulent; tender. Ray.
The dried ripened fruit of Capsicum annuum or various other species of pepper.
A tall rushlike plant (Cyperus Papyrus) of the Sedge family, formerly growing in Egypt, and now found in Abyssinia, Syria, Sicily, etc.
A piece of Turkish money, usually copper, the fortieth part of a piaster, or about one ninth of a cent.
A contrivance somewhat in the form of an umbrella, by means of which a descent may be made from a balloon, or any eminence.
The ground where a military display is held, or where troops are drilled.
a typical example or pattern of something; a model
characteristic: Think of the master blueprint or framework that everything else is modeled after.

definition: Exemplary. -- Par`a*dig*mat"ic*al*ly, adv. A writer of memoirs of religious persona, as examples of Christian excellence.
A tenet or proposition contrary to received opinion; an assertion or sentiment seemingly contradictory, or opposed to common sense.
A companion; a match; an equal. Spenser. Philoclea, who indeed had no paragon but her sister.
Originally, a marginal mark or note, set in the margin to call attention to something in the text, e.
The quality or state of being parallel. 2. Resemblance; correspondence.
Abolition of function, whether complete or partial; esp., the loss of the power of voluntary motion, with or without that of sensation, in any part of the body.
To affect or strike with paralysis or palsy. 2. Fig.: To unnerve; to destroy or impair the energy of.

A term applied to some characteristic magnitude whose value, invariable as long as one and the same function, curve, surface, etc., is considered, serves to distinguish that function, curve, surface, etc., from...
Having the highest rank or jurisdiction; superior to all others; chief.
Mental derangement; insanity.
A low wall, especially one serving to protect the edge of a platform, roof, bridge, or the like.
Something reserved to a wife, over and above her dower, being chiefly apparel and ornaments suited to her degree.
A restatement of a text, passage, or work, expressing the meaning of the original in another form, generally for the sake of its clearer and fuller exposition.
One who frequents the tables of the rich, or who lives at another's expense, and earns his welcome by flattery.
To burn the surface of; to scorch; to roast over the fire, as dry grain.
The skin of a lamb, sheep, goat, young calf, or other animal, prepared for writing on.
To cut off, or shave off, the superficial substance or extremities of.
Mitigating; assuaging or soothing pain.
One who begets, or brings forth, offspring; a father or a mother. Children, obey your parents in the Lord.
Descent from parents or ancestors; parents or ancestors considered with respect to their rank or character.
A word, phrase, or sentence, by way of comment or explanation, inserted in, or attached to, a sentence which would be grammatically complete without it.
One of an aboriginal people of Southern India, regarded by the four castes of the Hindoos as of very low grade.
That circuit of ground committed to the charge of one parson or vicar, or other minister having cure of souls therein.
The quality or condition of being equal or equivalent; A like state or degree.
Conversation; discourse; talk; diction; phrase.
Mutual discourse or conversation; discussion; hence, an oral conference with an enemy, as with regard to a truce.
A room for business or social conversation, for the reception of guests, etc.
A writing in which the language or sentiment of an author is mimicked.
Having the same derivation; allied radically; conjugate; -- said of certain words, as man, mankind, manhood, etc.
The fit, attack, or exacerbation, of a disease that occurs at intervals, or has decided remissions or intermissions.
A body of seats on the floor of a music hall or theater nearest the orchestra.
A species of joinery or cabinet-work consisting of an inlay of geometric or other patterns, generally of different colors, -- used especially for floors.
Properly, one who murders one's own father; in a wider sense, one who murders one's father or mother or any ancestor.

In a general sense, any bird of the order Psittaci. 2. (Zoöl.) Any species of Psittacus, Chrysotis, Pionus, and other genera of the family Psittacidæ, as distinguished from the parrakeets, macaws, and lories.
To ward off; to stop, or to turn aside.
To resolve into its elements, as a sentence, pointing out the several parts of speech, and their relation to each other by government or agreement.
Exhibiting parsimony; sparing in expenditure of money; frugal to excess.
Closeness or sparingness in the expenditure of money; -- generally in a bad sense.
An aromatic umbelliferous herb (Carum Petroselinum), having finely divided leaves which are used in cookery and as a garnish.
A person who represents a parish in its ecclesiastical and corporate capacities.
Of, pertaining to, or affecting, a part only; not general or universal.
The quality or state of being partial; inclination to favor one party, or one side of a question, more than the other.
In part; not totally.
Admitting of being parted; divisible; separable; susceptible of severance or partition.
Sharing; participating; having a share of part. Bacon. A participator.
A minute part or portion of matter; a morsel; a little bit; an atom; a jot.
To give as a particular, or as the particulars; to mention particularly.
An adherent to a party or faction; esp., one who is strongly and passionately devoted to a party or an interest.
In part; in some measure of degree; not wholly. "I partly believe it." 1 Cor.
The state or condition of being a partner.
Any one of numerous species of small gallinaceous birds of the genus Perdix and several related genera of the family Perdicidæ, of the Old World.
An upstart; a man newly risen into notice.
An honorary title given to officers of high rank in Turkey, as to governers of provinces, military commanders, etc.
A way for passage; a hall. See Passage, 5.
Past; gone by; hence, past one's prime; worn; faded.
A passer or passer-by; a wayfarer. Shak. 2. A traveler by some established conveyance, as a coach, steamboat, railroad train, etc.
Susceptible of feeling or suffering, or of impressions from external agents.
In a passionate manner; with strong feeling; ardently. Sorrow expresses itself .
Not active, but acted upon; suffering or receiving impressions or influences.

A feast of the Jews, instituted to commemorate the sparing of the Hebrews in Egypt, when God, smiting the firstborn of the Egyptians, passed over the houses of the Israelites which were marked with the blood of a lamb.
Permission to pass; a document given by the competent officer of a state, permitting the person therein named to pass or travel from place to place, without molestation, by land or by water.
A word to be given before a person is allowed to pass; a watchword; a countersign.
A soft composition, as of flour moistened with water or milk, or of earth moistened to the consistence of dough, as in making potter's ware.
A shepherd; one who has the care of flocks and herds. 2. A guardian; a keeper.
Like paste, as in color, softness, stickness. "A pasty complexion." G.
A piece of cloth, or other suitable material, sewed or otherwise fixed upon a garment to repair or strengthen it, esp.
Note: (Oftener pronounced pat"ent in this sense) Open; expanded; evident.
The relation of a father to his child; fathership; fatherhood; family headship.
One skilled in pathology; an investigator in pathology.
The science which treats of diseases, their nature, causes, progress, symptoms, etc.
That quality or property of anything which touches the feelings or excites emotions and passions, esp., that which awakens tender emotions, such as pity, sorrow, and the like.
A footpath; a beaten track; any path or course. Also used figuratively.
In a patient manner. Cowper.
A dish or plate of metal or earthenware; a patella. 2. (Fine Arts) The color or incrustation which age gives to works of art.
A paved yard or floor where ores are cleaned and sorted, or where ore, salt, mercury, etc., are trampled by horses, to effect intermixture and amalgamation.
A dialect peculiar to the illiterate classes; a provincial form of speech.
The father and ruler of a family; one who governs his family or descendants by paternal right.
The murderer of his father. 2. The crime of one who murders his father.
A right or estate inherited from one's father; or, in a larger sense, from any ancestor.
One who loves his country, and zealously supports its authority and interests.
Inspired by patriotism; actuated by love of one's country; zealously and unselfishly devoted to the service of one's country.
Love of country; devotion to the welfare of one's country; the virtues and actions of a patriot.
To go the rounds along a chain of sentinels; to traverse a police district or beat.
One who patrols; a watchman; especially, a policeman who patrols a particular precinct of a town or city.
To act as patron toward; to support; to countenance; to favor; to aid.
Showing condescending favor; assuming the manner of airs of a superior toward another.
Derived from ancestors.
Anything proposed for imitation; an archetype; an exemplar; that which is to be, or is worthy to be, copied or imitated.
A little pie.
Fewness; smallness of number; scarcity. Hooker. Revelation denies it by the stern reserve, the paucity, and the incompleteness, of its communications.
A poor person; especially, one development on private or public charity.
The state of being a pauper; the state of indigent persons requiring support from the community.
The pavement. Nymphe du pavé ( 1. To lay or cover with stone, brick, or other material, so as to make a firm, level, or convenient surface for horses, carriages, or persons on foot, to travel on.
A temporary movable habitation; a large tent; a marquee; esp., a tent raised on posts.
One or two whom a pledge is delivered as security; one who takes anything in pawn.
The person to whom money is to be, or has been, paid; the person named in a bill or note, to whom, or to whose order, the amount is promised or directed to be paid.
Begin in or at peace; tranquil; quiet; free from, or not disposed to, war, disorder, or excitement.
Possessing or enjoying peace; not disturbed by war, tumult, agitation, anxiety, or commotion.
Resembling a peach or peaches.
The male of any pheasant of the genus Pavo, of which at least two species are known, native of Southern Asia and the East Indies.
A point; the sharp end or top of anything that terminates in a point.
Pointed; ending in a point.
The fruit of a trailing leguminous plant (Arachis hypogæa); also, the plant itself, which is widely cultivated for its fruit.
The fleshy pome, or fruit, of a rosaceous tree (Pyrus communis), cultivated in many varieties in temperate climates.
Containing pearls; abounding with, or yielding, pearls.
A small roundish stone or bowlder; especially, a stone worn and rounded by the action of water.
A species of hickory (Carya olivæformis), growing in North America, chiefly in the Mississippi valley and in Texas, where it is one of the largest of forest trees.
Liable to sin; subject to transgress the divine law. "A frail and peccable mortal." Sir W.
A slight trespass or offense; a petty crime or fault. Sir W. Scott.
The fourth part of a bushel; a dry measure of eight quarts.
One who, or that which, pecks; specif., a bird that pecks holes in trees.
To appropriate to one's own use the property of the public; to steal public moneys intrusted to one's care.
The act or practice of peculating, or of defrauding the public by appropriating to one's own use the money or goods intrusted to one's care for management or disbursement.
One's own; belonging solely or especially to an individual; not possessed by others.
Relating to money; monetary.
The science or art of teaching; the principles and rules of teaching; pedagogy.
Pedagogics; pedagogism. South.
To travel about with wares for sale; to go from place to place, or from house to house, for the purpose of retailing goods.
One who peddles; a traveling trader; one who travels about, retailing small wares.
Hawking; acting as a peddler. 2. Petty; insignificant. "The miserable remains of a peddling commerce." Burke.
The base or foot of a column, statue, vase, lamp, or the like; the part on which an upright work stands.
Going on foot; performed on foot.
Pertaining to the care and medical treatment of children.
That branch of medical science which treats of the hygiene and diseases of children.
The care of the feet and nails. 2. One who cares for the feet and nails.
A line of ancestors; descent; lineage; genealogy; a register or record of a line of ancestors.
Originally, in classical architecture, the triangular space forming the gable of a simple roof.
The five of trumps in certain varieties of auction pitch. (b) A variety of auction pitch in which the five of trumps counts five.
To look slyly, or with the eyes half closed, or through a crevice; to peep.
To cry, as a chicken hatching or newly hatched; to chirp; to cheep. There was none that moved the wing, or opened the mouth, or peeped.
The rank or dignity of a peer. Blackstone. 2. The body of peers; the nobility, collectively.
Having no peer or equal; matchless; superlative. "Her peerless feature." Shak.
Habitually fretful; easily vexed or fretted; hard to please; apt to complain.
A winged horse fabled to have sprung from the body of Medusa when she was slain.
expressing contempt or disapproval
characteristic: It describes an insulting word chosen to put someone down and show scorn.

definition: Money; riches; lucre; gain; -- generally conveying the idea of something ill-gotten or worthless.
Any large webfooted bird of the genus of Pelecanus, of which about a dozen species are known.
Transparent; clear; limpid; translucent; not opaque. "Pellucid crystal." Dr.
Of, pertaining to, or in the region of, the pelvis.
The pelvic arch, or the pelvic arch together with the sacrum. See Pelvic arch, under Pelvic, and Sacrum.
Penal retribution; punishment for crime or offense; the suffering in person or property which is annexed by law or judicial decision to the commission of a crime, offense, or trespass.
Inclination; decided taste; bias.
A small, fine brush of hair or bristles used by painters for laying on colors.
Supported from above; suspended; depending; pendulous; hanging.
Not yet decided; in continuance; in suspense.
A body so suspended from a fixed point as to swing freely to and fro by the alternate action of gravity and momentum.
Capable of being penetrated, entered, or pierced. Used also figuratively.
To enter into; to make way into the interior of; to effect an entrance into.
Having the power of entering, piercing, or pervading; sharp; subtile; penetrative.
The act or process of penetrating, piercing, or entering; also, the act of mentally penetrating into, or comprehending, anything difficult.
Any bird of the order Impennes, or Ptilopteri. They are covered with short, thick feathers, almost scalelike on the wings, which are without true quills.
A portion of land nearly surrounded by water, and connected with a larger body by a neck, or isthmus.
The male member, or organ of generation.
The quality or condition of being penitent; the disposition of a penitent.
Feeling pain or sorrow on account of sins or offenses; repentant; contrite.
A small flag; a pennon. The narrow, or long, pennant (called also whip or coach whip) is a long, narrow piece of bunting, carried at the masthead of a government vessel in commission.
Winged; plume-shaped. 2. (Bot.) Same as Pinnate.
Destitute of money; impecunious; poor. -- Pen"ni*less*ness, n.
The science or art of punishment.
Thoughtful, sober, or sad; employed in serious reflection; given to, or favorable to, earnest or melancholy musing.
Any element, atom, or radical, having a valence of five, or which can be combined with, substituted for, or compared with, five atoms of hydrogen or other monad.
A plane figure having five angles, and, consequently, five sides; any figure having five angles.
A pentacle or a pentalpha. "Like a wizard pentagram." Tennyson.
A solid figure having five sides.
A verse of five feet. Note: The dactylic pentameter consists of two parts separated by a diæresis.
A fivefold athletic performance peculiar to the great national games of the Greeks, including leaping, foot racing, wrestling, throwing the discus, and throwing the spear.
Having a valence of five; -- said of certain atoms and radicals.
A shed or roof sloping from the main wall or building, as over a door or window.
Last but one.
An incomplete or partial shadow. 2. (Astron.) The shadow cast, in an eclipse, where the light is partly, but not wholly, cut off by the intervening body.
Excessively sparing in the use of money; sordid; stingy; miserly. "A penurious niggard of his wealth." Milton.
Absence of resources; want; privation; indigence; extreme poverty; destitution.
A well-known, pungently aromatic condiment, the dried berry, either whole or powdered, of the Piper nigrum.
An aromatic and pungent plant of the genus Mentha (M. piperita), much used in medicine and confectionery.
To walk through or over; especially, to travel over for the purpose of surveying or examining.
To obtain knowledge of through the senses; to receive impressions from by means of the bodily organs.
A certain rate per cent; the allowance, duty, rate of interest, discount, or commission, on a hundred.
Capable of being perceived; cognizable; discernible; perceivable. With a perceptible blast of the air.
Any fresh-water fish of the genus Perca and of several other allied genera of the family Percidæ, as the common American or yellow perch (Perca flavescens, or Americana), and the European perch (P.
By chance; perhaps; peradventure.
The faculty, act or power of perceiving; perception. Mrs. Browning.
Having the faculty of perception; perceiving.
To cause to pass through fine interstices, as a liquor; to filter; to strain.
One who, or that which, filters. " act as percolators." Henfrey.
The act of percussing, or striking one body against another; forcible collision, esp.
To travel from place to place, or from one country to another; hence, to sojourn in foreign countries.
A traveling from one country to another; a wandering; sojourn in foreign countries.
Precluding debate or expostulation; not admitting of question or appeal.
ing or continuing through the year.
Capable of becoming, or being made, perfect.
One pretending to perfection; esp., one pretending to moral perfection.
Very fervid; too fervid; glowing; ardent.
Guilty of perfidy; violating good faith or vows; false to trust or confidence reposed.
The act of violating faith or allegiance; violation of a promise or vow, or of trust reposed.
To bore through; to pierce through with a pointed instrument; to make a hole or holes through by boring or piercing.
To carry through; to bring to completion; to achieve; to accomplish; to execute.
One who performs, accomplishes, or fulfills.
To fill or impregnate with a perfume; to scent. And Carmel's flowery top perfumes the skies.
Perfumes, in general. 2. Etym:  The art of preparing perfumes.
carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection; cursory
characteristic: Picture a quick, careless handshake done just to get it over with, with no real interest.

definition: By chance; peradventure; perchance; it may be. And pray God, if perhaps the thought of thine heart may be forgiven thee.
An imaginary being, male or female, like an elf or fairy, represented as a descendant of fallen angels, excluded from paradise till penance is accomplished.
That point in the orbit of the moon which is nearest to the earth; -- opposed to Ant: apogee.
Full of, attended with, or involving, peril; dangerous; hazardous.
The outer boundary of a body or figure, or the sum of all the sides. 2.
Pertaining to, derived from, or designating, the highest oxygen acid (HIO 1.
The quality or state of being periodical, or regularly recurrent.
Walking about; itinerant. 2. Of or pertaining to the philosophy taught by Aristotle (who gave his instructions while walking in the Lyceum at Athens), or to his followers.
The outside or superficial portions of a body; the surface. 2. (Geom.) The circumference of a circle, ellipse, or other figure.
A range of columns with their entablature, etc.; specifically, a complete system of columns, whether on all sides of a court, or surrounding a building, such as the cella of a temple.
False swearing. 2. (Law) At common law, a willfully false statement in a fact material to the issue, made by a witness under oath in a competent judicial proceeding.
To make trim or smart; to straighten up; to erect; to make a jaunty or saucy display of.
Perk; pert; jaunty; trim. There amid perky larches and pines. Tennyson.
The quality or state of being permanent; continuance in the same state or place.
Continuing in the same state, or without any change that destroys form or character.
In a permanent manner.
Capable of being permeated, or passed through; yielding passage; passable.
To pass through the pores or interstices of; to penetrate and pass through without causing rupture or displacement.
That may be permitted; allowable; admissible. -- Per*mis"si*ble*ness, n.
Permitting; granting leave or liberty. "By his permissive will." Milton.
To consent to; to allow or suffer to be done; to tolerate; to put up with.
The act of permuting; exchange of the thing for another; mutual transference.
having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way

definition: The concluding part of an oration; especially, a final summing up and enforcement of an argument.
Exactly upright or vertical; pointing to the zenith; at right angles to the plane of the horizon.
To do or perform; to carry through; to execute, commonly in a bad sense.
One who perpetrates; esp., one who commits an offense or crime.
Neverceasing; continuing forever or for an unlimited time; unfailing; everlasting.
To make perpetual; to cause to endure, or to be continued, indefinitely.
Something gained from a place or employment over and above the ordinary salary or fixed wages for services rendered.
To pursue in a manner to injure, grieve, or afflict; to beset with cruelty or malignity.
The act of persevering; persistence in anything undertaken; continued pursuit or prosecution of any business, or enterprise begun.
To persist in any business or enterprise undertaken; to pursue steadily any project or course begun.
Frivolous or bantering talk; a frivolous manner of treating any subject, whether serious or otherwise.
To stand firm; to be fixed and unmoved; to stay; to continue steadfastly.
The quality or state of being persistent; staying or continuing quality.

Having a well-formed body, or person; graceful; comely; of good appearance.
Form, appearance, or belongings of a person; the external appearance, stature, figure, air, and the like, of a person.
Pertaining to human beings as distinct from things. Every man so termed by way of personal difference.
That which constitutes distinction of person; individuality. Personality is individuality existing in itself, but with a nature as a ground.
To regard, treat, or represent as a person; to represent as a rational being.
The body of persons employed in some public service, as the army, navy, etc.
Having the power of seeing clearly; quick-sighted; sharp of sight. 2. Fig.: Of acute discernment.
The state of being perspicacious; acuteness of sight or of intelligence.

Holding or adhering to any opinion, purpose, or design, with obstinacy.

Belonging or related to the subject or matter in hand; fit or appropriate in any way.

The act of perturbing, or the state of being perturbed; esp., agitation of mind.
