The act of pervading, passing, or spreading through the whole extent of a thing.
Tending to pervade, or having power to spread throughout; of a pervading quality.
Turned aside; hence, specifically, turned away from the right; willfully erring.
The act of perverting, or the state of being perverted; a turning from truth or right.
The quality or state of being perverse; perverseness.
Pestering; vexatious; troublesome. Used also as an intensive. Judd.
The opinion or doctrine that everything in nature is ordered for or tends to the worst, or that the world is wholly evil.
A fatal epidemic disease; a pestilence; specif., the plague. England's sufferings by that scourge, the pest.
Specifically, the disease known as the plague; hence, any contagious or infectious epidemic disease that is virulent and devastating.
Pestilential; noxious; pernicious; mischievous. "Corrupt and pestilent." Milton.
Having the nature or qualities of a pestilence. "Sends the pestilential vapors." Longfellow.
One of the leaves of the corolla, or the colored leaves of a flower. See Corolla, and Illust.
A common baptismal name for a man. The name of one of the apostles, Peter boat, a fishing boat, sharp at both ends, originally of the Baltic Sea, but now common in certain English rivers.
Small; little; insignificant; mean; -- Same as Petty. By what small, petit hints does the mind catch hold of and recover a vanishing notion.
Small, little; of a woman or girl, of small size and trim figure.
A prayer; a supplication; an imploration; an entreaty; especially, a request of a solemn or formal kind.
To convert, as any animal or vegetable matter, into stone or stony substance.

Rock oil, mineral oil, or natural oil, a dark brown or greenish inflammable liquid, which, at certain points, exists in the upper strata of the earth, from whence it is pumped, or forced by pressure of the gas...
To do a petty business as a lawyer; also, to do law business in a petty or tricky way.
Little; trifling; inconsiderable; also, inferior; subordinate.
The quality or state of being petulant; temporary peevishness; pettishness.
Forward; pert; insolent; wanton. Burton. 2. Capriciously fretful; characterized by ill-natured freakishness.
A body of heavy-armed infantry formed in ranks and files close and deep.
That which has only an apparent existence; an apparition; a specter; a phantasm.
A title by which the sovereigns of ancient Egypt were designated. 2. See Faro.
One skilled in pharmacy; a pharmaceutist; a druggist.
The art or practice of preparing and preserving drugs, and of compounding and dispensing medicines according to prescriptions of physicians.
That which is exhibited to the eye; the appearance which anything manifests, especially any one among different and varying appearances of the same object.
Any one of numerous species of large gallinaceous birds of the genus Phasianus, and many other genera of the family Phasianidæ, found chiefly in Asia.
Relating to, or of the nature of, a phenomenon; hence, extraordinary; wonderful.
An appearance; anything visible; whatever, in matter or spirit, is apparent to, or is apprehended by, observation.
A glass vessel or bottle, especially a small bottle for medicines; a vial.
One who practices philanthropy; one who loves mankind, and seeks to promote the good of others.
Love to mankind; benevolence toward the whole human family; universal good will.
One versed in philately; one who collects postage stamps.
The collection of postage stamps of various issues.
Loving harmony or music.
A native or an inhabitant of ancient Philistia, a coast region of southern Palestine.
A lover or friend of women; one who esteems woman as the higher type of humanity.
One versed in philology.
To reason like a philosopher; to search into the reason and nature of things.
Literally, the love of, including the search after, wisdom; in actual usage, the knowledge of phenomena as explained by, and resolved into, causes and reasons, powers and laws.
having an unemotional and stolidly calm disposition
characteristic: Picture someone utterly unruffled, staying cool and calm even in a crisis.

definition: The pewee, or pewit.
A letter, character, or mark used to represent a particular sound. Phonograms are of three kinds: (1) Verbal signs, which stand for entire words.
A character or symbol used to represent a sound, esp. one used in phonography.
The science or doctrine of the elementary sounds uttered by the human voice in speech, including the various distinctions, modifications, and combinations of tones.
A picture or likeness obtained by photography. To take a picture or likeness of by means of photography.
One who practices, or is skilled in, photography.
The science which relates to the action of light on sensitive bodies in the production of pictures, the fixation of images, and the like.
An instrument for measuring the intensity of light, or, more especially, for comparing the relative intensities of different lights, or their relative illuminating power.
That branch of science which treats of the measurement of the intensity of light.
One of the larger divisions of the animal kingdom; a branch; a grand division.
A person skilled in physic, or the art of healing; one duty authorized to prescribe remedies for, and treat, diseases.
One versed in physics. 2. (Biol.) A believer in the theory that the fundamental phenomena of life are to be explained upon purely chemical and physical principles.
The science of nature, or of natural objects; that branch of science which treats of the laws and properties of matter, and the forces acting upon it.

The science which treats of the earth's exterior physical features, climate, life, etc., and of the physical movements or changes on the earth's surface, as the currents of the atmosphere and ocean, the secular...
The science which treats of the phenomena of living organisms; the study of the processes incidental to, and characteristic of, life.
The natural constitution, or physical structure, of a person. With his white hair and splendid physique.
A performer, esp. a skilled performer, on the piano.
An open square in a European town, especially an Italian town; hence (Arch.), an arcaded and roofed gallery.
Applied to that class of literature in which the principal personage is the Spanish picaro, meaning a rascal, a knave, a rogue, an adventurer.
A small coin of the value of six and a quarter cents. See Fippenny bit.
A high, stiff collar for the neck; also, a hem or band about the skirt of a garment, -- worn by men in the 17th century.
A small, shrill flute, the pitch of which is an octave higher than the ordinary flute.
One who, or that which, picks, in any sense, --.
Preserved in a pickle.
One who steals purses or other articles from pockets. Bentley.
Act of picking up,.
Formerly, an entertainment at which each person contributed some dish to a common table.
Furnished with pictures; represented by a picture or pictures.
Forming, or fitted to form, a good or pleasing picture; representing with the clearness or ideal beauty appropriate to a picture.
Trifling; trivial; frivolous; paltry; -- applied to persons and things.
Having spots and patches of black and white, or other colors; mottled.
In pieces; in parts or fragments. "On which it piecemeal brake." Chapman.
Any detached mass of masonry, whether insulated or supporting one side of an arch or lintel, as of a bridge.
To thrust into, penetrate, or transfix, with a pointed instrument. "I pierce .
Penetrated; entered; perforated.
Forcibly entering, or adapted to enter, at or by a point; perforating.
Veneration or reverence of the Supreme Being, and love of his character.
Any bird of the order Columbæ, of which numerous species occur in nearly all parts of the world.
Any material from which a dye, a paint, or the like, may be prepared; particularly, the refined and purified coloring matter ready for mixing with an appropriate vehicle.
Any one of several species of rodents of the genus Lagomys, resembling small tailless rabbits.
The small, troublesome tumors or swellings about the anus and lower part of the rectum which are technically called hemorrhoids.
To steal in small quantities, or articles of small value; to practice petty theft.
The journey of a pilgrim; a long journey; especially, a journey to a shrine or other sacred place.
The act of heaping up. 2. (Iron Manuf.) The process of building up, heating, and working, fagots, or piles, to form bars, etc.
The act of pillaging; robbery. Shak. 2. That which is taken from another or others by open force, particularly and chiefly from enemies in war.
A frame of adjustable boards erected on a post, and having holes through which the head and hands of an offender were thrust so as to be exposed in front of it.

One employed to steer a vessel; a helmsman; a steersman. Dryden. 2. Specifically, a person duly qualified, and licensed by authority, to conduct vessels into and out of a port, or in certain waters, for a fixed rate...
One who provides gratification for the lust of others; a procurer; a pander.
Any small acuminated elevation of the cuticle, whether going on to suppuration or not.
An instrument having two handles and two grasping jaws working on a pivot.
Compressing; nipping; griping; niggardly.
Woe; torment; pain. "Pyne of hell." Chaucer. 1. To inflict pain upon; to torment.
A tropical plant (Ananassa sativa); also, its fruit; -- so called from the resemblance of the latter, in shape and external appearance, to the cone of the pine tree.
The sound made by a bullet in striking a solid object or in passing through the air.
Languishing; drooping; wasting away, as with longing. 2. Wasting; consuming.
A moth of the genus Lithophane, as L. antennata, whose larva bores large holes in young peaches and apples.
An architectural member, upright, and generally ending in a small spire, -- used to finish a buttress, to constitute a part in a proportion, as where pinnacles flank a gable or spire, and the like.
Lit., painted; hence, piebald; mottled; pied. Any pied animal; esp., a pied or "painted" horse.
A soldier detailed or employed to form roads, dig trenches, and make bridges, as an army advances.
Playing on a musical pipe. "Lowing herds and piping swains." Swift. 2.
Stimulating to the taste; giving zest; tart; sharp; pungent.
A cotton fabric, figured in the loom, -- used as a dress goods for women and children, and for vestings, etc.
The act or crime of a pirate. 2. (Common Law) Robbery on the high seas.
A robber on the high seas; one who by open violence takes the property of another on the high seas.

The nut of the Pistacia vera, a tree of the order Anacardiaceæ, containing a kernel of a pale greenish color, which has a pleasant taste, resembling that of the almond, and yields an oil of agreeable taste and odor.
The smallest firearm used, intended to be fired from one hand, -- now of many patterns, and bearing a great variety of names.
A sliding piece which either is moved by, or moves against, fluid pressure.
A fork, or farming utensil, used in pitching hay, sheaves of grain, or the like.
The act of throwing or casting; a cast; a pitch.
A pit deceitfully covered to entrap wild beasts or men; a trap of any kind.
The soft spongy substance in the center of the stems of many plants and trees, especially those of the dicotyledonous or exogenous classes.
Consisting wholly, or in part, of pith; abounding in pith.
Deserving pity; wworthy of, or exciting, compassion; miserable; lamentable.
Full of pity; tender-hearted; compassionate; kind; merciful; sympathetic.
Destitute of pity; hard-hearted; merciless.
An allowance of food bestowed in charity; a mess of victuals; hence, a small charity gift.
A fixed pin or short axis, on the end of which a wheel or other body turns.
to make someone less angry or hostile; to soothe
characteristic: Like offering a crying child a toy, it calms an upset person and smooths things over.

definition: The first antiphon of the vespers for the dead. 2. (Med.) A prescription intended to humor or satisfy.
The act of placing, or the state of being placed. 2. Position; place.
The vascular appendage which connects the fetus with the parent, and is cast off in parturition with the afterbirth.
Pleased; contented; unruffied; undisturbed; serene; peaceful; tranquil.
The act or practice of plagiarizing. 2. That which plagiarized.
To steal or purloin from the writings of another; to appropriate without due acknowledgement (the ideas or expressions of another).
That which smites, wounds, or troubles; a blow; a calamity; any afflictive evil or torment.
A rectangular garment or piece of cloth, usually made of the checkered material called tartan, but sometimes of plain gray, or gray with black stripes.
In a plain manner; clearly.
Repining; complaining; lamenting. Dryden. 2. Expressive of sorrow or melancholy.
A flat fold; a doubling, as of cloth; a pleat.
An orrery. See Orrery.

The representation of the circles of the sphere upon a plane; especially, a representation of the celestial sphere upon a plane with adjustable circles, or other appendages, for showing the position of the heavens,...
A broad piece of sawed timber, differing from a board only in being thicker.
All the animals and plants, taken collectively, which live at or near the surface of salt or fresh waters.
One who plans; a projector.
Fixed in place, as a projecting member wrought on a separate piece of stuff.
The act or operation of setting in the ground for propagation, as seeds, trees, shrubs, etc.
Any flat, thin piece of metal, clay, ivory, or the like, used for ornament, or for painting pictures upon, as a slab, plate, dish, or the like, hung upon a wall.
A variety of quartz, of a color between grass green and leek green, which is found associated with common chalcedony.
An external application of a consistency harder than ointment, prepared for use by spreading it on linen, leather, silk, or other material.
Having the power to give form or fashion to a mass of matter.
The quality or state of being plastic. 2. (Physiol.) Plastic force. Dunglison.
A flat surface; especially, a broad, level, elevated area of land; a table-land.

The art or process of covering anything with a plate or plates, or with metal, particularly of overlaying a base or dull metal with a thin plate of precious or bright metal, as by mechanical means or by...
A metallic element, intermediate in value between silver and gold, occurring native or alloyed with other metals, also as the platinum arsenide (sperrylite).
a remark or statement that is overused and lacks originality
characteristic: Think of a tired saying like 'everything happens for a reason,' flat and meaningless from overuse.

definition: Formerly, a body of men who fired together; also, a small square body of soldiers to strengthen the angles of a hollow square.
One who plats or braids. A large plate or shallow dish on which meat or other food is brought to the table.
The duck mole. See under Duck.
A mark or expression of applause; praise bestowed. Not in the shouts and plaudits of the throng.
Worthy of being applauded; praiseworthy; commendable; ready. Bp. Hacket.
A beach; a strand; in the plains and deserts of Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona, a broad, level spot, on which subsequently becomes dry by evaporation.
A book of dramatic compositions; a book of the play. Swift.
Sportive; gamboling; frolicsome; indulging a sportive fancy; humorous.
A piece of ground used for recreation.
A companion in diversions; a playfellow.
A thing to play with; a toy; anything that serves to amuse. A child knows his nurse, and by degrees the playthings of a little more advanced age.
Time for play or diversion.
A maker or adapter of plays.
A public square in a city or town.
That which is alleged by a party in support of his cause; in a stricter sense, an allegation of fact in a cause, as distinguished from a demurrer.
The act of advocating, defending, or supporting, a cause by arguments.
In a pleasant manner.
Capable of affording pleasure or satisfaction; gratifying; abounding in pleasantness or pleasantry.
A vote by universal male suffrage; especially, in France, a popular vote, as first sanctioned by the National Constitution of 1791.
The one to whom a pledge is given, or to whom property pledged is delivered.
One who pledges, or delivers anything in pledge; a pledger; -- opposed to Ant: pledgee.
Containing plenty; abundant; copious; plentiful; sufficient for every purpose.
Overfullness; especially, excessive fullness of the blood vessels; repletion.
Capable of being plied, turned, or bent; easy to be bent; flexible; pliant.
Capable of plying or bending; readily yielding to force or pressure without breaking.
A kind of small pinchers with long jaws, -- used for bending or cutting metal rods or wire, for handling small objects such as the parts of a watch, etc.
To travel slowly but steadily; to trudge. Shak. 2. To toil; to drudge.
Progressing in a slow, toilsome manner; characterized by laborious diligence.
To fall, drop, or move in any way, with a sudden splash or slap, as on the surface of water.
Sport; frolic. To form a column from a line of troops on some designated subdivision.
Having pluck or courage; characterized by pluck; displaying pluck; courageous.
Any piece of wood, metal, or other substance used to stop or fill a hole.
The act of stopping with a plug. 2. The material of which a plug or stopple is made.
The edible drupaceous fruit of the Prunus domestica, and of several other species of Prunus.
The entire clothing of a bird. Note: It consist of the contour feathers, or the ordinary feathers covering the head, neck, and body.
A little mass or weight of lead, or the like, attached to a line, and used by builders, etc., to indicate a vertical direction.
One who works in lead; esp., one who furnishes, fits, and repairs lead, iron, or glass pipes, and other apparatus for the conveyance of water, gas, or drainage in buildings.
The art of casting and working in lead, and applying it to building purposes.
A piece of lead attached to a line, used in sounding the depth of water.
To take the goods of by force, or without right; to pillage; to spoil.
One who, or that which, plunges; a diver. 2. A long solid cylinder, used, instead of a piston or bucket, as a forcer in pumps.
More than perfect; past perfect; -- said of the tense which denotes that an action or event was completed at or before the time of another past action or event.
Relating to, or containing, more than one; designating two or more.
The state of being plural, or consisting of more than one; a number consisting of two or more of the same kind.
More, required to be added; positive, as distinguished from negative; -- opposed to Ant: minus.
The son of Saturn and Rhea, brother of Jupiter and Neptune; the dark and gloomy god of the Lower World.
Consisting of, or resembling, air; having the properties of an elastic fluid.

Inflammation of the lungs. Note: Catarrhal pneumonia, or Broncho-pneumonia, is inflammation of the lung tissue, associated with catarrh and with marked evidences of inflammation of bronchial membranes, often chronic.
One who poaches; one who kills or catches game or fish contrary to law.
A low wall, serving as a foundation, a substructure, or a terrace wall.
A metrical composition; a composition in verse written in certain measures, whether in blank verse or in rhyme, and characterized by imagination and poetic diction.
The art of composing poems; poetical skill or faculty.
One skilled in making poetry; one who has a particular genius for metrical composition.
An inferior rhymer, or writer of verses; a dabbler in poetic art. The talk of forgotten poetasters.
The principles and rules of the art of poetry. J. Warton.
The art of apprehending and interpreting ideas by the faculty of imagination.
The quality or state of being poignant.
Pricking; piercing; sharp; pungent. "His poignant spear." Spenser. "Poynaunt sauce." Chaucer.
Sharp; having a sharp point.
One who, or that which, points. Specifically: (a) The hand of a timepiece.
The act of sharpening. 2. The act of designating, as a position or direction, by means of something pointed, as a finger or a rod.
Having no point; blunt; wanting keenness; obtuse.
Weight; gravity; that which causes a body to descend; heaviness. "Weights of an extraordinary poise." Evelyn.
Having the qualities or effects of poison; venomous; baneful; corrupting.
A large North American herb of the genus Phytolacca (P. decandra), bearing dark purple juicy berries.
Drudging; servile. Bred to some poking profession. Gray.
That quality or condition of a body in virtue of which it exhibits opposite, or contrasted, properties or powers, in opposite, or contrasted, parts or directions.
To communicate polarity to.
Polemic; controversial; disputatious. -- Po*lem"ic*al*ly, adv. Polemical and impertinent disputations.
The art or practice of disputation or controversy, especially on religious subjects.
Made smooth and glossy, as by friction; hence, highly finished; refined.
a. & n. from Polish. Polishing iron, an iron burnisher; esp., a small smoothing iron used in laundries.
One versed or experienced in the science of government; one devoted to politics.
A dance of Polish origin, but now common everywhere. It is performed by two persons in common time.
A marine gadoid food fish of Europe (Pollachius virens). Called also greenfish, greenling, lait, leet, lob, lythe, and whiting pollack.
A marine gadoid fish (Pollachius carbonarius), native both of the European and American coasts.
Defiled; made unclean or impure; debauched. -- Pol*lut"ed*ly, adv. -- Pol*lut"ed*ness, n.
The act of polluting, or the state of being polluted (in any sense of the verb).
A game of ball of Eastern origin, resembling hockey, with the players on horseback.
An arrant coward; a dastard; a craven; a mean-spirited wretch. Shak. Base.
A whitish woolly plant (Teucrium Polium) of the order Labiatæ, found throughout the Mediterranean region.
A government by many persons, of whatever order or class. Cudworth.
Government by many rulers; polyarchy.
One who practices polygamy, or maintains its lawfulness.
The having of a plurality of wives or husbands at the same time; usually, the marriage of a man to more than one woman, or the practice of having several wives, at the same time.
Containing, or made up, of, several languages.
A plane figure having many angles, and consequently many sides; esp., one whose perimeter consists of more than four sides.
An instrument for multiplying copies of a writing; a manifold writer; a copying machine.
A body or solid contained by many sides or planes. 2. (Opt.) A polyscope, or multiplying glass.
A word of many syllables, or consisting of more syllables than three; -- words of less than four syllables being called monosyllables, dissyllables, and trisyllables.
Comprehending, or relating to, many arts and sciences; -- applied particularly to schools in which many branches of art and science are taught with especial reference to their practical application.
The doctrine of, or belief in, a plurality of gods. In the Old Testament, the gradual development of polytheism from the primitive monotheism may be learned.

The fruit of the tree Punica Granatum; also, the tree itself (see Balaustine), which is native in the Orient, but is successfully cultivated in many warm countries, and as a house plant in colder climates.
A knob or ball; an object resembling a ball in form; as: (a) The knob on the hilt of a sword.
The quality or state of being pompous; pompousness. Thackeray.
Displaying pomp; stately; showy with grandeur; magnificent.
A kind of cloak worn by the Spanish Americans, having the form of a blanket, with a slit in the middle for the head to pass through.
Capable of being weighed; having appreciable weight. -- Pon"der*a*ble*ness, n.
Deliberating. -- Pon"der*ing*ly, adv.
Very heavy; weighty.
A high priest. Especially: (a) One of the sacred college, in ancient Rome, which had the supreme jurisdiction over all matters of religion, at the head of which was the Pontifex Maximus.
The state or dignity of a high priest; specifically, the office of the pope.
A small horse. 2. Twenty-five pounds sterling. 3. A translation or a key used to avoid study in getting lessons.
A breed of dogs having curly hair, and often showing remarkable intelligence in the performance of tricks.
Pshaw! pish! nonsense! -- an expression of scorn, dislike, or contempt.
Having a poop; furnished with a poop. (b) Struck on the poop.
The act or shock of striking a vessel's stern by a following wave or vessel.
In a poor manner or condition; without plenty, or sufficiency, or suitable provision for comfort.
a. & n. from Pop. Popping crease. (Cricket) See under Crease.
Any plant or species of the genus Papaver, herbs with showy polypetalous flowers and a milky juice.
The common people; the vulgar; the multitude, -- comprehending all persons not distinguished by rank, office, education, or profession.
The act or process of populating; multiplication of inhabitants. 2. The whole number of people, or inhabitants, in a country, or portion of a country.
A member of the People's party. -- Pop`u*lis"tic (#), a.
Any Old Word rodent of the genus Hystrix, having the back covered with long, sharp, erectile spines or quills, sometimes a foot long.
One of the minute orifices in an animal or vegetable membrane, for transpiration, absorption, etc.
The flesh of swine, fresh or salted, used for food.
Licentious painting or literature; especially, the painting anciently employed to decorate the walls of rooms devoted to bacchanalian orgies.
Full of pores; having interstices in the skin or in the substance of the body.
A term used somewhat loosely to designate a rock consisting of a fine-grained base (usually feldspathic) through which crystals, as of feldspar or quartz, are disseminated.
A food made by boiling some leguminous or farinaceous substance, or the meal of it, in water or in milk, making of broth or thin pudding.
To indicate (events, misfortunes, etc.) as in future; to foreshow; to foretoken.
That which portends, or foretoken; esp., that which portends evil; a sign of coming calamity.
A man who has charge of a door or gate; a doorkeeper; one who waits at the door to receive messages.
A house where porter is sold. Porterhouse steak, a steak cut from a sirloin of beet, including the upper and under part.
A portable case for holding loose papers, prints, drawings, etc. 2. Hence: The office and functions of a minister of state or member of the cabinet.
That which is divided off or separated, as a part from a whole; a separated part of anything.
Having a dignified port or mien; of a noble appearance; imposing. 2. Bulky.
A person who poses or attitudizes, esp. mentally.
To dispose or set firmly or fixedly; to place or dispose in relation to other objects.
In a positive manner; absolutely; really; expressly; with certainty; indubitably.
To occupy in person; to hold or actually have in one's own keeping; to have and to hold.
One who possesses; one who occupies, holds, owns, or controls; one who has actual participation or enjoyment, generally of that which is desirable.
Capable of existing or occurring, or of being conceived or thought of.
An opossum. To play possum, To act possum, to feign ignorance, indifference or inattention, with the intent to deceive.
The price established by law to be paid for the conveyance of a letter or other mailable matter by a public post.
Belonging to the post office or mail service.
To date after the real time.
A large bill or placard intended to be posted in public places. 2. One who posts bills.
Later in time; hence, later in the order of proceeding or moving; coming after.
The race that proceeds from a progenitor; offspring to the furthest generation.
Of, pertaining to, or designating, the studies pursued after graduation, esp., after receiving the bachelor's degree at a college.
Born after the death of the father, or taken from the dead body of the mother.
The act of traveling post. 2. (Bookkeeping) The act of transferring an account, as from the journal to the ledger.
A post or courier; a letter carrier. 2. (Eng. Law) One of the two most experienced barristers in the Court of Exchequer, who have precedence in motions.
To defer to a future or later time; to put off; also, to cause to be deferred or put off.
Happening, or done, after dinner; after-dinner.
A paragraph added to a letter after it is concluded and signed by the writer.
Fit to be drunk; drinkable. "Water fresh and potable." Bacon. -- n. A potable liquid.
An Alkali element, occurring abundantly but always combined, as in the chloride, sulphate, carbonate, or silicate, in the minerals sylvite, kainite, orthoclase, muscovite, etc.
A plant (Solanum tuberosum) of the Nightshade family, and its esculent farinaceous tuber, of which there are numerous varieties used for food.
A term applied derisively to any literary or artistic work, and esp. a painting, done simply for money and the means of living.
The quality or state of being potent; physical or moral power; inherent strength.
One who is potent; one who possesses great power or sway; a prince, sovereign, or monarch.
To render active or potent. Coleridge.
A medley or mixture. Specifically: (a) A ragout composed of different sorts of meats, vegetables, etc., cooked together.
The vessels or ware made by potters; earthenware, glazed and baked. 2.
A soft composition, as of bread, bran, or a mucilaginous substance, to be applied to sores, inflamed parts of the body, etc.
Domestic fowls reared for the table, or for their eggs or feathers, such as cocks and hens, capons, turkeys, ducks, and geese.
To strike repeatedly with some heavy instrument; to beat. With cruel blows she pounds her blubbered cheeks.
Childish sullenness.
The quality or state of being poor or indigent; want or scarcity of means of subsistence.
Destitute of power, force, or energy; weak; impotent; not able to produce any effect.
A priest, or conjurer, among the North American Indians. Be it sagamore, sachem, or powwow.
That may be practiced or performed; capable of being done or accomplished with available means or resources.
In a practical way; not theoretically; really.
Experienced; expert; skilled.
One who is engaged in the actual use or exercise of any art or profession, particularly that of law or medicine.
dealing with things sensibly and realistically; practical
characteristic: Picture a no-nonsense problem solver who cares about what actually works, not theory.

definition: One who is pragmatic.
An extensive tract of level or rolling land, destitute of trees, covered with coarse grass, and usually characterized by a deep, fertile soil.
To spring or bound, as a horse in high mettle. Now rule thy prancing steed.
To talk much and to little purpose; to be loquacious; to speak foolishly.
To talk much and idly; to prate; hence, to talk lightly and artlessly, like a child.
Any one of numerous species of large shrimplike Crustacea having slender legs and long antennæ.
One who prays; a supplicant. 1. The act of praying, or of asking a favor.
a. & n. from Pray, v. Praying insect, locust, or mantis (Zoöl.), a mantis, especially Mantis religiosa.
One who preaches; one who discourses publicly on religious subjects. How shall they hear without a preacher Rom.
The act of delivering a religious discourse; the art of sermonizing; also, a sermon.
A introductory portion; an introduction or preface, as to a book, document, etc.
Depending on the will or pleasure of another; held by courtesy; liable to be changed or lost at the pleasure of another.
The act or state of preceding or going before in order of time; priority.
Going before; anterior; preceding; antecedent.
The act of going before, or forward. Lunisolar precession. (Astron.) See under Lunisolar.
The limit or exterior line encompassing a place; a boundary; a confine.
Falling or rushing headlong; rushing swiftly, violently, or recklessly.
Overhasty; rash.
The act of precipitating, or the state of being precipitated, or thrown headlong.
Steep, like a precipice.
A concise or abridged statement or view; an abstract; a summary.
Having determinate limitations; exactly or sharply defined or stated; definite.
The quality or state of being precise; exact limitation; exactness; accuracy.
to prevent something from happening; to make impossible in advance
characteristic: Like locking a door ahead of time, it shuts off a possibility before it can occur.

definition: Ripe or mature before the proper or natural time; early or prematurely ripe or developed.
One who, or that which, precedes an event, and indicates its approach.
One who precedes; one who has preceded another in any state, position, office, etc.
To decree beforehand; to foreordain; to predestinate. Young.
To determine (something) beforehand. Sir M. Hale. 2. To doom by previous decree.
A class or kind described by any definite marks; hence, condition; particular situation or state.
To assert to belong to something; to affirm (one thing of another).
That may be predicted.
The act of foretelling; also, that which is foretold; prophecy. The predictions of cold and long winters.
A previous liking; a prepossession of mind in favor of something; predisposition to choose or like.
To dispose or incline beforehand; to give a predisposition or bias to.
The quality or state of being predominant; superiority; ascendency; prevalence.
Having the ascendency over others; superior in strength, influence, or authority.
The quality or state of being preëminent; superiority in prominence or in excellence.
Eminent above others; prominent among those who are eminent; superior in excellence.
To settle upon (public land) with a right of preemption, as under the laws of the United States.
The act or right of purchasing before others. Specifically: (a) The privilege or prerogative formerly enjoyed by the king of buying provisions for his household in preference to others.
To engage by previous contract; to bind or attach previously; to preoccupy.
To establish beforehand.
To exist previously; to exist before something else.
Existence in a former state, or previous to something else. Wisdom declares her antiquity and preëxistence to all the works of this earth.
Something spoken as introductory to a discourse, or written as introductory to a book or essay.
Pertaining to, or of the nature of, a preface; introductory to a book, essay, or discourse.
A Roman officer who controlled or superintended a particular command, charge, department, etc.
The office, position, or jurisdiction of a prefect; also, his official residence.
Worthy to be preferred or chosen before something else; more desirable.
In preference; by choice. To choose Plautus preferably to Terence. Dennis.
The act of Preferring, or the state of being preferred; the setting of one thing before another.
Giving, indicating, or having a preference or precedence.
To show, suggest, or announce, by antecedent types and similitudes; to foreshadow.
The condition of being pregnant; the state of being with young. 2. Figuratively: The quality of being heavy with important contents, issue, significance, etc.
Adapted to seize or grasp; seizing; grasping.
The act of taking hold, seizing, or grasping, as with the hand or other member.
The office or dignity of a prelate; church government by prelates. Prelacies may be termed the greater benefices.
Introductory; previous; preceding the main discourse or business; prefatory.
An introductory performance, preceding and preparing for the principal matter.
Mature or ripe before the proper time.
To think on, and revolve in the mind, beforehand; to contrive and design previously.
First; chief; principal.
First; chief.
A reward or recompense; a prize to be won by being before another, or others, in a competition.
Previous warning, notice, or information; forewarning.
Giving previous warning or notice.
An apprentice. Piers Plowman. "My accuser is my prentice." Shak.
To take possession of before another.
To ordain or appoint beforehand: to predetermine: to foreordain. Milton.
Preparing the way for anything by previous measures of adaptation; antecedent and adapted to what follows.
The quality or state of being preponderant; superiority or excess of weight, influence, or power, etc.
Preponderating; outweighing; overbalancing; -- used literally and figuratively.
Tending to invite favor; attracting confidence, favor, esteem, or love.
Preoccupation; prior possession. Hammond. 2. Preoccupation of the mind by an opinion, or impression, already formed.
Something which foreshows or portends a future event; a prognostic; an omen.
Knowledge of events before they take place; foresight. God's certain prescience of the volitions of moral agents.
Having knowledge of coming events; foreseeing; conscious beforehand. Pope.
Depending on, or derived from, prescription; proper to be prescribed. Grafton.
The act of prescribing, directing, or dictating; direction; precept; also, that which is prescribed.
One who presents.
Feeling or perceiving beforehand.
Previous sentiment, conception, or opinion; previous apprehension; especially, an antecedent impression or conviction of something unpleasant, distressing, or calamitous, about to happen.
The act of presenting, or the state of being presented; presentation. " Upon the heels of my presentment." Shak.
The act or process of preserving, or keeping safe; the state of being preserved, or kept from injury, destruction, or decay.
Having the power or quality of preserving; tending to preserve, or to keep from injury, decay, etc.
To keep or save from injury or destruction; to guard or defend from evil, harm, danger, etc.
The function or condition of one who presides; superintendence; control and care.
Presiding or watching over. "Presidential angels." Glanvill. 2. Of or pertaining to a president.
a. & n. from Preside. Presiding elder. See under 2d Elder.
Urgent; exacting; importunate.
Quickly; immediately; in haste; suddenly. Presto! begone! 'tis here again.
In a presumable manner; by, or according to, presumption.
To assume or take beforehand; esp., to do or undertake without leave or authority previously obtained.
The act of presuming, or believing upon probable evidence; the act of assuming or taking for granted.
Based on presumption or probability; grounded on probable evidence; probable.
Full of presumption; presuming; overconfident or venturesome; audacious.
Making a false appearance; unreal; false.
One who lays claim, or asserts a title (to something); a claimant. Specifically, The pretender (Eng.
The act of laying claim; the claim laid; assumption; pretension. Spenser.
The act of pretending, or laying claim; the act of asserting right or title.
Full of pretension; disposed to lay claim to more than is one's; presuming.
Beyond of different from what is natural, or according to the regular course of things, but not clearly supernatural or miraculous.
Ostensible reason or motive assigned or assumed as a color or cover for the real reason or motive.
A kind of German biscuit or cake in the form of a twisted ring, salted on the outside.
To overcome; to gain the victory or superiority; to gain the advantage.
The quality or condition of being prevalent; superior strength, force, or influence.
Gaining advantage or superiority; having superior force, influence, or efficacy.
To shift or turn from one side to the other, from the direct course, or from truth.
Going before in time; being or happening before something else; antecedent.
Anything, as goods, etc., taken or got by violence; anything taken by force from an enemy in war.
Rated in price; valued.
A little prick; a small, sharp point; a fine, sharp process or projection, as from the skin of an animal, the bark of a plant, etc.
Full of sharp points or prickles; armed or covered with prickles.
A woman who officiated in sacred rites among pagans. Abp. Potter.
The office or character of a priest; the priestly function. Bk. of Com.
Like a prig; conceited; pragmatical. -- Prig"gish*ly, adv. -- Prig"gish-ness, n.
The state or condition of being prime or first, as in time, place, rank, etc., hence, excellency.
First; primary; original; chief. It hath the primal eldest curse upon it.
In a primary manner; in the first place; in the first place; in the first intention.
First in order of time or development or in intention; primitive; fundamental.
The chief ecclesiastic in a national church; one who presides over other bishops in a province.
The highest order of mammals. It includes man, together with the apes and monkeys.
First in order of time; original; primeval; primitive; primary. "Prime forests." Tennyson.
Belonging to the first ages; pristine; original; primitive; primary.
First; chief.
The state of being the firstborn of the same parents; seniority by birth among children of the same family.
First in order; primary; original; of earliest origin.
To be formal or affected in dress or manners; -- often with up. Halliwell.
Sovereignty; supreme power; hence, superiority; predominance; high, or the highest, station.
To fix or impress, as a stamp, mark, character, idea, etc., into or upon something.
One who prints; especially, one who prints books, newspapers, engravings, etc., a compositor.
The act, art, or practice of impressing letters, characters, or figures on paper, cloth, or other material.
Preceding in the order of time; former; antecedent; anterior; previous.
The quality or state of being prior or antecedent in time, or of preceding something else.
A religious house presided over by a prior or prioress; -- sometimes an offshoot of, an subordinate to, an abbey, and called also cell, and obedience.
A solid whose bases or ends are any similar, equal, and parallel plane figures, and whose sides are parallelograms.
Belonging to the earliest period or state; original; primitive; primeval.
An armed private vessel which bears the commission of the sovereign power to cruise against the enemy.
In a private manner; not openly; without the presence of others. 2. In a manner affecting an individual.
The act of depriving, or taking away; hence, the depriving of rank or office.
A peculiar benefit, advantage, or favor; a right or immunity not enjoyed by others or by all.
Invested with a privilege; enjoying a peculiar right, advantage, or immunity.
Privacy; secrecy; confidence. Chaucer. I will unto you, in privity, discover .
The quality or state of being probable; appearance of reality or truth.
To examine, as a wound, an ulcer, or some cavity of the body, with a probe.
Tried virtue or integrity; approved moral excellence; honesty; rectitude.
Having the nature of a problem; not shown in fact; questionable; uncertain.
A hollow organ or tube attached to the head, or connected with the mouth, of various animals, and generally used in taking food or drink.
The act of one who proceeds, or who prosecutes a design or transaction.
That which comes forth or results; effect; yield; issue; product; sum accruing from a sale, etc.
To make known by public announcement; to give wide publicity to; to publish abroad.
The act of proclaiming; official or general notice; publication. King Asa made a proclamation throughout all Judah.
Inclination; propensity; proneness; tendency. "A proclivity to steal." Abp.
To put off till to-morrow, or from day to day; to defer; to postpone; to delay.
The act of procuring or obtaining; obtainment; attainment. 2. Efficient contrivance.
A pointed instrument for pricking or puncturing, as a goad, an awl, a skewer, etc.
spending money or resources freely and recklessly; wastefully extravagant
characteristic: Think of someone burning through a fortune on luxuries until nothing is left.

definition: Something extraordinary, or out of the usual course of nature, from which omens are drawn.
To bring forward; to lead forth; to offer to view or notice; to exhibit.
One who produces, brings forth, or generates. 2. One who grows agricultural products, or manufactures crude materials into articles of use.
Having the quality or power of producing; yielding or furnishing results.
The quality or state of being productive; productiveness. Emerson. Not indeed as the product, but as the producing power, the productivity.
Not sacred or holy; not possessing peculiar sanctity; unconsecrated; hence, relating to matters other than sacred.
The act of professing or claiming; open declaration; public avowal or acknowledgment.
In a professional manner or capacity; by profession or calling; in the exercise of one's profession.
One who professed, or makes open declaration of, his sentiments or opinions.
The quality of state of being proficient; advance in the acquisition of any art, science, or knowledge.
One who has made considerable advances in any business, art, science, or branch of learning.
An outline, or contour.
In the construction of fieldworks, the erection at proper intervals of wooden profiles, to show to the workmen the sectional form of the parapets at those points.
Acquisition beyond expenditure; excess of value received for producing, keeping, or selling, over cost.
Yielding or bringing profit or gain; gainful; lucrative; useful; helpful.
The quality of state of being profligate; a profligate or very vicious course of life.
Overthrown; beaten; conquered. The foe is profligate, and run. Hudibras.
Descending far below the surface; opening or reaching to a great depth.
In a profound manner. Why sigh you so profoundly Shak.
The quality or state of being profound; depth of place, knowledge, feeling, etc.
Pouring forth with fullness or exuberance; bountiful; exceedingly liberal.
The act of one who is profuse; a lavishing or pouring out without sting.
An ancestor in the direct line; a forefather. And reverence thee their great progenitor.
Descendants of the human kind, or offspring of other animals; children.
Having the jaws projecting beyond the upper part of the face; - - opposed to orthognathous.
The act or art of foretelling the course and termination of a disease.
To indicate as future; to foretell from signs or symptoms; to prophesy.
That which is written or printed as a public notice or advertisement; a scheme.
The act of moving forward; a proceeding in a course; motion onward. 2.
Moving forward; proceeding onward; advancing; evincing progress; increasing.
The act of prohibiting; a declaration or injunction forbidding some action.
One who favors prohibitory duties on foreign goods in commerce; a protectionist.
That prohibits; prohibitory.
Tending to prohibit, forbid, or exclude; implying prohibition; forbidding.
Projecting or impelling forward.
The act of throwing or shooting forward. 2. A jutting out; also, a part jutting out, as of a building.
One who projects a scheme or design; hence, one who forms fanciful or chimerical schemes.
The indigent class in the State; the body of proletarians.
to increase rapidly in numbers; to multiply
characteristic: Picture cells dividing or rabbits multiplying, spreading and growing fast everywhere.

definition: Having the quality of generating; producing young or fruit; generative.
Extending to a great length; unnecessarily long; minute in narration or argument.
The quality or state of being prolix; great length; minute detail.
The preface or introduction to a discourse, poem, or performance.
To extend in space or length.
A walk for pleasure, display, or exercise. Burke. 2. A place for walking.

The son of Iapetus (one of the Titans) and Clymene, fabled by the poets to have surpassed all mankind in knowledge, and to have formed men of clay to whom he gave life by means of fire stolen from heaven.
The quality or state of being prominent; a standing out from something.
Standing out, or projecting, beyond the line surface of something; jutting.
Consisting of individuals united in a body or mass without order; mingled.
Making a promise or promises; affording hope or assurance.
Containing a promise or binding declaration of something to be done or forborne.
A high point of land or rock projecting into the sea beyond the line of coast.
The act of promoting, advancing, or encouraging; the act of exalting in rank or honor.
In a prompt manner.
To make known by open declaration, as laws, decrees, or tidings; to publish.
Bending forward; inclined; not erect. Towards him they bend With awful reverence prone.
A sharp-pointed instrument. Prick it on a prong of iron. Sandys. 2. The tine of a fork, or of a similar instrument.
Strongly marked; unequivocal; decided. Note:   views became every day more pronounced.
The act of uttering with articulation; the act of giving the proper sound and accent.
A shell, used as a die. See Props. To support, or prevent from falling, by placing something under or against.
A congregation of cardinals, established in 1622, charged with the management of missions.
To cause to continue or multiply by generation, or successive production.
A heavy gaseous hydrocarbon, C3H8, of the paraffin series, occurring naturally dissolved in crude petroleum, and also made artificially.
To drive forward; to urge or press onward by force; to move, or cause to move.
One who, or that which, propels. 2. A contrivance for propelling a steam vessel, usually consisting of a screw placed in the stern under water, and made to revolve by an engine.
The quality or state of being propense; natural inclination; disposition to do good or evil.
A declaration of something to come; a foretelling; a prediction; esp., an inspired foretelling.
To foretell; to predict; to prognosticate. He doth not prophesy good concerning me.
Containing, or pertaining to, prophecy; foretelling events.
A medicine which preserves or defends against disease; a preventive. Defending or preserving from disease.
Nearness in place; neighborhood; proximity. 2. Nearness in time. Sir T.
To appease to render favorable; to make propitious; to conciliate. Let fierce Achilles, dreadful in his rage, The god propitiate, and the pest assuage.
Convenient; auspicious; favorable; kind.
Making proposals; proposing. 1. One who makes a proposal, or lays down a proposition.
The relation or adaptation of one portion to another, or to the whole, as respect magnitude, quantity, or degree.
Adjusted to something else according to a proportion; proportional. Longfellow.
That which is proposed, or propounded for consideration or acceptance.
The act of setting or placing before; the act of offering. "Oblations for the altar of proposition." Jer.
To offer for consideration; to exhibit; to propose.
One who has the legal right or exclusive title to anything, whether in possession or not.
Individual right to hold property; ownership by personal title; property.
A game of chance, in which four sea shells, each called a prop, are used instead of dice.
The act driving forward or away; the act or process of propelling.
Tending, or having power, to propel; driving on; urging. " propulsive movement of the verse." Coleridge.
having the style or diction of prose; commonplace and unromantic
characteristic: Think of plain, flat everyday language with no poetry or sparkle to it.

definition: The part where the actors performed; the stage. 2. (Modern Theater) The part of the stage in front of the curtain.
To doom to destruction; to put out of the protection of law; to outlaw.
The act of proscribing; a dooming to death or exile; outlawry; specifically, among the ancient Romans, the public offer of a reward for the head of a political enemy.
The ordinary language of men in speaking or writing; language not cast in poetical measure or rhythm.
The act or process of prosecuting, or of endeavoring to gain or accomplish something.
One who prosecutes or carries on any purpose, plan, or business. 2. (Law) The person who institutes and carries on a criminal suit against another in the name of the government.
A new convert especially a convert to some religion or religious sect, or to some particular opinion, system, or party.
To convert to some religion, system, opinion, or the like; to bring, or cause to come, over.
That part of grammar which treats of the quantity of syllables, of accent, and of the laws of versification or metrical composition.
One who prospects; especially, one who explores a region for minerals and precious metals.
A summary, plan, or scheme of something proposed, affording a prospect of its nature.
The state of being prosperous; advance or gain in anything good or desirable.
Tending to prosperity; favoring; favorable; helpful. A happy passage and a prosperous wind.
Standing before; -- applied to a gland which is found in the males of most mammals, and is situated at the neck of the bladder where this joins the urethra.
To offer, as a woman, to a lewd use; to give up to lewdness for hire. "Do not prostitute thy daughter." Lev.
The act or practice of prostituting or offering the body to an indiscriminate intercourse with men.
Lying at length, or with the body extended on the ground or other surface.
One who takes the leading part in a drama; hence, one who takes lead in some great scene, enterprise, conflict, or the like.
The act of protecting, or the state of being protected; preservation from loss, injury, or annoyance.
Affording protection; sheltering; defensive. " The favor of a protective Providence." Feltham.
One who, or that which, defends or shields from injury, evil, oppression, etc.
One under the care and protection of another.
A body now known as alkali albumin, but originally considered to be the basis of all albuminous substances, whence its name.
To affirm in a public or formal manner; to bear witness; to declare solemnly.
One who protests; -- originally applied to those who adhered to Luther, and protested against, or made a solemn declaration of dissent from, a decree of the Emperor Charles V.
The original copy of any writing, as of a deed, treaty, dispatch, or other instrument.
The first martyr; the first who suffers, or is sacrificed, in any cause.
The viscid and more or less granular material of vegetable and animal cells, possessed of vital properties by which the processes of nutrition, secretion, and growth go forward.
An original or model after which anything is copied; the pattern of anything to be engraved, or otherwise copied, cast, or the like.
To thrust forward; to drive or force along. Locke. 2. To thrust out, as through a narrow orifice or from confinement.
The act of protruding or thrusting forward, or beyond the usual limit.
That which is protuberant swelled or pushed beyond the surrounding or adjacent surface.
Prominent, or excessively prominent; bulging beyond the surrounding or adjacent surface.
To swell, or be prominent, beyond the adjacent surface; to bulge out. S.
In a proud manner; with lofty airs or mien; haughtily; arrogantly; boastfully.
Proved. "Accusations firmly proven in his mind." Thackeray. Of this which was the principal charge, and was generally believed to beproven, he was acquitted.
Origin; source; provenience. Their age attested by their provenance and associations.
Dry food for domestic animals, as hay, straw, corn, oats, or a mixture of ground grain.
Mentioned or comprised in a proverb; used as a proverb; hence, commonly known.
On condition; by stipulation; with the understanding; if; -- usually followed by that.
The act of providing or preparing for future use or application; a making ready.
Foreseeing wants and making provision to supply them; prudent in preparing for future exigencies.
Effected by, or referable to, divine direction or superintendence.
One who provides, furnishes, or supplies; one who procures what is wanted.
The act of providing, or making previous preparation. Shak. 2. That which is provided or prepared.
An article or clause in any statute, agreement, contract, grant, or other writing, by which a condition is introduced, usually beginning with the word provided.
Serving or tending to provoke, excite, or stimulate; exciting. Anything that is provocative.
Having the power or quality of exciting resentment; tending to awaken passion or vexation.
Distinguished bravery; valor; especially, military bravery and skill; gallantry.
One that prowls. Thomson.
Accustomed to prowl, or engaged in roving stealthily, as for prey. "A prowling wolf." Milton.
In a proximate manner, position, or degree; immediately.
The quality or state of being next in time, place, causation, influence, etc.
The agency for another who acts through the agent; authority to act for another, esp.
A woman of affected modesty, reserve, or coyness; one who is overscrupulous or sensitive.
The quality or state of being prudent; wisdom in the way of caution and provision.
Sagacious in adapting means to ends; circumspect in action, or in determining any line of conduct.
Proceeding from, or dictated or characterized by, prudence; prudent; discreet.
The quality or state of being prudish; excessive or affected scrupulousness in speech or conduct.
Like a prude; very formal, precise, or reserved; affectedly severe in virtue.
To lop or cut off the superfluous parts, branches, or shoots of; to clear of useless material.
Uneasy with desire; itching; especially, having a lascivious curiosity or propensity.
Inspecting closely or impertinently. Syn. -- Inquisitive; curious. See Inquisitive.
A fictitious name assumed for the time, as by an author; a pen name.
The using of fictitious names, as by authors.
A lovely maiden, daughter of a king and mistress of Eros, or Cupid. She is regarded as the personification of the soul.
The application of the healing art to mental diseases. Dunglison.
= Psychanalysis, Psychanalytic.
One who is versed in, devoted to, psychology.
The science of the human soul; specifically, the systematic or scientific knowledge of the powers and functions of the human soul, so far as they are known by consciousness.
Any vital action or activity. Mivart. 2. (Med.) A disease of the mind.
Psychotherapeutics.
An extinct flying reptile; one of the Pterosauria. See Illustration in Appendix.
The earliest age at which persons are capable of begetting or bearing children, usually considered, in temperate climates, to be about fourteen years in males and twelve in females.
The hair which appears upon the lower part of the hypogastric region at the age of puberty.
The act of publishing or making known; notification to the people at large, either by words, writing, or printing.
A writer on the laws of nature and nations; one who is versed in the science of public right, the principles of government, etc.
The quality or state of being public, or open to the knowledge of a community.
With exposure to popular view or notice; without concealment; openly.
To make public; to make known to mankind, or to people in general; to divulge, as a private transaction.
One who publishes.
To gather into small folds or wrinkles; to contract into ridges and furrows.
Resembling Puck; merry; mischievous. "Puckish freaks." J. R. Green.
Short and fat or sturdy; dumpy; podgy.
Boyish; childish; trifling; silly. The French have been notorious through generations for their puerile affectation of Roman forms, models, and historic precedents.
Swelled with air, or any soft matter; tumid with a soft substance; bloated.
The practice of boxing, or fighting with the fist.
One who fights with his fists; esp., a professional prize fighter; a boxer.
Disposed to fight; inclined to fighting; quarrelsome; fighting. --Pug*na"cious*ly, adv.
Inclination or readiness to fight; quarrelsomeness. " A national pugnacity of character." Motley.
Power; strength; might; force; potency. " Youths of puissance." Tennyson.
Powerful; strong; mighty; forcible.
To eject the contests of the stomach; to vomit; to spew. The infant Mewling and puking in the nurse's arms.
That quality of appearance which pleases the eye; beauty; comeliness; grace.
A moist, slightly cohering mass, consisting of soft, undissolved animal or vegetable matter.
An elevated place, or inclosed stage, in a church, in which the clergyman stands while preaching.
To throb, as a pulse; to beat, as the heart. The heart of a viper or frog will continue to pulsate long after it is taken from the body.
To reduce of fine powder or dust, as by beating, grinding, or the like.
A large American carnivore (Felis concolor), found from Canada to Patagonia, especially among the mountains.

A low shoe with a thin sole. Swift. An hydraulic machine, variously constructed, for raising or transferring fluids, consisting essentially of a moving piece or piston working in a hollow cylinder or other cavity,...
a. & n. from pump. Pumping engine, a steam engine and pump combined for raising water.
A well-known trailing plant (Cucurbita pepo) and its fruit, -- used for cooking and for feeding stock.
Attentive to punctilio; very nice or exact in the forms of behavior, etiquette, or mutual intercourse.
The quality or state of being punctual; especially, adherence to the exact time of an engagement.
The act of puncturing; perforating with something pointed. 2. A small hole made by a point.
Having the surface covered with minute indentations or dots. 2. (Med.) Produced by puncture.
A learned man; a teacher; esp., a Brahman versed in the Sanskrit language, and in the science, laws, and religion of the Hindoos.
The quality or state of being pungent or piercing; keenness; sharpness.
Causing a sharp sensation, as of the taste, smell, or feelings; pricking.
To impose a penalty upon; to afflict with pain, loss, or suffering for a crime or fault, either with or without a view to the offender's amendment.
Deserving of, or liable to, punishment; capable of being punished by law or right.
Wood so decayed as to be dry, crumbly, and useful for tinder; touchwood.
The aperture in the iris; the sight, apple, or black of the eye. See the Note under Eye, and Iris.
The state of being a pupil. As sons of kings, loving in pupilage, Have turned to tyrants when they came to power.
A small image in the human form; a doll. 2. A similar figure moved by the hand or by a wire in a mock drama.
The young of a canine animal, esp. of the common dog; a whelp. 2. A name of contemptuous reproach for a conceited and impertinent person.
Wholly blind. "Purblind Argus, all eyes and no sight." Shak. 2. Nearsighted, or dim-sighted.
A dish made by boiling any article of food to a pulp and rubbing it through a sieve.
Tending to cleanse; cleansing; expiatory. Burke. A state or place of purification after death.
To cleanse, clear, or purify by separating and carrying off whatever is impure, heterogeneous, foreign, or superfluous.
The act of purifying; the act or operation of separating and removing from anything that which is impure or noxious, or heterogeneous or foreign to it.
To make pure or clear from material defilement, admixture, or imperfection.
The condition of being pure. Specifically: (a) freedom from foreign admixture or deleterious matter.
Originally, the ground near a royal forest, which, having been unlawfully added to the forest, was afterwards severed from it, and disafforested so as to remit to the former owners their rights.
To take or carry away for one's self; hence, to steal; to take by theft.
A color formed by, or resembling that formed by, a combination of the primary colors red and blue.
With purpose or design; intentionally; with predetermination; designedly.
A small bag or pouch, the opening of which is made to draw together closely, used to carry money in.
Acting in consequence or in prosecution (of anything); hence, agreeable.
To follow with a view to overtake; to follow eagerly, or with haste; to chase.
One who provides victuals, or whose business is to make provision for the table.
The body of a statute, or that part which begins with " Be it enacted, " as distinguished from the preamble.
One who, or that which, pushes.
Destitute of a manly or courageous strength and firmness of mind; of weak spirit.
A cat; -- a fondling appellation. 2. A hare; -- so called by sportsmen.
Commonly thought or deemed; supposed; reputed.
To render putrid; to cause to decay offensively; to cause to be decomposed.
Becoming putrid or rotten. Externally powerful, although putrescent at the core.
Tending to decomposition or decay; decomposed; rotten; -- said of animal or vegetable matter.
A stroke made on the putting green to play the ball into a hole. To make a putt.

Something which perplexes or embarrasses; especially, a toy or a problem contrived for testing ingenuity.
A garment, similar to the Oriental pyjama (which see), adopted among Europeans, Americans, and other Occidentals, for wear in the dressing room and during sleep.
A solid body standing on a triangular, square, or polygonal base, and terminating in a point at the top.
A funeral pile; a combustible heap on which the dead are burned; hence, any pile to be burnt.
An insane disposition to incendiarism.
Any species of very large snakes of the genus Python, and allied genera, of the family Pythonidæ.
To utter a sound like the cry of a duck. 2. To make vain and loud pretensions.
Having four feet. An animal having four feet, as most mammals and reptiles.
To drink with relish; to drink copiously of; to swallow in large draughts.
Soft, wet, miry land, which shakes or yields under the feet. "A spot surrounded by quagmires, which rendered it difficult of access." Palfrey.
To die; to perish; hence, to wither; to fade. Spenser. 2. To become quelled.
The act of qualifying, or the condition of being qualified. 2. That which qualifies.
Fitted by accomplishments or endowments. 2. Modified; limited.
To make such as is required; to give added or requisite qualities to; to fit, as for a place, office, occupation, or character.
Sickness; disease; pestilence; death. thousand slain and not of qualm ystorve .
A state of difficulty or perplexity; doubt; uncertainty. To bring into a state of uncertainty, perplexity, or difficulty.
The attribute of being so much, and not more or less; the property of being measurable, or capable of increase and decrease, multiplication and division.
Quantity; amount. "Without authenticating . . . the quantum of the charges." Burke.

A space of forty days; -- used of Lent. 2. Specifically, the term, originally of forty days, during which a ship arriving in port, and suspected of being infected a malignant contagious disease, is obliged to forbear...

Apt or disposed to quarrel; given to brawls and contention; easily irritated or provoked to contest.
Containing, or consisting of, a fourth part.
A composition in four parts, each performed by a single voice or instrument.

Same as Squash. To abate, annul, overthrow, or make void.
To tremble; to vibrate; to shake. Sir I. Newton. 2. Especially, to shake the voice.
A mole, bank, or wharf, formed toward the sea, or at the side of a harbor, river, or other navigable water, for convenience in loading and unloading vessels.
Sick at the stomach; affected with nausea; inclined to vomit; qualmish.
At variance with what is usual or normal; differing in some odd way from what is ordinary.
To die. Yet he did quake and quaver, like to quell. Spenser. 2. To be subdued or abated.
Given to quarreling; quarrelsome. land. 2. Apt to find fault; habitually complaining.
A question; an inquiry to be answered or solved. I shall conclude with proposing only some queries, in order to a .
= Questionary, above.
A tail-like appendage of hair; a pigtail. (b) A line of persons waiting anywhere.
A shift or turn from the point in question; a trifling or evasive distinction.

The metal mercury; -- so called from its resemblance to liquid silver.
A portion suitable to be chewed; a cud.
The state or quality of being quiescent. "Quiescence, bodily and mental." H.
Being in a state of repose; at rest; still; not moving.
One who, or that which, quiets.
Rest; repose; quiet; tranquillity. Shelley.
Final discharge or acquittance, as from debt or obligation; that which silences claims.
One of the large feathers of a bird's wing, or one of the rectrices of the tail.
Anything that is quilted; esp., a quilted bed cover, or a skirt worn by women.
The fruit of a shrub (Cydonia vulgaris) belonging to the same tribe as the apple.
A set or sequence of five, as in piquet. 2. (Mus.) The interval of a fifth.
A composition for five voices or instruments; also, the set of five persons who sing or play five-part music.
A smart, sarcastic turn or jest; a taunt; a severe retort; a gibe. Quips, and cranks, and wanton wiles.
Full of quirks; tricky.
exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical
characteristic: Picture a dreamer charging at windmills, chasing noble but hopeless and impractical goals.

definition: A riddle or obscure question; an enigma; a ridiculous hoax. 2. One who quizzes others.
Relating to quizzing: given to quizzing; of the nature of a quiz; farcical.
Such a number of the officers or members of any body as is competent by law or constitution to transact business.
A proportional part or share; the share or proportion assigned to each in a division.
Occurring or returning daily.
Master; lord; teacher; -- a Jewish title of respect or honor for a teacher or doctor of the law.
Furious; raging; extremely violent. The rabid flight Of winds that ruin ships.
A North American nocturnal carnivore (Procyon lotor) allied to the bears, but much smaller, and having a long, full tail, banded with black and gray.
One who, or that which, races, or contends in a race; esp., a race horse.

Spun yarn used in racking ropes.
A relater; a storyteller.
Having a strong flavor indicating origin; of distinct characteristic taste.
The quality of being radiant; brilliancy; effulgence; vivid brightness.
Emitting or proceeding as from a center; rays; radiating; radiate. 2. Especially, emitting or darting rays of light or heat.

That which radiates or emits rays, whether of light or heat; especially, that part of a heating apparatus from which the heat is radiated or diffused.
The pungent fleshy root of a well-known cruciferous plant (Paphanus sativus).
A right line drawn or extending from the center of a circle to the periphery.
Resembling, or having the character of, raff, or a raff; worthless; low.

One who engages in a raid.
Expressing reproach; insulting. Angels which are greater in power and might, bring not railing accusation against them.
Pleasantry or slight satire; banter; jesting language; satirical merriment.

A road or way consisting of one or more parallel series of iron or steel rails, patterned and adjusted to be tracks for the wheels of vehicles, and suitably supported on a bed or substructure.
Clothing in general; vesture; garments; -- usually singular in form, with a collective sense.

Abounding with rain; wet; showery.

Dissolute; lewd; debauched. The arduous task of converting a rakish lover.

To collect, and reduce to order, as troops dispersed or thrown into confusion.
A name sometimes given to the raven.

Roving; wandering; discursive.

Ramping; leaping; springing; rearing upon the hind legs; hence, raging.
That which fortifies and defends from assault; that which secures safety.

Having a rank smell or taste, from chemical change or decomposition; musty.
The deepest malignity or spite; deep-seated enmity or malice; inveterate hatred.
Full of rancor; evincing, or caused by, rancor; deeply malignant; implacably spiteful or malicious.
In a random manner.

To become, or be, rank; to grow rank or strong; to be inflamed; to fester.
To rave in violent, high-sounding, or extravagant language, without dignity of thought.
Given to plunder; disposed or accustomed to seize by violence; seizing by force.
Very swift or quick; moving with celerity; fast.
In a rapid manner.
One who, or that which, raps or knocks; specifically, the knocker of a door.
Relation; proportion; conformity; correspondence; accord. 'T is obvious what rapport there is between the conceptions and languages in every country.
Act or fact of coming or being drawn near or together; establishment or state of cordial relations.
A rascal; a good-for-nothing fellow. Howitt.
Rapacious; living upon prey; -- said especially of certain birds. (b) Adapted for seizing prey.
To make rare, thin, porous, or less dense; to expand or enlarge without adding any new portion of matter to.
In a rare manner or degree; seldom; not often.
The quality or state of being rare; rareness; thinness.
To pull off or pluck violently. 2. To slash; to hack; to slice. Rushing of helms and riving plates asunder.
The thimble-shaped fruit of the Rubus Idæus and other similar brambles.
Like a rasp, or the sound made by a rasp; grating. R. D. Blackmore.
A pawl, click, or detent, for holding or propelling a ratchet wheel, or ratch, etc.
To approve and sanction; to make valid; to establish; to settle; especially, to give sanction to, as something done by an agent or servant.
The relation which one quantity or magnitude has to another of the same kind.
The process of reasoning, or deducing conclusions from premises; deductive reasoning.
A fixed daily allowance of provisions assigned to a soldier in the army, or a sailor in the navy, for his subsistence.
