As relating to each; particularly; as each belongs to each; as each refers to each in order.
A divice of gauze or wire, covering the mouth or nose, to prevent the inhalation of noxious substances, as dust or smoke.
A putting off of that which was appointed; a postponement or delay. I crave but four day's respite.
Shining with brilliant luster; very bright. -- Re*splen"dent*ly, adv. With royal arras and resplendent gold.
Disposed or expected to respond; answering; according; corresponding. Wealth respondent to payment and contributions.
The act of responding. 2. An answer or reply. Specifically: (a) Reply to an objection in formal disputation.
a. & n. from Rest, v. t. & i. Resting spore (Bot.), a spore in certain orders of algæ, which remains quiescent, retaining its vitality, for long periods of time.
The act of restoring anything to its rightful owner, or of making good, or of giving an equivalent for any loss, damage, or injury.
Unwilling to go on; obstinate in refusing to move forward; stubborn; drawing back.
Never resting; unquiet; uneasy; continually moving.
The act of restoring or bringing back to a former place, station, or condition.
The act or process of restraining, or of holding back or hindering from motion or action, in any manner.
The act of restricting, or state of being restricted; confinement within limits or bounds.
A summing up; a condensed statement; an abridgment or brief recapitulation.
The act of resuming.
The act of rising again; resurrection.
Rising again, as from the dead. Coleridge. One who rises again, as from the dead.
To take from the grave; to disinter. 2. To reanimate; to restore to life.
A rising again; the resumption of vigor. 2. Especially, the rising again from the dead.
The sale of commodities in small quantities or parcels; -- opposed to wholesale.
One who, or that which, retains. 2. One who is retained or kept in service.
To take or receive again. 2. To take from a captor; to recapture.
To return the like for; to repay or requite by an act of the same kind.
The act of retaliating, or of returning like for like; retribution; now, specifically, the return of evil for evil.
To keep delaying; to continue to hinder; to prevent from progress; to render more slow in progress.
The quality or state of being reticent, or keeping silence; the state of holding one's tonque.
not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily; reserved
characteristic: Picture a quiet, guarded person who keeps their feelings locked away and says little.

definition: Resembling network; having the form or appearance of a net; netted.
The delicate membrane by which the back part of the globe of the eye is lined, and in which the fibers of the optic nerve terminate.
Private; secluded; quiet.
Reserved; shy; not forward or obtrusive.
To bend or curve back.
To touch again, or rework, in order to improve; to revise.
To trace back, as a line. Then if the line of Turnus you retrace, He springs from Inachus of Argive race.
To draw back; to draw up or shorten.
The act of retracting, or drawing back; the state of being retracted.
The act of retiring or withdrawing one's self, especially from what is dangerous or disagreeable.
To cut off; to pare away. Thy exuberant parts retrench. Denham. 2. To lessen.
The act of retributing; repayment. In good offices and due retributions, we may not be pinching and niggardly.
The act retrieving.
Fitted or designed to retroact; operating by returned action; affecting what is past.
Apparently moving backward, and contrary to the succession of the signs, that is, from east to west, as a planet.
The act of retrograding, or going backward; retrogradation. 2. (Biol.) Backward development.
To look backward; hence, to affect or concern what is past. It may be useful to retrospect to an early period.
Looking backward; contemplating things past; -- opposed to prospective.
A second union; union formed anew after separation, secession, or discord.
To unite again; to join after separation or variance. Shak.
To vamp again; hence, topatch up; to reconstruct.
The act of revealing, disclosing, or discovering to others what was before unknown to them.
The act of engaging in a revel; noisy festivity; reveling. And pomp and feast and revelry.
That which returns, or comes back, from an investment; the annual rents, profits, interest, or issues of any species of property, real or personal.
To regard with reverence, or profound respect and affection, mingled with awe or fear.
Profound respect and esteem mingled with fear and affection, as for a holy being or place.
Worthy of reverence; entitled to respect mingled with fear and affection.
Disposed to revere; impressed with reverence; submissive; humble; respectful.
Turned side for side, or end for end; changed to the contrary; specifically (Bot.
Serving to effect reversal, as of motion; capable of being reversed. Reversing engine, a steam engine having a reversing gear by means of which it can be made to run in either direction at will.
To turn back, or to the contrary; to reverse. Till happy chance revert the cruel scence.
One who reviles. 1. Cor. vi. 10.
The act of revising, or reviewing and reëxamining for correction and improvement.
To look at again for the detection of errors; to reëxamine; to review.
The act of revising; reëxamination for correction; review.
The act of reviving, or the state of being revived. Specifically: (a) Renewed attention to something, as to letters or literature.
To return to life; to recover life or strength; to live anew; to become reanimated or reinvigorated.
The act of calling back, or the state of being recalled; recall. One that saw the people bent for the revocation of Calvin, gave him notice of their affection.
Causing abhorrence mixed with disgust; exciting extreme repugnance; loathsome.
One who, or that which, revolves; specifically, a firearm ( commonly a pistol) with several chambers or barrels so arranged as to revolve on an axis, and be discharged in succession by the same lock.
Making a revolution or revolutions; rotating; -- used also figuratively of time, seasons, etc., depending on the revolution of the earth.
A strong pulling or drawing back; withdrawal. "Revulsions and pullbacks." SSir T.
To write again. Young.
To utter as a rhapsody, or in the manner of a rhapsody Sterne. To utter rhapsodies.
A recitation or song of a rhapsodist; a portion of an epic poem adapted for recitation, or usually recited, at one time.
The ramie or grass-cloth plant. See Grass-cloth plant, under Grass. Any one of three species of large South American ostrichlike birds of the genera Rhea and Pterocnemia.
The art of composition; especially, elegant composition in prose. 2. Oratory.
One well versed in the rules and principles of rhetoric. The understanding is that by which a man becomes a mere logician and a mere rhetorician.
A general disease characterized by painful, often multiple, local inflammations, usually affecting the joints and muscles, but also extending sometimes to the deeper organs, as the heart.
Gold and silver, or money. W. Wagstaffe. As long as the rhino lasted. Marryat.
Any pachyderm belonging to the genera Rhinoceros, Atelodus, and several allied genera of the family Rhinocerotidæ, of which several living, and many extinct, species are known.
An expression of thought in numbers, measure, or verse; a composition in verse.
In the widest sense, a dividing into short portions by a regular succession of motions, impulses, sounds, accents, etc., producing an agreeable effect, as in music poetry, the dance, or the like.
Pertaining to, or of the nature of, rhythm DAy and night I worked my rhythmic thought.
In a rhythmical manner.
A low, vulgar, brutal, foul-mouthed wretch; a lewd fellow. Spenser. Pope.
A fillet or narrow woven fabric, commonly of silk, used for trimming some part of a woman's attire, for badges, and other decorative purposes.
That which makes one rich; an abundance of land, goods, money, or other property.
The quality or state of being rich (in any sense of the adjective).
Affected with rickets. 2. Feeble in the joints; imperfect; weak; shaky.
The act of ridding or freeing; deliverance; a cleaning up or out. Thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field.
A sieve with coarse meshes, usually of wire, for separating coarser materials from finer, as chaff from grain, cinders from ashes, or gravel from sand.
One who, or that which, rides. 2. Formerly, an agent who went out with samples of goods to obtain orders.
The back, or top of the back; a crest. Hudibras. 2. A range of hills or mountains, or the upper part of such a range.
Fitted to excite ridicule; absurd and laughable; unworthy of serious consideration.
Prevailing; prevalent; abounding. Before the plague of London, inflammations of the lungs were rife and mortal.
Sweepings; refuse; the lowest order of society. Beau & Fl.
DRess; tackle; especially (Naut.), the ropes, chains, etc., that support the masts and spars of a vessel, and serve as purchases for adjusting the sails, etc.
Doing, or according with, that which is right; yielding to all their due.
The quality or state of being righteous; holiness; purity; uprightness.
According to right or justice.
Firm; stiff; unyielding; not pliant; not flexible. Upright beams innumerable Of rigid spears.
A succession of confused or nonsencial statements; foolish talk; nonsense.
Rigidity; stiffness. 2. (ed.) A sense of chilliness, with contraction of the skin.
To render turbid or muddy; to stir up; to roil. 2. To stir up in feelings.
A rent or long aperture; a chink; a fissure; a crack. Sir T. Browne. White frost.
An implement for cutting, trimming, or ornamenting the rim of anything, as the edges of pies, etc.
One who, or that which, rings; especially, one who rings chimes on bells.
The leader of a circle of dancers; hence, the leader of a number of persons acting together.
The smooth and level extent of ice marked off for the game of curling.
To wash lightly; to cleanse with a second or repeated application of water after washing.
Wanton or unrestrained behavior; uproar; tumult. His headstrong riot hath no curb.
To grow ripe; to become mature, as grain, fruit, flowers, and the like.
An implement, with teeth like those of a comb, for removing the seeds and seed vessels from flax, broom corn, etc.
A small ripple.
Having the faculty or power of laughing; disposed to laugh. Laughing is our busines, .
Attaining a higher place; taking, or moving in, an upward direction; appearing above the horizon.
Attended with risk or danger; hazardous. "A risky matter." W. Collins.
A kind of pottage.
The act of performing divine or solemn service, as established by law, precept, or custom.
The act of rivaling, or the state of being a rival; a competition. "Keen contention and eager rivalries." Jeffrey.
The side or bank of a river.

A metallic pin with a head, used for uniting two plates or pieces of material together, by passing it through them and then beating or pressing down the point so that it shall spread out and form a second head.
The act of joining with rivets; the act of spreading out and clinching the end, as of a rivet, by beating or pressing.
A small stream or brook; a streamlet. By fountain or by shady rivulet He sought them.
A cockroach. 1. (Zoöl.) (a) A European fresh-water fish of the Carp family (Leuciscus rutilus).
Land adjoining a road or highway; the part of a road or highway that borders the traveled part.
To go from place to place without any certain purpose or direction; to rove.
Having a bay, chestnut, brown, or black color, with gray or white thickly interspersed.
To cry with a full, loud, continued sound. Specifically: (a) To bellow, or utter a deep, loud cry, as a lion or other beast.
A loud, deep, prolonged sound, as of a large beast, or of a person in distress, anger, mirth, etc., or of a noisy congregation.
To cook by exposure to radiant heat before a fire.
a. & n., from Roast, v. Roasting ear, an ear of Indian corn at that stage of development when it is fit to be eaten roasted.
One who robs; in law, one who feloniously takes goods or money from the person of another by violence or by putting him in fear.
An outer garment; a dress of a rich, flowing, and elegant style or make.
Evincing strength; indicating vigorous health; strong; sinewy; muscular.
A seaport town in France. Rochelle powders. Same as Seidlitz powders. -- Rochelle salt (Chem.), the double tartrate of sodium and potassium, a white crystalline substance.
One who rocks; specifically, one who rocks a cradle. It was I, sir, said the rocker, who had the honor, some thirty years since, to attend on your highness in your infancy.
A cruciferous plant (Eruca sativa) sometimes eaten in Europe as a salad.
Having a swaying, rolling, or back-and-forth movement; used for rocking.
Full of, or abounding in, rocks; consisting of rocks.
A florid style of ornamentation which prevailed in Europe in the latter part of the eighteenth century.
Gnawing; biting; corroding; (Med.) applied to a destructive variety of cancer or ulcer.
A round-up. See Round-up.
To bluster; to swagger; to bully; to be bold, noisy, vaunting, or turbulent.
One who, or that which, rolls; especially, a cylinder, sometimes grooved, of wood, stone, metal, etc., used in husbandry and the arts.
Rotating on an axis, or moving along a surface by rotation; turning over and over as if on an axis or a pivot.
A species of fictitious writing, originally composed in meter in the Romance dialects, and afterward in prose, such as the tales of the court of Arthur, and of Amadis of Gaul.
To play rudely and boisterously; to leap and frisk about in play. 1. A girl who indulges in boisterous play.
A composition, vocal or instrumental, commonly of a lively, cheerful character, in which the first strain recurs after each of the other strains.
In Japan, under the feudal system, a samurai who had renounced his clan or who had been discharged or ostracized and had become a wanderer without a lord.
The breeding place of a colony of rooks; also, the birds themselves. Tennyson.
One of twe or more occupying the same room or rooms; one who shares the occupancy of a room or rooms.
The male of the domestic fowl; a cock. Nor, when they  wrung the neck of a rooster, did they trouble their heads whether he crowed for Congress or King George.
To turn up the earth with the snout, as swine. 2. Hence, to seek for favor or advancement by low arts or groveling servility.
Having taken root; firmly implanted; fixed in the heart. "A rooted sorrow." Shak.
A maker of ropes. P. Plowman. 2. One who ropes goods; a packer. 3. One fit to be hanged.
A bed of roses, or place where roses grow. "Thick rosaries of scented thorn." Tennyson.
Full of roses; rosy.
The flower of a rose before it opens, or when but partially open.
A labiate shrub (Rosmarinus officinalis) with narrow grayish leaves, growing native in the southern part of France, Spain, and Italy, also in Asia Minor and in China.
A valuable cabinet wood of a dark red color, streaked and variegated with black, obtained from several tropical leguminous trees of the genera Dalbergia and Machærium.
The rough, scaly matter on the surface of the bark of trees. To divest of the ross, or rough, scaly surface.
A register or roll showing the order in which officers, enlisted men, companies, or regiments are called on to serve.
Resembling a rose in color, form, or qualities; blooming; red; blushing.
Turning, as a wheel on its axis; pertaining to, or resembling, the motion of a wheel on its axis.
Having the parts spreading out like a wheel; wheel-shaped.
The act of turning, as a wheel or a solid body on its axis, as distinguished from the progressive motion of a revolving round another body or a distant point.
Having rotted; putrid; decayed.
A round building; especially, one that is round both on the outside and inside, like the Pantheon at Rome.
The state or quality of being rotu Smite flat the thick rotundity o'the world! Shak.
One devoted to a life of sensual pleasure; a debauchee; a rake.
In a rough manner; unevenly; harshly; rudely; severely; austerely.

A game of chance, in which a small ball is made to move round rapidly on a circle divided off into numbered red and black spaces, the one on which it stops indicating the result of a variety of wagers permitted by...
Circuitous; going round; indirect.
Modified by contraction of the lip opening; labialized; labial. See Guide to Pronunciation, § 11.
Round or nearly round; becoming round; roundish. 1. (Naut.) Small rope, or strands of rope, or spun yarn, wound round a rope to keep it from chafing.
Having power to awaken or excite; exciting. I begin to feel Some rousing motions in me.
A round of business, amusement, or pleasure, daily or frequently pursued.
Rowan tree. Rowan barry, a barry of the rowan tree.
One who engages in rows, or noisy quarrels; a ruffianly fellow. M. Arnold.
One who, or that which, rubs. Specifically: (a) An instrument or thing used in rubbing, polishing, or cleaning.
a. & n. from Rub, v.
Waste or rejected matter; anything worthless; valueless stuff; trash; especially, fragments of building materials or fallen buildings.
Water-worn or rough broken stones; broken bricks, etc., used in coarse masonry, or to fill up between the facing courses of walls.
Inclining to redness; ruddy; red. "His rubicund face." Longfellow.
That part of any work in the early manuscripts and typography which was colored red, to distinguish it from other portions.
That which is unformed or undeveloped; the principle which lies at the bottom of any development.
Causing one to rue or lament; woeful; mournful; sorrowful. 2. Expressing sorrow.
Full of asperities on the surface; broken into sharp or irregular points, or otherwise uneven.
One who rules; one who exercises sway or authority; a governor. And he made him ruler over all the land.
Predominant; chief; reigning; controlling.
a. & n. from Rumble, v. i.
Chewing the cud; characterized by chewing again what has been swallowed.
To chew the cud; to chew again what has been slightly chewed and swallowed.
The end of the backbone of an animal, with the parts adjacent; the buttock or buttoks.
To make uneven; to form into irregular inequalities; to wrinkle; to crumple.
One who, or that which, flees from danger, duty, restraint, etc.; a fugitive.
A letter, or character, belonging to the written language of the ancient Norsemen, or Scandinavians.
One who, or that which, runs; a racer. 2. A detective. Dickens. 3. A messenger.
Any animal which is unusually small, as compared with others of its kind.
The channel of a stream. 2. The beaten path made, by deer or other animals in passing to and from their feeding grounds.

A silver coin, and money of account, in the East Indies. Note: The valuation of the rupee of sixteen annas, the standard coin of India, by the United States Treasury departament, varies from time to time with the...
The act of breaking apart, or separating; the state of being asunder.
Having a rupture, or hernia.
An artifice; trick; stratagem; wile; fraund; deceit. Ruse de guerre ( Etym: , a stratagem of war.
Abounding or covered with rushes.
A Russian, or the Russians. 2. The language of the Russians. Of or pertaining to the Russians.
The reddish yellow coating formed on iron when exposed to moist air, consisting of ferric oxide or hydroxide.
To go into or reside in the country; to ruralize. Pope. To require or compel to reside in the country.
To make a quick succession of small sounds, like the rubbing or moving of silk cloth or dry leaves.
Covered or affected with rust.
Having no ruth; cruel; pitiless. Their rage the hostile bands restrain, All but the ruthless monarch of the main.

A season or day of rest; one day in seven appointed for rest or worship, the observance of which was enjoined upon the Jews in the Decalogue, and has been continued by the Christian church with a transference of the...
A sword with a broad and heavy blade, thick at the back, and usually more or less curved like a scimiter.
A carnivorous animal of the Weasel family (Mustela zibellina) native of the northern latitudes of Europe, Asia, and America, -- noted for its fine, soft, and valuable fur.
Scamped work. (b) Malicious waste or destruction of an employer's property or injury to his interests by workmen during labor troubles.
A sword with a broad and heavy blade, thick at the back, and usually more or less curved like a scimiter.
A anme formerly given to various dry Spanish wines. "Sherris sack." Shak.
The offering of anything to God, or to a god; consecratory rite. Great pomp, and sacrifice, and praises loud, To Dagon.
The sin or crime of violating or profaning sacred things; the alienating to laymen, or to common purposes, what has been appropriated or consecrated to religious persons or uses.
Violating sacred things; polluted with sacrilege; involving sacrilege.
Sacred; inviolable. Dr. H. More.
A seat for a rider, -- usually made of leather, padded to span comfortably a horse's back, furnished with stirrups for the rider's feet to rest in, and fastened in place with a girth.
Having a broad patch of color across the back, like a saddle; saddle-backed.
One who, or that which, defends or protects; defense; protection. Shak.
In a safe manner; danger, injury, loss, or evil consequences.
A Scandinavian legend, or heroic or mythic tradition, among the Norsemen and kindred people.
A suffriticose labiate plant (Salvia officinalis) with grayish green foliage, much used in flavoring meats, etc.
A respectful title or appelation given to Europeans of rank.
A respectful title or appelation given to Europeans of rank.
An extent of canvas or other fabric by means of which the wind is made serviceable as a power for propelling vessels through the water.
A boat propelled by a sail or sails.
The act of one who, or that which, sails; the motion of a vessel on water, impelled by wind or steam.
One who follows the business of navigating ships or other vessels; one who understands the practical management of ships.
Any one of several species of South American monkeys of the genus Pithecia.
Having a propensity to venery; lustful; lecherous. Dryden. -- Sa*la"cious*ly, dv.
A preparation of vegetables, as lettuce, celery, water cress, onions, etc., usually dressed with salt, vinegar, oil, and spice, and eaten for giving a relish to other food.
One who sells anything; one whose occupation is to sell goods or merchandise.
That quality or condition of being salient; a leaping; a springing forward.
Moving by leaps or springs; leaping; bounding; jumping. "Frogs and salient animals." Sir T.
Consisting of salt, or containing salt.

The secretion from the salivary glands. Note: In man the saliva is a more or less turbid and slighty viscid fluid, generally of an alkaline reaction, and is secreted by the parotid, submaxillary, and sublingual glands.
The willow; willow twigs. Tennyson. And bend the pliant sallow to a shield.
Any one of several species of fishes of the genus Salmo and allied genera.
An apartment for the reception of company; hence, in the plural, faschionable parties.
A spacious and elegant apartment for the reception of company or for works of art.
Somewhat salt; saltish.
Favorable to health; healthful; promoting health.
Wholesome; healthful; promoting health.
The act of saluting, or paying respect or reverence, by the customary words or actions.
The act of saving; preservation or deliverance from destruction, danger, or great calamity.
Hail! To say "Salve" to; to greet; to salute. By this that stranger knight in presence came, And goodly salved them.
An exception; a reservation; an excuse. They admit many salvos, cautions, and reservations.
An Israelite of Bible record (see Judges xiii.), distinguished for his great strength.
In the former feudal system of Japan, the class or a member of the class, of military retainers of the daimios, constituting the gentry or lesser nobility.
An establishment for the treatment of the sick; a resort for invalids.
The nine of trumps in sancho pedro.
To make sacred or holy; to set apart to a holy or religious use; to consecrate by appropriate rites.
Possessing sanctimony; holy; sacred; saintly. Shak. 2. Making a show of sanctity.
Solemn or ceremonious ratification; an official act of a superior by which he ratifies and gives validity to the act of some other person or body.

A sacred place; a consecrated spot; a holy and inviolable site. Hence, specifically: (a) The most retired part of the temple at Jerusalem, called the Holy of Holies, in which was kept the ark of the covenant, and...
A sacred place; hence, a place of retreat; a room reserved for personal use.
An old name of sandalwood, now applied only to the red sandalwood. See under Sandalwood.
A mythical person who makes children sleepy, so that they rub their eyes as if there were sand in them.
Consisting of, abounding with, or resembling, sand; full of sand; covered or sprinkled with sand.
Being in a healthy condition; not deranged; acting rationally; -- said of the mind.
Attended with much bloodshed; bloody; murderous.
optimistic or positive, especially in an apparently bad or difficult situation
characteristic: Think of a cheerful, hopeful person who expects the best even when things look grim.

definition: Abounding with blood; sanguine. 2. Of or pertaining to blood; bloody; constituting blood.
A health station or retreat; a sanatorium. "A sanitarium for troops." L.
The act of rendering sanitary; the science of sanitary conditions; the preservation of health.
The condition or quality of being sane; soundness of health of body or mind, especially of the mind.
Without; deprived or destitute of. Rarely used as an English word. "Sans fail." Chaucer.
The ancient language of the Hindoos, long since obsolete in vernacular use, but preserved to the present day as the literary and sacred dialect of India.
Having the power of affecting the organs of taste; possessing savor, or flavor.
The quality of being sapient; wisdom; sageness; knowledge. Cowper. Woman, if I might sit beside your feet, And glean your scattered sapience.
Wise; sage; discerning; -- often in irony or contempt. Where the sapient king Held dalliance with his fair Egyptian spouse.
Having or affording wisdom. -- Sa`pi*en"tial*ly, adv. The sapiential books of the Old .
Resembling soap; having the qualities of soap; soapy. Note: Saponaceous bodies are compounds of an acid and a base, and are in reality a kind of salt.
Native alumina or aluminium sesquioxide, Al2O3; corundum; esp., the blue transparent variety of corundum, highly prized as a gem.
Anciently, an Arab; later, a Mussulman; in the Middle Ages, the common term among Christians in Europe for a Mohammedan hostile to the crusaders.
A keen, reproachful expression; a satirical remark uttered with some degree of scorn or contempt.
Expressing, or expressed by, sarcasm; characterized by, or of the nature of, sarcasm.
In a sarcastic manner.
A species of limestone used among the Greeks for making coffins, which was so called because it consumed within a few weeks the flesh of bodies deposited in it.
Any one of several small species of herring which are commonly preserved in olive oil for food, especially the pilchard, or European sardine (Clupea pichardus).
grimly mocking or cynical
characteristic: Picture a bitter half-smile paired with a sharp, sneering, sarcastic remark.

definition: The principal garment of a Hindoo woman. It consists of a long piece of cloth, which is wrapped round the middle of the body, a portion being arranged to hang down in front, and the remainder passed across the bosom...
A scarf or band worn about the waist, over the shoulder, or otherwise.
The grand adversary of man; The Devil, or Prince of darkness; the chief of the fallen angels.
A little sack or bag for carrying papers, books, or small articles of wearing apparel.
To satisfy the desire or appetite of; to satiate; to glut; to surfeit.
An attendant attached to a prince or other powerful person; hence, an obsequious dependent.
Filled to satiety; glutted; sated; -- followed by with or of. "Satiate of applause." Pope.
The state of being satiated or glutted; fullness of gratification, either of the appetite or of any sensual desire.
A silk cloth, of a thick, close texture, and overshot woof, which has a glossy surface.
A composition, generally poetical, holding up vice or folly to reprobation.
To make the object of satire; to attack with satire; to censure with keenness or severe sarcasm.
The act of satisfying, or the state of being satisfied; gratification of desire.
Giving or producing satisfaction; yielding content; especially, relieving the mind from doubt or uncertainty, and enabling it to rest with confidence.
In general, to fill up the measure of a want of (a person or a thing).
The governor of a province in ancient Persia; hence, a petty autocrat despot.
To cause to become completely penetrated, impregnated, or soaked; to fill fully.
Filled to repletion; holding by absorption, or in solution, all that is possible.
Born under, or influenced by, the planet Saturn. 2. Heavy; grave; gloomy.
A sylvan deity or demigod, represented as part man and part goat, and characterized by riotous merriment and lasciviousness.
Showing impertinent boldness or pertness; transgressing the rules of decorum.
Cabbage cut fine and allowed to ferment in a brine made of its own juice with salt, -- a German dish.
To wander or walk about idly and in a leisurely or lazy manner; to lounge.
An article of food consisting of meat (esp. pork) minced and highly seasoned, and inclosed in a cylindrical case or skin usually made of the prepared intestine of some animal.
A tract of level land covered with the vegetable growth usually found in a damp soil and warm climate, -- as grass or reeds, -- but destitute of trees.
A man of learning; one versed in literature or science; a person eminent for acquirements.
One who saves.
One who saves, preserves, or delivers from destruction or danger. 2. Specifically: The (or our, your, etc.) Savior, he who brings salvation to men.
That property of a thing which affects the organs of taste or smell; taste and odor.
Pleasing to the organs of taste or smell. The chewing flocks Had ta'en their supper on the savory herb.
A variety of the common cabbage (Brassica oleracea major), having curled leaves, -- much cultivated for winter use.
To understand; to comprehend; know. Comprehension; knowledge of affairs.
Dust or small fragments of wood 9or of stone, etc.) made by the cutting of a saw.
A mill for sawing, especially one for sawing timber or lumber.
One whose occupation is to saw timber into planks or boards, or to saw wood for fuel.
A wind instrument of brass, containing a reed, and partaking of the qualities both of a brass instrument and of a clarinet.
An incrustation over a sore, wound, vesicle, or pustule, formed by the drying up of the discharge from the diseased part.
The case in which the blade of a sword, dagger, etc., is kept; a sheath.
A temporary structure of timber, boards, etc., for various purposes, as for supporting workmen and materials in building, for exhibiting a spectacle upon, for holding the spectators at a show, etc.
A scaffold; a supporting framework.
Any one of numerous species of marine bivalve mollusks of the genus Pecten and allied genera of the family Pectinidæ.
A small knife with a thin, keen blade, -- used by surgeons, and in dissecting.
A rascal; a swindler; a rogue. De Quincey. To perform in a hasty, neglectful, or imperfect manner.
Giving offense to the conscience or moral feelings; exciting reprobation.
Not full, large, or plentiful; scarcely sufficient; less than is wanted for the purpose.
Wanting amplitude or extent; narrow; small; not abundant. his dominions were very narrow and scanty.
A goat upon whose head were symbolically placed the sins of the people, after which he was suffered to escape into the wilderness.
A mark in the skin or flesh of an animal, made by a wound or ulcer, and remaining after the wound or ulcer is healed.
The quality or condition of being scarce; smallness of quantity in proportion to the wants or demands.
To scratch or cut the skin of; esp. (Med.), to make small incisions in, by means of a lancet or scarificator, so as to draw blood from the smaller vessels without opening a large vein.
A scar; a mark. We find upon the limestone rocks the scarrings of the ancient glacier which brought the bowlder here.
To strew about; to sprinkle around; to throw down loosely; to deposit or place here and there, esp.
Dispersed; dissipated; sprinkled, or loosely spread. 2. (Bot.) Irregular in position.
To cleanse, as streets, from filth. C. Kingsley.
A person whose employment is to clean the streets of a city, by scraping or sweeping, and carrying off the fifth.
A preliminary sketch of the plot, or main incidents, of an opera.
Assemblage of scenes; the scenes of a play; the disposition and arrangement of the scenes in which the action of a play, poem, etc., is laid.
A staff or baton borne by a sovereign, as a ceremonial badge or emblem of authority.
A combination of things connected and adjusted by design; a system. The appearance and outward scheme of things.
Given to forming schemes; artful; intriguing. -- Schem"ing*ly, adv.
The peculiar bronzelike luster observed in certain minerals, as hypersthene, schiller spar, etc.
Division or separation; specifically (Eccl.), permanent division or separation in the Christian church.
Holland gin.
One who attends a school; one who learns of a teacher; one under the tuition of a preceptor.
Like a scholar, or learned person; showing the qualities of a scholar.
Pertaining to, or suiting, a scholar, a school, or schools; scholarlike.
A shoal; a multitude.
A boy belonging to, or attending, a school.
A girl belonging to, or attending, a school.
Instruction in school; tuition; education in an institution of learning.
The man who presides over and teaches a school; a male teacher of a school.
In a scientific manner; according to the rules or principles of science.
One learned in science; a scientific investigator; one devoted to scientific study.
A spark; the least particle; an iota; a tittle. R. North.
To emit sparks, or fine igneous particles. As the electrical globe only scintillates when rubbed against its cushion.
The knowledge of a sciolist; superficial knowledge.
A shoot or sprout of a plant; a sucker. (b) A piece of a slender branch or twig cut for grafting.
The act of dividing with an instrument having a sharp edge. Wiseman.
To cut with scissors or shears; to prepare with the aid of scissors. Massinger.
Derision; ridicule; mockery; derisive or mocking expression of scorn, contempt, or reproach.
To find fault or rail with rude clamor; to brawl; to utter harsh, rude, boisterous rebuke.
a. & n. from Scold, v. Scolding bridle, an iron frame. See Brank, n., 2.
A large ladle; a vessel with a long handle, used for dipping liquids; a utensil for bailing boats.
To walk fast; to go quickly; to run hastily away.
To burn superficially; to parch, or shrivel, the surface of, by heat; to subject to so much heat as changes color and texture without consuming.
Burning; parching or shriveling with heat. -- Scorch"ing*ly, adv. -- Scorch"ing*ness, n.
Extreme and lofty contempt; haughty disregard; that disdain which aprings from the opinion of the utter meanness and unworthiness of an object.
A scorpion. 2. (Astron.) (a) The eighth sign of the zodiac, which the sun enters about the twenty-third day of October, marked thus  in almanacs.
Any one of numerous species of pulmonate arachnids of the order scorpiones, having a suctorial mouth, large claw-bearing palpi, and a caudal sting.
A concave molding used especially in classical architecture. Scotland  O Scotia! my dear, my native soil! Burns.
A mean, worthless fellow; a rascal; a villain; a man without honor or virtue.
To rub hard with something rough, as sand or Bristol brick, especially for the purpose of cleaning.
A lash; a strap or cord; especially, a lash used to inflict pain or punishment.
To wrinkle the brows, as in frowning or displeasure; to put on a frowning look.
To scrape, paw, or scratch with the hands; to proceed by clawing with the hands and feet.
To clamber with hands and knees; to scrabble.
Confused and irregular; awkward; scambling. -- Scram"bling*ly, adv. A huge old scrambling bedroom.
Something scraped off; hence, a small piece; a bit; a fragment; a detached, incomplete portion.
A blank book in which extracts cut from books and papers may be pasted and kept.
To rub over the surface of (something) with a sharp or rough instrument.
The act of scraping; the act or process of making even, or reducing to the proper form, by means of a scraper.
Consisting of scraps; fragmentary; lacking unity or consistency.
To rub and tear or mark the surface of with something sharp or ragged.
With the action of scratching.
Characterized by scratches.
Meager; thin; rawboned; bony; scranny.
A strip of plaster of the thickness proposed for the coat, applied to the wall at intervals of four or five feet, as a guide.
a. & n. from Screw, v. t. Screwing machine. See Screw machine, under Screw.
To card coarsely; to run through the scribling machine. 1. To write hastily or carelessly, without regard to correctness or elegance.
The act or process of carding coarsely. Scribbing machine, the machine used for the first carding of wool or other fiber.
One who writes; a draughtsman; a writer for another; especially, an offical or public writer.
Formerly, a skirmish; now, a general row or confused fight or struggle.
Contained in the Scriptures; according to the Scriptures, or sacred oracles.
Anything written; a writing; a document; an inscription. I have put it in scripture and in remembrance.
A roll of paper or parchment; a writing formed into a roll; a schedule.
The bag or pouch which contains the testicles; the cod.
To rub hard; to wash with rubbing; usually, to rub with a wet brush, or with something coarse or rough, for the purpose of cleaning or brightening.
Dwarfed or stunted; scrubby.
Full ofscrupules; inclined to scruple; nicely doubtful; hesitating to determine or to act, from a fear of offending or of doing wrong.
To examine closely; to inspect or observe with critical attention; to regard narrowly.
A scalion. A servant who cleans pots and kettles, and does other menial services in the kitchen.
One who sculptures; one whose occupation is to carve statues, or works of sculpture.
The art of carving, cutting, or hewing wood, stone, metal, etc., into statues, ornaments, etc., or into figures, as of men, or other things.
Using the low and indecent language of the meaner sort of people, or such as only the license of buffoons can warrant.
To hasten away or along; to move rapidly; to hurry.
Covered or affected with scurf or scabs; scabby; scurfy; specifically, diseased with the scurvy.
A broad, shallow basket. 2. A wide-mouthed vessel for holding coal: a coal hod.
A dangerous rock on the Italian coast opposite the whirpool Charybdis on the coast of Sicily, -- both personified in classical literature as ravenous monsters.
Grease; tallow; lard. Shak. Dryden. 1. The fold or line formed by sewing together two pieces of cloth or leather.
A woman whose occupation is sewing; a needlewoman.
Having a seam; containing seams, or showing them. "Many a seamy scar." Burns.
A session, as of some public body; especially, a meeting of spiritualists to receive spirit communication, so called.
Dry; withered; no longer green; -- applied to leaves. Milton. I have lived long enough.
Scorched; cauterized; hence, figuratively, insensible; not susceptible to moral influences.
A picture representing a scene at sea. Thackeray.
Affected with seasickness.
The land bordering on, or adjacent to, the sea; the seashore. Also used adjectively.
Occurring in good time, in due season, or in proper time for the purpose.
The act or process by which anything is seasoned. 2. That which is added to any species of food, to give it a higher relish, as salt, spices, etc.
The act of providong with a seat or seats.
Popularly, any plant or plants growing in the sea. 2. (Bot.) Any marine plant of the class Algæ, as kelp, dulse, Fucus, Ulva, etc.
Pertaining to, or secreting, fat; composed of fat; having the appearance of fat.
Cutting; divivding into two parts.
To withdraw from fellowship, communion, or association; to separate one's self by a solemn act.
The act of seceding; separation from fellowship or association with others, as in a religious or political organization.
The act of secluding, or the state of being secluded; separation from society or connection.
Suceeding next in order to the first; of second place, origin, rank, rank, etc.
Not original or primary; received from another. They have but a secondhand or implicit knowledge.
In the second place.
The office of a secretary; the place where a secretary transacts business, keeps records, etc.
To deposit in a place of hiding; to hide; to conceal.
Tending to secrete, or to keep secret or private.
In a secret manner.
Pertaining to a sect, or to sects; peculiar to a sect; bigotedly attached to the tenets and interests of a denomination.
A part of a circle comprehended between two radii and the included arc.
Coming or observed once in an age or a century. The secular year was kept but once a century.
In a secure manner; without fear or apprehension; without danger; safely.
A portable chair or covered vehicle for carrying a single person, -- usually borne on poles by two men.
Undisturbed by passion or caprice; calm; tranquil; serene; not passionate or giddy.
Tending to calm, moderate, or tranquilize; specifically (Med.), allaying irritability and irritation.
The matter which subsides to the bottom, frrom water or any other liquid.
The raising of commotion in a state, not amounting to insurrection; conduct tending to treason, but without an overt act.
To draw aside from the path of rectitude and duty in any manner; to entice to evil.
Seductive. "Thy sweet seducing charms." Cowper. -- Se*du"cing*ly, adv.
The act of seducing; enticement to wrong doing; specifically, the offense of inducing a woman to consent to unlawful sexual intercourse, by enticements which overcome her scruples.
Tending to lead astray; apt to mislead by flattering appearances; tempting.
Diligent in application or pursuit; constant, steady, and persevering in business, or in endeavors to effect an object.
A ripened ovule, consisting of an embryo with one or more integuments, or coverings.
Abounding with seeds; bearing seeds; having run to seeds. 2. Having a peculiar flavor supposed to be derived from the weeds growing among the vines.

One who seeks; that which is used in seeking or searching. 2. (Eccl.) One of a small heterogeneous sect of the 17th century, in Great Britain, who professed to be seeking the true church, ministry, and sacraments.
In appearance; in show; in semblance; apparently; ostensibly. This the father seemingly complied with.
Suited to the object, occasion, purpose, or character; suitable; fit; becoming.
To run or soak through fine pores and interstices; to ooze. Water seeps up through the sidewalks.
To decoct or prepare for food in hot liquid; to boil.
One of the parts into which any body naturally separates or is divided.
The act of segregating, or the state of being segregated; separation from others.
A lord; the lord of a manor. 2. A title of honor or of address in the South of Europe, corresponding to Sir or Mr.
A large net, one edge of which is provided with sinkers, and the other with floats.
An apparatus for registering the shocks and undulatory motions of earthquakes.
The act of taking or grasping suddenly. 2. (Naut.) (a) The operation of fastening together or lashing.
The act of seizing, or the state of being seized; sudden and violent grasp or gripe.
Rarely; not often; not frequently. Wisdom and youth are seldom joined in one.
Taken from a number by preferance; picked out as more valuable or exellent than others.
The act of selecting, or the state of being selected; choice, by preference.
Selecting; tending to select. This selective providence of the Almighty.
Caring supremely or unduly for one's self; regarding one's own comfort, advantage, etc., in disregard, or at the expense, of those of others.
The quality or state of being selfish; exclusive regard to one's own interest or happiness.
Having no regard to self; unselfish. Lo now, what hearts have men! they never mount As high as woman in her selfless mood.
One who sells. Chaucer.
Seeming; appearance; show; figure; form. Thier semblance kind, and mild their gestures were.
The seed of plants. 2. (Physiol.) The seed or fecundating fluid of male animals.
A period of six months; especially, a term in a college or uneversity which divides the year into two terms.
Half-yearly.
The half of a circle; the part of a circle bounded by its diameter and half of its circumference.
Half conscious; imperfectly conscious. De Quincey.
A group of students engaged, under the guidance of an instructor, in original research in a particular line of study, and in the exposition of the results by theses, lectures, etc.
An assembly or council having the highest deliberative and legislative functions.
One who sends. Shak.
The state of growing old; decay by time.
The quality or state of being senile; old age.
The quality or state of being senior.
A Spanish title of courtesy corresponding to the English Mr. or Sir; also, a gentleman.
A Spanish title of courtesy given to a lady; Mrs.; Madam; also, a lady.
A Spanish title of courtesy given to a young lady; Miss; also, a young lady.
An impression, or the consciousness of an impression, made upon the central nervous organ, through the medium of a sensory or afferent nerve or one of the organs of sense.

A faculty, possessed by animals, of perceiving external objects by means of impressions made upon certain organs (sensory or sense organs) of the body, or of perceiving changes in the condition of the body.
Destitute of, deficient in, or contrary to, sense; without sensibility or feeling.
The quality or state of being sensible, or capable of sensation; capacity to feel or perceive.
The quality or state of being sensitive; -- used chiefly in science and the arts.
The seat of sensation; the nervous center or centers to which impressions from the external world must be conveyed before they can be perceived.
Pertaining to, consisting in, or affecting, the sense, or bodily organs of perception.
Abounding with sentences, axioms, and maxims; full of meaning; terse and energetic in expression.
The quality or state of being sentient; esp., the quality or state of having sensation.
Having a faculty, or faculties, of sensation and perception. Specif. (Physiol.), especially sensitive.
A thought prompted by passion or feeling; a state of mind in view of some subject.
Having, expressing, or containing a sentiment or sentiments; abounding with moral reflections.
A soldier placed on guard; a sentinel. 2. Guard; watch, as by a sentinel.
Capable of being separated, disjoined, disunited, or divided.
To disunite; to divide; to disconnect; to sever; to part in any manner.
Designed or employed to separate. Separating funnel (Chem.), a funnel, often globe-shaped, provided with a stopcock for the separate drawing off of immiscible liquids of different specific gravities.
The act of separating, or the state of being separated, or separate. Specifically: (a) Chemical analysis.
One who withdraws or separates himself; especially, one who withdraws from a church to which he has belonged.
The ninth month of the year, containing thurty days.
Lasting or continuing seven years.
The place in which the dead body of a human being is interred, or a place set apart for that purpose.
Inclined to follow a leader; following; attendant. Trees uprooted left their place, Sequacious of the lyre.
One who, or that which, follows. Specifically: (a) An adherent, or a band or sect of adherents.
The state of being sequent; succession; order of following; arrangement.
To sequester.

Music sung or performed in the open air at nights; -- usually applied to musical entertainments given in the open air at night, especially by gentlemen, in a spirit of gallantry, under the windows of ladies.
Bright; clear; unabscured.
The quality or state of being serene; clearness and calmness; quietness.
The state or condition of a serf.
A woolen twilled stuff, much used as material for clothing for both sexes.
Formerly, in England, an officer nearly answering to the more modern bailiff of the hundred.
Notched on the edge, like a saw. 2. (Bot.) Beset with teeth pointing forwards or upwards.
Notched on the edge, like a saw. 2. (Bot.) Beset with teeth pointing forwards or upwards.
Crowded; compact; dense; pressed together. Nor seemed it to relax their serried files.

The watery portion of certain animal fluids, as blood, milk, etc. (b) A thin watery fluid, containing more or less albumin, secreted by the serous membranes of the body, such as the pericardium and peritoneum.
One who serves. 2. A tray for dishes; a salver. Randolph.
A name given to several trees and shrubs of the genus Pyrus, as Pyrus domestica and P.
Doing service; promoting happiness, interest, advantage, or any good; useful to any end.
a & n. from Serve. Serving board (Naut.), a flat piece of wood used in serving ropes.
Either of two annual herbaceous plants of the genus Sesamum (S. Indicum, and S.
Offset, n., 4. 2. A backset; a countercurrent; an eddy. 3. A backset; a check.
The act of setting, or the state of being settled. Specifically: -.
The act of one who, or that which, settles; the act of establishing one's self, of colonizing, subsiding, adjusting, etc.
One more than sixteen; ten and seven added.
Next in order after the sixth;; coming after six others. On the seventh day, God ended his work which he had made.
Seven times ten; one more than sixty-nine. 1. The sum of seven times ten.
To separate, as one from another; to cut off from something; to divide.
The act of severing, or the state of being severed; partition; separation.
Serious in feeeling or manner; sedate; grave; austere; not light, lively, or cheerful.
The quality or state of being severe. Specifically: -- (a) Gravity or austerity.
The contents of a sewer or drain; refuse liquids or matter carried off by sewers 2.
One who sews, or stitches. 2. (Zoöl.) A small tortricid moth whose larva sews together the edges of a leaf by means of silk.
The act or occupation of one who sews. 2. That which is sewed with the needle.
The sixth part of a circle. 2. An instrument for measuring angular distances between objects, -- used esp.
Six times as much; sixfold. 2. (Mus.) Divisible by six; having six beats.
The quality or state of being distinguished by sex. Lindley.
In a sexual manner or relation.
Torn or worn to rage; poor; mean; ragged. Wearing shabby coats and dirty shirts.
Full of shade or shadows; causing shade or shadow. "Shadowy verdure." Fenton.
Abounding in shade or shades; overspread with shade; causing shade. The shady trees cover him with their shadow.
Coarse hair or nap; rough, woolly hair. True Witney broadcloth, with its shag unshorn.
Shaggy; rough. Milton. -- Shag"ged*ness, n. Dr. H. More.
Rough with long hair or wool. About his shoulders hangs the shaggy skin.
The title of the supreme ruler in certain Eastern countries, especially Persia.
A temporary substitute for a bed, as one made on the floor or on chairs.
Caused to shake; agitated.
A person or thing that shakes, or by means of which something is shaken.
Shaking or trembling.
A shell or husk; a cod or pod. "The green shales of a bean." Chapman. 2.
That which deceives expectation; any trick, fraud, or device that deludes and disappoint.
A priest of Shamanism; a wizard among the Shamanists.
Bringing shame or disgrace; injurious to reputation; disgraceful. His naval preparations were not more surprising than his quick and shameful retreat.
Destitute of shame; wanting modesty; brazen-faced; insensible to disgrace.
To press or knead the whole surface of the body of (a person), and at the same time to stretch the limbs and joints, in connection with the hot bath.
To intoxicate and ship (a person) as a sailor while in this condition.
A plant; chard. Dryden. 1. A piece or fragment of an earthen vessel, or a like brittle substance, as the shell of an egg or snail.
One who holds or owns a share or shares in a joint fund or property.
To make sharp. Specifically: (a) To give a keen edge or fine point to.
A person who bargains closely, especially, one who cheats in bargains.
In a sharp manner,; keenly; acutely. They are more sharply to be chastised and reformed than the rude Irish.
The act of one who, or that which, shaves; specifically, the act of cutting off the beard with a razor.
A square or oblong cloth of wool, cotton, silk, or other textile or netted fabric, used, especially by women, as a loose covering for the neck and shoulders.
A case for the reception of a sword, hunting knife, or other long and slender instrument.
Bright; glittering; radiant; fair; showy; sheeny. This holy maiden, that is so bright and sheen.
Any one of several species of ruminants of the genus Ovis, native of the higher mountains of both hemispheres, but most numerous in Asia.
In general, a large, broad piece of anything thin, as paper, cloth, etc.
The head of an Arab family, or of a clan or a tribe; also, the chief magistrate of an Arab village.
A flat tablet or ledge of any material set horizontally at a distance from the floor, to hold objects of use or ornament.
A hard outside covering, as of a fruit or an animal. Specifically: (a) The covering, or outside part, of a nut.
Any aquatic animal whose external covering consists of a shell, either testaceous, as in oysters, clams, and other mollusks, or crustaceous, as in lobsters and crabs.
Groats; hulled oats. Simmonds.
Abounding with shells; consisting of shells, or of a shell. "The shelly shore." Prior.
That which covers or defends from injury or annoyance; a protection; a screen.
A man employed in tending, feeding, and guarding sheep, esp. a flock grazing at large.
A refreshing drink, common in the East, made of the juice of some fruit, diluted, sweetened, and flavored in various ways.
A Spanish light-colored dry wine, made in Andalusia. As prepared for commerce it is colored a straw color or a deep amber by mixing with it cheap wine boiled down.
A word which was made the criterion by which to distinguish the Ephraimites from the Gileadites.
Changing in place, position, or direction; varying; variable; fickle.
Destitute of expedients, or not using successful expedients; characterized by failure, especially by failure to provide for one's own support, through negligence or incapacity.
Full of, or ready with, shifts; fertile in expedients or contrivance. Wright.

A silver coin, and money of account, of Great Britain and its dependencies, equal to twelve pence, or the twentieth part of a pound, equivalent to about twenty-four cents of the United States currency.
A word used by Jacob on his deathbed, and interpreted variously, as "the Messiah," or as the city "Shiloh," or as "Rest.".
A kind of shallow plow used in tillage to break the ground, and clear it of weeds.
To shine with a tremulous or intermittent light; to shine faintly; to gleam.
A gleam or glimmering. "A little shimmering of a light." Chaucer.
The front part of the leg below the knee; the front edge of the shin bone.
Emitting light, esp. in a continuous manner; radiant.
Bright; luminous; clear; unclouded. Like distant thunder on a shiny day.
The act or process of shipping.

Arranged in a manner befitting a ship; hence, trim; tidy; orderly. Even then she expressed her scorn for the lubbery executioner's mode of tying a knot, and did it herself in a shipshape orthodox manner.
The breaking in pieces, or shattering, of a ship or other vessel by being cast ashore or driven against rocks, shoals, etc., by the violence of the winds and waves.
A yard, place, or inclosure where ships are built or repaired.
To procure by petty fraud and trickery; to obtain by mean solicitation.
The bullfinch.
One of the small pieces, or splinters, into which a brittle thing is broken by sudden violence.
A great multitude assembled; a crowd; a throng; -- said especially of fish.
Causing to shake or tremble, as by a blow; especially, causing to recoil with horror or disgust.
A fibrous material obtained by "deviling," or tearing into fibers, refuse woolen goods, old stockings, rags, druggets, etc.
One whose occupation it is to make shoes and boots. 2. (Zoöl.) (a) The threadfish.
A title originally conferred by the Mikado on the military governor of the eastern provinces of Japan.
The office or dignity of a Shogun.
Begone; away; -- an expression used in frightening away animals, especially fowls.
An inclined plane, either artificial or natural, down which timber, coal, etc., are caused to slide.
A trader who sells goods in a shop, or by retail; -- in distinction from one who sells by wholesale.
Larceny committed in a shop; the stealing of anything from a shop.
Somewhat worn or damaged by having been kept for a time in a shop.
Amount or extent of deficiency, as determined by some requirement or standard.
To make short or shorter in measure, extent, or time.
A compendious and rapid method or writing by substituting characters, abbreviations, or symbols, for letters, words, etc.
In a short or brief time or manner; soon; quickly. Chaucer. I shall grow jealous of you shortly.
The quality or state of being short; want of reach or extension; brevity.
A light, smooth-bored gun, often double-barreled, especially designed for firing small shot at short range, and killing small game.
To drive along by the direct and continuous application of strength; to push.
An implement consisting of a broad scoop, or more or less hollow blade, with a handle, used for lifting and throwing earth, coal, grain, or other loose substances.
A room or apartment where a show is exhibited. 2. A room where merchandise is exposed for sale, or where samples are displayed.
Applied as an appellation to a kind of shell invented by Gen. H. Shrapnel of the British army.
To utter a loud, sharp, shrill sound or cry, as do some birds and beasts.
Acute; sharp; piercing; having or emitting a sharp, piercing tone or sound.
A case, box, or receptacle, especially one in which are deposited sacred relics, as the bones of a saint.
The act of shrinking; a contraction into less bulk or measurement. 2. The amount of such contraction.
from Shrink. Shrinking head (Founding), a body of molten metal connected with a mold for the purpose of supplying metal to compensate for the shrinkage of the casting.
To draw, or be drawn, into wrinkles; to shrink, and form corrugations.

Provided with a shroud or shrouds. Shrouded gear (Mach.), a cogwheel or pinion having flanges which form closed ends to the spaces between the teeth and thus strengthen the teeth by tying them together.
To draw up or contract (the shoulders), especially by way of expressing dislike, dread, doubt, or the like.
To tremble or shake with fear, horrer, or aversion; to shiver with cold.
To shove one way and the other; to push from one to another.
Moving with a dragging, scraping step. "A shuffling nag." Shak. 2. Evasive.
To avoid; to keep clear of; to get out of the way of; to escape from; to eschew.
To shun; to move from. 2. To cause to move suddenly; to give a sudden start to.
One who shuts or closes. 2. A movable cover or screen for a window, designed to shut out the light, to obstruct the view, or to be of some strength as a defense.
The quality or state of being shy. Frequency in heavenly contemplation is particularly important to prevent a shyness bewtween God and thy soul.
A trickish knave; one who carries on any business, especially legal business, in a mean and dishonest way.
The quality or state of being sibilant; sibilation. Milton would not have avoided them for their sibilancy, he who wrote .
Making a hissing sound; uttered with a hissing sound; hissing.
To pronounce with a hissing sound, like that of the letter s; to mark with a character indicating such pronunciation.
Pertaining to the sibyls; uttered, written, or composed by sibyls; like the productions of sibyls.
Causing sickness; specif., causing surfeit or disgust; nauseating. -- Sick"en*ing*ly, adv.
A reaping instrument consisting of a steel blade curved into the form of a hook, and having a handle fitted on a tang.
The quality or state of being sick or diseased; illness; sisease or malady.
Having (such or so many) sides; -- used in composition.
Laterally; obliquely; in the direction of the side. 2. On the side.
Relating to the stars; starry; astral.

To transfer to a siding from a main line of track. 2. Hence, fig., to divert or reduce to a position or condition that is relatively secondary or subordinate in activity, importance, effectiveness, or the like.
A walk for foot passengers at the side of a street or road; a foot pavement.
Toward the side; sidewise. A second refraction made sideways. Sir I. Newton.
Attaching one's self to a party. 2. A side track, as a railroad; a turnout.
Clay that is colored red or brown by the oxides of iron or manganese, and used as a pigment.
A ridge of mountain and craggy rocks, with a serrated or irregular outline.
A short sleep taken about the middle of the day, or after dinner; a midday nap.
