State of being a sponsor.
The quality or state of being spontaneous, or acting from native feeling, proneness, or temperament, without constraint or external force.
Proceding from natural feeling, temperament, or disposition, or from a native internal proneness, readiness, or tendency, without constraint.
A spirit; a ghost; an apparition; a hobgoblin. Ld. Lytton. 2. (Zoöl.) The chimæra.
The quantity which a spoon contains, or is able to contain.
Occuring singly, or apart from other things of the same kind, or in scattered instances.
That which diverts, and makes mirth; pastime; amusement. It is as sport a fool do mischief.
Tending to, engaged in, or provocate of, sport; gay; froliscome; playful.
One who pursues the sports of the field; one who hunts, fishes, etc.
Without a spot; especially, free from reproach or impurity; pure; untained.
The projected spot or circle of light used to illuminate brilliantly a single person or object or group on the stage.
Marked with spots.
One who spots.
Full of spots; marked with spots.
To throw out forcibly and abudantly, as liquids through an office or a pipe.
To weaken, as a joint, ligament, or muscle, by sudden and excessive exertion, as by wrenching.
A small shoot or branch; a twig. Chaucer. The painted birds, companions of the spring, Hopping from spray, were heard to sing.
A merry frolic; especially, a drinking frolic; a carousal.
Sprightlike, or spiritlike; lively; brisk; animated; vigorous; airy; gay.
The act or process of one who, or that which, springs. 2. Growth; increase.
The season of spring; springtide.
One who sprinkles. 2. An instrument or vessel used in sprinkling; specifically, a watering pot.
To run very rapidly; to run at full speed. A runner  should be able to sprint the whole way.
Having great power of leaping or running; nimble; active. She is as spry as a cricket.
Frothy matter raised on liquids by boiling, effervescence, or agitation.
Wood that readily takes fire; touchwood; also, a kind of tinder made from a species of fungus.
not being what it purports to be; false or fake
characteristic: Picture a counterfeit bill or a phony claim that looks real but is completely fake.

definition: To drive back or away, as with the foot; to kick. with his foot will spurn adown his cup.
To contend for superiority in an unseemly maner; to scuffle; to struggle.
Dirty through neglect; foul; filthy; extremely dirty. Uncomed his locks, and squalid his attrie.
A sudden violent gust of wind often attended with rain or snow. The gray skirts of a lifting squall.
Squalidness; foulness; filthness; squalidity. The heterogenous indigent multitude, everywhere wearing nearly the same aspect of squalor.
to waste something in a reckless and foolish manner
characteristic: Think of pouring money down the drain or throwing away a golden opportunity.

definition: One who squats; specifically, one who settles unlawfully upon land without a title.
A female; a woman; -- in the language of Indian tribes of the Algonquin family, correlative of sannup.
To utter a sharp, shrill cry, usually of short duration; to cry with an acute tone, as an animal.
To cry with a sharp, shrill, prolonged sound, as certain animals do, indicating want, displeasure, or pain.
Having a stomach that is easily or nauseated; hence, nice to excess in taste.
To press between two bodies; to press together closely; to compress; often, to compress so as to expel juice, moisture, etc.
The act of pressing; compression; oppression. 2. pl. That which is forced out by pressure.
To quell; to crush; to silence or put down. Oh 't was your luck and mine to be squelched.
Any one of numerous species of ten-armed cephalopods having a long, tapered body, and a caudal fin on each side.
Looking obliquely. Specifically (Med.), not having the optic axes coincident.
To twist about briskly with contor.
Any one of numerous species of small rodents belonging to the genus Sciurus and several allied genera of the famly Sciuridæ.
To drive or eject in a stream out of a narrow pipe or orifice.
To pierce with a pointed weapon; to wound or kill by the thrust of a pointed instrument.
The state or quality of being stable, or firm; steadiness; firmness; strength to stand without being moved or overthrown.
A stable keeper. De Foe.
Disconnected; separated; distinct; -- a direction to perform the notes of a passage in a short, distinct, and pointed manner.
A large pile of hay, grain, straw, or the like, usually of a nearly conical form, but sometimes rectangular or oblong, contracted at the top to a point or ridge, and sometimes covered with thatch.
from Stack. Stacking band, Stacking belt, a band or rope used in binding thatch or straw upon a stack.
A Greek measure of length, being the chief one used for itinerary distances, also adopted by the Romans for nautical and astronomical measurements.
A coach that runs regularly from one stage, station, or place to another, for the conveyance of passengers.
A structure of posts and boards for supporting workmen, etc., as in building.
That stagnates; not flowing; not running in a current or steam; motionless.
The condition of being stagnant; cessation of flowing or circulation, as of a fluid.
Having an air or manner characteristic of the stage; theatrical; artificial.
Free from stain; immaculate. Shak. The veery care he took to keep his name Stainless, with some was evidence of shame.
One step of a series for ascending or descending to a different level.
A flight of stairs with their supporting framework, casing, balusters, etc.
A flight of stairs or steps; a staircase. "A rude and narrow stairway." Moore.
A piece of wood, usually long and slender, pointed at one end so as to be easily driven into the ground as a support or stay.
The position of the king when he can not move without being placed on check and there is no other piece which can be moved.
Having a stalk or stem; borne upon a stem. Stalked barnacle (Zoöl.), a goose barnacle, or anatifer.
One who stalks. 2. A kind of fishing net.
Put or kept in a stall; hence, fatted. "A stalled ox." Prov. xv. 17.
Stabling. Tennyson.
A male horse not castrated; a male horse kept for breeding.
Brave; bold; strong; redoubted; daring; vehement; violent. "A stalwart tiller of the soil." Prof.
To make involuntary stops in uttering syllables or words; to hesitate or falter in speaking.
Apt to stammer; hesitating in speech; stuttering. -- Stam"mer*ing*ly, adv.
A wild, headlong scamper, or running away, of a number of animals; usually caused by fright.
from Stamp, v. Stamping ground, a place frequented, and much trodden, by animals, wild or domesticated.
To stop the flowing of, as blood; to check; also, to stop the flowing of blood from.
A prop or support; a piece of timber in the form of a stake or post, used for a support or stay.
A flag; colors; a banner; especially, a national or other ensign. His armies, in the following day, On those fair plains their standards proud display.
Remaining erect; not cut down.
A fixed point or station; a basis or fundamental principle; a position from which objects or principles are viewed, and according to which they are compared and judged.
A standing without moving forward or backward; a stop; a state or rest.
A number of lines or verses forming a division of a song or poem, and agreeing in meter, rhyme, number of lines, etc., with other divisions.
A dealer in staple goods. 2. One employed to assort wool according to its staple.
That side of a vessel which is one of the right hand of a person who stands on board facing the bow.
Any one of numerous species of echinoderms belonging to the class Asterioidea, in which the body is star-shaped and usually has five rays, though the number of rays varies from five to forty or more.
Stiff; rigid. Chaucer. Whose senses all were straight benumbed and stark.
The light given by the stars. Nor walk by moon, Or glittering starlight, without thee is sweet.
Any passerine bird belonging to Sturnus and allied genera. The European starling (Sturnus vulgaris) is dark brown or greenish black, with a metallic gloss, and spotted with yellowish white.
Abounding with stars; adorned with stars. "Above the starry sky." Pope.
To leap; to jump. 2. To move suddenly, as with a spring or leap, from surprise, pain, or other sudden feeling or emotion, or by a voluntary act.
One who, or that which, starts.

The act of starving, or the state of being starved. Note: This word was first used, according to Horace Walpole, by Henry Dundas, the first Lord Melville, in a speech on American affairs in 1775, which obtained for...
To die; to perish. Lydgate. In hot coals he hath himself raked . . . Thus starved this worthy mighty Hercules.
A slackening or arrest of the blood current in the vessels, due not to a lessening of the heart's beat, but presumably to some abnormal resistance of the capillary walls.
The art of conducting state affairs; state management; statesmanship.
Settled; established; fixed. He is capable of corruption who receives more than what is the stated and unquestionable fee of his office.
Evincing state or dignity; lofty; majestic; grand.
Resting; acting by mere weight without motion.
That branch of mechanics which treats of the equilibrium of forces, or relates to bodies as held at rest by the forces acting on them.
The act of one who states anything; statement.
Not moving; not appearing to move; stable; fixed. Charles Wesley, who is a more stationary man, does not believe the story.
The articles usually sold by stationers, as paper, pens, ink, quills, blank books, etc.
In the way of statistics.
One versed in statistics; one who collects and classifies facts for statistics.
The science which has to do with the collection and classification of certain facts respecting the condition of the people in a state.
Partaking of, or exemplifying, the characteristics of a statue; having the symmetry, or other excellence, of a statue artistically made.

A small statue; -- usually applied to a figure much less than life size, especially when of marble or bronze, or of plaster or clay as a preparation for the marble or bronze, as distinguished from a figure in terra...
The natural height of an animal body; -- generally used of the human body.
State; condition; position of affairs.
Enacted by statute; depending on statute for its authority.
Firmly fixed or established; fast fixed; firm. "This steadfast globe of earth." Spenser.
In a steady manner.
A slice of beef, broiled, or cut for broiling; -- also extended to the meat of other large animals.
A vessel propelled by steam; a steamship or steamboat. 2. A steam fire engine.
Consisting of, or resembling, steam; full of steam; vaporous; misty. Cowper.
A horse, especially a spirited horse for state of war; -- used chiefly in poetry or stately prose.
from Steer, v. Steering wheel (Naut.), the wheel by means of which the rudder of a vessel is turned and the vessel is steered.
To gleam. His head bald, that shone as any glass, . . . stemed as a furnace of a leed .
A thin plate of metal, leather, or other material, used in painting, marking, etc.
A daughter of one's wife or husband by a former marriage.
The husband of one's mother by a subsequent marriage.
The wife of one's father by a subsequent marriage.
One of the vast plains in Southeastern Europe and in Asia, generally elevated, and free from wood, analogous to many of the prairies in Western North America.
Provided with a step or steps; having a series of offsets or parts resembling the steps of stairs.
A son of one's husband or wife by a former marriage.
A plate forming an exact faximile of a page of type or of an engraving, used in printing books, etc.
Formed into, or printed from, stereotype plates. 2. Fig.: Formed in a fixed, unchangeable manner.
Producing little or no crop; barren; unfruitful; unproductive; not fertile.
The black tern. Having a certain hardness or severity of nature, manner, or aspect.
A plate of cartilage, or a series of bony or cartilaginous plates or segments, in the median line of the pectoral skeleton of most vertebrates above fishes.
Characterized by a deep snoring, which accompaines inspiration in some diseases, especially apoplexy.
An instrument used in auscultation for examining the organs of the chest, as the heart and lungs, by conveying to the ear of the examiner the sounds produced in the thorax.
One whose occupation is to load and unload vessels in port; one who stows a cargo in a hold.
A female steward; specifically, a woman employed in passenger vessels to attend to the wants of female passengers.
One who, or that which, sticks.
a. & n. from Stick, v. Sticking piece, a piece of beef cut from the neck.
Having the quality of sticking to a surface; adhesive; gluey; viscous.
Not easily bent; not flexible or pliant; not limber or flaccid; rigid.
The joint next above the hock, and near the flank, in the hind leg of the horse and allied animals.
Stifling. The close and stifled study. Hawthorne.
A mark made with a burning iron; a brand. 2. Any mark of infamy or disgrace.
To mark with a stigma, or brand.
The quality or state of being still; quietness; silence; calmness; inactivity.
Elevated as if on stilts; hence, pompous; bombastic.
Serving to stimulate. 2. (Physiol.) Produced increased vital action in the organism, or in any of its parts.
To excite as if with a goad; to excite, rouse, or animate, to action or more vigorous exertion by some pungent motive or by persuasion.
The act of stimulating, or the state of being stimulated. 2. (Physiol.) The irritating action of various agents (stimuli) on muscles, nerves, or a sensory end organ, by which activity is evoked.
A goad; hence, something that rouses the mind or spirits; an incentive.
Any sharp organ of offense and defense, especially when connected with a poison gland, and adapted to inflict a wound by piercing.
One who, or that which, stings. Professor E. Forbes states that only a small minority of the medusæ of our seas are stingers.
Piercing, or capable of piercing, with a sting; inflicting acute pain as if with a sting, goad, or pointed weapon.
Stinging; able to sting. Extremely close and covetous; meanly avaricious.
To emit a strong, offensive smell; to send out a disgusting odor. To cause to stink.
One who, or that which, stinks. 2. (Zoöl.) Any one of the several species of large antarctic petrels which feed on blubber and carrion and have an offensive odor, as the giant fulmar.
from Stink, v. Stinking badger (Zoöl.), the teledu. -- Stinking cedar (Bot.), the California nutmeg tree.
Any one of several species of small sandpipers, as the sanderling of Europe and America, the dunlin, the little stint of India (Tringa minuta), etc.
Settled pay or compensation for services, whether paid daily, monthly, or annually.
To engrave by means of dots, in distinction from engraving in lines. The interlaying of small pieces can not altogether avoid a broken, stippled, spotty effect.
Furnished with stipules.
To change the place of in any manner; to move. My foot I had never yet in five days been able to stir.
Putting in motion, or being in motion; active; active in business; habitually employed in some kind of business.
The act of one who stitches. 2. Work done by sewing, esp. when a continuous line of stitches is shown on the surface.
A line of stout posts or timbers set firmly in the earth in contact with each other (and usually with loopholes) to form a barrier, or defensive fortification.
A broker who deals in stocks.
A close-fitting covering for the foot and leg, usually knit or woven. Blue stocking.

A disciple of the philosopher Zeno; one of a Greek sect which held that men should be free from passion, unmoved by joy or grief, and should submit without complaint to unavoidable necessity, by which all things are...
The opinions and maxims of the Stoics. 2. A real or pretended indifference to pleasure or pain.
Hopelessly insensible or stupid; not easily aroused or excited; dull; impassive.
To stamp with the foot. "In gallant procession, the priests mean to stomp." R.
An assemblage of upright stones with others placed horizontally on their tops, on Salisbury Plain, England, -- generally supposed to be the remains of an ancient Druidical temple.
One who stones; one who makes an assault with stones. 2. One who walls with stones.
The act of depositing in a store or warehouse for safe keeping; also, the safe keeping of goods in a warehouse.
Collected or accumulated as a reserve supply.
Room in a storehouse or repository; a room in which articles are stored.
Any one of several species of large wading birds of the family Ciconidæ, having long legs and a long, pointed bill.
from Storm, v. Storming party (Mil.), a party assigned to the duty of making the first assault in storming a fortress.
Characterized by, or proceeding from, a storm; subject to storms; agitated with furious winds.
To place or arrange in a compact mass; to put in its proper place, or in a suitable place.
One who conceals himself board of a vessel about to leave port, or on a railway train, in order to obtain a free passage.
To make straight; to reduce from a crooked to a straight form. 2. To make right or correct.
Race; stock; generation; descent; family. He is of a noble strain. Shak.
Subjected to great or excessive tension; wrenched; weakened.
from Strain. Straining piece (Arch.), a short piece of timber in a truss, used to maintain the ends of struts or rafters, and keep them from slipping.
One of the twists, or strings, as of fibers, wires, etc., of which a rope is composed.
To compress the windpipe of (a person or animal) until death results from stoppage of respiration.
One who, or that which, strangles. "The very strangler of their amity." Shak.
The act of strangling, or the state of being strangled. 2. (Med.) Inordinate compression or constriction of a tube or part, as of the throat.
A long, narrow, pliable strip of leather, cloth, or the like; specifically, a strip of thick leather used in flogging.
Tall; strong; lusty; large.
An artifice or trick in war for deceiving the enemy; hence, in general, artifice.
One skilled in strategy, or the science of directing great military movements.
The science of military command, or the science of projecting campaigns and directing great military movements.
Having its substance arranged in strata, or layers.
To form or deposit in strata, or layers, as substances in the earth; to arrange in strata.
A bed of earth or rock of one kind, formed by natural causes, and consisting usually of a series of layers, which form a rock as it lies between beds of other kinds.
To spread or scatter. See Strew, and Strow. Chaucer. 1. A stalk or stem of certain species of grain, pulse, etc., especially of wheat, rye, oats, barley, more rarely of buckwheat, beans, and pease.
A fragrant edible berry, of a delicious taste and commonly of a red color, the fruit of a plant of the genus Fragaria, of which there are many varieties.
A current water or other fluid; a liquid flowing continuously in a line or course, either on the earth, as a river, brook, etc., or from a vessel, reservoir, or fountain.
Sending forth streams. 1. The act or operation of that which streams; the act of that which sends forth, or which runs in, streams.
A small stream; a rivulet; a rill.
To make strong or stronger; to add strength to.
Eagerly pressing or urgent; zealous; ardent; earnest; bold; valiant; intrepid.
Having much stress. Rush.
To reach out; to extend; to put forth. And stretch forth his neck long and small.
One who, or that which, stretches. 2. (Masonry) A brick or stone laid with its longer dimension in the line of direction of the wall.
from Stretch, v. Stretching course (Masonry), a course or series of stretchers.
To scatter; to spread by scattering; to cast or to throw loosely apart.
To mark with striaæ. "Striated longitudinally." Owen. Marked with striaæ, or fine grooves, or lines of color.
Marked with striaæ, or fine grooves, or lines of color; showing narrow structural bands or lines.
Strained; drawn close; tight.
In a strict manner; closely; precisely.
Strictness. A man of stricture and firm abstinence. Shak. 2. A stroke.
To walk with long steps, especially in a measured or pompous manner. Mars in the middle of the shining shield Is graved, and strides along the liquid field.
Characterized by harshness; grating; shrill. "A strident voice." Thackeray.
The act of striving; earnest endeavor. Shak. 2. Exertion or contention for superiority.
a. & n. from Strike, v. Striking distance, the distance through which an object can be reached by striking.
A small cord, a line, a twine, or a slender strip of leather, or other substance, used for binding together, fastening, or tying things.
The quality or state of being stringent.
Binding strongly; making strict requirements; restrictive; rigid; severe.
Having stripes of different colors; streaked. Striped bass. (Zoöl.) See under Bass.
A youth in the state of adolescence, or just passing from boyhood to manhood.
One who, or that which, strips; specifically, a machine for stripping cards.
The act of one who strips. The mutual bows and courtesies . . . are remants of the original prostrations and strippings of the captive.
from Strive. -- Striv"ing*ly, adv.
The act of rubbing gently with the hand, or of smoothing; a stroke. I doubt not with one gentle stroking to wipe away ten thousand tears.
To wander on foot; to ramble idly or leisurely; to rove. These mothers stroll to beg sustenance for their helpless infants.
One who strolls; a vagrant.
A fastness; a fort or fortress; fortfield place; a place of security.
In a strong manner; so as to be strong in action or in resistance; with strength.
Having a definite organic structure; showing differentiation of parts.
To swell; to bulge out. The bellying canvas strutted with the gale. Dryden.
from Strut, v. -- Strut"ting*ly, adv.
Firm as a stub or stump; stiff; unbending; unyielding; persistent; hence, unreasonably obstinate in will or opinion.
The working room of an artist.
Given to study; devoted to the acquisition of knowledge from books.
That which is used for filling anything.
To make foolish; to make a fool of.
To trip in walking or in moving in any way with the legs; to strike the foot so as to fall, or to endanger a fall.
To make senseless or dizzy by violence; to render senseless by a blow, as on the head.
Overpowering consciousness; overpowering the senses; especially, overpowering the sense of hearing.
To hinder from growing to the natural size; to prevent the growth of; to stint, to dwarf.
Astonishing; wonderful; amazing; especially, astonishing in magnitude or elevation.
Great diminution or suspension of sensibility; suppression of sense or feeling.
Foolishly obstinate or resolute; stubborn; unrelenting; unfeeling; stern.
The act of one who stutters; -- restricted by some physiologists to defective speech due to inability to form the proper sounds, the breathing being normal, as distinguished from stammering.
Having style or artistic quality; given to, or fond of, the display of style.
One who is a master or a model of style, especially in writing or speaking.

An instrument for writing. See Style, n., 1. That needle-shaped part at the tip of the playing arm of phonograph which sits in the groove of a phonograph record while it is turning, to detect the undulations in the...
The position of two balls on the putting green such that, being more than six inches apart, one ball lies directly between the other and the hole at which the latter must be played.
The act of persuading; persuasion.
Sweet; pleasant; delightful; gracious or agreeable in manner; bland. -- Suave"ly, adv.
Moderately acid or sour.
Ranked or ranged below; subordinate; inferior; specifically (Mil.), ranking as a junior officer.
Being under water, or beneath the surface of water; adapted for use under water.
Occurring without the possibility or the fact of an attendant consciousness.
To bring under; to conquer by force or the exertion of superior power, and bring into permanent subjection.
Conquered; overpowered; crushed; submissive; mild. 2. Not glaring in color.
Lying under or below. 2. Being in a lower situation, though not directly beneath.
The act of subjecting, or of bringing under the dominion of another; the act of subduing.
To subdue, and bring under the yoke of power or dominion; to conquer by force, and compel to submit to the government or absolute control of another.
To bring by heat into the state of vapor, which, on cooling, returns again to the solid state.
Lifted up; high in place; exalted aloft; uplifted; lofty. Sublime on these a tower of steel is reared.
Existing in the mind, but below the surface or threshold of consciousness.
Situated under the tongue.
Being, acting, or growing, under water in the sea.
To put under water; to plunge. 2. To cover or overflow with water; to inundate.
The act of submerging, or the state of being submerged; submersion.
The act of submerging, or putting under water or other fluid, or of causing to be overflowed.
The act of submitting; the act of yielding to power or authority; surrender of the person and power to the control or government of another.
Inclined or ready to submit; acknowledging one's inferiority; yielding.
Placed in a lower order, class, or rank; holding a lower or inferior position.
To procure or cause to take a false oath amounting to perjury, such oath being actually taken.
A writ commanding the attendance in court, as a witness, of the person on whom it is served, under a penalty.
Following in time; coming or being after something else at any time, indefinitely.
At a later time; afterwards.
The quality or state of being subservient; instrumental fitness or use.
Fitted or disposed to subserve; useful in an inferior capacity; serving to promote some end.
To sink or fall to the bottom; to settle, as lees. 2. To tend downward.
Furnishing aid; assisting; auxiliary; helping; tributary; especially, aiding in an inferior position or capacity.
Support; aid; coöperation; esp., extraordinary aid in money rendered to the sovereign or to a friendly power.
To be; to have existence; to inhere. And makes what happiness we justly call, Subsist not in the good of one, but all.
Real being; existence. Not only the things had subsistence, but the very images were of some creatures existing.
Belonging to substance; actually existing; real.
To make to exist; to make real. Ayliffe. 2. To establish the existence or truth of by proof or competent evidence.
One who, or that which, is substituted or put in the place of another.
To take up into or under, as individual under species, species under genus, or particular under universal.
To extend under, or be opposed to.
That to which one resorts for escape or concealment; an artifice employed to escape censure or the force of an argument, or to justify opinions or conduct.
Being or lying under the surface of the earth; situated within the earth, or under ground.
Sly in design; artful; cunning; insinuating; subtile; -- applied to persons.
The quality or state of being subtle, or sly; cunning; craftiness; artfulness.
To withdraw, or take away, as a part from the whole; to deduct.
The sum or number to be subtracted, or taken from another.
The act of overturning, or the state of being overturned; entire overthrow.
Tending to subvert; having a tendency to overthrow and ruin. Lying is a vice subversive of the very ends and design of conversation.

To overturn from the foundation; to overthrow; to ruin utterly. These are his substance, sinews, arms, and strength, With which he yoketh your rebellious necks, Razeth your cities, and subverts your towns.
An underground way or gallery; especially, a passage under a street, in which water mains, gas mains, telegraph wires, etc., are conducted.
The act of one who, or that which, succeeds; also, that which succeeds, or follows after.
Resulting in success; assuring, or promotive of, success; accomplishing what was proposed.
One who succeeds or follows; one who takes the place which another has left, and sustains the like part or character.
Girded or tucked up; bound; drawn tightly together. His habit fit for speed succinct.
tiono run to, or run to support; hence, to help or relieve when in difficulty, want, or distress.
A demon or fiend; especially, a lascivious spirit supposed to have sexual intercourse with the men by night.
Full of juice; juicy. Succulent plants (Bot.), plants which have soft and juicy leaves or stems, as the houseleek, the live forever, and the species of Mesembryanthemum.
To yield; to submit; to give up unresistingly.
One who, or that which, sucks; esp., one of the organs by which certain animals, as the octopus and remora, adhere to other bodies.
Drawing milk from the mother or dam; hence, colloquially, young, inexperienced,.
The act or process of sucking; the act of drawing, as fluids, by exhausting the air.
Causing sweat.
Swedish glove leather, --usually made from lambskins tanned with willow bark.
The quality or state of being sufficient, or adequate to the end proposed.
Equal to the end proposed; adequate to wants; enough; ample; competent.
To a sufficient degree; to a degree that answers the purpose, or gives content.
Suffocated; choked. Shak. 1. To choke or kill by stopping respiration.
from Suffocate, v. -- Suf"fo*ca`ting*ly, adv.
The act of suffocating, or the state of being suffocated; death caused by smothering or choking.
One who possesses or exercises the political right of suffrage; a voter.
To overspread, as with a fluid or tincture; to fill or cover, as with something fluid.
Containing a suggestion, hint, or intimation. -- Sug*gest"ive*ly, adv.
Partaking of, or of the nature of, the crime or suicide. -- Su"i*ci`dal*ly, adv.
Capable of suiting; fitting; accordant; proper; becoming; agreeable; adapted.
A retinue or company of attendants, as of a distinguished personage.
One who sues, petitions, or entreats; a petitioner; an applicant. She hath been a suitor to me for her brother.
Lonely; solitary; desolate. Wyclif (Job iii. 14). 2. Gloomy; dismal; foreboding.
To soil; to dirty; to spot; to tarnish; to stain; to darken; -- used literally and figuratively.
A ruler, or sovereign, of a Mohammedan state; specifically, the ruler of the Turks.
The wife of a sultan; a sultaness. 2. pl. A kind of seedless raisin produced near Smyrna in Asiatic Turkey.
Very hot, burning, and oppressive.
A member of the most prominent tribe of the Moro tribes, occupying the Sulu Archipelago.
To comprise in, or reduce to, a summary; to present briefly. Chambers.
Formed into a sum; summed up; reduced into a narrow compass, or into few words.
The act of summing, or forming a sum, or total amount; also, an aggregate.
The top; the highest point. Fixed on the summit of the highest mount. Shak.
To call, bid, or cite; to notify to come to appear; -- often with up. Stiffen the sinews, summon up the blood.
The act of summoning; a call by authority, or by the command of a superior, to appear at a place named, or to attend to some duty.
Relating to expense; regulating expense or expenditure. Bacon. Sumptuary laws or regulations, laws intended to restrain or limit the expenditure of citizens in apparel, food, furniture, etc.
Involving large outlay or expense; costly; expensive; hence, luxurious.
To burn or discolor by the sun; to tan. Sunburnt and swarthy though she be.
The setting of the sun; sunset. "When sundown skirts the moor." Tennyson.

Lying on the bottom of a river or other water; sunk.

The light of the sun, or the place where it shines; the direct rays of the sun, the place where they fall, or the warmth and light which they give.
The quality or state of being superabundant; a superabundant quantity.

Grand; magnificent; august; stately.

definition: To heat too much, to overheat.

More elevated in place or position; higher; upper.
The quality, state, or condition of being superior.

= Overman, above.

Exceeding the number stated or prescribed.

Lying on the back, or with the face upward; -- opposed to prone. 2. Leaning backward, or inclining with exposure to the sun.

Pliant; flexible; easily bent.
Added to supply what is wanted; additional; being, or serving.
Added to supply what is wanted; additional; being, or serving.
Asking earnestly and submissively; entreating; beseeching; supplicating.

To entreat for; to seek by earnest prayer; to ask for earnestly and humbly.
One who supplies.
One who, or that which, supports.
The act of supposing, laying down, imagining, or considering as true or existing, what is known not to be true, or what is not proved.

Being or situated above the world or above our system; celestial.

By means of surgery.
Arrogant; haughty. Cotgrave. 2. Gloomily morose; ill-natured, abrupt, and rude.

That which remains when use or need is satisfied, or when a limit is reached.
Exciting surprise; extraordinary; of a nature to excite wonder and astonishment.
To yield to the power of another; to give or deliver up possession of (anything) upon compulsion or demand.
Done or made by stealth, or without proper authority; made or introduced fraudulently.
A four-wheeled pleasure carriage, (commonly two-seated) somewhat like a phaeton, but having a straight bottom.
A deputy; a delegate; a substitute. 2. The deputy of an ecclesiastical judge, most commonly of a bishop or his chancellor, especially a deputy who grants marriage licenses.
Inclosing; encircling. 1. An encompassing. 2. pl. The things which surround or environ.
Oversight; watch; inspection; supervision. That sort of surveillance of which .

Capable of admitting anything additional, or any change, affection, or influence.

Distrusted; doubted. -- Sus*pect"ed*ly, adv. -- Sus*pect"ed*ness, n.

Wet, spongy land; soft, low ground saturated with water, but not usually covered with it.
Any one of numerous species of large aquatic birds belonging to Cygnus, Olor, and allied genera of the subfamily Cygninæ.

a. & n. from Sweat, v. Sweating bath, a bath producing sensible sweat.

reserved or uncommunicative in speech; saying little

For that, or this; for it. With certain officers ordained therefore. Chaucer.
In that or this place, time, or thing; in that particular or respect. Wyclif.
An instrument for measuring temperature, founded on the principle that changes of temperature in bodies are accompained by proportional changes in their volumes or dimensions.

A self-acting apparatus for regulating temperature by the unequal expansion of different metals, liquids, or gases by heat, as in opening or closing the damper of a stove, or the like, as the heat becomes greater or...
A position or proposition which a person advances and offers to maintain, or which is actually maintained by argument.

A letter of the Greek alphabet corresponding to th in English; -- sometimes called the unlucky letter, from being used by the judges on their ballots in passing condemnation on a prisoner, it being the first letter...
The quality or state of being thick (in any of the senses of the adjective).
The proximal segment of the hind limb between the knee and the trunk. See Femur.
One who thinks; especially and chiefly, one who thinks in a particular manner.
One who thins, or makes thinner.
Feeling thirst; having a painful or distressing sensation from want of drink.
One more than twelve; ten and three.
Being three times ten; consisting of one more than twenty-nine; twenty and ten.
A strap of leather; especially, one used for fastening anything. And nails for loosened spears, and thongs for shields, provide.
The god of thunder, and son of Odin.
A hard and sharp-pointed projection from a woody stem; usually, a branch so transformed.
Full of thorns or spines; rough with thorns; spiny.
Through. Spenser. Shak. 1. Passing through.
Bred from the best blood through a long line; pure-blooded; -- said of stock, as horses.
Going through, or to the end or bottom; very thorough; complete. 2. Going all lengths.
In a thorough manner; fully; entirely; completely.
A group of houses in the country; a small village; a hamlet; a dorp; -- now chiefly occurring in names of places and persons.
Full of thought; employed in meditation; contemplative.
To beat out grain from, as straw or husks; to beat the straw or husk of (grain) with a flail.
a. & n. from Thrash, v. Thrashing floor, Threshing-floor, or Threshing floor, a floor or area on which grain is beaten out.
Worn to the naked thread; having the nap worn off; threadbare clothes.
The expression of an intention to inflict evil or injury on another; the declaration of an evil, loss, or pain to come.
To utter threats against; to menace; to inspire with apprehension; to alarm, or attempt to alarm, as with the promise of something evil or disagreeable.
a. & n. from Threaten, v. -- Threat"en*ing*ly, adv. Threatening letters (Law), letters containing threats, especially those designed to extort money, or to obtain other property, by menaces.
A song of lamentation; a threnode. Sir T. Herbert.
To beat out grain from, as straw or husks; to beat the straw or husk of (grain) with a flail.
The plank, stone, or piece of timber, which lies under a door, especially of a dwelling house, church, temple, or the like.
Three times. "Thrice in vain." Spenser. Verily I say unto thee. That this night, before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice.
A thriving state; good husbandry; economical management in regard to property.
Given to, or evincing, thrift; characterized by economy and good menegement of property.
Causing a thrill; causing tremulous excitement; deeply moving.
To posper by industry, economy, and good management of property; to increase in goods and estate.
To beat, or pulsate, with more than usual force or rapidity; to beat in consequence of agitation.
A chair of state, commonly a royal seat, but sometimes the seat of a prince, bishop, or other high dignitary.
A multitude of persons or of living beings pressing or pressed into a close body or assemblage.
Quite through; from one extremity to the other of; also, every part of.
One who thrusts or stabs.
A dull sound without resonance, like that produced by striking with, or striking against, some comparatively soft substance.
The short, thick first digit of the human hand, differing from the other fingers in having but two phalanges.
The sound made by the sudden fall or blow of a heavy body, as of a hammer, or the like.
Heavy; large.
An Australian insectivorous singing bird (Pachycephala gutturalis). The male is conspicuously marked with black and yellow, and has a black crescent on the breast.
A shaft of lightning; a brilliant stream of electricity passing from one part of the heavens to another, or from the clouds to the earth.
A sharp burst of thunder; a sudden report of a discharge of atmospheric electricity.
Emitting thunder. Roll the thundering chariot o'er the ground. J. Trumbull.
A storm accompanied with lightning and thunder.
The commoner kind of frankincense, or that obtained from the Norway spruce, the long-leaved pine, and other conifers.
Situated or placed across something else; transverse; oblique. Moved contrary with thwart obliquities.
Any plant of the labiate genus Thymus. The garden thyme (Thymus vulgaris) is a warm, pungent aromatic, much used to give a relish to seasoning and soups.
Shaped like an oblong shield; shield-shaped.
An emphasized form of the personal pronoun of the second person; -- used as a subject commonly with thou.
The inner, or preaxial, and usually the larger, of the two bones of the leg or hind limb below the knee.
One who, or that which, ticks, or produces a ticking sound, as a watch or clock, a telegraphic sounder, etc.
A strong, closely woven linen or cotton fabric, of which ticks for beds are made.
Sensible to slight touches; easily tickled.
Account of what has taken place, and was not before known; news. I shall make my master glad with these tidings.
One who, or that which, ties. A chold's apron covering the upper part of the body, and tied with tape or cord.
A species of gause, or very silk. The smoke of sulphur . . . is commonly used by women to whiten tiffanies.
To draw tighter; to straiten; to make more close in any manner. Just where I please, with tightened rein I'll urge thee round the dusty plain.
In a tight manner; closely; nearly.
Close-fitting garments, especially for the lower part of the body and the legs.
The female of the tiger. Holland.
Being or occurring in good time; sufficiently early; seasonable. "The timely dew of sleep." Milton.
A timekeeper; especially, a watch by which small intervals of time can be measured.
Wanting courage to meet danger; easily frightened; timorous; not bold.
The quality or state of being timid; timorousness; timidness.
Fearful of danger; timid; deficient in courage. Shak. 2. Indicating, or caused by, fear.
A kind of grass (Phleum pratense) with long cylindrical spikes; -- called also herd's grass, in England, cat's-tail grass, and meadow cat's-tail grass.
A tinge or shade of color; a tint.
Something very inflammable, used for kindling fire from a spark, as scorched linen.
To imbue or impregnate with something different or foreign.
To feel a kind of thrilling sensation, as in hearing a shrill sound. At which both the ears of every one that heareth it shall tingle.
To make a sharp, shrill noise; to tinkle. Wyclif (1 Cor. xiii. 1). A sharp, quick sound.
A tinkle, or succession of tinkles. Drowsy tinklings lull the distant folds.
Pertaining to, abounding with, or resembling, tin. "The tinny strand." Drayton.
A slight coloring. Specifically: -- (a) A pale or faint tinge of any color.
A distinct articulation given in playing quick notes on the flute, by striking the tongue against the roof of the mouth.
To drink spirituous or strong liquors habitually; to indulge in the frequent and improper used of spirituous liquors.
Being under the influence of strong drink; rendered weak or foolish by liquor, but not absolutely or completely drunk.
The end, or tip, of the toe. He must . . . stand on his typtoon . Chaucer.
A declamatory strain or flight of censure or abuse; a rambling invective.
Fitted or tending to tire; exhausted; wearisome; fatiguing; tedious.
A woven fabric. 2. A fine transparent silk stuff, used for veils, etc.
Titanic. The Titan physical difficulties of his enterprise. I. Taylor.
An elementary substance found combined in the minerals manaccanite, rutile, sphene, etc., and isolated as an infusible iron- gray amorphous powder, having a metallic luster.
An inscription put over or upon anything as a name by which it is known.
Having or bearing a title.
To laugh with the tongue striking against the root of the upper teeth.
A little teat; a nipple.
A mean flatterer; a toadeater; a sycophant. Before I had been standing at the window five minutes, they somehow conveyed to me that they were all toadies and humbugs.
One who toasts. 2. A kitchen utensil for toasting bread, cheese, etc. Toaster oven.
a. & n. from Toast, v. Toasting fork, a long-handled fork for toasting bread, cheese, or the like, by the fire.
A small jug, pitcher, or mug, generally used for ale, shaped somewhat like a stout man, with a cocked hat forming the brim.
An alarm bell, or the ringing of a bell for the purpose of alarm. The loud tocsin tolled their last alarm.
One who toddles; especially, a young child. Mrs. Gaskell.
A juice drawn from various kinds of palms in the East Indies; or, a spirituous liquor procured from it by fermentation.
The loose outer garment worn by the ancient Romans, consisting of a single broad piece of woolen cloth of a shape approaching a semicircle.
Attended with toil, or fatigue and pain; laborious; wearisome.
Something intended or supposed to represent or indicate another thing or an event.
A sword or sword blade made at Toledo in Spain, which city was famous in the 16th and 17th centuries for the excellence of its weapons.
Capable of being borne or endured; supportable, either physically or mentally.
The power or capacity of enduring; the act of enduring; endurance. Diogenes, one frosty morning, came into the market place,shaking, to show his tolerance.
Inclined to tolerate; favoring toleration; forbearing; ingulgent.
To suffer to be, or to be done, without prohibition or hindrance; to allow or permit negatively, by not preventing.
The act of tolerating; the allowance of that which is not wholly approved.
The fruit of a plant of the Nightshade family (Lycopersicum esculentun).
A pit in which the dead body of a human being is deposited; a grave; a sepulcher.
A romping girl; a hoiden. J. Fletcher.
A stone erected over a grave, to preserve the memory of the deceased.
As many writings as are bound in a volume, forming part of a larger work.
An instrument, usually of metal, consisting of two parts, or long shafts, jointed together at or near one end, or united by an elastic bow, used for handling things, especially hot coals or metals.
The act of clipping the hair, or of shaving the crown of the head; also, the state of being shorn.
Pain in a tooth or in the teeth; odontalgia. Toothache grass (Bot.), a kind of grass (Ctenium Americanum) having a very pungent taste.
A brush for cleaning the teeth.
Having no teeth. Cowper.
A pointed instument for clearing the teeth of substances lodged between them.
Having no top, or no visble fop; hence, fig.: very lofty; supreme; unequaled.
The description of a particular place, town, manor, parish, or tract of land.
One that tops, in any sense of the verb; specif.: (a) A cover of a top layer or part.
Rising above; surpassing. 2. Hence, assuming superiority; proud. The great and flourishing condition of some of the topping sinners of the world.
To fall forward; to pitch or tumble down. Though castles topple on their warders' heads.
The upper layer of soil; surface soil.
A law; a precept. A considerable body of priestly Toroth. S. R. Driver.
A light or luminary formed of some combustible substance, as of resinous wood.
Causing torment.

A violent whirling wind; specifically (Meteorol.), a tempest distinguished by a rapid whirling and slow progressive motion, usually accompaned with severe thunder, lightning, and torrents of rain, and commonly of...
Any one of numerous species of elasmobranch fishes belonging to Torpedo and allied genera.
Having lost motion, or the power of exertion and feeling; numb; benumbed.
Loss of motion, or of the motion; a state of inactivity with partial or total insensibility.
A collar or neck chain, usually twisted, especially as worn by ancient barbaric nations, as the Gauls, Germans, and Britons.
A violent stream, as of water, lava, or the like; a stream suddenly raised and running rapidly, as down a precipice.
Parched; dried with heat.
The human body, as distinguished from the head and limbs; in sculpture, the trunk of a statue, mutilated of head and limbs.
An unleavened cake, as of maize flour, baked on a heated iron or stone.

Any one of numerous species of reptiles of the order Testudinata. Note: The term is applied especially to the land and fresh-water species, while the marine species are generally called turtles, but the terms...
Bent in different directions; wreathed; twisted; winding.
A member of the conservative party, as opposed to the progressive party which was formerly called the Whig, and is now called the Liberal, party.
Ohe who tosser. J. Fletcher.
The act of throwing upward; a rising and falling suddenly; a rolling and tumbling.
To carry or bear.
A rude picture, as of a bird, beast, or the like, used by the Nord American Indians as a symbolic designation, as of a family or a clan.
To shake so as to threaten a fall; to vacillate; to be unsteady; to stagger.
The act of touching the football down behind the opponents' goal . Safety touchdown.
Affecting; moving; pathetic.
Lydian stone; basanite; -- so called because used to test the purity of gold and silver by the streak which is left upon the stone when it is rubbed by the metal.
Peevish; irritable; irascible; techy; apt to take fire. It may be said of Dryden that he was at no time touchy about personal attacks.
To grow or make tough, or tougher.
A little tuft; a curl or artificial lock of hair. 2. A small wig, or a toppiece of a wig.
One who makes a tour, or performs a journey in a circuit.
A mock fight, or warlike game, formerly in great favor, in which a number of combatants were engaged, as an exhibition of their address and bravery.
An instrument for arresting hemorrhage. It consists essentially of a pad or compress upon which pressure is made by a band which is tightened by a screw or other means.
To act as a tout. See 2d Tout. 2. To ply or seek for customers. One who secretly watches race horses which are in course of training, to get information about their capabilities, for use in betting.
In the direction of; to. He set his face toward the wilderness. Num. xxiv.
Very high; elevated; rising aloft.

The district or territory of a town. Note: In the United States, many of the States are divided into townships of five, six, seven, or perhaps ten miles square, and the inhabitants of such townships are invested with...
The inhabitants of a town or city, especially in distinction from country people.
The science which treats of poisons, their effects, antidotes, and recignition.
A poisonous product formed by pathogenic bacteria, as a toxic proteid or poisonous ptomaine.
One who, or that which, traces.
The windpipe. See Illust. of Lung. 2. (Zoöl.) One of the respiratory tubes of insects and arachnids.
The act of one who traces; especially, the act of copying by marking on thin paper, or other transparent substance, the lines of a pattern placed beneath.
One who, or that which, tracks or pursues, as a man or dog that follows game.
Capable of being easily led, taught, or managed; docile; manageable; governable.
That which draws, or is used for drawing. 2. pl. (Med.) Two small, pointed rods of metal, formerly used in the treatment called Perkinism.
One engaged in trade or commerce; one who makes a business of buying and selling or of barter.
In a traditional manner.
A dramatic poem, composed in elevated style, representing a signal action performed by some person or persons, and having a fatal issue.
One who, or that which, trails. A part of an object which extends some distance beyond the main body of the object.

a. & vb. n. from Trail. Trailing arbutus. (Bot.) See under Arbutus. -- Trailing spring, a spring fixed in the axle box of the trailing wheels of a locomotive engine, and so placed as to assist in deadening any shock...
One who trains; an instructor; especially, one who trains or prepares men, horses, etc., for exercises requiring physical agility and strength.
A stroke; a touch. By this single trait Homer makes an essential difference between the Iliad and Odyssey.
The curve which a body describes in space, as a planet or comet in its orbit, or stone thrown upward obliquely in the air.
To tread under foot; to tread down; to prostrate by treading.
Quiet; calm; undisturbed; peaceful; not agitated.
To render tranquil; to allay when agitated; to compose; to make calm and peaceful.
One who, or that which, tranquilizes.
The quality or state of being tranquil; calmness; composure.
The doing or performing of any business; management of any affair; performance.
Being on the farther side of the Alps in regard to Rome, that is, on the north or west side of the Alps.
Lying or being beyond the Atlantic Ocean. Note: When used by a person in Europe or Africa, transatlantic signifies being in America.
To rise above; to surmount.
Very excellent; superior or supreme in excellence; surpassing others.
To write over again, or in the same words; to copy.
That which has been transcribed; a writing or composition consisting of the same words as the original.
To convey from one place or person another; to transport, remove, or cause to pass, to another place or person.
Capable of being transferred or conveyed from one place or person to another.
The person to whom a transfer in made.
The act of transferring; conveyance; passage; transfer.
One who makes a transfer or conveyance.
To change the outward form or appearance of; to metamorphose; to transform.
The act of transforming, or the state of being transformed; change of form or condition.
One who, or that which, transforms. Specif. (Elec.), an apparatus for producing from a given electrical current another current of different voltage.
To pour, as liquid, out of one vessel into another; to transfer by pouring.
Capable of being transfused; transferable by transfusion.
The act of transfusing, or pouring, as liquor, out of one vessel into another.
The act of transgressing, or of passing over or beyond any law, civil or moral.
The quality of being transient; transientness.
lasting only for a short time; impermanent
characteristic: Like a passing traveler or a fleeting moment, here briefly and then gone.

definition: The act of passing; passage through or over. In France you are now . .
Passage from one place or state to another; charge.
Continuing only for a short time; not enduring; fleeting; evanescent. Comfort and succor all those who, in this transitory life, are in trouble.
One who translates; esp., one who renders into another language; one who expresses the sense of words in one language by equivalent words in another.
The quality or state of being translucent; clearness; partial transparency.
Transmitting rays of light without permitting objects to be distinctly seen.
Capable of being transmitted from one to another; capable of being passed through any body or substance.
The act of transmitting, or the state of being transmitted.
To cause to pass over or through; to communicate by sending; to send from one person or place to another.
One who, or that which, transmits; specifically, that portion of a telegraphic or telephonic instrument by means of which a message is sent.
To change from one nature, form, or substance, into another; to transform.
The quality or condition of being transparent; transparence. 2. That which is transparent.
Having the property of transmitting rays of light, so that bodies can be distinctly seen through.
To pass off in the form of vapor or insensible perspiration; to exhale.
To remove, and plant in another place.
To carry or bear from one place to another; to remove; to convey.
Conveyed from one place to another; figuratively, carried away with passion or pleasure.
One who transports.
That transports; fig., ravishing. Your transporting chords ring out. Keble.
The act of transposing, or the state of being transposed. Specifically: --(a) (Alg.) The bringing of any term of an equation from one side over to the other without destroying the equation.
A trapezium. See Trapezium, 1. 2. A swinging horizontal bar, suspended at each end by a rope.
One who traps animals; one who makes a business of trapping animals for their furs.
That which serves to trap or adorn; ornaments; dress; superficial decorations.
Small or portable articles for dress, furniture, or use; goods; luggage.
Like trash; containing much trash; waste; rejected; worthless; useless.
Labor with pain; severe toil or exertion. As everything of price, so this doth require travail.
Having made journeys; having gained knowledge or experience by traveling.
One who travels; one who has traveled much. 2. A commercial agent who travels for the purpose of receiving orders for merchants, making collections, etc.
Disguised by dress so as to be ridiculous; travestied; -- applied to a book or shorter composition.
Like a traitor; involving treachery; violating allegiance or faith pledged.
Violation of allegiance or of faith and confidence; treasonable or perfidious conduct.
Like, or composed of, treacle.
A mill worked by persons treading upon steps on the periphery of a wide wheel having a horizontal axis.
The offense of attempting to overthrow the government of the state to which the offender owes allegiance, or of betraying the state into the hands of a foreign power.
Pertaining to treason; consisting of treason; involving the crime of treason, or partaking of its guilt.
Wealth accumulated; especially, a stock, or store of money in reserve.

One who has the care of a treasure or treasure or treasury; an officer who receives the public money arising from taxes and duties, or other sources of revenue, takes charge of the same, and disburses it upon orders...
A written composition on a particular subject, in which its principles are discussed or explained.
In a treble manner; with a threefold number or quantity; triply. Swift.
To draw or haul a load, as oxen. 2. To travel, esp. by ox wagon; to go from place to place.
To shake involuntarily, as with fear, cold, or weakness; to quake; to quiver.
Shaking; tottering; quivering. -- Trem"bling*ly, adv. Trembling poplar (Bot.), the aspen.
Fitted to excite fear or terror; such as may astonish or terrify by its magnitude, force, or violence.
A trembling; a shivering or shaking; a quivering or vibratory motion.
Shaking; shivering; quivering.
Fitted to trench or cut; gutting; sharp. " Trenchant was the blade." Chaucer.
An involuntary trembling, sometimes an effect of paralysis, but usually caused by terror or fear.
A movable frame or support for anything, as scaffolding, consisting of three or four legs secured to a top piece, and forming a sort of stool or horse, used by carpenters, masons, and other workmen.
Three, at cards, dice, or dominoes; a card, die, or domino of three spots or pips.
A union of three; three objects treated as one; a ternary; a trinity.

A figure bounded by three lines, and containing three angles. Note: A triangle is either plane, spherical, or curvilinear, according as its sides are straight lines, or arcs of great circles of a sphere, or any...
That which occasions distress, trouble, or vexation; severe affliction.
The seat of a judge; the bench on which a judge and his associates sit for administering justice.

An officer or magistrate chosen by the people, to protect them from the oppression of the patricians, or nobles, and to defend their liberties against any attempts that might be made upon them by the senate and consuls.
The art of dressing up; artifice; stratagem; fraud; imposture.
Given to tricks; tricky. Sir W. Scott. Dress; ornament. Shak.
To flow in a small, gentle stream; to run in drops. His salt tears trickled down as rain.
One who tricks; a deceiver; a tricker; a cheat.
Given to tricks; practicing deception; trickish; knavish.
The national French banner, of three colors, blue, white, and red, adopted at the first revolution.
A three-wheeled velocipede. See Illust. under Velocipede. Cf. Bicycle.
A kind of scepter or spear with three prongs, -- the common attribute of Neptune.
Continuing three years.
Being of small value or importance; trivial; paltry.
A catch to hold the wheel of a carriage on a declivity. 2. (Mech.) A piece, as a lever, which is connected with a catch or detent as a means of releasing it.
One of tree children born at the same birth. Wright. 2. (Crystallog.) A compound crystal, consisting of three individuals.
According to the French notation, which is used upon the Continent generally and in the United States, the number expressed by a unit with twelve ciphers annexed.
A series of three dramas which, although each of them is in one sense complete, have a close mutual relation, and form one historical and poetical picture.
To make trim; to put in due order for any purpose; to make right, neat, or pleasing.
a. from Trim, v. The Whigs are, essentially, an inefficient, trimming, halfway sort of a party.
The quality or state of being trim; orderliness; compactness; snugness.
The union of three persons (the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost) in one Godhead, so that all the three are one God as to substance, but three persons as to individuality.
Three, considered collectively; three in company or acting together; a set of three.
The large stomach of ruminating animals, when prepared for food. How say you to a fat tripe finely broiled Shak.
A collection or combination of three of a kind; three united. 2. (Poetry) Three verses rhyming together.
Made thrice as much; threefold; tripled. Triplicate ratio (Math.), the ratio of the cubes of two quantities.
The quality or state of being triple, or threefold; trebleness. In their trinal triplicities on high.
Any utensil or vessel, as a stool, table, altar, caldron, etc., supported on three feet.
Quick; nimble; stepping lightly and quickly. 2. (Her.) Having the right forefoot lifted, the others remaining on the ground, as if he were trotting.
To cut or divide into three parts. 2. (Geom.) To cut or divide into three equal parts.
Worn out; common; used until so common as to have lost novelty and interest.
One of tree men united in public office or authority. Note: In later times the triumvirs of Rome were three men who jointly exercised sovereign power.
Government by three in coalition or association; the term of such a government.
Found anywhere; common. 2. Ordinary; commonplace; trifling; vulgar. As a scholar, meantime, he was trivial, and incapable of labor.
A supernatural being, often represented as of diminutive size, but sometimes as a giant, and fabled to inhabit caves, hills, and like places.
A powerful brass instrument of the trumpet kind, thought by some to be the ancient sackbut, consisting of a tube in three parts, bent twice upon itself and ending in a bell.
A collection of people; a company; a number; a multitude. That which should accompany old age --As honor, love, obedience, troops of friends --I must not look to have.
A soldier in a body of cavalry; a cavalryman; also, the horse of a cavalryman.
The use of a word or expression in a different sense from that which properly belongs to it.
A sign or memorial of a victory raised on the field of battle, or, in case of a naval victory, on the nearest land.
To proceed by a certain gait peculiar to quadrupeds; to ride or drive at a trot.
Belief; faith; fidelity. Bid her alight And hertroth plight. Shak. 2. Truth.
One that trots; especially, a horse trained to be driven in trotting matches.
Giving trouble or anxiety; vexatious; burdensome; wearisome. This troublesome world.
A long, hollow vessel, generally for holding water or other liquid, especially one formed by excavating a log longitudinally on one side.
A company or troop, especially the company pf performers in a play or an opera.
A garment worn by men and boys, extending from the waist to the knee or to the ankle, and covering each leg separately.
Any one of numerous species of fishes belonging to Salmo, Salvelinus, and allied genera of the family Salmonidæ.
Troy weight. Troy weight, the weight which gold and silver, jewels, and the like, are weighed.
A suspension of arms by agreement of the commanders of opposing forces.
One who trucks; a trafficker. No man having ever yet driven a saving bargain with this great trucker for souls.
The business of conveying goods on trucks.
A small wheel or caster. Hudibras. To yield or bend obsequiously to the will of another.
The quality or state of being truculent; savageness of manners; ferociousness.
Fierce; savage; ferocious; barbarous.
To walk or march with labor; to jog along; to move wearily. And trudged to Rome upon my naked feet.
Any one of several kinds of roundish, subterranean fungi, usually of a blackish color.
An undoubted or self-evident truth; a statement which is pliantly true.
A wind instrument of great antiquity, much used in war and military exercises, and of great value in the orchestra.
A channel cut behind the brick lining of a shaft. Raymond.
A plant (Sarracenia flava) with long, hollow leaves.
To cut off; to lop; to maim. Appearing as if cut off at the tip.
Cut off; cut short; maimed. 2. (Min.) Replaced, or cut off, by a plane, especially when equally inclined to the adjoining faces.
A person to whom property is legally committed in trust, to be applied either for the benefit of specified individuals, or for public uses.
Having or exercising trust; confiding; unsuspecting; trustful. -- Trust"ing*ly, adv.
Worthy of trust or confidence; trusty. -- Trust"wor`thi*ness, n.
Full of truth; veracious; reliable. -- Truth"ful*ly, adv. -- Truth"ful*ness, n.
The title of the emperor of Russia. See Czar.
An ancient trumpet. (b) A sax-tuba. See Sax-tuba.
Resembling a tub; specifically sounding dull and without resonance, like a tub.
A hollow cylinder, of any material, used for the conveyance of fluids, and for various other purposes.
A constitutional disease characterized by the production of tubercles in the internal organs, and especially in the lungs, where it constitutes the most common variety of pulmonary consumption.
Superintending care over a young person; the particular watch and care of a tutor or guardian over his pupil or ward.
Any plant of the liliaceous genus Tulipa. Many varieties are cultivated for their beautiful, often variegated flowers.
To roll over, or to and fro; to throw one's self about.
a. & vb. n. from Tumble, v. Tumbling barrel. Same as Rumble, n., 4. -- Tumbling bay, an overfall, or weir, in a canal.
A cucking stool for the punishment of scolds. 2. A rough cart. Tusser.
Swelled, enlarged, or distended.
The commotion or agitation of a multitude, usually accompanied with great noise, uproar, and confusion of voices.

Having the appearance of truth; probable; likely. "How verisimilar it looks." Carlyle.
The quality or state of being verisimilar; the appearance of truth; probability.
Agreeable to truth or to fact; actual; real; true; genuine. "The veritable Deity." Sir W.
The quality or state of being true, or real; consonance of a statement, proposition, or other thing, with fact.
Belonging to the country of one's birth; one's own by birth or nature.
A portrait or representation of the face of our Savior on the alleged handkerchief of Saint Veronica, preserved at Rome.
Capable of being turned round. Harte. 2. Liable to be turned in opinion.
Acquainted or familiar, as the result of experience, study, practice, etc.
A change of form, direction, or the like; transformation; conversion; turning.
A turning point; the principal or highest point; top; summit; crown; apex.
Turning round; whirling; rotary; revolving.

Dizziness or swimming of the head; an affection of the head in which objects, though stationary, appear to move in various directions, and the person affected finds it difficult to maintain an erect posture.
Excitement of imagination such as animates a poet, artist, or musician, in composing or performing.
Clothed; robed; wearing vestments. "The vested priest." Milton. 2. (Law) Not in a state of contingency or suspension.
The mark of the foot left on the earth; a track or footstep; a trace; a sign.
A covering or garment; some part of clothing or dress; specifically (Eccl.), any priestly garment.
Long exercised in anything, especially in military life and the duties of a soldier.
One skilled in the diseases of cattle or domestic animals; a veterinary surgeon.
An authoritative prohibition or negative; a forbidding; an interdiction.
The act of vexing, or the state of being vexed; agitation; disquiet; trouble.
Capable of living; born alive and with such form and development of organs as to be capable of living.
A small bottle, usually of glass; a little glass vessel with a narrow aperture intended to be closed with a stopper.
An article of food; provisions; food; victuals; -- used chiefly in the plural.
Vibrating; tremulous; resonant.
To brandish; to move to and fro; to swing.
The act of vibrating, or the state of being vibrated, or in vibratory motion.
A trembler, as of an electric bell. (2) A vibrating reed for transmitting or receiving pulsating currents in a harmonic telegraph system.
The governor of a country or province who rules in the name of the sovereign with regal authority, as the king's substitute.
The quality or state of being near, or not remote; nearness; propinquity.
Characterized by vice or defects; defective; faulty; imperfect. Though I perchance am vicious in my guess.
Regular change or succession from one thing to another; alternation; mutual succession.
To make a victim of, esp. by deception; to dupe; to cheat.
Food for human beings, esp. when it is cooked or prepared for the table.
One who views or examines. 2. (Law) A person appointed to inspect highways, fences, or the like, and to report upon the same.
Attentive to discover and avoid danger, or to provide for safety; wakeful.
A running ornament consisting of leaves and tendrils, used in Gothic architecture.
Active strength or force of body or mind; capacity for exertion, physically, intellectually, or morally.
Possessing vigor; full of physical or mental strength or active force.
One belonging to the pirate crews from among the Northmen, who plundered the coasts of Europe in the eighth, ninth, and tenth centuries.
Low; base; worthless; mean; despicable. A poor man in vile raiment. James ii.
to speak or write about someone in an abusively disparaging manner
characteristic: Picture a smear campaign painting a person as evil to ruin their good name.

definition: Capable of being overcome or subdued; conquerable. "He, not vincible in spirit .

Tending or serving to vindicate or justify; justificatory; vindicative.
Disposed to revenge; prompted or characterized by revenge; revengeful.
An inclosure or yard for grapevines; a plantation of vines producing grapes.
The produce of the vine for one season, in grapes or in wine.
One who deals in wine; a wine seller, or wine merchant.
The hypothetical radical C2H3, regarded as the characteristic residue of ethylene and that related series of unsaturated hydrocarbons with which the allyl compounds are homologous.
A stringed musical instrument formerly in use, of the same form as the violin, but larger, and having six strings, to be struck with a bow, and the neck furnished with frets for stopping the strings.
To treat in a violent manner; to abuse. His wife Boadicea violated with stripes, his daughters with rape.
The act of violating, treating with violence, or injuring; the state of being violated.
One who violates; an infringer; a profaner; a ravisher.
In a violent manner.

Any plant or flower of the genus Viola, of many species. The violets are generally low, herbaceous plants, and the flowers of many of the species are blue, while others are white or yellow, or of several colors, as...

A player on the violin.
A stringed instrument of music; a bass viol of four strings, or a bass violin with long, large strings, giving sounds an octave lower than the viola, or tenor or alto violin.
Any one of numerous species of Old World venomous makes belonging to Vipera, Clotho, Daboia, and other genera of the family Viperidæ.
A woman of extraordinary stature, strength, and courage; a woman who has the robust body and masculine mind of a man.
One of the States of the United States of America. -- a. Of or pertaining to the State of Virginia.
A sign of the zodiac which the sun enters about the 21st of August, marked thus [ (b) A constellation of the zodiac, now occupying chiefly the sign Libra, and containing the bright star Spica.
Having the nature, properties, or qualities, of an adult man; characteristic of developed manhood.
A love of the fine arts; a taste for curiosities. J. Spence. An article, or piece, of virtu, an object of art or antiquity.
Having the power of acting or of invisible efficacy without the agency of the material or sensible part.
In a virtual manner; in efficacy or effect only, and not actually; to all intents and purposes.
One devoted to virtu; one skilled in the fine arts, in antiquities, and the like.
The quality or state of being virulent or venomous; poisonousness; malignancy.
Extremely poisonous or venomous; very active in doing injury. A contagious disorder rendered more virulent by uncleanness.
Contagious or poisonous matter, as of specific ulcers, the bite of snakes, etc.
Sticking or adhering, and having a ropy or glutinous consistency; viscous.
An officer who formerly supplied the place of the count, or earl; the sheriff of the county.
Adhesive or sticky, and having a ropy or glutinous consistency; viscid.
A divinity of the modern Hindoo trimurti, or trinity. He is regarded as the preserver, while Brahma is the creator, and Siva the destroyer of the creation.
The quality or state of being visible.
Perceivable by the eye; capable of being seen; perceptible; in view.
a. & vb. n. from Visit. Visiting ant. (Zoöl.) See Driver ant, under Driver.
One who visits; one who comes or goes to see another, as in civility or friendship.
A part of a helmet, arranged so as to lift or open, and so show the face.
A view; especially, a view through or between intervening objects, as trees.
To make visual, or visible; to see in fancy. No one who has not seen them  can possibly visualize them.
The quality or state of being vital; the principle of life; vital force.
To endow with life, or vitality; to give life to; to make alive.
Organs that are necessary for life; more especially, the heart, lungs, and brain.
To make vicious, faulty, or imperfect; to render defective; to injure the substance or qualities of.
Consisting of, or resembling, glass; glassy.
To convert into, or cause to resemble, glass or a glassy substance, by heat and fusion.
A sulphate of any one of certain metals, as copper, iron, zinc, cobalt.
Liable to, or deserving, vituperation, or severe censure.
To find fault with; to scold; to overwhelm with wordy abuse; to censure severely or abusively.
Uttering or writing censure; containing, or characterized by, abuse; scolding.
Having vigorous powers of life; tenacious of life; long-lived. Hitherto the English bishops have been vivacious almost to wonder.
True to the life; exhibiting the appearance of life or freshness; animated.
To endue with life; to make to be living; to quicken; to animate. Sitting on eggs doth vivify, not nourish.
The dissection of an animal while alive, for the purpose of making physiological investigations.
A female fox. 2. A cross, ill-tempered person; -- formerly used of either sex, now only of a woman.
A word; a term; a name; specifically, a word considered as composed of certain sounds or letters, without regard to its meaning.
A list or collection of words arranged in alphabetical order and explained.
A singer, or vocal musician, as opposed to an instrumentalist.
A call; a summons; a citation; especially, a designation or appointment to a particular state, business, or profession.

Making a loud outcry; clamorous; noisy.
A Russian drink distilled from rye.
Containing nothing; empty; vacant; not occupied; not filled. The earth was without form, and void.
Passing through the air upon wings, or as if upon wings; flying; hence, passing from place to place.
Passing through the air on wings, or by the buoyant force of the atmosphere.
A mountain or hill, usually more or less conical in form, from which lava, cinders, steam, sulphur gases, and the like, are ejected.
The act of willing or choosing; the act of forming a purpose; the exercise of the will.
Electric potential or potential difference, expressed in volts.
The quality or state of being voluble (in any of the senses of the adjective).
Easily rolling or turning; easily set in motion; apt to roll; rotating.
In a voluntary manner; of one's own will; spontaneously.
One who enters into, or offers for, any service of his own free will. 2.
Full of delight or pleasure, especially that of the senses; ministering to sensuous or sensual gratification.
To eject the contents of the stomach by the mouth; to puke; to spew. 1.
The spasmodic ejection of matter from the stomach through the mouth.
Greedy in eating; very hungry; eager to devour or swallow; ravenous; gluttonous.

A mass of fluid, especially of a liquid, having a whirling or circular motion tending to form a cavity or vacuum in the center of the circle, and to draw in towards the center bodies subject to its action.
Consecrated by a vow or promise; consequent on a vow; devoted; promised.
One who votes; one who has a legal right to vote, or give his suffrage.

Given by vow, or in fulfillment of a vow; consecrated by a vow; devoted.

One who voyages; one who sails or passes by sea or water.

The quality or state of being vulnerable; vulnerableness.
Capable of being wounded; susceptible of wounds or external injuries.

A soft, earthy, dark-colored rock or clay derived from the alteration of basalt.

Something deposited, laid, or hazarded on the event of a contest or an unsettled question.
A compensation given to a hired person for services; price paid for labor.
Like a wag; mischievous in sport; roguish in merriment or good humor; frolicsome.

Goods found of which the owner is not known; originally, such goods as a pursued thief threw away to prevent being apprehended, which belonged to the king unless the owner made pursuit of the felon, took him, and...
A short, sleeveless coat or garment for men, worn under the coat, extending no lower than the hips, and covering the waist.
One who, or that which, waits; an attendant; a servant in attendance, esp.
A female waiter or attendant; a waiting maid or waiting woman.
The act of waiving, or not insisting on, some right, claim, or privilege.
One who builds walls. The wels.
To roll one's self about, as in mire; to tumble and roll about; to move lazily or heavily in any medium.
The fruit or nut of any tree of the genus Juglans; also, the tree, and its timber.

A dance performed by two persons in circular figures with a whirling motion.
Beads made of shells, used by the North American Indians as money, and also wrought into belts, etc., as an ornament.
A small stick; a rod; a verge. With good smart blows of a wand on his back.
One who wanders; a rambler; one who roves; hence, one who deviates from duty.
a. & n. from Wander, v. Wandering albatross (Zoöl.), the great white albatross.
To be diminished; to decrease; -- contrasted with wax, and especially applied to the illuminated part of the moon.
The state of not having; the condition of being without anything; absence or scarcity of what is needed or desired.
Absent; lacking; missing; also, deficient; destitute; needy.
Untrained; undisciplined; unrestrained; hence, loose; free; luxuriant.
The quality or state of being wanton; negligence of restraint; sportiveness.
A small, hard tumor which is produced on the back of a horse by the heat or pressure of the saddle in traveling.
A storehouse for wares, or goods. Addison. 1. To deposit or secure in a warehouse.
Military service; military life; contest carried on by enemies; hostilities.
Fit for war; disposed for war.
One who, or that which, warms.

In a warm manner; ardently.
The quality or state of being warm; gentle heat.

A man engaged or experienced in war, or in the military life; a soldier.
The black grouper (Epinephelus nigritus) of the southern coasts of the United States.
A small, usually hard, tumor on the skin formed by enlargement of its vascular papillæ, and thickening of the epidermis which covers them.
feeling or showing caution about possible dangers or problems
