definition: Appearing as if overlaid with a thin layer of different color; -- said of the colors of certain birds and insects.
The act of one who washes; the act of cleansing with water; ablution. 2.
Any one of numerous species of stinging hymenopterous insects, esp. any of the numerous species of the genus Vespa, which includes the true, or social, wasps, some of which are called yellow jackets.
A dog kept to watch and guard premises or property, and to give notice of the approach of intruders.
One who watches; one who sits up or continues; a diligent observer; specifically, one who attends upon the sick during the night.
The leaves of Sarace. See Trumpets.
Full of watch; vigilant; attentive; careful to observe closely; observant.
One set to watch; a person who keeps guard; a guard; a sentinel. 2. Specifically, one who guards a building, or the streets of a city, by night.
A tower in which a sentinel is placed to watch for enemies, the approach of danger, or the like.
A fall, or perpendicular descent, of the water of a river or stream, or a descent nearly perpendicular.
a. & n. from Water, v. Watering call (Mil.), a sound of trumpet or bugle summoning cavalry soldiers to assemble for the purpose of watering their horses.
The very large ovoid or roundish fruit of a cucurbitaceous plant (Citrullus vulgaris) of many varieties.
Proof against penetration or permeation by water; impervious to water.
The whole region or extent of country which contributes to the supply of a river or lake.
A unit of power or activity equal to 107 C.G.S. units of power, or to work done at the rate of one joule a second.
Exhibiting a wavelike form or outline; undulating; intended; wavy.
A little wave; a ripple.
To play or move to and fro; to move one way and the other; hence, to totter.

To make weak; to lessen the strength of; to deprive of strength; to debilitate.
A weak or feeble creature. Shak. "All looking on him as a weakling, which would post to the grave." Fuller.

The quality or state of being weak; want of strength or firmness; lack of vigor.
To accustom and reconcile, as a child or other young animal, to a want or deprivation of mother's milk.
Weapons, collectively.
Causing weariness; tiresome; tedious; weariful.
Having the strength exhausted by toil or exertion; worn out in respect to strength, endurance, etc.
Any one of various species of small carnivores belonging to the genus Putorius, as the ermine and ferret.
The state of the air or atmosphere with respect to heat or cold, wetness or dryness, calm or storm, clearness or cloudiness, or any other meteorological phenomena.
To unite, as threads of any kind, in such a manner as to form a texture.
One who weaves, or whose occupation is to weave. "Weavers of linen." P.
The act of one who, or that which, weaves; the act or art of forming cloth in a loom by the union or intertexture of threads.
Joined in wedlock; married. Let wwedded dame. Pope. 2. Of or pertaining to wedlock, or marriage.
A piece of metal, or other hard material, thick at one end, and tapering to a thin edge at the other, used in splitting wood, rocks, etc., in raising heavy bodies, and the like.
The fourth day of the week; the next day after Tuesday. Ash Wednesday.
The lapwing; the wipe; -- so called from its cry. imp. of Weep, for wept.
The act of one who weeps; lamentation with tears; shedding of tears. 1.
a. & n. from Weigh, v. Weighing cage, a cage in which small living animals may be conveniently weighed.
A dam in a river to stop and raise the water, for the purpose of conducting it to a mill, forming a fish pond, or the like.

Well-doing or well-being in any respect; the enjoyment of health and the common blessings of life.
The visible regions of the air; the vault of heaven; the sky. On the welkne shoon the sterres lyght.
To roll, as the body of an animal; to tumble about, especially in anything foul or defiling.
A young woman; a girl; a maiden. Shak. Lord and lady, groom and wench.
A person transformed into a wolf in form and appetite, either temporarily or permanently, whether by supernatural influences, by witchcraft, or voluntarily.
To strike; to beat; to give a heavy or resounding blow to; to thrash; to make with whacks.
Very large; whapping.
A kind of stand, or piece of furniture, having shelves for books, ornaments, etc.
Whatever. "In whatsoever shape he lurk." Milton. Whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do.
A cereal grass (Triticum vulgare) and its grain, which furnishes a white flour for bread, and, next to rice, is the grain most largely used by the human race.
To entice by soft words; to cajole; to flatter; to coax. The unlucky art of wheedling fools.
A light vehicle for conveying small loads. It has two handles and one wheel, and is rolled by a single person.
Having wheels; -- used chiefly in composition.
One of the young of a dog or a beast of prey; a puppy; a cub.
From what place; hence, from what or which source, origin, antecedent, premise, or the like.
About where; near what or which place; -- used interrogatively and relatively.
At which place; where. Chaucer. At last they came whereas that lady bode.
By which; -- used relatively. "You take my life when you take the means whereby I life." Shak.
For which reason; so; -- used relatively. Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them.
In which; in which place, thing, time, respect, or the like; -- used relatively.
Upon which; in consequence of which; after which. The townsmen mutinied and sent to Essex.
At or in whatever place; wheresoever. He can not but love virtue wherever it is.
With which; -- used relatively. The love wherewith thou hast loved me.

Full of, or characterized by, whims; actuated by a whim; having peculiar notions.

To utter a plaintive cry, as some animals; to mean with a childish noise.
To utter the ordinary call or cry of a horse; to neigh. The ordinary cry or call of a horse.
The lash of a whip, -- usually made of thongs of leather, or of cords, braided or twisted.

To turn round rapidly; to cause to rotate with velocity; to make to revolve.
A violent windstorm of limited extent, as the tornado, characterized by an inward spiral motion of the air with an upward current in the center.
A light carriage built for rapid motion; -- called also tim- whiskey. An intoxicating liquor distilled from grain, potatoes, etc., especially in Scotland, Ireland, and the United States.
One who whispers. 2. A tattler; one who tells secrets; a conveyer of intelligence secretly.
The smallest part or particle imaginable; a bit; a jot; an iota; -- generally used in an adverbial phrase in a negative sentence.
The blue-winged snow goose. (b) The surf scoter. A form of self-propelling torpedo.
Leucorrh 2. The finest flour made from white wheat. 3. Cloth or garments of a plain white color.
Any wash or liquid composition for whitening something, as a wash for making the skin fair.

Sale of goods by the piece or large quantity, as distinguished from retail.

The hoopoe. 1. To utter a whoop, or loud cry, as eagerness, enthusiasm, or enjoyment.

A circle of two or more leaves, flowers, or other organs, about the same part or joint of a stem.
A street; a village; a castle; a dwelling; a place of work, or exercise of authority.
The quality or state of being wicked; departure from the rules of the divine or the moral law.

To make wide or wider; to extend in breadth; to increase the width of.
Spread to a great distance; widely extended; extending far and wide.
A man who has lost his wife by death, and has not married again. Shak.
The quality of being wide; extent from side to side; breadth; wideness.
To move to and fro with a quick, jerking motion; to bend rapidly, or with a wavering motion, from side to side.
To bewilder; to perplex. Long lost and wildered in the maze of fate. Pope.
In a wild manner; without cultivation; with disorder; rudely; distractedly.
In a willing manner; with free will; without reluctance; cheerfully. Chaucer.
The quality or state of being willing; free choice or consent of the will.
Abounding with willows. Where willowy Camus lingers with delight. Gray.
Full of wiles, tricks, or stratagems; using craft or stratagem to accomplish a purpose.
To shrink, as from a blow, or from pain; to flinch; to start back. I will not stir, nor wince, nor speak a word.
To wince; to shrink; to kick with impatience or uneasiness. A kick, as of a beast, from impatience or uneasiness.
Anything blown down or off by the wind, as fruit from a tree, or the tree itself, or a portion of a forest prostrated by a violent wind, etc.
A call by the boatswain's whistle. Twisting from a direct line or an even surface.

Consisting of wind; accompanied or characterized by wind; exposed to wind.
A place where grapes are converted into wine.
One of the casks stowed in the wings of a vessel's hold, being smaller than such as are stowed more amidships.
To separate, and drive off, the chaff from by means of wind; to fan.
Cheerful; merry; gay; light-hearted. Misled by ill example, and a winsome nature.
Suitable to winter; resembling winter, or what belongs to winter; brumal.
Having no wire; specif. (Elec.), designating, or pertaining to, a method of telegraphy, telephony, etc., in which the messages, etc., are transmitted through space by electric waves.
The act of one that wires anything. 2. The wires or conductors employed in a system of electric distribution.
Made of wire; like wire; drawn out like wire. 2. Capable of endurance.
The quality of being wise; knowledge, and the capacity to make due use of it.
In a wise manner; prudently; judiciously; discreetly; with wisdom. And wisely learn to curb thy sorrows wild.
a. & n. from Wish, v. t. Wishing bone. See Wishbone. -- Wishing cap, a cap fabled to give one whatever he wishes for when wearing it.
Longing; wishful; desirous. Lifting up one of my sashes, I cast many a wistful, melancholy look towards the sea.
The practices or art of witches; sorcery; enchantments; intercourse with evil spirits.
To take back or away, as what has been bestowed or enjoyed; to draw back.
The act of withdrawing; withdrawment; retreat; retraction. Fielding.
To fade; to lose freshness; to become sapless; to become sapless; to dry or shrivel up.
Faded; dried up; shriveled; wilted; wasted; wasted away. -- With"ered*ness, n.
The ridge between the shoulder bones of a horse, at the base of the neck.
To stand against; to oppose; to resist, either with physical or moral force.
Destitute of wit or understanding; wanting thought; hence, indiscreet.
A person who has little wit or understanding; a pretender to wit or smartness.
A witty saying; a sentence or phrase which is affectedly witty; an attempt at wit.
Knowingly; with knowledge; by design.
, pl of Wife.
The character or practices o "He acquired a reputation bordering on wizardry." J.
To wither; to dry. Wizened; thin; weazen; withered. A little lonely, wizen, strangely clad boy.
Dried; shriveled; withered; shrunken; weazen.
Full of woe; sorrowful; distressed with grief or calamity; afflicted; wretched.
The glutton. 2. A nickname for an inhabitant of Michigan.

Woke. Chaucer.

One who, or that which, wraps. 2. That in which anything is wrapped, or inclosed.
Something twisted, intertwined, or curled.
The act of wrecking, or state of being wrecked. 2. That which has been wrecked.

Any one of numerous species of small singing birds belonging to Troglodytes and numerous allied of the family Troglodytidæ.
